Mumbai floods regularly cause outbreaks. Agricultural & Occupational Risk: Farmers (rice paddy workers), sewer workers, animal handlers, and military personnel are
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations CRITICAL — Start Doxycycline on Suspicion: Leptospirosis mortality increases dramatically with delayed treatment. During monsoon, any patient with fever + myalgia + exposure to floodwater should receive Doxycycline immediately. Do NOT wait for lab results. Early antibiotics

diseases.name: Snakebite Envenomation
tests: 1. Clinical Assessment (Immediate — at Bedside)

20-Minute Whole Blood Clotting Test (WBCT20): Place 2ml venous blood in clean, dry glass test tube. Leave undisturbed for 20 minutes at room temperature. Tilt — if blood remains liquid (non-clotting) = positive = viper envenomation with coagulopathy. THE single most important bedside test. Available everywhere. Repeat every 6 hours for 24 hours.

Neurological Assessment: Check for ptosis (ask patient to look up — eyelid droops), diplopia (double vision), difficulty swallowing (ask to drink water), difficulty breathing (count respiratory rate, single-breath count test — normal >20). Document every 1-2 hours.
Local Examination: Mark swelling margin with pen and time. Measure limb circumference at fixed points hourly. Document progressive swelling, blistering, necrosis.
2. Laboratory Tests
Coagulation Profile: PT/INR, APTT, Fibrinogen, FDP/D-Dimer. Fibrinogen <100 mg/dL and elevated FDP confirm consumption coagulopathy (DIC) from viper venom.
Complete Blood Count: May show hemolysis (fragmented RBCs), thrombocytopenia.
Renal Function (Creatinine, BUN, Electrolytes): Monitor for AKI — creatinine rising, hyperkalemia, oliguria. Especially with Russell's viper.
Urine: Dark/red urine = myoglobinuria/hemoglobinuria. Dipstick positive for blood without RBCs on microscopy.
3. Species Identification
generic_medicines: 1. Anti-Snake Venom (ASV) — Life-Saving Treatment

Indian Polyvalent ASV: Effective against Big Four (Russell's Viper, Cobra, Krait, Saw-Scaled Viper). Reconstitute 10 vials (100ml) in 200ml Normal Saline, infuse IV over 1 hour. Repeat dose if WBCT20 remains positive after 6 hours (hemotoxic) or paralysis progresses after 1-2 hours (neurotoxic).

ASV Reaction Management: Anaphylaxis/anaphylactoid reactions occur in 10-40% of cases. Pre-medicate with IV Hydrocortisone 200mg + SC Adrenaline 0.25mg (subcutaneous, low dose). Keep Adrenaline (1:1000) ready for anaphylaxis (0.5mg IM for adults).
2. First Aid (BEFORE Hospital)
DO: Immobilize bitten limb (splint like fracture — Pressure Immobilization for neurotoxic bites), reassure, transport to hospital IMMEDIATELY, remove tight jewelry/clothing.
DO NOT: Apply tourniquet (causes gangrene), cut/suck wound, apply ice/heat, give traditional remedies, waste time with faith healers. These are harmful and delay life-saving ASV.
3. Organ-Specific Management

Respiratory Failure: Intubate and ventilate. Neostigmine test for Krait/Cobra (Neostigmine 0.05mg/kg IV with Atropine 0.6mg — if improvement = acetylcholinesterase inhibitor-responsive neurotoxicity, continue Neostigmine). Ventilate until paralysis reverses (may take 24-72 hours for Krait).
Renal Failure: Aggressive IV fluids to maintain urine output >30ml/hr. Dialysis if oliguric/anuric AKI. Alkalinize urine (IV NaHCO3) for myoglobinuria.
Coagulopathy: ASV is the ONLY treatment (FFP/platelets are NOT effective as venom continues consuming clotting factors until neutralized by ASV). Repeat WBCT20 every 6 hours.
4. Wound Management
Tetanus Prophylaxis: TT/Td for all snakebite patients. TIG if indicated.
Antibiotics: For secondary infection of necrotic wounds. Amoxicillin-Clavulanate + Metronidazole.
Surgical: Fasciotomy for compartment syndrome. Debridement for necrosis. Skin grafting if needed.
janaushadhi_medicines: Adrenaline Injection (Epinephrine 1:1000): For ASV anaphylaxis management.

Note: Anti-Snake Venom is manufactured by Haffkine, VINS Bioproducts, Bharat Serums, Premium Serums in India. Supplied to government hospitals through state procurement. Cost: ₹300-500 per vial (10 vials per dose = ₹3,000-5,000).
ayurvedic_medicines: IMPORTANT NOTE — No Ayurvedic/Herbal Antidote for Snakebite: There is NO proven Ayurvedic, herbal, or traditional remedy that neutralizes snake venom. Anti-Snake Venom (ASV) is the ONLY effective treatment. Going to faith healers/ojhas instead of hospital KILLS patients. However, traditional first-aid and recovery support exists:
Guduchi/Giloy: Published research on anti-venom/anti-inflammatory properties but INSUFFICIENT as standalone treatment. May support post-envenomation recovery. Not a substitute for ASV.
Andrographis paniculata (Kalmegh): Some in-vitro anti-venom activity in research. NOT clinically proven. Do NOT rely on this.
Supportive Recovery Herbs: After hospital discharge, Ashwagandha (stress recovery), Guduchi (immune support), and Punarnava (renal recovery for AKI patients) may aid rehabilitation.

Traditional Tourniquet/Incision — HARMFUL: Traditional practices of tight tourniquets, wound incision, suction, and cauterization cause more harm than the venom itself — leading to gangrene, infection, and tissue loss.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors India — World's Snakebite Capital: India has ~58,000 snakebite deaths annually (Lancet 2020) — highest globally. Most deaths occur in rural farming communities (June-October, during monsoon/harvest when snake-human encounters peak). Bihar, UP, MP, Jharkhand, Rajasthan, and AP are highest-burden states. Big Four Venomous Snakes: Russell's Viper (Daboia russelii), Common Cobra (Naja naja), Common Krait (Bungarus caeruleus), and Saw-Scaled Viper (Echis ca
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations ABSOLUTE CRITICAL — Hospital + ASV Only: Snakebite kills 58,000 Indians annually. The ONLY life-saving treatment is Anti-Snake Venom given in a hospital. Every minute spent with traditional healers instead of travelling to hospital increases death risk. ASHA workers must edu

diseases.name: Rabies
tests: 1. Post-Bite Assessment (Critical for PEP Decision)

WHO Category Classification: Category I: touching/feeding animal, lick on intact skin → NO PEP needed. Category II: nibbling on uncovered skin, minor scratch without bleeding, lick on broken skin → Vaccine only. Category III: Single/multiple transdermal bites, scratches with bleeding, licks on mucous membranes, bat exposure → Vaccine + RIG (Rabies Immunoglobulin). This classification GUIDES treatment.
Animal Observation: If biting dog can be identified and observed: observe for 10 days. If healthy after 10 days, rabies excluded. However, START PEP immediately — do not wait for observation result.
2. Diagnosis of Clinical Rabies (Once Symptoms Appear)
Clinical Diagnosis: Hydrophobia + aerophobia in a patient with history of animal bite is virtually diagnostic. Lab confirmation is post-mortem in most cases.
Nuchal Skin Biopsy: Full-thickness skin biopsy from nape of neck (hair follicle area). Direct Fluorescent Antibody (DFA) test for rabies antigen in nerve fibers. Antemortem diagnostic test.
Saliva RT-PCR: Detects rabies virus RNA in saliva. Sensitivity improves with repeated sampling.
Corneal Impression Smear: DFA testing of corneal epithelial cells. Less sensitive than skin biopsy.
3. Post-Mortem Confirmation
Brain DFA/PCR: Gold standard. Examination of brain tissue (hippocampus) for Negri bodies (intracytoplasmic inclusion bodies) and rabies antigen by DFA.
generic_medicines: 1. Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP) — LIFE-SAVING (Before Symptoms)

Wound Washing (IMMEDIATE — Most Critical First Aid): Wash bite wound immediately with running water and soap/detergent for 15 minutes. Apply povidone-iodine. This single step reduces rabies risk by up to 90%. Must be done at the SCENE before going to hospital.

Anti-Rabies Vaccine (ARV): Intramuscular (Essen regimen): 5 doses on days 0, 3, 7, 14, 28. Intradermal (Updated Thai Red Cross regimen): 4 visits — 2 sites on days 0, 3, 7, 28. ID regimen saves 60-80% vaccine. India recommends both regimens.

Rabies Immunoglobulin (RIG): For Category III bites. Infiltrate maximum dose around wound, remainder IM at site distant from vaccine. ERIG: 40 IU/kg. HRIG: 20 IU/kg. Provides immediate passive immunity until vaccine generates active immunity.
2. Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis (PrEP)

For High-Risk Groups: Veterinarians, lab workers, animal handlers, wildlife workers. 3 doses on days 0, 7, 21-28 (IM or ID). If bitten after PrEP: only 2 booster doses needed (days 0, 3) — NO RIG needed.
3. Clinical Rabies — Palliative Care Only

Once symptoms appear, rabies is 100% fatal. Milwaukee Protocol (intensive care attempt) has near-zero success. Palliative care: sedation (Midazolam/Morphine), pain management, family counseling. Compassionate end-of-life care.
4. Animal Bite Prevention
Dog Population Management: Animal Birth Control (ABC) programme. Stray dog sterilization and vaccination. India's ABC programme is the largest globally.
Community Education: Do not approach/provoke stray dogs. Supervise children around dogs. Report stray dog packs to municipal authorities.
janaushadhi_medicines: Povidone-Iodine Solution 5%: For wound cleaning. Essential first aid.
Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: For pain at bite site.

Note: Anti-Rabies Vaccine (ARV) and Rabies Immunoglobulin (RIG) are provided FREE at government Anti-Rabies Clinics in district hospitals. Private clinics charge ₹300-500 per ARV dose and ₹350-8,000 per RIG vial depending on type (ERIG/HRIG).
ayurvedic_medicines: CRITICAL NOTE — No Ayurvedic/Herbal Cure for Rabies: Rabies is 100% fatal once symptoms develop. There is NO herbal, Ayurvedic, homeopathic, or traditional remedy that can treat rabies. The ONLY effective intervention is post-exposure prophylaxis (vaccine + RIG) given BEFORE symptoms appear.

Wound Washing Is the Best First Aid: Thorough washing of the bite wound with soap and running water for 15 minutes is the single most effective first-aid measure — reducing rabies risk by up to 90%. This can be done at home immediately after a bite.

Turmeric Paste on Wound: Traditional Indian practice of applying haldi paste on wounds has mild antisepial properties but is NOT a substitute for proper wound washing with soap + povidone-iodine. Apply AFTER thorough washing if desired.
Post-PEP Recovery: Ashwagandha and Guduchi may support recovery from the stress and anxiety of dog bite and PEP process, but have NO anti-rabies activity.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors India — 36% of Global Rabies Deaths: India accounts for approximately 20,000 rabies deaths annually — the highest globally. Stray dog bites are the primary cause (>95%). Dog population: estimated 60 million stray dogs. Dog Bite Epidemic: India reports 17+ million dog bites annually. Only 50-60% receive proper post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP). Major gaps: rural areas lack anti-rabies vaccine (ARV) and Rabies Immunoglobulin (RIG) stock. Child Victims: Chi
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations ABSOLUTE CRITICAL — Rabies Is 100% Fatal Without PEP: Once clinical symptoms appear, NO treatment can save the patient. Death is certain within 7-10 days. The ONLY chance of survival is post-exposure prophylaxis (vaccine + RIG) given AFTER bite but BEFORE symptom onset. Wash

diseases.name: Tetanus
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (Tetanus Is a Clinical Diagnosis)

Clinical Recognition: Trismus + generalized rigidity + reflex spasms in patient with wound history (or neonatal cord contamination) = tetanus. NO laboratory test confirms tetanus. Diagnosis is ENTIRELY clinical. Culture of wound for C. tetani is unreliable and NOT required.

Spatula Test: Touch posterior pharyngeal wall with spatula. Normal response: gag and push spatula out. Tetanus: reflex jaw spasm — patient bites the spatula. Sensitivity 94%, Specificity 100%. Simple, bedside, definitive.
2. Severity Grading (Ablett Classification)

Grade I (Mild): Trismus, mild spasticity, no spasms, no respiratory compromise. Grade II (Moderate): Moderate trismus/rigidity, mild-moderate spasms, tachycardia. Grade III (Severe): Severe rigidity, frequent prolonged spasms, respiratory compromise, tachycardia >120. Grade IV (Very Severe): Grade III + autonomic instability.
3. Blood Tests (Supportive Only)
Complete Blood Count: Usually normal. Leukocytosis may indicate wound infection.
Serum Creatine Kinase (CK): Elevated from massive muscle spasm/rhabdomyolysis.
ABG: Respiratory acidosis from hypoventilation during spasms.
Anti-Tetanus Antibody Level: >0.1 IU/mL = protective. If antibody levels are protective, diagnosis of tetanus is unlikely. Research use only.
generic_medicines: 1. Neutralize Unbound Toxin

Human Tetanus Immunoglobulin (TIG): 3000-5000 IU IM (single dose). Neutralizes circulating (unbound) tetanus toxin. Given as soon as possible. DOES NOT reverse already bound toxin (hence treatment only limits progression).
Equine Anti-Tetanus Serum (ATS): 10,000-20,000 IU IV/IM if TIG unavailable. Risk of anaphylaxis — test dose first. Less preferred than TIG.
2. Control Spasms & Rigidity
IV Diazepam: First-line for spasm control. 10-40mg IV every 1-8 hours. High doses may be needed (up to 200mg/day). Monitor for respiratory depression.
IV Midazolam: Continuous infusion 0.1-0.3mg/kg/hr. For ICU-managed severe tetanus. Titratable.
IV Magnesium Sulfate: 40mg/kg loading then 1-3g/hr infusion. Controls spasms AND autonomic dysfunction. Emerging first-line in many centres.
3. Airway & Respiratory Management
Tracheostomy: Early tracheostomy recommended for Grade III-IV tetanus. Secures airway, prevents death from laryngospasm.
Mechanical Ventilation: Muscle relaxants (Vecuronium/Pancuronium) + ventilation for severe spasms unresponsive to sedation. May need ventilation for 2-4 weeks.
4. Wound Management
Wound Debridement: Clean, debride contaminated wound under anesthesia. Remove foreign bodies, necrotic tissue — the source of ongoing toxin production.
Metronidazole: 500mg IV every 8 hours for 7-10 days. Kills C. tetani at wound site. Preferred over Penicillin (which has GABA-antagonist properties that may worsen spasms).
5. Active Immunization
Tetanus Toxoid (TT/Td): Full primary vaccination course during recovery — tetanus infection does NOT provide natural immunity. Patient must be actively immunized.
janaushadhi_medicines: Metronidazole Injection 500mg/100ml: Antibiotic for C. tetani. Available at Jan Aushadhi.
Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: For pain. Pack of 10.
Note: TIG (Human Tetanus Immunoglobulin) and ATS (Anti-Tetanus Serum) are available at government hospitals. TT vaccine provided free under UIP.
ayurvedic_medicines: CRITICAL NOTE — No Ayurvedic Cure for Tetanus: Tetanus is caused by Clostridium tetani neurotoxin — one of the most potent biological toxins known. There is NO herbal or Ayurvedic remedy that neutralizes tetanus toxin. Treatment requires TIG + ICU care. However:
Ashwagandha: May support recovery from prolonged ICU stay and muscle wasting. 300-600mg twice daily during convalescence.
Bala (Sida cordifolia): Classical Balya (strength-promoting) herb for post-illness muscle recovery. Part of Bala taila for massage.
Abhyanga (Oil Massage): Gentle oil massage during recovery phase to restore muscle tone and reduce stiffness after prolonged rigidity. Bala Ashwagandhadi taila recommended.
Nutritional Recovery: Chyawanprash and high-protein diet essential during recovery from the extreme metabolic demands of tetanus spasms.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Tetanus Still Kills in India: India reports 500-700+ tetanus deaths annually (likely underreported). Neonatal tetanus deaths reduced but still occur from home deliveries with unhygienic cord practices. Adult tetanus from wounds, especially in agricultural workers. Wound Contamination in Rural Settings: Soil contamination of wounds, nail pricks, thorn injuries during barefoot farming, rusty metal cuts — common in rural India where Clostridium tetani spo
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations ABSOLUTE CRITICAL — Tetanus Is Preventable: A ₹10 TT vaccine prevents a disease that costs ₹5-10 lakhs to treat in ICU and still kills 10-50%. Ensure COMPLETE vaccination: childhood DPT/Pentavalent + adult boosters every 10 years + TT in pregnancy. Neonatal Tetanus — Clean C

diseases.name: Rheumatic Fever
tests: 1. Revised Jones Criteria (2015) — Diagnosis of Acute Rheumatic Fever

Jones Criteria (Moderate-High Risk Populations like India): Initial ARF: 2 Major criteria, OR 1 Major + 2 Minor criteria, PLUS evidence of preceding GAS infection. Major criteria: Carditis (clinical/subclinical on echo), Arthritis (polyarthritis OR monoarthritis OR polyarthralgia), Chorea, Erythema marginatum, Subcutaneous nodules. Minor criteria: Polyarthralgia, Fever ≥38.5°C, ESR ≥30 or CRP ≥3, Prolonged PR interval on ECG.
2. Evidence of Preceding GAS Infection
Throat Swab Culture / Rapid Antigen Detection Test (RADT): Positive for Group A Streptococcus. Confirms recent GAS pharyngitis. RADT gives results in 10 minutes.
ASO Titer (Anti-Streptolysin O): Elevated >200 IU/mL (adults), >400 (children). Rises 1-3 weeks after infection, peaks at 3-5 weeks. Most commonly used serological evidence.
Anti-DNase B Titer: Alternative/complementary to ASO. Elevated values confirm recent GAS infection.
3. Cardiac Assessment

Echocardiography: Essential for ALL suspected ARF. Detects subclinical carditis (valvular regurgitation not audible on auscultation). Identifies chronic valvular damage. WHO recommends echo-based RHD screening.
12-Lead ECG: Prolonged PR interval (first-degree heart block) is a minor criterion. Also detects AF, LVH in chronic RHD.
Chest X-ray: Cardiomegaly, pulmonary congestion in heart failure. Straightening of left heart border in mitral stenosis.
generic_medicines: 1. Acute Rheumatic Fever Treatment

Benzathine Penicillin G (IM): 600,000 IU for <27kg, 1.2 million IU for ≥27kg — single dose. Eradicates GAS from pharynx. First-line. Alternative: Oral Penicillin V 250-500mg 2-3 times daily for 10 days, or Amoxicillin 50mg/kg/day for 10 days.

Aspirin (Anti-Inflammatory): 80-100mg/kg/day in 4 divided doses for 2 weeks, then taper over 4-6 weeks. For arthritis — dramatic response within 24-48 hours. Monitor for salicylism (tinnitus, GI upset).
Prednisolone (For Carditis): 1-2mg/kg/day for 2-3 weeks, then taper over 4-6 weeks. For moderate-severe carditis. Suppresses inflammation but does NOT prevent long-term valve damage.
Heart Failure Management: Furosemide, ACE inhibitors, Digoxin as indicated for congestive heart failure from acute carditis.
2. Secondary Prophylaxis (LIFELONG for RHD)

Benzathine Penicillin G (IM): 1.2 million IU every 3-4 weeks. LIFELONG for patients with RHD (or until age 40, whichever is longer). Every 21 days preferred in India (high recurrence rate). THE most important intervention — prevents recurrent ARF attacks that worsen valve damage.
Oral Penicillin V: 250mg twice daily. If IM injection not feasible. Less reliable due to compliance issues.
3. Chronic RHD Management
Anticoagulation: Warfarin for AF with RHD (target INR 2-3). Mechanical valve replacement requires lifelong anticoagulation.
Valve Surgery: Balloon Mitral Valvotomy (BMV) for severe mitral stenosis. Valve repair/replacement for severe regurgitation. Available at cardiac surgery centres.
janaushadhi_medicines: Benzathine Penicillin G Injection 1.2MU: Secondary prophylaxis. Available at government hospitals FREE.
Aspirin Tablets IP 325mg: Anti-inflammatory for arthritis. Pack of 10.
Prednisolone Tablets IP 5mg: For carditis. Pack of 10.
Furosemide Tablets IP 40mg: For heart failure. Pack of 10.
Enalapril Tablets IP 5mg: ACE inhibitor for heart failure. Pack of 10.
Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):
ayurvedic_medicines: Arjuna (Terminalia arjuna): Classical Hridya (cardiotonic). Published evidence for cardiac support. 500mg-1g bark powder twice daily with milk. Arjunarishta 15-30ml twice daily. Supports cardiac recovery in chronic RHD.
Ashwagandha: Adaptogenic cardioprotective. Supports recovery from inflammatory conditions. 300-600mg twice daily.
Guduchi/Giloy: Anti-inflammatory Rasayana. May help modulate autoimmune inflammatory response. 500mg-1g twice daily.
Punarnava: Diuretic and cardiotonic. Supports fluid management in heart failure. Punarnavadi Kwatha.
Dabur Arjunarishta: Branded cardiotonic preparation. 15-30ml twice daily.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors India — Largest RHD Burden Globally: India has an estimated 10-12 million people with RHD — more than any other country. RHD causes 25-50% of all cardiac hospitalizations in India. 250,000+ die annually from RHD-related complications. Poverty-Driven Streptococcal Infections: Overcrowded living, poor hygiene, and limited healthcare access mean Group A Streptococcal (GAS) pharyngitis often goes untreated → triggers autoimmune response → rheumatic fever.
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations CRITICAL — Treat Every Sore Throat: Rheumatic fever is ENTIRELY PREVENTABLE by treating GAS pharyngitis with a complete 10-day course of Penicillin/Amoxicillin. A ₹20 antibiotic course prevents a lifelong heart disease. ASHA workers must educate: sore throat in children aged

diseases.name: Goiter & Iodine Deficiency
tests: 1. Clinical Assessment

Thyroid Palpation & WHO Goiter Grading: Palpate thyroid from behind patient. Grade goiter 0/1/2. Assess for nodules, tenderness, fixity, lymphadenopathy. ASHA workers can be trained for basic goiter screening.
2. Thyroid Function Tests

Serum TSH (Thyroid Stimulating Hormone): SINGLE BEST screening test. Elevated TSH (>4.5 mIU/L) = hypothyroidism. Suppressed TSH (<0.1) = hyperthyroidism. Neonatal TSH screening identifies congenital hypothyroidism.
Free T4 (Thyroxine): Low free T4 with elevated TSH = overt hypothyroidism. Normal free T4 with elevated TSH = subclinical hypothyroidism.
Free T3: Elevated in hyperthyroidism. Less useful for IDD diagnosis.
3. Iodine Status Assessment
Urinary Iodine Concentration (UIC): Population-level indicator. Median UIC <100 μg/L = iodine deficiency. <20 μg/L = severe deficiency. Used in NIDDCP surveillance — NOT for individual diagnosis.
4. Imaging
Thyroid Ultrasound: Measures thyroid volume (confirms goiter), identifies nodules, guides FNAC. Available at CHC/district level.
generic_medicines: 1. Iodine Supplementation
Iodized Salt (Universal): The PRIMARY intervention. 15-40 ppm iodine at production level (translates to 15 ppm at consumer level). ALL cooking salt must be iodized — this is law in India.
Iodized Oil (For Severely Deficient Areas): Single oral dose of iodized oil (400mg iodine) provides protection for 1 year. Used in severe endemic areas where salt iodization coverage is inadequate.
2. Thyroid Hormone Replacement (For Hypothyroidism)

Levothyroxine (T4): 1.6 μg/kg/day for adults (start low 25-50 μg in elderly/cardiac patients). Titrate to normalize TSH. Lifelong in permanent hypothyroidism. Take on empty stomach, 30-60 minutes before breakfast.

Congenital Hypothyroidism: Levothyroxine 10-15 μg/kg/day started within first 2 weeks of life to prevent intellectual disability. Neonatal screening detects this. EARLY treatment = NORMAL development.
3. Goiter-Specific Treatment
Small Goiter: Iodized salt + Levothyroxine (TSH suppression therapy). Many goiters shrink with adequate iodine.
Large/Compressive Goiter: Thyroidectomy (surgical removal) for compressive symptoms, cosmetic concerns, or suspected malignancy.
4. Prevention (Population Level)
NIDDCP Programme: Universal salt iodization, monitoring salt quality at production/retail, urinary iodine surveillance, IEC campaigns. India's most successful micronutrient programme.
janaushadhi_medicines: Levothyroxine Tablets IP 25/50/100mcg: Thyroid replacement. Pack of 100.
Iodized Salt: Available at Jan Aushadhi stores.
Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):
ayurvedic_medicines: Kanchanara Guggulu: Classical formulation for Galaganda (goiter) in Ayurveda. Contains Kanchanara (Bauhinia variegata) bark and Guggulu. Specific for thyroid/neck swelling. 2 tablets twice daily. Published evidence for thyroid-supportive activity.
Varunadi Kashaya: Classical decoction for Gandamala (lymphatic/glandular swelling). Contains Varuna (Crataeva nurvala). 15-30ml twice daily.
Jalakumbhi (Pistia stratiotes): Natural source of iodine in Ayurvedic texts. Used in iodine-deficient areas traditionally.
Ashwagandha: Adaptogenic with thyroid-stimulatory activity. Published evidence for improving TSH and T4 levels in subclinical hypothyroidism. 300-600mg twice daily.
Guggulu (Commiphora mukul): Traditional Medohara and metabolism-supporting resin. Guggulsterones may support thyroid function. Part of Kanchanara Guggulu formulation.
Himalaya Guggul/Kanchanara: Branded formulation for thyroid and metabolic support.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors India's Iodine Deficiency Belt: The sub-Himalayan belt (J&K, HP, Uttarakhand, NE states), tribal areas, and flood-prone regions have iodine-depleted soil → iodine-deficient crops/water. 200+ million Indians were at IDD risk before universal salt iodization. Universal Salt Iodization (USI): India's NIDDCP (National Iodine Deficiency Disorders Control Programme) mandated iodized salt nationwide. Coverage now >90%. However, non-iodized salt (rock salt/sen
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations CRITICAL — Use ONLY Iodized Salt: The simplest way to prevent IDD is using iodized salt for ALL cooking. Non-iodized rock salt (sendha namak), sea salt, or Himalayan pink salt used exclusively can cause iodine deficiency. Even during Navratri fasting, use iodized sendha nama

diseases.name: Rickets & Vitamin D Deficiency
tests: 1. Clinical Assessment
Growth Chart Monitoring: Plot weight/height on WHO growth charts. Faltering growth + skeletal deformities = suspect rickets.
Skeletal Examination: Systematically check for bowing, wrist widening, rachitic rosary, Harrison's sulcus, craniotabes, delayed fontanelle closure.
2. Blood Tests
Serum 25(OH) Vitamin D: THE definitive test. Deficient: <20 ng/mL (50 nmol/L). Insufficient: 20-29 ng/mL. Sufficient: 30-100 ng/mL. Measure in ALL suspected cases.
Serum Calcium (Total & Ionized): Low or low-normal in Vitamin D deficiency. Severe hypocalcemia (<7 mg/dL) causes tetany and seizures.
Serum Phosphate: Low in nutritional rickets. High in renal rickets.
Alkaline Phosphatase (ALP): Elevated — sensitive marker of active rickets. ALP >500 IU/L strongly suggests rickets.
Serum PTH (Parathyroid Hormone): Elevated (secondary hyperparathyroidism) in response to low calcium/Vitamin D.
3. Radiology
generic_medicines: 1. Treatment of Vitamin D Deficiency Rickets

Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3) — Stoss Therapy: Single high dose — 60,000 IU oral weekly for 6-8 weeks (total 360,000-480,000 IU), then 400-1000 IU daily maintenance. OR single dose 300,000-600,000 IU IM (for compliance concerns). Dramatic improvement in 2-4 weeks.
Calcium Supplementation: Elemental calcium 30-75 mg/kg/day for 3-6 months during healing phase. Calcium carbonate or calcium citrate. Essential alongside Vitamin D for bone mineralization.
2. Prevention (Population Approach)
Vitamin D Supplementation for ALL Infants: 400 IU/day from birth to 1 year (especially exclusively breastfed). IAP recommendation. Continue 600 IU/day for 1-18 years.
Maternal Supplementation: 1000-2000 IU/day during pregnancy and lactation. Prevents neonatal deficiency.
Sun Exposure: 20-30 minutes of midday sun exposure (10 AM-3 PM) on face, arms, and legs — 2-3 times weekly. More needed for dark-skinned individuals. Not always practical in polluted urban India.
Food Fortification: India's FSSAI has approved Vitamin D fortification of milk and edible oils. Encourage fortified dairy products.
3. Surgical Correction
Orthopedic Surgery: For severe, established bowing that does not correct with medical treatment and growth. Corrective osteotomy after Vitamin D repletion and skeletal maturity.
janaushadhi_medicines: Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3) Sachets 60,000 IU: For weekly supplementation. Available at Jan Aushadhi.
Calcium Carbonate Tablets IP 500mg: Calcium supplementation.
Calcium + Vitamin D3 Tablets: Combination supplement.
Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):
ayurvedic_medicines: Praval Pishti (Coral Calcium): Classical Ayurvedic calcium supplement. Contains natural calcium carbonate from coral. Cooling (Pittashamaka). 250-500mg twice daily with milk. Traditional bone-strengthening preparation.
Mukta Pishti (Pearl Calcium): Classical calcium-rich preparation from pearls. Balya (strengthening) and calcium supplement. 125-250mg twice daily.

Laksha Guggulu: Classical formulation for Asthi-Sandhi Vikara (bone and joint disorders). Contains Laksha (shellac), Ashwagandha, Nagabala, Arjuna, Guggulu. Used for bone healing and strengthening. 2 tablets twice daily.
Ashwagandha: Adaptogenic, promotes bone density and muscle strength. Published evidence for improving calcium balance. 300-600mg twice daily.
Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus): Calcium-rich Rasayana. Supports bone health, especially in women. 3-5g powder with milk.

Cow's Milk (Go-Ksheera): Ayurveda's primary Asthi-Dhatu nourishing food. Rich in calcium and (when fortified) Vitamin D. 2 glasses daily for children. Traditional and still the best dietary calcium source.
Til/Sesame (Sesamum indicum): Rich in calcium (1000mg per 100g). Include tahini, til laddu, and til chikki in diet.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Paradox of the Sunny Country: Despite abundant sunlight, India has one of the highest Vitamin D deficiency burdens globally — 50-90% of Indians are deficient. Reasons: dark skin pigmentation (melanin blocks UV-B), cultural practices of covering skin/staying indoors, urban air pollution blocking UV-B, and vegetarian diet (low in Vitamin D-rich foods). Exclusive Breastfeeding Without Supplementation: Breast milk contains very little Vitamin D (10-80 IU/L
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations CRITICAL — Supplement ALL Breastfed Infants: Breast milk alone does NOT provide enough Vitamin D. Every exclusively breastfed infant needs 400 IU Vitamin D drops daily from birth. This prevents rickets entirely. Pediatricians and ASHA workers must recommend this routinely. I

diseases.name: Conjunctivitis
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (Usually Sufficient)

Clinical Differentiation: Viral: bilateral, watery discharge, preauricular lymph node, follicular conjunctiva. Bacterial: unilateral initially, mucopurulent discharge, papillary conjunctiva. Allergic: bilateral, intense itch, watery/stringy discharge, chemosis, history of atopy.
2. Laboratory Tests (Special Situations Only)
Conjunctival Swab Culture & Sensitivity: For severe bacterial conjunctivitis not responding to empirical therapy, neonatal conjunctivitis, and suspected gonococcal/chlamydial infection.
Gram Stain: Rapid identification — Gram-negative intracellular diplococci (Gonococcus), inclusion bodies (Chlamydia).
Chlamydia PCR/DFA: For suspected trachoma or chlamydial conjunctivitis.
3. Key Assessment
Slit Lamp Examination: To rule out corneal involvement (keratitis), anterior uveitis, and assess severity. Available at ophthalmology clinics.
Visual Acuity Check: MUST check in all conjunctivitis cases. Decreased vision = NOT simple conjunctivitis → urgent referral.
generic_medicines: 1. Bacterial Conjunctivitis
Chloramphenicol Eye Drops 0.5%: 1-2 drops every 2 hours for 2 days, then every 4 hours for 5 more days. First-line in India. Broad-spectrum and affordable.
Moxifloxacin Eye Drops 0.5%: 1 drop thrice daily for 7 days. For moderate-severe bacterial conjunctivitis. Broader spectrum.
Tobramycin Eye Drops 0.3%: Alternative. 1 drop every 4 hours for 7 days.
2. Viral Conjunctivitis

Supportive Care Only: Cold compresses, artificial tears for comfort. Antibiotics NOT needed for viral conjunctivitis. Self-limiting in 7-14 days. Hygiene to prevent spread — frequent handwashing, separate towels.
3. Allergic Conjunctivitis
Olopatadine Eye Drops 0.1%: Dual-action antihistamine + mast cell stabilizer. 1 drop twice daily. First-line for allergic conjunctivitis.
Oral Cetirizine: 10mg daily for systemic allergic symptoms accompanying eye allergy.
Cold Compresses: Soothing symptom relief for allergic conjunctivitis.
4. Neonatal Conjunctivitis
Ceftriaxone IM (Gonococcal): 25-50mg/kg single dose (max 125mg) for gonococcal ophthalmia neonatorum. EMERGENCY — refer immediately.
Azithromycin Oral (Chlamydial): 20mg/kg/day for 3 days.
Prophylaxis at Birth: 1% Tetracycline or 0.5% Erythromycin eye ointment within 1 hour of birth. Part of essential newborn care.
5. Trachoma

Ciprofloxacin Eye Drops 0.3%: Alternative antibiotic. Bottle.

Highly contagious — spreads rapidly in schools, communities, and families. Trachoma (Chlamydial) — Still Endemic: Trachoma (Chlamydia trachomatis) remains endemic in parts of rural India (Rajasthan, UP, tribal areas). Chronic infection causes scarring, trichiasis, and preventable blindness. WHO SAFE strategy (Su
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations CRITICAL — NEVER Use Steroid Eye Drops Without Ophthalmologist: Over-the-counter Dexamethasone/Betamethasone eye drops are dangerous when self-prescribed. They worsen herpes keratitis (causing blindness), raise eye pressure (glaucoma), and promote fungal infections. This is

diseases.name: Dental Caries & Oral Health
tests: 1. Clinical Oral Examination

Visual Examination: Inspect teeth for cavities, discoloration, plaque, calculus. Examine gums for redness, swelling, bleeding. Check for mucosal lesions (leukoplakia, erythroplakia — premalignant). Available at any health facility.
Dental Probing: Use dental explorer to detect cavity depth, softened dentin. Periodontal probe for gum pocket depth (>3mm = periodontitis).
2. Radiography
Intraoral Periapical X-ray (IOPA): Shows individual tooth structure, periapical pathology (abscess), and interproximal caries. Available at dental clinics.
Orthopantomogram (OPG): Panoramic view of all teeth, jaws, and TMJ. Assesses overall dental status, impactions, bone loss.
3. Additional Assessments
Pulp Vitality Testing: Cold test / Electric pulp test to determine if tooth nerve is alive. Guides treatment — filling vs root canal.
Oral Cancer Screening: Visual inspection + palpation of oral mucosa for suspicious lesions. Biopsy of persistent ulcers (>2 weeks), white/red patches. Toluidine blue staining for screening.
4. Key Diagnostic Criteria
generic_medicines: 1. Pain Management
Ibuprofen: 400-600mg every 6-8 hours. Best evidence-based dental analgesic. Anti-inflammatory.
Paracetamol: 500-1000mg every 4-6 hours. Alternative/combination with Ibuprofen.
Diclofenac: 50mg twice-thrice daily. Alternative NSAID.
2. Dental Infections
Amoxicillin: 500mg thrice daily for 5-7 days. First-line for dental abscess/infection.
Amoxicillin-Clavulanate: 625mg thrice daily for 5-7 days. For resistant infections.
Metronidazole: 400mg thrice daily. Add to Amoxicillin for mixed anaerobic infections (common in dental abscess).
3. Definitive Dental Treatment

Dental Filling (Restoration): Glass Ionomer Cement (GIC) — releases fluoride, bonds to tooth. Preferred in field settings (ART — Atraumatic Restorative Treatment). Composite resin for permanent teeth.
Root Canal Treatment: For deep caries with pulp involvement. Removes infected nerve, preserves tooth.
Extraction: For irreversibly damaged teeth. Simple extraction at PHC dental clinic. Surgical extraction for impactions.
Scaling & Root Planing: Professional cleaning to remove calculus/plaque. Treats gingivitis and early periodontitis.
4. Prevention
Fluoride Toothpaste (1000-1500 ppm): Brush twice daily with fluoride toothpaste. Single most effective caries prevention measure.

Oil Pulling (Kavala/Gandusha): Traditional Ayurvedic oral hygiene practice. Swish 1 tablespoon sesame/coconut oil in mouth for 15-20 minutes, spit out. Published evidence for reducing S. mutans count, plaque, and gingivitis. Daily practice.

India has ~3 lakh dentists but >80% practice in urban areas, leaving rural India severely underserved. Dentist:population ratio in rural India is ~1:300,000 vs recommended 1:7,500. Sugar Consumption & Oral Hygiene: Rising sugar consumption (sugar-sweetened beverages, sweets, processed foods) combined with poor brushing habits drives the caries epidemic. Only 50%
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Clove Oil — Genuinely Effective Dental First Aid: Clove oil (eugenol) is a proven dental analgesic accepted even by modern dentistry. For acute toothache in rural areas without dental access, clove oil applied on cotton to the painful tooth provides genuine relief while pati

diseases.name: Ascariasis
tests: 1. Stool Examination

Stool Microscopy (Ova Detection): Ascaris eggs — characteristic bile-stained, mammillated (bumpy-surfaced), oval, 60x45μm. Both fertilized (embryonated) and unfertilized eggs may be seen. Direct smear or concentration technique.
Kato-Katz Quantitative: Egg count per gram of stool determines intensity: Light (<5000 EPG), Moderate (5000-50,000), Heavy (>50,000). WHO recommended for programme monitoring.
2. Imaging
Abdominal Ultrasound: Can visualize live worms in intestine (characteristic 'railway track' sign — parallel echogenic lines), bile ducts, and gallbladder. Useful for hepatobiliary ascariasis.
Abdominal X-ray: For suspected intestinal obstruction — dilated bowel loops, air-fluid levels. Worm bolus may be visible.
3. Blood Tests
Complete Blood Count: Eosinophilia (5-15%) in active infection or during larval migration (Löffler's phase).
4. Diagnostic Criteria

Ascariasis confirmed by: Ascaris eggs in stool OR visualization of adult worm (passed or on imaging). In endemic settings with consistent symptoms, empirical Albendazole is appropriate without waiting for stool results.
generic_medicines: 1. Anti-Helminthic Treatment
Albendazole: 400mg single oral dose. WHO/NTDD recommended. >95% cure rate. First-line for all soil-transmitted helminths. Single dose makes it ideal for mass treatment.
Mebendazole: 500mg single dose or 100mg twice daily for 3 days. Alternative to Albendazole.
Pyrantel Pamoate: 11mg/kg single dose (max 1g). Alternative. Safe in pregnancy (when Albendazole contraindicated in first trimester).
2. Complication Management
Intestinal Obstruction: Initial conservative management — NGT decompression, IV fluids, Piperazine citrate 75mg/kg (paralyzes worms for expulsion). Surgery if conservative management fails.
Biliary Ascariasis: ERCP for worm extraction from bile duct. Conservative approach often successful — antihelminthic + antispasmodics.
3. Prevention
National Deworming Day: Biannual Albendazole (February/August) for all children 1-19 years. FREE. Ensure 100% participation.
WASH: Handwashing with soap before eating and after defecation. Wash fruits/vegetables thoroughly. Proper toilet use. Eliminate open defecation.
Food Hygiene: Avoid raw/unwashed vegetables and salads from areas using human fecal matter as fertilizer.

Neem Juice: Nimbidin and azadirachtin have anti-helminthic properties. 10-20ml fresh neem juice on empty stomach for 3 days.

Soil Contamination & Open Defecation: Ascaris eggs persist in soil for years. Children playing on contaminated soil, eating unwashed vegetables, or practicing geophagia acquire infection. Open defecation directly contaminates agricultural

diseases.name: Heat Stroke
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (IMMEDIATE — Do Not Wait for Lab)

Heat Stroke Diagnostic Criteria: Core temperature >40°C (104°F) + CNS dysfunction (confusion, seizures, coma) + history of heat exposure. This is a CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS requiring immediate cooling. Do NOT delay cooling for laboratory confirmation.
Differentiation: Heat Exhaustion (temperature <40°C, sweating present, CNS intact, responds to cooling/rest) vs Heat Stroke (>40°C, CNS dysfunction, may have stopped sweating, medical emergency).
2. Laboratory Tests (During/After Initial Cooling)
Core Temperature (Rectal): Most accurate. Axillary/oral unreliable in heat stroke. Target cooling to <39°C.
Complete Blood Count: Hemoconcentration (high Hct), leukocytosis.
Serum Electrolytes: Hypernatremia or hyponatremia depending on hydration. Hyperkalemia from rhabdomyolysis/AKI.
Renal Function (Creatinine, BUN): Elevated in AKI. Monitor urine output hourly.
Creatine Kinase (CK): Markedly elevated (>10,000 IU/L) in exertional heat stroke with rhabdomyolysis. CK >100,000 = severe rhabdomyolysis.
Liver Function Tests: AST/ALT may rise dramatically (hepatic injury from heat). Peak at 48-72 hours.
generic_medicines: 1. IMMEDIATE COOLING (Start at Scene — Every Minute Counts)

Cold Water Immersion: Immerse patient in cold water (1-5°C) up to neck. THE most effective cooling method. Reduces temperature fastest (cooling rate 0.15-0.35°C/minute). Gold standard for exertional heat stroke. Use any available container — bathtub, trough, tarp with ice water.

Evaporative Cooling (If Immersion Unavailable): Remove clothing, spray/sponge entire body with lukewarm water, fan vigorously. Apply ice packs to neck, axillae, and groin (large vessel areas). Cooling rate 0.03-0.06°C/minute (slower than immersion but practical in resource-limited settings).
Target Temperature: Cool to <39°C (102.2°F) rectally within 30 minutes. STOP active cooling at 38.5°C to prevent overshoot hypothermia.
2. IV Fluid Resuscitation
Normal Saline: 1-2 liters IV rapidly, then titrate to urine output >0.5 ml/kg/hr. Cold IV fluids (4°C) provide additional internal cooling. Avoid overhydration in elderly/cardiac patients.
3. Organ Support (ICU Management)

Seizure Control: IV Benzodiazepines (Lorazepam/Midazolam). Avoid antipyretics (Paracetamol/Aspirin — ineffective in heat stroke as mechanism is thermoregulatory failure, not pyrexia. Also hepatotoxic in already-damaged liver).
Rhabdomyolysis Management: Aggressive IV fluids (200-300 ml/hr) to maintain UO >200 ml/hr. Alkalinize urine (IV NaHCO3) if pH <6.5. Dialysis for refractory AKI.
DIC Management: FFP, cryoprecipitate, platelets as indicated. Treat the cause (cooling) rather than the coagulopathy alone.
4. Prevention (Most Important)
Avoid Peak Heat: Stay indoors 12 PM-3 PM during heat waves. Reschedule outdoor labor to early morning/evening.
Hydration: Drink water regularly BEFORE feeling thirsty. ORS for laborers. 150-200ml every 15-20 minutes during outdoor work.
Clothing: Light-colored, loose, cotton clothing. Wet cloth on head/neck. Hats/umbrellas for sun protection.
Recognize Heat Exhaustion: Educate workers/communities to recognize early warning signs (heavy sweating, weakness, nausea, dizziness) and STOP, REST, COOL, HYDRATE before heat stroke develops.

Normal Saline 500ml: IV rehydration for heat stroke.
Paracetamol — DO NOT USE: Paracetamol is INEFFECTIVE and potentially HARMFUL in heat stroke (not a fever — it's thermoregulatory failure).

If someone collapses with confusion in heat, START COOLING IMMEDIATELY at the scene — pour cold water on them, move to shade, fan vigorously. Do NOT wait for ambulance. Co

diseases.name: Scrub Typhus
tests: 1. Clinical Suspicion (Most Critical Step)

Clinical Triad: Fever >5 days + eschar + lymphadenopathy in endemic area/season = highly probable scrub typhus. Even without eschar, undifferentiated fever not responding to antimalarials/antibiotics in endemic season should prompt Doxycycline trial.
Eschar Search Protocol: Systematically examine axillae, groin, perineum, behind ears, nape of neck, waistband area, under breasts (women). Use good lighting. Eschar may be hidden in body folds.
2. Serological Tests
Scrub Typhus IgM ELISA: Detects IgM antibodies from day 7-10. Sensitivity 80-95%. Available at sentinel labs. Most practical confirmatory test in India.

Weil-Felix Test (OXK Agglutination): Old, cheap test. OXK titer ≥1:160 suggestive. LOW sensitivity (50%) and specificity. Should NOT be used as sole diagnostic but still widely available at PHC/CHC level.
Immunofluorescence Assay (IFA): Gold standard. Detects antibody rise ≥4-fold in paired sera. Available at reference labs only.
3. Molecular Tests
Scrub Typhus PCR (Eschar/Blood): Highly specific. Detects O. tsutsugamushi DNA. Positive even after antibiotics started. Available at ICMR/reference labs.
4. Routine Blood Tests
generic_medicines: 1. Antibiotic Treatment (Start Empirically — Don't Wait for Labs)

Doxycycline: 100mg twice daily for 7-14 days (adults). 2.2mg/kg twice daily (children — AAP/WHO now permits Doxycycline in ALL ages for Rickettsial diseases). DRAMATIC response — fever resolves within 24-48 hours. This rapid defervescence is near-diagnostic. First-line per WHO/ICMR.
Azithromycin: 500mg daily for 5-7 days (adults), 10mg/kg/day (children). Alternative for pregnant women and Doxycycline-intolerant patients. Slightly less effective but adequate.
Chloramphenicol: 500mg four times daily for 7 days. Historical alternative. Risk of bone marrow suppression limits use. Still used where Doxycycline unavailable.
2. Severe Scrub Typhus (ICU Management)
IV Doxycycline: For patients unable to take oral (encephalitis, vomiting, shock). 100mg IV twice daily. Switch to oral once improving.
IV Azithromycin: 500mg IV daily. Alternative for severe cases in pregnant women.
Organ Support: Mechanical ventilation for ARDS, vasopressors for shock, dialysis for AKI, anticonvulsants for seizures. Standard sepsis management protocols alongside Doxycycline.
3. Supportive Care
Paracetamol: For fever and headache. Avoid NSAIDs if thrombocytopenic.
IV Fluids: Careful fluid management — avoid overload in myocarditis/ARDS.
4. Prevention
Personal Protection: DEET-based insect repellent on exposed skin, Permethrin-treated clothing/uniforms (military, forest workers), avoid sitting/lying on scrub vegetation, tuck trousers into boots.

Delay in starting Doxycycline is the primary cause of mortality. Every doctor seeing undifferentiated fever in endemic areas during monsoon/pos

diseases.name: Pertussis
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (High Index of Suspicion)

WHO Clinical Case Definition: Cough lasting ≥2 weeks with ≥1 of: paroxysms of coughing, inspiratory whoop, post-tussive vomiting, in absence of other apparent cause. In infants: apnea ± cyanosis with or without cough.
2. Laboratory Confirmation

Nasopharyngeal Swab Culture: B. pertussis culture on Bordet-Gengou or Regan-Lowe media. Gold standard but sensitivity only 30-60% (best in first 2 weeks). Takes 5-7 days. Requires special transport media.
PCR (Nasopharyngeal Swab): Detects B. pertussis DNA. Sensitivity 70-99%. Positive even after antibiotics started. Available at reference/tertiary labs. Fastest confirmatory method.
Serology (Anti-PT IgG): Single elevated anti-pertussis toxin IgG in patients not recently vaccinated. Useful for late-presenting cases (>3 weeks) when culture/PCR may be negative.
3. Blood Tests

Complete Blood Count: Marked lymphocytosis (WBC 20,000-100,000/mm³ with >60% lymphocytes) — characteristic in infants and young children. Severe lymphocytosis (>50,000) is a predictor of fatal pertussis in infants.
Chest X-ray: May show perihilar infiltrates, atelectasis, or 'shaggy heart' sign. Pneumothorax/pneumomediastinum from violent coughing.
4. Diagnostic Criteria
generic_medicines: 1. Antibiotic Treatment (Reduces Transmission — Start Early)

Azithromycin: 10mg/kg/day for 5 days (children), 500mg day 1 then 250mg days 2-5 (adults). First-line. Most effective if started in catarrhal phase (first 1-2 weeks). After paroxysmal phase established, antibiotics reduce transmission but may not shorten illness.
Erythromycin: 40-50mg/kg/day in 4 doses for 14 days. Alternative. More GI side effects than Azithromycin.
Cotrimoxazole (TMP-SMX): For macrolide-allergic patients. 8mg/kg/day TMP for 14 days.
2. Supportive Care (CRITICAL for Infants)
Hospital Admission for Infants <6 Months: All infants with suspected pertussis should be hospitalized for apnea monitoring, oxygen supplementation, and feeding support. ICU for severe cases.
Oxygen & Suction: Supplemental oxygen during paroxysms/desaturation. Gentle nasopharyngeal suctioning to clear mucus plugs.
Nutrition: Small frequent feeds between paroxysms. NG tube feeding if oral intake inadequate. IV fluids if dehydrated.
Avoid Cough Suppressants: Codeine and Dextromethorphan are INEFFECTIVE and potentially harmful in pertussis. Do NOT prescribe.
3. Post-Exposure Prophylaxis
Azithromycin for Contacts: Same treatment dose for ALL household contacts regardless of vaccination status. Critical for protecting infants in the household.
4. Prevention
DPT/Pentavalent Vaccine (UIP): Primary series at 6, 10, 14 weeks + DPT boosters at 16-24 months and 5-6 years. Provides 80-90% protection for 5-10 years.
Tdap Booster (IAP Recommended): At 10-12 years. NOT yet in India's UIP but important for reducing adolescent/adult reservoir.

Maternal Tdap (IAP Recommended): Single Tdap dose at 27-36 weeks pregnancy. Passive antibodies protect newborn for first 2-3 months (before infant's own vaccination). Increasingly practiced in Indian private healthcare.

Start Azithromycin empirically. Infants <6 M

diseases.name: Diphtheria
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (START TREATMENT IMMEDIATELY on Suspicion)

Clinical Recognition: Sore throat + adherent gray-white membrane on tonsils/pharynx + cervical lymphadenopathy + relatively low fever with disproportionate toxicity = diphtheria until proven otherwise. Do NOT wait for lab confirmation — START Antitoxin + Antibiotics immediately.
2. Microbiological Confirmation

Throat Swab Culture: Swab from beneath membrane edge. Culture on Löffler's serum slope (shows metachromatic granules) and Tellurite agar (black colonies). Takes 24-48 hours. Simultaneously send to reference lab for toxigenicity testing (Elek test or PCR for tox gene).
Albert's Stain (Gram Stain Alternative): Shows characteristic Chinese-letter arrangement of gram-positive bacilli with metachromatic granules (volutin). Quick presumptive identification.
Elek Test / Tox Gene PCR: Confirms toxin production by the isolated C. diphtheriae strain. Essential because non-toxigenic strains exist. Available at reference labs.
3. Complication Monitoring
ECG (Serial — Daily): Monitor for myocarditis — ST-T changes, heart block (first-degree → complete), arrhythmias. ECG changes may appear 1-2 weeks after symptom onset.
Cardiac Enzymes (Troponin/CK-MB): Elevated in myocarditis. Serial monitoring.
Complete Blood Count, Renal Function, Liver Function: Baseline and monitoring for systemic toxicity.
generic_medicines: 1. Diphtheria Antitoxin (DAT) — LIFE-SAVING (Give Immediately)

Diphtheria Antitoxin (Equine): 20,000-100,000 units IV/IM based on severity. MUST be given within 48 hours — neutralizes only CIRCULATING (unbound) toxin. Delayed administration dramatically increases mortality. Skin test for horse serum sensitivity before IV administration. Available through state health departments — request immediately on clinical suspicion.
2. Antibiotic Treatment
Penicillin G (IV/IM): 100,000 IU/kg/day in 4 doses for 14 days. Kills C. diphtheriae and stops toxin production. First-line.
Erythromycin: 40mg/kg/day in 4 doses for 14 days. Alternative for Penicillin-allergic. Also first-line for carrier eradication.
3. Supportive Care
Airway Management: Close monitoring for airway obstruction. Tracheostomy if stridor/respiratory distress from laryngeal membrane.
Cardiac Monitoring: Continuous ECG monitoring for 2-3 weeks. Temporary pacemaker for complete heart block. Bed rest for 2-3 weeks to reduce cardiac workload.
Isolation: Strict droplet precautions until 2 consecutive negative throat cultures 24 hours apart after antibiotic completion.
4. Contacts & Prevention
Post-Exposure Prophylaxis: ALL close contacts — throat culture + Erythromycin 7-10 days (or single Benzathine Penicillin) + Td/DPT booster if >5 years since last dose.
DPT/Pentavalent Vaccine: Primary series at 6, 10, 14 weeks + boosters. Active immunization during convalescence (diphtheria infection does NOT confer reliable immunity).
Carrier Treatment: Erythromycin 40mg/kg/day for 7-10 days. Reculture after treatment to confirm clearance.
janaushadhi_medicines: Erythromycin Tablets IP 250/500mg: First-line antibiotic. Pack of 10.
Amoxicillin Capsules IP 500mg: Alternative antibiotic. Pack of 10.
Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: Symptomatic relief. Pack of 10.
Note: Diphtheria Antitoxin (DAT) is supplied through Government health channels. DPT/Pentavalent vaccine provided FREE under UIP.
ayurvedic_medicines: CRITICAL NOTE — No Ayurvedic Substitute for Antitoxin: Diphtheria toxin causes fatal myocarditis and neuropathy. Only DAT (Diphtheria Antitoxin) neutralizes the toxin. No herbal preparation can substitute. Ayurvedic support is for recovery only:
Guduchi/Giloy: Immunomodulatory recovery support after acute phase. 500mg-1g twice daily.
Yashtimadhu: Demulcent for sore throat relief. Gargle with Yashtimadhu decoction. 3-5g with honey.
Sitopaladi Churna: For associated cough during recovery. 1-3g with honey.
Chyawanprash: Immune recovery Rasayana post-illness. 10-20g daily.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Outbreaks Continue Despite Vaccination: India reports periodic diphtheria outbreaks — recent clusters in Kerala (2016), Gujarat, Rajasthan, and NE states. Under-vaccinated pockets, waning immunity, and inadequate booster coverage allow transmission to persist. India reported 3,000-6,000 cases/year in recent years. Vaccination Gap: DPT/Pentavalent 3-dose completion rate is ~85-90% nationally but lower in specific populations (migrants, tribal, remote).
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations ABSOLUTE EMERGENCY — Give Antitoxin Within 48 Hours: Diphtheria Antitoxin must be given as EARLY as possible on clinical suspicion. It neutralizes only unbound toxin — once toxin binds to heart/nerves, it CANNOT be reversed. Every hour of delay worsens outcome. Do NOT wait f

diseases.name: Mumps
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (Primary Method)

Clinical Recognition: Acute parotid gland swelling (lifts earlobe, obscures mandible angle) + fever + pain on chewing = mumps. Bilateral involvement is highly characteristic. Lab confirmation rarely needed for typical cases.
2. Laboratory Confirmation (When Needed)
Mumps IgM ELISA (Serum/Saliva): Positive from symptom onset. Confirms acute infection. Available at tertiary/reference labs.
Mumps RT-PCR (Throat Swab/Urine): Detects viral RNA. Most sensitive test. Available at virology labs.
Serum Amylase: Elevated in parotitis (salivary amylase) AND pancreatitis (pancreatic amylase). Lipase specifically elevated in pancreatitis.
3. Complication Assessment
Testicular Ultrasound: For orchitis — shows enlarged, hypoechoic testis with increased blood flow on Doppler.
CSF Analysis: If meningitis suspected — lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, mildly elevated protein.
Complete Blood Count: Normal or mild leukopenia with lymphocytosis.
generic_medicines: 1. NO Antiviral — Treatment Is Entirely Supportive
Paracetamol/Ibuprofen: For pain and fever. 500-1000mg Paracetamol or 400mg Ibuprofen every 6-8 hours.
Warm/Cold Compresses: Applied to swollen parotids for pain relief. Warm compresses generally preferred.
Soft Diet: Avoid acidic, sour foods (trigger parotid pain). Soft, bland diet — khichdi, dal, porridge, banana. Adequate fluids.
2. Orchitis Management

Bed Rest + Scrotal Support: Ice packs, supportive underwear/jockstrap, bed rest. NSAIDs for pain. Short course Prednisolone (40mg/day × 5 days) sometimes used for severe orchitis (controversial evidence).
3. Prevention
MMR Vaccine: 2 doses — 12-15 months and 4-6 years. 88% effective after 2 doses. IAP recommended. NOT in India's UIP (only MR is). Available in private practice for ₹250-500/dose.
Isolation: Isolate from school/work for 5 days after parotid swelling onset. Respiratory droplet precautions.
janaushadhi_medicines: Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: For pain and fever. Pack of 10.
Ibuprofen Tablets IP 400mg: Anti-inflammatory for pain. Pack of 10.
Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):
Note: MMR vaccine available privately. MR vaccine provided FREE under UIP.
ayurvedic_medicines: Pippali + Haridra Paste (External): Warm paste of Pippali and turmeric applied over swollen parotid glands. Traditional anti-inflammatory. Provides local warmth and swelling reduction.
Shirisha / Siris (Albizia lebbeck): Classical Shothahara (anti-edema) herb. Used for glandular swelling in Ayurveda. 3-5g powder twice daily.
Guduchi/Giloy: Immunomodulatory and Jwaraghna. 500mg-1g twice daily for immune support during illness.
Kanchanara Guggulu: Classical for Gandamala (glandular swelling). 2 tablets twice daily. Supports resolution of parotid swelling.
Warm Sesame Oil Massage: Gentle external massage around (not directly on) swollen area with warm sesame oil to improve circulation and reduce discomfort.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors India — Low Mumps Vaccination Coverage: Mumps vaccine (as MMR) is NOT in India's Universal Immunization Programme — only MR (Measles-Rubella) is included. MMR is given in private practice only. This means most Indian children are susceptible to mumps. Outbreaks in Schools & Colleges: Mumps outbreaks occur regularly in Indian schools, hostels, and military barracks. Close quarters facilitate transmission via respiratory droplets. Adult Mumps — More Comp
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Supportive Care Is Sufficient for Most Cases: Mumps is self-limiting in 7-10 days. Paracetamol, soft diet, rest, and warm compresses are adequate. No antiviral or antibiotic needed. Do NOT prescribe antibiotics for mumps (it's viral). Watch for Orchitis in Post-Pubertal Male

diseases.name: Peptic Ulcer & Gastritis
tests: 1. Clinical Assessment

Alarm Features Screening: Age >55 new-onset dyspepsia, unintentional weight loss, GI bleeding (hematemesis/melena), progressive dysphagia, persistent vomiting, anemia, palpable mass, family history of GI cancer. ANY alarm feature = urgent endoscopy.
2. H. pylori Testing (Test-and-Treat Strategy)

Urea Breath Test (UBT): Non-invasive gold standard. Patient ingests C13/C14-labeled urea — H. pylori urease produces labeled CO2 measured in breath. Sensitivity/specificity >95%. Available at gastroenterology clinics.
Stool H. pylori Antigen Test: Non-invasive stool test. Sensitivity 90-95%. Useful for diagnosis and confirming eradication. Increasingly available.
Rapid Urease Test (CLO Test): During endoscopy — biopsy placed in urea medium. Color change within hours = positive. Sensitivity 90-95%.
H. pylori Serology (IgG): Blood test. Cannot distinguish active from past infection. NOT useful for confirming eradication. Inexpensive but less accurate.
3. Endoscopy

Upper GI Endoscopy (EGD): Direct visualization of esophagus, stomach, and duodenum. Identifies ulcers, erosions, tumors, varices. Biopsy for H. pylori and histopathology (rule out malignancy). MANDATORY for alarm features.
4. Other Tests
generic_medicines: 1. Proton Pump Inhibitors (PPI) — Cornerstone of Acid Suppression
Pantoprazole: 40mg once daily before breakfast for 4-8 weeks. India's most prescribed PPI. Heals 90%+ of ulcers.
Omeprazole: 20mg once daily. Alternative PPI. Equally effective.
Rabeprazole/Esomeprazole: Alternatives. Esomeprazole 40mg for severe erosive disease.
2. H. pylori Eradication (Triple/Quadruple Therapy)
Standard Triple Therapy: PPI (Pantoprazole 40mg) + Amoxicillin 1g + Clarithromycin 500mg — all twice daily for 14 days. First-line where Clarithromycin resistance is <15%.

Bismuth Quadruple Therapy: PPI + Bismuth subsalicylate + Metronidazole 500mg + Tetracycline 500mg — all four times daily for 14 days. For Clarithromycin-resistant areas or failed first-line. Increasingly used in India due to resistance.
Sequential/Concomitant Therapy: PPI + Amoxicillin for 5-7 days → PPI + Clarithromycin + Metronidazole for 5-7 days. Alternative approach.
3. Other Medications

H2 Receptor Blockers (Ranitidine/Famotidine): Ranitidine 150mg twice daily. Less effective than PPIs but useful for mild symptoms and maintenance. Famotidine 20mg twice daily preferred (Ranitidine recalled in many countries for NDMA contamination).
Sucralfate: 1g four times daily before meals. Mucosal protectant — coats ulcer base. Useful adjunct.
Antacids (Aluminium/Magnesium Hydroxide): Quick symptom relief. Neutralize gastric acid. Not for primary treatment but useful PRN.
4. GI Bleeding Management
NPO + IV PPI (Pantoprazole 80mg bolus then 8mg/hr infusion): For acute upper GI bleeding from peptic ulcer.
Urgent Endoscopy: Within 24 hours for hemodynamically stable, within 12 hours for unstable. Endoscopic hemostasis (injection, cautery, clipping).
janaushadhi_medicines: Pantoprazole Tablets IP 40mg: First-line PPI. Pack of 10.
Omeprazole Capsules IP 20mg: Alternative PPI. Pack of 10.
Amoxicillin Capsules IP 500mg: For H. pylori eradication. Pack of 10.
Clarithromycin Tablets IP 500mg: For H. pylori eradication. Pack of 4.
Ranitidine/Famotidine Tablets: H2 blocker. Pack of 10.
Antacid Suspension (Al/Mg Hydroxide): Quick relief. Bottle.
Sucralfate Tablets IP 1g: Mucosal protectant. Pack of 10.

ayurvedic_medicines: Yashtimadhu / Mulethi (Glycyrrhiza glabra): Classical Pittashamaka and mucosal protectant. Contains glycyrrhizin with anti-ulcer activity (stimulates mucus production). Published evidence for ulcer healing. Deglycyrrhizinated licorice (DGL) 380-760mg before meals. Mulethi powder 3-5g with milk/honey twice daily.

Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus): Classical Pittashamaka Rasayana. Published evidence for gastroprotective and anti-ulcer activity (comparable to Ranitidine in animal studies). 3-5g powder with milk twice daily.
Amla/Amalaki (Emblica officinalis): Potent antioxidant and Pittashamaka. Reduces gastric acid secretion. Amla juice or Amalaki Rasayana.

Avipattikar Churna: Classical formulation for Amlapitta (acid peptic disease). Contains Trikatu, Triphala, Musta, Vidanga, Ela. 3-5g with warm water before meals. Most prescribed Ayurvedic antacid in India.
Kamdudha Ras (Mauktik Yukta): Classical herbo-mineral antacid containing Praval Pishti, Mukta Pishti, Shankha Bhasma. Cooling, Pittashamaka. 125-250mg twice daily with milk.
Sutshekhar Ras: Classical for Amlapitta and Pitta disorders. Contains Suvarna Bhasma, Shankha Bhasma, Tamra Bhasma. Practitioner supervision required. 125-250mg twice daily.
Coconut Water: Natural Pittashamaka. Alkaline, soothing for gastric mucosa. 200-300ml twice daily.
Isabgol / Psyllium Husk: Mucilaginous fiber that coats and protects gastric mucosa. 1-2 tsp with water at bedtime.
Himalaya Gasex: Proprietary carminative and antacid. 2 tablets after meals.
Dabur Avipattikar Churna: Branded classical formulation. 3-5g before meals.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors H. pylori — Very High Prevalence: Helicobacter pylori infection rate in India is 60-80% of adults (one of highest globally). Acquired in childhood via fecal-oral route. Main cause of peptic ulcer and gastric cancer risk. Testing and eradication underutilized. NSAID/Aspirin Overuse: Widespread OTC availability of Diclofenac, Ibuprofen, and Aspirin in India. Self-medication for body aches, headaches, and joint pain without gastroprotection causes NSAID-i
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Test and Treat H. pylori: 60-80% of Indian adults carry H. pylori — the main cause of peptic ulcers. Testing (Urea Breath Test or Stool Antigen) and eradication with triple therapy CURES most peptic ulcers permanently. Without eradication, ulcers recur despite PPI therapy. A

diseases.name: Burns
tests: 1. Primary Assessment (ATLS Approach)

Airway Assessment: Look for signs of inhalation injury — singed nasal hairs, soot in mouth, hoarse voice, stridor. If suspected, early intubation BEFORE edema develops (airway can obstruct within hours).

TBSA Estimation (Wallace Rule of 9s): Adults: head 9%, each arm 9%, each leg 18%, anterior trunk 18%, posterior trunk 18%, perineum 1%. Children: Lund-Browder chart more accurate (head proportionally larger). Palm of patient's hand = ~1% TBSA. Accurate TBSA determines fluid resuscitation volume.

Burn Depth Assessment: Superficial (red, painful, blanches), Partial thickness (blisters, weeping, painful), Full thickness (painless, leathery/charred, doesn't blanch). Depth may evolve over 48-72 hours (burn wound conversion).
2. Laboratory Tests (For Major Burns >15-20% TBSA)
Complete Blood Count: Hemoconcentration initially (raised Hct from plasma loss). Anemia later from hemolysis and blood loss.
Serum Electrolytes: Hyperkalemia (from tissue destruction), hyponatremia (dilutional after resuscitation).
Renal Function: Monitor for AKI from hypovolemia, myoglobinuria (if associated electrical/crush injury).
Blood Group & Crossmatch: For anticipated blood product needs.
ABG: For inhalation injury — assess oxygenation, ventilation, carboxyhemoglobin level (CO poisoning from enclosed-space fires).
generic_medicines: 1. Emergency First Aid (AT THE SCENE — Critical)

COOL the Burn: Run cool (NOT ice-cold) water over burn for 20 minutes within first 3 hours. This is the SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT first aid. Reduces pain, limits burn depth progression, and improves outcome. Works even if delayed up to 3 hours post-burn.
Remove Clothing/Jewelry: Remove clothing near burn (unless stuck to skin). Remove rings, bangles before swelling develops.
Cover with Clean Cloth: After cooling, cover with clean, non-fluffy material (clean sheet/cling film). Keep patient warm (hypothermia risk in large burns).
DO NOT Apply: Toothpaste, egg, potato, mud, butter, oil, ink, or any home remedy to burn wounds. These contaminate the wound, increase infection risk, and delay healing.
2. Fluid Resuscitation (>15-20% TBSA — Parkland Formula)

Ringer's Lactate: 4 ml × body weight (kg) × %TBSA. Give half in first 8 hours (from TIME OF BURN, not time of arrival), remaining half over next 16 hours. Titrate to urine output >0.5 ml/kg/hr (adults), >1 ml/kg/hr (children). Start 2 large-bore IVs immediately.
3. Wound Care
Silver Sulfadiazine 1% Cream: Apply 2-3mm thick layer on partial-thickness burns. Change dressing daily. Broad-spectrum antimicrobial. First-line topical in Indian burns units.
Collagen Dressings / Amniotic Membrane: Biological dressings promoting healing. Increasingly available at burns centres.
Escharotomy: Emergent longitudinal incision through full-thickness circumferential eschar for limb/chest compartment release.
4. Pain Management
Paracetamol + NSAIDs: For mild-moderate burns.
Tramadol / Morphine: For moderate-severe pain. IV Morphine 0.1mg/kg for severe burns. Adequate pain control is often neglected.
Procedural Sedation: Ketamine IV for painful dressing changes.
janaushadhi_medicines: Silver Sulfadiazine Cream 1%: First-line burn wound cream. Tube.
Paracetamol Tablets IP 500mg: Pain relief. Pack of 10.
Ibuprofen Tablets IP 400mg: Anti-inflammatory analgesic. Pack of 10.
ORS Powder: For oral rehydration in minor burns.
Approximate Jan Aushadhi Prices (MRP incl. taxes):
ayurvedic_medicines: IMPORTANT NOTE — Burns Require Medical Treatment: Ayurvedic preparations should NOT be applied to fresh burn wounds. However, Ayurveda has established applications for burn healing and scar management:

Jatyadi Ghrita / Jatyadi Taila: Classical medicated ghee/oil for Vrana Ropana (wound healing). Contains Jati (Jasminum), Nimba (Neem), Haridra (Turmeric), Patola, Karanja. Published evidence for promoting wound healing. Apply on healing (NOT acute) burn wounds after 3-5 days.
Kumkumadi Taila: For post-burn SCAR management. Apply after wound has fully healed (epithelialized). Reduces hyperpigmentation and scar contracture.
Aloe Vera (Kumari/Ghritkumari): Cooling, soothing for superficial burns. Published evidence for burn wound healing. Apply fresh Aloe gel on first-degree (superficial) burns after cooling with water.

Honey (Madhu): Published evidence for burn wound healing — antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, promotes granulation. Medical-grade honey dressings used in modern wound care. Apply on superficial-partial thickness burns after initial cooling.
Coconut Oil: For healed burn scar moisturization and massage. Softens scar tissue. NOT on fresh/open wounds.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors India — Highest Global Burn Burden: India accounts for ~1 million burn injuries annually, with ~1 lakh hospitalizations and ~25,000 deaths. Leading cause: open-flame cooking on kerosene stoves and chulhas. Women and children in kitchens are most vulnerable. Dowry-Related Burns & Self-Immolation: India has a tragic burden of intentional burns — dowry-related 'stove bursting' (reported as 'accidents'), bride burning, and self-immolation (suicide). These
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations CRITICAL FIRST AID — Cool Running Water for 20 Minutes: This is the MOST IMPORTANT intervention for ANY burn. Run cool tap water over the burn for 20 minutes as soon as possible. Effective even up to 3 hours post-burn. Reduces pain, prevents deepening, and improves long-term

diseases.name: Organophosphate Poisoning
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (TREAT IMMEDIATELY — Don't Wait for Labs)

Clinical Recognition: Cholinergic syndrome (miosis + excessive secretions + bradycardia + muscle fasciculations) + history/smell of pesticide exposure = OP poisoning. Start Atropine IMMEDIATELY on clinical grounds. Do NOT wait for lab confirmation.
2. Laboratory Confirmation

Serum Cholinesterase (Pseudocholinesterase/Butyrylcholinesterase): Low levels confirm OP exposure. Mild: 20-50% of normal. Moderate: 10-20%. Severe: <10%. Useful for diagnosis and monitoring recovery. Available at most hospital labs.
Red Cell Acetylcholinesterase (RBC-AChE): More specific than serum cholinesterase for assessing true enzyme inhibition. Less widely available.
3. Routine Blood Tests
ABG: Respiratory acidosis from hypoventilation. Metabolic acidosis from tissue hypoxia.
Serum Electrolytes, Renal Function, Liver Function: Baseline and monitoring.
Blood Glucose: Hyperglycemia common from sympathetic stimulation.
ECG: QTc prolongation, arrhythmias. Continuous monitoring essential.
generic_medicines: 1. Immediate Resuscitation (ABC)

Airway & Breathing: Suction secretions aggressively. Intubate early if respiratory distress/failure. Mechanical ventilation often needed for hours-days. Oxygen via bag-mask while preparing intubation.

Decontamination: Remove clothing, wash entire body with soap and water. Healthcare workers must wear gloves to avoid secondary exposure. If ingested: do NOT induce vomiting. Gastric lavage ONLY within 1 hour of ingestion with protected airway.
2. Atropine — LIFE-SAVING Antidote

Atropine Protocol: 1.8-3mg IV bolus (adults), 0.05mg/kg (children). Repeat every 5 minutes, DOUBLING the dose each time until: (1) secretions dry up, (2) heart rate >80/min, (3) pupils dilate. May require massive doses — 10-100+ mg in first 24 hours for severe cases. Then continuous infusion titrated to dry secretions. Do NOT under-dose — inadequate Atropine kills. Target: dry secretions, NOT pupil size or heart rate.
3. Pralidoxime (PAM) — Oxime Reactivator

Pralidoxime (2-PAM): 1-2g IV over 15-30 minutes, then 500mg/hr continuous infusion for 24-48 hours (or repeated boluses every 4-6 hours). Reactivates inhibited AChE if given within 24-48 hours (before 'aging'). Most effective for nicotinic symptoms (fasciculations, weakness). Start alongside Atropine.
4. Seizure Management
Diazepam: 5-10mg IV for seizures. May need repeated doses. Midazolam continuous infusion for refractory seizures.
Avoid Succinylcholine: Contraindicated for intubation (prolonged paralysis due to impaired pseudocholinesterase). Use Rocuronium instead.
5. Monitoring & Complications

ICU Monitoring: Continuous ECG, SpO2, frequent ABG. Mechanical ventilation readiness. Monitor for Intermediate Syndrome (day 2-4: proximal muscle weakness → respiratory failure even after initial recovery).
OPIDN: Organophosphate-Induced Delayed Neuropathy — distal sensorimotor neuropathy developing 2-4 weeks post-exposure. Supportive treatment only.
6. Prevention
Pesticide Access Restriction: Lock pesticides away from children and distressed individuals. India has banned several highly toxic OPs (Monocrotophos, Phorate) but enforcement is poor.
janaushadhi_medicines: Atropine Injection 0.6mg/ml: CRITICAL life-saving antidote. Must be stocked at ALL health facilities.
Pralidoxime (PAM) Injection 500mg: Oxime antidote. Available at district hospitals.
Note: Atropine and PAM must be available at EVERY PHC/CHC in agricultural districts. Stock adequacy should be audited regularly.
ayurvedic_medicines: CRITICAL NOTE — No Ayurvedic Antidote for OP Poisoning: Organophosphate poisoning is an acute medical emergency requiring Atropine (antidote), airway management, and ICU care. There is NO Ayurvedic herb that reverses cholinesterase inhibition. Every minute spent on non-medical treatment increases mortality.
Post-Recovery Support: After hospital discharge, Ashwagandha (adaptogenic neuroprotective), Brahmi (cognitive recovery), and nutritious diet support neurological recovery from chronic OP effects.

Visha Chikitsa (Toxicology in Ayurveda): Ayurvedic texts describe Visha (poison) management principles including Shodhana (detoxification) and Shamana (pacification), but these are NOT applicable to acute OP poisoning which requires modern intensive care.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors India — Global Epicenter of Pesticide Poisoning: India reports ~100,000-200,000 pesticide poisoning cases annually with ~25,000-30,000 deaths. Organophosphate compounds (Monocrotophos, Chlorpyrifos, Malathion, Phorate) are most common. Agricultural states (Maharashtra, AP, Telangana, Punjab, Kerala) have highest burden. Suicide by Pesticide: Pesticide ingestion is the most common method of suicide in rural India. Easy availability of highly toxic pesti
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations ABSOLUTE EMERGENCY — Atropine Saves Lives: Every PHC/CHC in agricultural India must have adequate Atropine stock. A patient arriving with cholinergic crisis needs Atropine within minutes. Give 1.8-3mg IV immediately, double the dose every 5 minutes until secretions dry up. U

diseases.name: Preeclampsia & Eclampsia
tests: 1. Antenatal Screening (MOST IMPORTANT — Every Visit)
BP Measurement: Check at EVERY antenatal visit. ≥140/90 on 2 occasions 4 hours apart = hypertension in pregnancy. ≥160/110 = severe — urgent action.
Urine Dipstick for Protein: At every visit after 20 weeks. ≥1+ proteinuria = send for urine protein:creatinine ratio. Combined with HTN = preeclampsia.
2. Laboratory Tests (Once Preeclampsia Suspected)
Complete Blood Count: Platelet count — falling platelets (<150,000 and especially <100,000) indicate worsening preeclampsia/HELLP.
Liver Function Tests: AST/ALT elevation (>70 IU/L) = liver involvement. Part of HELLP criteria.
Renal Function (Creatinine): Rising creatinine indicates renal involvement. Normal pregnancy creatinine is <0.8 mg/dL — anything above 1.0 is concerning.
LDH: Elevated (>600 IU/L) in hemolysis/HELLP. Non-specific but useful marker.
Peripheral Smear: Schistocytes (fragmented RBCs) = microangiopathic hemolytic anemia of HELLP.
Uric Acid: Elevated (>6 mg/dL) — correlates with preeclampsia severity. Reflects renal dysfunction.
generic_medicines: 1. Magnesium Sulfate — LIFE-SAVING for Eclampsia Prevention & Treatment

MgSO4 (Pritchard Regimen — India Standard): Loading: 4g IV over 20 minutes + 5g IM each buttock (total 14g). Maintenance: 5g IM alternate buttock every 4 hours for 24 hours after delivery or last seizure. Monitor: patellar reflex (must be present), respiratory rate (must be >16/min), urine output (must be >25ml/hr). Antidote for toxicity: Calcium Gluconate 1g IV over 10 minutes.
MgSO4 (Zuspan Regimen): Loading: 4g IV over 20 minutes. Maintenance: 1g/hr IV continuous infusion. Preferred in settings with IV pump availability.
2. Antihypertensive Treatment
Labetalol (IV): 20mg IV bolus, repeat 40mg, 80mg every 10-15 minutes (max 300mg). First-line for acute severe hypertension (≥160/110) in pregnancy.
Nifedipine (Oral): 10-20mg oral, repeat after 30 minutes if needed. Alternative to IV Labetalol. Widely used in India due to oral route convenience.
Methyldopa (Oral): 250-500mg 2-3 times daily. First-line for chronic/maintenance antihypertensive in pregnancy. Safe long-term.
3. Definitive Treatment — DELIVERY

Delivery Is the ONLY Cure: Preeclampsia resolves ONLY after delivery of placenta. Timing depends on gestational age and severity: Severe preeclampsia at ≥34 weeks → deliver. Severe preeclampsia <34 weeks → stabilize with MgSO4 + antihypertensives, corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity, then deliver within 24-48 hours.
4. Supportive Care

Corticosteroids for Fetal Lung Maturity: Dexamethasone 6mg IM × 4 doses 12 hours apart (or Betamethasone 12mg IM × 2 doses 24 hours apart) if <34 weeks gestation. Accelerates fetal lung maturation before preterm delivery.
5. Postpartum Monitoring
Continue MgSO4 for 24 hours postpartum. Monitor BP for at least 72 hours (eclampsia can occur postpartum). Antihypertensives as needed. Follow up at 6 weeks for BP resolution.
janaushadhi_medicines: Magnesium Sulfate Injection 50% (2ml/5ml ampoules): ESSENTIAL life-saving drug. Must be available at ALL delivery points.
Nifedipine Tablets IP 10mg: Oral antihypertensive for preeclampsia. Pack of 10.
Methyldopa Tablets IP 250mg: Maintenance antihypertensive in pregnancy. Pack of 10.
Calcium Gluconate Injection 10%: MgSO4 toxicity antidote.
Urine Protein Dipsticks: For antenatal screening.
