Note: MgSO4 injection should be available FREE at all government delivery facilities.
ayurvedic_medicines: CRITICAL NOTE — Eclampsia Is a Medical Emergency: Preeclampsia/eclampsia requires MgSO4, antihypertensives, and timely delivery. NO Ayurvedic herb can substitute for these interventions. Delay for alternative treatment risks maternal and fetal death.
Sarpagandha (Rauvolfia serpentina): Has traditional antihypertensive use but is NOT safe in pregnancy (uterotonic effects). DO NOT use for gestational hypertension.
Ashwagandha: Adaptogenic but NOT studied for safety in preeclampsia. Avoid during active preeclampsia.
Jatamansi: Mild antihypertensive but insufficient for preeclampsia management. Not recommended.

Post-Delivery Recovery: After delivery and stabilization, Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus — galactagogue and Rasayana), Dashamoola Kwatha (anti-inflammatory for postpartum recovery), and nutritious diet support maternal recovery.

Dietary Prevention: Adequate calcium intake (1g/day — WHO recommendation for low-calcium-diet populations like India) during pregnancy may reduce preeclampsia risk by 50%. Calcium supplementation is recommended by WHO for all pregnant women in India.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Major Cause of Maternal Mortality in India: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy account for ~10% of maternal deaths in India — the third leading cause after hemorrhage and sepsis. India contributes the largest absolute number of eclampsia deaths globally. Late Antenatal Booking & Irregular Visits: Many Indian women, especially in rural areas, book late for antenatal care (>20 weeks) and attend irregularly. BP and urine protein screening at each visit w
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations ABSOLUTE CRITICAL — MgSO4 at Every Delivery Point: Magnesium Sulfate prevents 50% of eclamptic seizures and treats established eclampsia. EVERY health facility conducting deliveries MUST have MgSO4 and staff trained to administer it. Its absence at delivery points is the pri

diseases.name: Sickle Cell Disease
tests: 1. Screening & Diagnosis

Solubility Test (Sickling Test): Simple screening test — hemoglobin in reducing solution (sodium dithionite). Turbid = HbS present. Cannot distinguish trait (HbAS) from disease (HbSS). Point-of-care test for community screening under NSCEM.

Hemoglobin Electrophoresis / HPLC: Definitive diagnostic test. HbSS: >80% HbS, absent HbA. HbAS (trait): 30-40% HbS, >50% HbA. Also identifies HbSC, HbS-Beta Thalassemia. Available at district hospital level.
Newborn Screening (Heel Prick): IEF (Isoelectric Focusing) or HPLC on dried blood spot from heel prick. Identifies SCD at birth — enables early intervention. Being scaled up under NSCEM.
2. Crisis Assessment
Complete Blood Count: Baseline Hb 6-10 g/dL (HbSS). During crisis: may drop further. Reticulocyte count elevated (compensatory). Leukocytosis suggests infection triggering crisis.
Peripheral Blood Smear: Sickle-shaped RBCs, target cells, Howell-Jolly bodies (functional asplenia). Characteristic morphology.
Chest X-ray: For Acute Chest Syndrome — new pulmonary infiltrate involving at least one complete lung segment.
Blood Culture: For febrile episodes to identify bacterial sepsis (functional asplenia makes SCD patients vulnerable to overwhelming infection).
Transcranial Doppler (TCD): Annual screening in children 2-16 years. Cerebral blood flow velocity >200 cm/sec = high stroke risk → start chronic transfusion programme. Life-saving screening.
generic_medicines: 1. Disease-Modifying Therapy

Hydroxyurea: 15-35mg/kg/day. THE most important drug for SCD. Increases fetal hemoglobin (HbF) which prevents sickling. Reduces VOC frequency by 50%, reduces ACS, reduces transfusion need, reduces mortality. Should be offered to ALL SCD patients from age 9 months. Monitor CBC monthly (can cause myelosuppression).
2. Pain Crisis Management
Paracetamol + NSAIDs: First-line for mild-moderate pain. Ibuprofen 400-600mg every 8 hours + Paracetamol 1g every 6 hours.
Tramadol: 50-100mg every 6 hours for moderate pain.
Morphine/Hydromorphone: For severe VOC. Morphine 0.1-0.15mg/kg IV every 3-4 hours. Patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) in hospital. Do NOT under-treat pain — SCD pain is real and severe.
IV Fluids: Aggressive hydration (1.5x maintenance) to reduce sickling. NS or D5-0.45NS.
3. Infection Prevention

Penicillin V Prophylaxis: 125mg twice daily (under 5 years), 250mg twice daily (over 5 years). Continue until at least age 5. Prevents overwhelming pneumococcal sepsis from functional asplenia. Compliance is life-saving.
Vaccinations: Pneumococcal (PCV13 + PPSV23), Meningococcal, H. influenzae b, annual Influenza. CRITICAL given functional asplenia.
Malaria Prophylaxis: In endemic areas, insecticide-treated bed nets and prompt malaria treatment are essential. SCD patients with malaria have higher mortality.
4. Acute Chest Syndrome
Oxygen + Antibiotics (Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin) + Exchange transfusion if severe. Incentive spirometry preventive in hospitalized patients.
5. Blood Transfusion
Simple Transfusion: For severe anemia (Hb <5 g/dL), aplastic crisis, splenic sequestration. Target Hb 10 g/dL — do NOT over-transfuse (hyperviscosity).

diseases.name: Thalassemia
tests: 1. Screening & Diagnosis

Complete Blood Count + Peripheral Smear: Microcytic hypochromic anemia (low MCV <70 fL, low MCH <25 pg). Target cells, basophilic stippling on smear. Mentzer Index: MCV/RBC count — <13 suggests thalassemia, >13 suggests iron deficiency.

Hemoglobin Electrophoresis / HPLC: Definitive. Thal Major (β0/β0): HbF 60-90%, HbA2 variable, HbA absent/minimal. Thal Intermedia: HbF 20-60%. Thal Trait (carrier): HbA2 >3.5% (typically 4-7%), mild microcytosis. HbE-Beta Thal: HbE + HbF predominant. Available at district hospital level.

Genetic/DNA Analysis: For precise mutation identification. Useful for prenatal diagnosis and genetic counseling. PCR-based detection of common Indian mutations (IVS1-5, 619bp deletion, IVS1-1, Codon 41/42).
2. Iron Overload Monitoring
Serum Ferritin: Every 3 months. Target <1000 ng/mL with chelation. >2500 = high risk of cardiac complications.
Liver Iron Concentration (LIC) by MRI: T2* or R2 MRI quantifies liver iron. Gold standard for iron loading. Available at tertiary centres.
Cardiac T2* MRI: Measures cardiac iron. T2* <20ms = cardiac iron loading. <10ms = high risk of heart failure/arrhythmia. Annual screening from age 10.
3. Key Diagnostic Criteria

Thalassemia Major: Transfusion-dependent anemia (Hb <7 without transfusion), elevated HbF, absent/reduced HbA on electrophoresis, onset <2 years. Thalassemia Intermedia: Anemia not requiring regular transfusions (Hb 7-10), moderate HbF elevation. Thalassemia Trait (Carrier): Asymptomatic, mild microcytic anemia (Hb 10-12), elevated HbA2 (>3.5%).
generic_medicines: 1. Transfusion Therapy (Lifeline for Thal Major)

Regular Red Cell Transfusions: Every 2-4 weeks to maintain pre-transfusion Hb 9.5-10.5 g/dL. Leukoreduced, phenotype-matched packed RBCs. Extended phenotyping (Rh, Kell) to minimize alloimmunization. Lifelong unless cured by transplant.
2. Iron Chelation (EQUALLY IMPORTANT as Transfusion)
Deferasirox (Oral): 20-40mg/kg/day once daily dispersible tablet. First-line oral chelator. Convenient, good adherence. Monitor renal and hepatic function. Most prescribed chelator in India.

Deferoxamine (SC Infusion): 25-50mg/kg/day via subcutaneous infusion pump 8-12 hours/day, 5-7 days/week. Gold standard efficacy but cumbersome. Used for cardiac iron loading or when oral chelators fail.

Deferiprone (Oral): 75-100mg/kg/day in 3 doses. Particularly effective for cardiac iron removal. Can cause agranulocytosis — monitor CBC weekly. Often combined with Deferoxamine for severe iron overload.
3. Supportive Care
Folic Acid: 5mg daily. For increased demand from chronic hemolysis.
Calcium + Vitamin D: For bone health (osteoporosis risk).
Endocrine Monitoring: Annual assessment for growth hormone deficiency, hypothyroidism, diabetes, hypogonadism. Hormone replacement as needed.
Vaccinations: Hepatitis B (transfusion risk), Pneumococcal, annual Influenza.
4. Curative Treatment

Bone Marrow Transplant (BMT): Only CURE. Matched sibling donor gives 80-90% cure rate (Pesaro Class I-II). Haploidentical transplant expanding access. Centres in India: CMC Vellore, Tata Memorial, AIIMS, SGPGI.
Gene Therapy: Emerging curative option (Exagamglogene autotemcel/Casgevy approved globally). Not yet available in India but represents future hope.
5. Prevention (Most Impactful)

Without adequate transfusion and chelation, most die by age 20-30. Without chelation therapy, iron deposits destroy the heart, liver, and endocrine organs — killing patients by age 20-30. Chelation (Deferasirox daily) mu

diseases.name: Mucormycosis
tests: 1. Clinical Suspicion (Critical for Early Diagnosis)

Clinical Red Flags: Unilateral facial pain/swelling/nasal discharge/proptosis in a diabetic/immunosuppressed patient = suspect mucormycosis. Black eschar on nasal/palatal examination is near-pathognomonic. DO NOT wait for biopsy results to start treatment.
2. Tissue Diagnosis (Definitive)

Nasal/Palatal Biopsy with KOH + Histopathology: Biopsy of necrotic tissue. KOH mount shows broad, ribbon-like, pauci-septate hyphae with 90° branching (distinguishes from Aspergillus which has acute-angle branching). Histopathology shows tissue invasion and vascular thrombosis with fungal elements. DIAGNOSTIC.
Fungal Culture: Tissue culture on SDA. Growth of Rhizopus/Mucor species. Takes 3-7 days. Culture negative in ~50% — negative culture does NOT rule out mucormycosis.
3. Imaging
CT Paranasal Sinuses: Sinus opacification, bone erosion, orbital extension. Essential for surgical planning.
MRI Brain + Orbits (With Contrast): Evaluates extent of spread — orbital, intracranial, cavernous sinus. Black turbinate sign on MRI (non-enhancing turbinate = infarcted tissue).
4. Blood Tests
Blood Glucose & HbA1c: Hyperglycemia/undiagnosed DM in vast majority. Aggressive glucose control essential alongside antifungal therapy.
generic_medicines: 1. Antifungal Therapy (START IMMEDIATELY)

Liposomal Amphotericin B (L-AmB): 5-10mg/kg/day IV. FIRST-LINE per ICMR guidelines. Less nephrotoxic than conventional AmB. Duration: 4-6 weeks minimum. Total dose guided by clinical response and imaging.

Amphotericin B Deoxycholate (Conventional): 1-1.5mg/kg/day IV if L-AmB unavailable. More nephrotoxic — aggressive IV hydration + potassium/magnesium supplementation essential. Was the workhorse during India's COVID mucormycosis epidemic when L-AmB supply was exhausted.
Posaconazole: 300mg IV/oral twice on day 1, then 300mg daily. Step-down therapy after initial AmB. Also for patients unable to tolerate AmB. Oral formulation enables outpatient continuation.
Isavuconazole: 200mg IV/oral thrice daily for 6 doses, then 200mg daily. Alternative to Posaconazole.
2. Surgical Debridement (EQUALLY CRITICAL)

Aggressive Surgical Debridement: SURGERY IS ESSENTIAL — antifungals alone are insufficient. Remove ALL necrotic tissue. May require: endoscopic sinus surgery, maxillectomy, orbital exenteration (if orbit involved and vision lost), palatal resection. Repeated debridements often needed. Surgery + antifungal together = best survival.
3. Control Underlying Risk Factors
Aggressive Glycemic Control: Blood glucose 140-180 mg/dL target. Insulin infusion. Correct DKA. This is as important as antifungal therapy.
Reduce Immunosuppression: Taper/stop steroids. Reduce other immunosuppressive medications where possible.
4. Supportive Care
Renal Monitoring: Serum creatinine, potassium, magnesium daily during AmB therapy. Aggressive hydration (1L NS pre-infusion).
Nutritional Support: High-protein diet. NG feeding if palatal/oral involvement prevents eating.
Rehabilitation: Post-surgical prosthetics (obturator for palatal defects), ocular prosthetics. Psychological support for disfigurement.
janaushadhi_medicines: Note: Mucormycosis treatment drugs (Liposomal Amphotericin B, Posaconazole) are specialized medications typically available at tertiary hospitals, not standard Jan Aushadhi products. During the 2021 epidemic, the Indian Government ensured centralized procurement and distribution.
Insulin Vials/Pens: For aggressive glycemic control. Available at Jan Aushadhi.
Potassium Chloride Syrup: For AmB-induced hypokalemia.
ayurvedic_medicines: CRITICAL NOTE — No Ayurvedic Treatment for Mucormycosis: Mucormycosis is an aggressive, rapidly fatal fungal infection requiring IV antifungals + surgery. NO herbal preparation can treat invasive mucormycosis. Ayurvedic consultation during active mucormycosis causes fatal delay.
Post-Treatment Recovery Support: After completing antifungal course and surgical healing, immunomodulatory Rasayanas (Ashwagandha, Guduchi, Chyawanprash) may support general immune recovery.

Diabetes Management (Ayurvedic Adjunct): Gudmar, Vijaysar, and Karela can support glycemic control alongside insulin/oral antidiabetics — helping prevent mucormycosis recurrence by maintaining better sugar control.

Caused by Rhizopus/Mucor species invading immunosuppressed, hyperglycemic COVID patients. Diabetes — Primary Risk Factor: Uncontrolled diabetes (especially DKA) is the leading risk factor in India — present in 70-80% of mucormycosis cases. Hyperglyc
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations ABSOLUTE EMERGENCY — Surgery + Antifungal Together: Mucormycosis has 50-80% mortality without treatment. Antifungals ALONE are insufficient — surgical debridement of all necrotic tissue is essential. Delay in either antifungals or surgery dramatically increases mortality. Co

diseases.name: Acute Otitis Media
tests: 1. Clinical Examination (Primary Diagnostic Method)

Otoscopic Examination: Visualization of tympanic membrane. Diagnostic findings: bulging, erythema, opacification, decreased mobility, fluid level, perforation with discharge. EVERY health facility should have an otoscope. Train PHC doctors and ANMs in otoscopy.
Pneumatic Otoscopy: Assesses tympanic membrane mobility. Decreased mobility = middle ear effusion. More accurate than simple otoscopy.
2. Additional Tests (When Indicated)
Tympanometry: Objective measurement of middle ear pressure and compliance. Type B (flat) tympanogram = middle ear effusion. Available at ENT clinics.
Pure Tone Audiometry: For children with recurrent/chronic OM — assess hearing loss. Available at district hospital ENT departments.

Ear Discharge Culture & Sensitivity: For CSOM or AOM not responding to first-line antibiotics. Identifies pathogen (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, M. catarrhalis, P. aeruginosa in CSOM) and guides targeted therapy.
3. Imaging
CT Temporal Bone: Only for suspected complications — mastoiditis, intracranial extension (rare).
4. Key Diagnostic Criteria
generic_medicines: 1. Pain Management (PRIORITY)
Paracetamol: 15mg/kg every 4-6 hours. FIRST intervention for AOM — pain relief is the immediate priority, not antibiotics.
Ibuprofen: 10mg/kg every 6-8 hours. Anti-inflammatory. Can alternate with Paracetamol for severe pain.
Topical Analgesic Ear Drops (Lignocaine + Phenazone): 3-4 drops in affected ear for rapid local pain relief. Available as Otocalm/Otalgia drops. Effective for 20-30 minutes.
2. Antibiotic Therapy
Amoxicillin: 80-90mg/kg/day in 2 divided doses for 7-10 days. FIRST-LINE antibiotic when indicated. High-dose to cover resistant Pneumococcus.
Amoxicillin-Clavulanate: 90/6.4mg/kg/day for treatment failure after 48-72 hours on Amoxicillin. Covers beta-lactamase producing H. influenzae and M. catarrhalis.
Ceftriaxone IM: 50mg/kg single dose for 3 days. For vomiting child unable to take oral antibiotics, or treatment failure.
Azithromycin: 10mg/kg day 1, then 5mg/kg days 2-5. For Penicillin-allergic patients.
3. When to Give Antibiotics vs Watch & Wait

Immediate Antibiotics: All children <6 months, severe symptoms (temp ≥39°C, severe pain, bilateral AOM), otorrhea. Watch & Wait (48-72 hours): Children ≥2 years with mild unilateral AOM without otorrhea — reassess if not improving. Many resolve spontaneously.
4. CSOM Management
Topical Antibiotic-Steroid Ear Drops: Ciprofloxacin + Dexamethasone ear drops for CSOM with active discharge. Aural toilet (dry mopping) before instillation.
Surgical: Tympanoplasty/Myringoplasty for CSOM with persistent perforation after infection control. Available at district hospital ENT.
5. Prevention

diseases.name: Pellagra & B-Vitamin Deficiency
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (3 Ds Pattern)

Clinical Recognition: Symmetric photosensitive dermatitis + diarrhea + neuropsychiatric symptoms in a patient with poor diet (maize/jowar-dependent) or alcoholism = pellagra. Casal's necklace is pathognomonic.
2. Laboratory Tests
Urinary N-Methylnicotinamide: Low levels (<0.5 mg/day) confirm niacin deficiency. Most specific test but not widely available.
Serum Niacin/NAD Levels: Low in deficiency but not routinely available in India.
Complete Blood Count: May show macrocytic anemia (if B12/folate co-deficiency) or normocytic.
Serum Vitamin B12 & Folate: Check for co-existing deficiencies.
Serum Albumin: Low in severe malnutrition.
3. Therapeutic Trial

Response to Niacin: Dramatic clinical improvement within 24-48 hours of niacin supplementation is practically diagnostic. Skin improves within days, diarrhea resolves in 1-2 days, mental symptoms over weeks.
generic_medicines: 1. Niacin Replacement (Curative)

Nicotinamide (Niacinamide): 100mg thrice daily for 3-4 weeks. Preferred over Nicotinic acid (less flushing). Dramatic improvement in 24-48 hours — diarrhea resolves, skin begins healing, mental symptoms improve over weeks. Continue until clinical resolution then maintenance through diet.
Nicotinic Acid (Niacin): 50-100mg thrice daily. Alternative. Causes flushing (harmless but uncomfortable).
2. B-Complex Supplementation
Vitamin B Complex: Multiple B vitamins often co-deficient. Supplement all: B1 (Thiamine), B2 (Riboflavin), B6 (Pyridoxine), B12, and Folic acid alongside Niacin.
3. Nutritional Rehabilitation
High-Protein Diet: Include dal/pulses, eggs, milk, fish, groundnuts — all rich in tryptophan (niacin precursor). Dietary diversity is the long-term solution.
4. Treat Underlying Cause
Alcohol Cessation: For alcoholic pellagra — address alcohol dependence.
Dietary Education: Diversify from maize/jowar monoculture. Include fortified cereals, pulses, groundnuts.
5. Prevention
Food Fortification: India's FSSAI-mandated fortification of rice and wheat flour with B vitamins. Support fortified food distribution through PDS.

Til/Sesame (Sesamum indicum): Rich in B vitamins and minerals. Traditional Indian ingredient. Include in diet.

Deccan plateau (Maharashtra, AP, Telangana, Karnataka) populations historically affected. Chronic Alcoholism: Alcohol impairs niacin absorption and metabolism. Alcoholic pellagra is common in Indian males with chronic alcohol
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Pellagra Is 100% Curable & Preventable: Niacin (Nicotinamide) 100mg thrice daily CURES pellagra within days. A disease that kills (4th D = Death) is easily reversed with a vitamin costing <₹1/day. Early recognition saves lives. Dietary Diversity Is the Long-Term Solution: Ma

diseases.name: Migraine & Headache
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (ICHD-3 Criteria)

Migraine Without Aura (Most Common): ≥5 attacks with: headache 4-72 hours + ≥2 of (unilateral, pulsating, moderate-severe intensity, aggravated by activity) + ≥1 of (nausea/vomiting, photophobia+phonophobia). NO investigation needed for typical migraine.

Migraine With Aura: ≥2 attacks with: fully reversible visual/sensory/speech symptoms + ≥3 of (at least 1 symptom spreads over ≥5 min, 2+ symptoms occur in succession, each lasts 5-60 min, followed by headache within 60 min).
Tension-Type Headache: ≥10 episodes, 30 min-7 days, ≥2 of (bilateral, pressing/tightening, mild-moderate, not aggravated by activity), NO nausea/vomiting.
2. Red Flag Headaches (SNOOP Criteria — Need Investigation)

Red Flags Requiring Imaging/LP: Systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss, cancer, HIV), Neurological signs (focal deficit, papilledema), Onset sudden ('thunderclap'), Onset after age 50 (new headache), Pattern change (progressive worsening). Any red flag → CT/MRI brain + CSF analysis to exclude secondary causes.
3. Investigations (Only for Red Flags)
MRI Brain (With/Without Contrast): For atypical headache, red flags, neurological signs. Excludes tumor, AVM, venous sinus thrombosis.
CT Brain: Emergency setting — for sudden severe headache to exclude subarachnoid hemorrhage.
Lumbar Puncture: After imaging — for suspected meningitis, SAH (xanthochromia), idiopathic intracranial hypertension.
generic_medicines: 1. Acute Migraine Treatment (Abort the Attack)
Paracetamol 1g + Metoclopramide 10mg: First-line combination for mild-moderate migraine. Metoclopramide treats nausea AND improves Paracetamol absorption.
Ibuprofen/Naproxen: Ibuprofen 400-600mg or Naproxen 500-750mg. NSAIDs are effective first-line for mild-moderate attacks. Take early in attack for best effect.

Sumatriptan: 50-100mg oral or 6mg subcutaneous. GOLD STANDARD for moderate-severe migraine. Triptan-specific — works on serotonin 5-HT1B/1D receptors. Take at onset of headache (not during aura). Contraindicated in uncontrolled hypertension, coronary artery disease, stroke history.
Avoid Analgesic Overuse: Limit acute medications to ≤10 days/month (triptans) or ≤15 days/month (simple analgesics) to prevent Medication Overuse Headache.
2. Preventive Therapy (If ≥4 attacks/month or disabling)
Propranolol: 40-160mg daily in 2 divided doses. First-line preventive. Also helps performance anxiety. Avoid in asthma.
Amitriptyline: 10-75mg at bedtime. Dual benefit for migraine + depression/insomnia. Dry mouth, drowsiness common initially.
Topiramate: 50-100mg daily. Effective preventive. Side benefit: weight loss. Side effects: cognitive slowing, paresthesias, kidney stones.
Flunarizine: 5-10mg at bedtime. Calcium channel blocker widely used in India for migraine prevention. Weight gain is main limitation.
Valproate: 500-1000mg daily. Effective but teratogenic — avoid in women of childbearing age.
CGRP Monoclonal Antibodies (Emerging): Erenumab, Fremanezumab — monthly injections. Highly effective, well-tolerated. Expensive in India but becoming available.
3. Non-Pharmacological (Important Adjuncts)
Trigger Avoidance: Maintain headache diary to identify triggers. Common Indian triggers: disrupted sleep, skipped meals, dehydration, heat, strong odors, alcohol, MSG, processed food.
Regular Exercise: 30 minutes moderate aerobic exercise 5 days/week reduces migraine frequency by 40%.

Pathyadi Kwatha / Godanti Bhasma: Godanti (gypsum) Bhasma is a classical Pitta-shamaka and Shirahshoolahara. 250-500mg twice daily with honey. Traditional migraine remedy.
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri): Medhya Rasayana. Reduces stress-triggered migraine. Published anxiolytic activity. 300-500mg daily.

Nasya (Nasal Oil Therapy): Anu Taila or Shadbindu Taila nasal drops. 2-3 drops each nostril daily. Classical Shirahshoola treatment through nasal route. Published evidence for migraine relief.
Yoga & Pranayama: Anulom-Vilom, Bhramari, Shavasana, meditation — published RCT evidence for migraine prevention. Daily practice reduces attack frequency.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Massive Prevalence — Under-treated: India has an estimated 150+ million people with migraine. Prevalence 14-25% in studies. Yet majority never receive proper diagnosis — labeled 'sinus headache', 'weak eyes', 'gas', or 'tension'. <5% receive preventive therapy. Self-Medication & Over-the-Counter Abuse: Widespread use of OTC analgesic combinations (Paracetamol+Caffeine+analgesic cocktails) and ergotamine without prescription. Leads to Medication Overuse
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Migraine Is a Real Neurological Disease: Migraine is NOT 'just a headache' or psychosomatic. It's a genuine neurological disorder affecting 150+ million Indians. Proper diagnosis and treatment dramatically improve quality of life. Take it seriously. Avoid Medication Overuse

diseases.name: Lower Back Pain
tests: 1. Clinical Assessment (Sufficient for Most Cases)

History & Red Flag Screening: Assess pain character, duration, radiation, neurological symptoms. Screen for RED FLAGS: age <20 or >55 with new back pain, history of cancer, unexplained weight loss, fever, night pain, bladder/bowel dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, progressive neurological deficit, thoracic pain, recent trauma. Red flags → immediate investigation.

Neurological Examination: Straight Leg Raise (SLR) — positive if pain radiates below knee at <60°. Test motor power (L4: knee extension, L5: big toe dorsiflexion, S1: plantarflexion). Test sensation (L4: medial leg, L5: dorsum foot, S1: lateral foot). Test ankle reflex (S1).
2. Imaging (Only When Indicated)

MRI Lumbosacral Spine: ONLY for red flags, progressive neurological deficit, or pain not improving after 6 weeks of conservative treatment. Do NOT MRI every acute LBP case — leads to overdiagnosis and unnecessary surgery.
X-ray Lumbosacral Spine: For trauma, suspected fracture, age >50, osteoporosis risk. Shows alignment, fractures, spondylolisthesis.
3. Blood Tests (If Red Flags Present)
ESR/CRP: Elevated in infection (spinal TB, discitis) or inflammatory cause (ankylosing spondylitis).
Complete Blood Count: For suspected infection or malignancy.
HLA-B27: In young males (<40) with inflammatory back pain (morning stiffness >30 min, improves with exercise, worse with rest) — suspect ankylosing spondylitis.
generic_medicines: 1. Acute LBP (First-Line Conservative)
Paracetamol: 1g every 6 hours. First-line analgesic. Safe for short-term use.

NSAIDs (Ibuprofen/Naproxen/Diclofenac): Ibuprofen 400mg thrice daily or Diclofenac 50mg twice daily for 7-14 days. Most effective pharmacotherapy for acute LBP. Use lowest dose for shortest duration. Gastroprotection with PPI if needed.
Muscle Relaxants (Thiocolchicoside/Cyclobenzaprine): Thiocolchicoside 4-8mg twice daily for 5-7 days. For acute muscle spasm. Short-term use only — sedation common.
Stay Active: MOST IMPORTANT advice — stay as active as possible. Bed rest WORSENS LBP. Resume normal activities within pain tolerance. Walking is therapeutic.
2. Neuropathic Pain / Radiculopathy
Pregabalin/Gabapentin: Pregabalin 75-150mg twice daily or Gabapentin 300-1200mg thrice daily. For shooting, nerve-type pain. Effective for sciatica.
Amitriptyline: 10-25mg at bedtime. For chronic LBP with neuropathic component + sleep disturbance.
Epidural Steroid Injection: For severe radiculopathy not responding to conservative treatment. Performed under fluoroscopic guidance. Provides 2-6 weeks relief.
3. Chronic LBP Management

Multimodal Approach: Exercise therapy (core strengthening, flexibility) + pharmacotherapy + psychological support (CBT for pain catastrophizing) + ergonomic advice. No single treatment is sufficient for chronic LBP.
Physical Therapy: Supervised exercise programme is the MOST effective treatment for chronic LBP. Core stability exercises, McKenzie exercises, aquatic therapy.
Yoga: Published RCT evidence (multiple Indian studies) showing Yoga reduces chronic LBP pain and disability. Recommended by international guidelines.
4. Surgical (Very Selective — <5% of LBP Patients)
Microdiscectomy: For disc herniation with progressive neurological deficit or failed 6-12 weeks conservative treatment. >90% success rate for appropriate indications.

Ibuprofen Tablets IP 400mg: NSAID for acute LBP. Pack of 10.
Diclofenac Tablets IP 50mg: Alternative NSAID. Pack of 10.

Amitriptyline Tablets 10mg: For chronic LBP. Pack of 10.

ayurvedic_medicines: Kati Basti (Warm Oil Pooling): Classical Ayurvedic Panchakarma procedure for Katigraha (lower back pain). Warm medicated oil pooled on lumbosacral area within a dough boundary for 30-45 minutes. Deeply relaxing and therapeutic. Published evidence for pain reduction.

Abhyanga (Oil Massage) with Mahanarayana Taila: Warm medicated oil full-body massage. Mahanarayana Taila is the classical oil for Vata-related musculoskeletal pain. Reduces muscle spasm and improves circulation.
Pinda Sweda (Bolus Fomentation): Medicated bolus fomentation using Njavara rice or sand bolus. Applied over lumbosacral area. Heat + herbs provide analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects.
Guggulu Preparations: Yogaraja Guggulu (for Vata disorders), Laksha Guggulu (for bone/joint), Simhanada Guggulu (for chronic back pain). 2 tablets twice daily.
Ashwagandha: Anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and muscle-strengthening. Published evidence for musculoskeletal pain. 300-600mg twice daily.
Shallaki / Boswellia (Boswellia serrata): Published evidence for anti-inflammatory activity (5-LOX inhibition). 300-500mg thrice daily. Reduces pain and improves mobility.
Nirgundi (Vitex negundo): Classical Shoolahara and Shothaghna. Nirgundi taila for external application. Nirgundi leaves fomentation. Published anti-inflammatory activity.
Dashamoola Kashaya: Ten-root decoction for Vata-Kapha disorders. Anti-inflammatory and analgesic. 15-30ml twice daily.

Yoga Therapy: Bhujangasana (Cobra pose), Shalabhasana (Locust pose), Setu Bandhasana (Bridge pose), Marjariasana (Cat-Cow), Shavasana. Published RCT evidence for chronic LBP. Daily practice essential.

Rising due to sedentary office work, smartphone posture, poor ergonomics, and manual labor without protection. Affects all age groups from college students to elderly. Occupational Back Pain: Agricultural laborers (rice planting — prolonged forward bending), construction workers (heavy lifting), auto-rickshaw/truck drivers (whole-body vibration), and office workers (prolonged
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Stay Active — Bed Rest WORSENS Back Pain: The most important message for acute LBP. Prolonged bed rest deconditions muscles and increases disability. Walk, move, stay active within pain tolerance. Resume normal activities as soon as possible. Do NOT MRI Every Back Pain: 85-9

diseases.name: Psoriasis
tests: 1. Primary Clinical Assessment
Dermatological Examination: Visual inspection for silvery scales, erythematous base, symmetrical distribution. Assess Body Surface Area (BSA) — 1 palm = 1% BSA.
PASI Score (Psoriasis Area and Severity Index): 0–72 scale measuring redness, thickness, scaling, and area. PASI <10 = mild; 10–20 = moderate; >20 = severe. Required for biologic therapy eligibility.
Auspitz Sign: Gentle scale removal reveals pinpoint bleeding — positive in psoriasis; differentiates from eczema and fungal infections.
Koebner Phenomenon Assessment: Document lesions at trauma sites; confirms pathergy response.
DLQI (Dermatology Life Quality Index): 10-item questionnaire; score >10 = major QoL impact. Mandatory before systemic/biologic therapy in IADVL guidelines.
2. Routine Blood Tests
Complete Blood Count (CBC): Mild leukocytosis in pustular/erythrodermic psoriasis. Anaemia possible with high inflammatory burden.
ESR & CRP: Elevated in active moderate-severe psoriasis — baseline before systemic therapy.
Liver Function Tests (LFT): Mandatory before and during Methotrexate. Psoriasis patients have higher NAFLD risk.
generic_medicines: 1. Topical Treatments (Mild-Moderate Psoriasis)
Betamethasone Dipropionate 0.05% Cream/Ointment: Potent topical corticosteroid, first-line for plaque psoriasis. Apply once/twice daily for 2–4 weeks; avoid prolonged use on face/flexures.
Calcipotriol 0.005% Cream/Ointment (Vit D3 analogue): Anti-proliferative; reduces plaque thickness. Highly effective combined with betamethasone (Daivobet). Max 100g/week.
Coal Tar 1–5% Cream/Shampoo: Antipruritic and anti-proliferative. Used for scalp psoriasis and chronic plaque psoriasis.
Salicylic Acid 2–6% Ointment: Keratolytic; removes thick scales before topical steroid/calcipotriol application — enhances penetration.
Tacrolimus 0.1% / Pimecrolimus 1% Cream: Steroid-free calcineurin inhibitors for face, flexures, and genitalia.
2. Systemic Treatments (Moderate-Severe Psoriasis)
Methotrexate 7.5–25mg once weekly (oral/IM): Gold standard systemic therapy. Co-prescribe Folic Acid 5mg/day (not on MTX day). Monitor LFT + CBC regularly.
Acitretin 25–50mg/day oral: Oral retinoid for pustular, erythrodermic, and palmoplantar psoriasis. Highly teratogenic — strict contraception mandatory.
Cyclosporine 2.5–5mg/kg/day oral: Rapid-acting immunosuppressant. Short courses (3–6 months) due to nephrotoxicity and hypertension risk.
Apremilast 30mg BD oral: PDE4 inhibitor; oral targeted therapy used when MTX fails or is not tolerated. GI side effects common.
3. Biologic Therapies (Severe/Refractory Psoriasis)
Adalimumab (Anti-TNF): SC 80mg initial → 40mg fortnightly. TB screening (Mantoux/IGRA) mandatory before initiation in India.
Secukinumab (Anti-IL17A): SC 300mg weekly x5 doses → monthly. Highly effective; preferred for nail and PsA involvement.
Ustekinumab (Anti-IL12/23): SC 45/90mg (weight-based) at 0, 4 weeks then 12-weekly. PASI 75 in ~67% patients.
janaushadhi_medicines: Betamethasone Valerate 0.1% Cream (15g/30g): First-line affordable topical corticosteroid for plaque psoriasis. Available at PMBJP stores.
Methotrexate Tablets 2.5mg (10 tablets): First-line systemic therapy for moderate-severe psoriasis and PsA. Available at Jan Aushadhi Kendras.
Salicylic Acid Ointment 6% (25g): Keratolytic for scale removal; available at PMBJP.
Coal Tar Shampoo (100ml): For scalp psoriasis; available as generic at Jan Aushadhi stores.
Folic Acid 5mg Tablets (10 tablets): Mandatory co-prescription with Methotrexate to prevent toxicity. All PMBJP stores.
Acitretin 10mg/25mg Capsules: Generic oral retinoid for severe psoriasis. Limited Jan Aushadhi availability.
Vitamin D3 + Calcium Tablets (60 tablets): Supportive supplement; psoriasis patients are frequently Vitamin D deficient.
ayurvedic_medicines: Panchatikta Ghrita Guggulu: Classical formulation for Kushtha (skin diseases). Contains Nimba (Neem), Patola, Guduchi, Vasa, and Guggulu. Anti-inflammatory and blood-purifying. 2 tablets twice daily with warm water/ghee.
Khadirarishta: Fermented Ayurvedic liquid with Khadira (Acacia catechu). Traditional blood purifier for chronic skin diseases including psoriasis. 15–20ml with equal water twice daily after meals.

Mahamanjisthadhi Kashaya: Classical decoction with Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia), Neem, and Guduchi. Powerful blood-purifier and anti-inflammatory for Vata-Pitta Kushtha. 15ml twice daily before meals.
Arogyavardhini Vati: Classical tablet with Kutki, Triphala, and purified minerals. Used for skin diseases with liver dysfunction. 2 tablets twice daily.
Neem (Azadirachta indica) Capsules/Tablets: Anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and immunomodulatory activity. Supports management of psoriatic plaques.
Turmeric (Curcumin) Extract Capsules (Himalaya/Organic India): Evidence-based anti-inflammatory (NF-kB, COX-2 inhibition). 1000–1500mg/day. Widely available in India.
Wrightia tinctoria (Pala Indigo) Oil: Traditional topical oil for psoriasis in South India. Anti-psoriatic activity demonstrated in clinical studies.
Indukantham Kashayam (Kerala Ayurveda): Classical Ayurvedic decoction used in Panchakarma preparation and skin disease management.
Virechana (Therapeutic Purgation — Panchakarma): Under qualified Ayurvedic physician, Virechana using Trivrit Avaleha + Castor oil for Pitta-dominant psoriasis. Removes accumulated Ama.
Chandana-Manjistha Lepam: Topical paste of Chandana (sandalwood), Manjistha, and Neem powder in coconut oil for anti-inflammatory and cooling effect on plaques.

Stress-Triggered Flares: Psychosocial stress from socioeconomic pressures and urban migration activate the HPA axis, worsening psoriatic inflammation. diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Never Use Topical Steroids Unsupervised Long-Term: Prolonged use of potent topical corticosteroids on face, folds, or genitalia causes skin atrophy, stretch marks, and rebound flares. Many unlicensed creams and 'Ayurvedic' preparations in India contain undisclosed steroids —

diseases.name: Atopic Dermatitis
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (Hanifin & Rajka / UK Working Party Criteria)
Hanifin & Rajka Criteria: Minimum 3 major + 3 minor criteria. Major: Pruritus, chronic/relapsing course, typical morphology/distribution, personal/family atopy history.
SCORAD Index: 0–103. Mild <25, Moderate 25–50, Severe >50. Measures extent (rule of nines), intensity (redness, swelling, oozing, scratch marks, lichenification, dryness), and subjective symptoms.
EASI (Eczema Area and Severity Index): 0–72. Mild <1.1, Moderate 1.1–21, Severe >21.
POEM (Patient-Oriented Eczema Measure): 7-item patient-reported score 0–28. Clear 0–2, Mild 3–7, Moderate 8–16, Severe 17–28.
IGA (Investigator Global Assessment): 5-point scale (0=clear to 4=severe). Standard endpoint in clinical trials.
2. Allergy & Immunological Tests
Total Serum IgE: Markedly elevated (often >2000 IU/mL) in AD. Correlates with severity; supports atopic diagnosis.
Specific IgE (RAST/ImmunoCAP): For house dust mite, cockroach, molds, food allergens (egg, milk, wheat). Guides allergen avoidance.
Skin Prick Testing (SPT): Identifies type I hypersensitivity. Performed by allergist; not available at PHC level.
generic_medicines: 1. Emollients & Skin Barrier Repair (First-Line for ALL Severity)
White Petrolatum (Petroleum Jelly): Most cost-effective emollient for Indian patients per IADVL. Apply liberally within 3 minutes of bathing to restore hydration and reduce transepidermal water loss.
Glycerine Lotion/Cream: Humectant; affordable, widely available. Apply daily after bathing — especially useful in dry Indian climate.
Paraffin Soft (White/Yellow 50% in Aqueous Cream): Standard emollient for AD. Cost-effective and readily available.
2. Topical Anti-Inflammatory Treatments
Hydrocortisone 1% Cream: Mild topical steroid for face, skin folds, and infants. Short-term use for flares — twice daily.
Betamethasone Valerate 0.1% Cream/Ointment: Moderate-potent topical steroid for body and limb plaques. Max 2 weeks per flare course.
Tacrolimus 0.03%/0.1% Ointment (Protopic): Calcineurin inhibitor; safe for face/folds; steroid-sparing. 0.03% for children, 0.1% for adults.
Pimecrolimus 1% Cream (Elidel): Mild calcineurin inhibitor for mild-moderate AD; suitable for sensitive areas.
3. Antihistamines (Symptomatic)
Cetirizine 10mg / Levocetirizine 5mg oral: Second-generation, minimal sedation, once daily — for pruritus control. Syrup for children.
Hydroxyzine 25mg oral: Sedating antihistamine; useful for nocturnal pruritus at bedtime. Not daytime use in children.
4. Antibiotics (Superinfected AD)
Cloxacillin / Flucloxacillin 500mg QID oral (7–10 days): First-line for S. aureus superinfection.
Mupirocin 2% Ointment: Topical for localized impetigo/infected eczema patches.
janaushadhi_medicines: Cetirizine Tablets 10mg: For pruritus control in AD. Very low cost at Jan Aushadhi Kendras.
Hydrocortisone Acetate Cream 1% (15g): Mild topical steroid for face/folds in AD. Available at PMBJP stores.
White Petrolatum 100g: Most cost-effective emollient for skin barrier repair. Available at Jan Aushadhi stores.
Cloxacillin Capsules 500mg: For secondary bacterial infection treatment. PM Jan Aushadhi Kendras.
Levocetirizine 5mg Tablets: Once-daily pruritus control. Available at PMBJP.
Prednisolone 10mg Tablets: Short-course for severe AD flares. Jan Aushadhi Kendras.

Vitamin D3 + Calcium Tablets (60 tabs): Vitamin D supplementation improves AD outcomes.
Zinc Sulphate Syrup 20mg/5ml (100ml): Zinc supplementation for children with AD plus nutritional deficiency.
ayurvedic_medicines: Nalpamaradi Tailam (Kottakkal/Nagarjuna): Traditional Kerala Ayurvedic oil with Nalpamara bark (Ficus species) and turmeric in coconut oil. Topically reduces pruritus, moisturizes, and treats eczematous plaques.
Madhuyashtyadi Tailam: Classical oil with Yashtimadhu (Licorice), Chandana, and Manjistha in sesame base. Anti-inflammatory topical for eczema plaques.
Nimbadi Churna: Powder with Neem, Haridra (turmeric), Khadira, and Chandana. Internal blood purifier for chronic skin diseases including atopic dermatitis.
Aragwadha (Cassia fistula) Decoction: Mild laxative and blood-purifying properties; used in Pitta-predominant skin diseases.
Haridra (Turmeric) Capsules (Organic India/Himalaya): Curcumin's anti-inflammatory activity (NF-kB, COX-2 inhibition) supports atopic inflammation management. 500–1000mg twice daily.
Ashwagandha + Brahmi Tablets: Adaptogenic; reduces cortisol-mediated immune dysregulation that triggers AD flares.
Panchanimba Churna: Classical combination of five Neem-family plants with potent anti-allergic and anti-inflammatory properties.
Avipattikar Churna: Pitta-pacifying digestive formulation; useful for AD with GI association.
Chyawanprash (10g daily): Immunomodulator with Amla (Vitamin C) and 40+ herbs. Supports immune regulation in atopic children.
Abhyanga with Bala Ashwagandhadi Tailam: Daily medicated oil massage for Vata-Pitta balance, skin moisturization, and stress management.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Hard Water & Pollution: India's northern states have high water hardness (>200 mg/L CaCO3); hard water damages skin barrier, worsening AD severity. Urban PM2.5 air pollution is an established AD trigger. Tropical Climate & Sweating: India's hot, humid climate leads to excessive sweating — a strong AD trigger causing miliaria-like heat rash and worsening pruritus. Cooling access is limited in rural areas. Nutritional Deficiencies: Zinc, Vitamin D, and e
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Emollients Are Non-Negotiable First-Line Treatment: Moisturizers must be applied at least 2–3 times daily even when skin appears clear. White petroleum jelly (affordable, widely available) is as effective as expensive branded products — teach families this explicitly. Topica

diseases.name: Vitiligo
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS
Vitiligo diagnosed clinically by experienced dermatologist; characteristic chalk-white patches, symmetry, distribution
WOOD'S LAMP EXAMINATION (365 nm UV)
Chalk-white blue-white fluorescent enhancement of vitiligo patches; essential for detecting subclinical lesions invisible in fair skin; assessing true extent of depigmentation
DERMOSCOPY
Reticular depigmentation; perifollicular pigmentation sparing (sign of repigmentation); leucotrichia; telangiectasias; perilesional inflammatory pigment network in active disease
DISEASE EXTENT AND ACTIVITY SCORING
VASI (Vitiligo Area Scoring Index) — 0–100; body area + percentage depigmentation at each site
VETF (Vitiligo European Task Force score) — extent, staging, and spreading parameters
Standardised photography at each visit — objective repigmentation documentation
generic_medicines: TOPICAL THERAPY
Topical corticosteroids — mainstay for localised vitiligo; Clobetasol propionate 0.05% (Class I) for body; Mometasone 0.1% for face/body; daily 3 months then 2 months rest; monitor for atrophy
Topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment (children: 0.03%) — calcineurin inhibitor; first-line for face, eyelids, genitalia (steroid-sensitive sites); combine with phototherapy for enhanced response
Topical pimecrolimus 1% cream — alternative calcineurin inhibitor; face and genitalia
Ruxolitinib 1.5% cream (JAK1/2 inhibitor) — most effective topical for NSV; apply BD; 75% facial repigmentation; currently expensive/imported in India — 2022 FDA approval
PHOTOTHERAPY

Narrowband UVB (NB-UVB, 311–312 nm) — GOLD STANDARD for generalised NSV; 3 sessions/week; response in 3–6 months; best repigmentation on face and trunk; minimal carcinogenicity; combine with topical tacrolimus (synergistic)
Excimer laser (308 nm) — targeted high-dose UV for focal stable vitiligo patches; up to 95% repigmentation in selected facial patches; faster response than NB-UVB
PUVA (psoralen + UVA) — Methoxypsoralen 0.6 mg/kg + UVA; effective but higher carcinogenicity and nausea; less preferred than NB-UVB
SYSTEMIC THERAPY

Oral minipulse (OMP) dexamethasone 2.5 mg on 2 consecutive weekend days each week — arrests progressive disease; India-developed protocol (Pasricha et al, AIIMS); 3–6 months; for STOPPING SPREAD, not repigmentation
Oral Methylprednisolone pulse — alternative stabilising therapy
Simvastatin 40 mg/day — immunomodulatory adjunct; some evidence for stabilising activity
SURGICAL (Only for stable vitiligo ≥12 months)
Suction blister epidermal grafting (SBEG) — gold standard surgical; segmental vitiligo excellent candidate
janaushadhi_medicines: Vitamin D3 60000 IU tablet (calcipotriol-similar adjunct role)
ayurvedic_medicines: BAKUCHI — THE CLASSICAL AYURVEDIC VITILIGO TREATMENT

Bakuchi (Psoralea corylifolia) — most important Ayurvedic treatment for Shwitra (vitiligo); specifically described in Charaka Samhita Chikitsa Sthana Chapter 7; contains Psoralen and Bakuchiol — natural photosensitisers functionally equivalent to medical PUVA psoralen; topical Bakuchi oil + regulated sun exposure = traditional PUVA; oral Bakuchi seed powder 500 mg with milk

Critical instruction: Bakuchi requires calibrated sun exposure — apply topical oil, wait 30 minutes, expose to morning or evening gentle sun for 5–10 minutes initially; gradually increase by 5 minutes/session; overexposure = severe burns; never use without medical supervision
Chitraka (Plumbago zeylanica) — used alongside Bakuchi in classical Ayurvedic vitiligo formulations; photosensitising and melanocyte-stimulating
SYSTEMIC
Khadirarista — blood-purifying fermented formulation; Khadira (Acacia catechu) base; classic Kushtha (skin disease) treatment; anti-inflammatory
Tikta Ghrita — bitter-component ghee preparation; blood purifying in chronic skin disease; reduces autoimmune inflammatory component
Arogyavardhini Vati — systemic liver and blood-purifying action; anti-inflammatory for autoimmune skin
Haridra (Turmeric) oral — anti-inflammatory; reduces autoimmune activity; 500 mg BD
COPPER SUPPLEMENTATION

diseases.name: Urticaria
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis & Classification

UAS7 (Urticaria Activity Score 7): Daily wheals (0–3) + itch intensity (0–3) summed over 7 days. Score 0–6=well controlled, 7–15=mild, 16–27=moderate, 28–42=severe. Monitors treatment response per EAACI/IADVL guidelines.
Duration Classification: Acute urticaria = <6 weeks. Chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU) = >6 weeks; daily/near-daily wheals. Diagnosis is primarily clinical.
Autologous Serum Skin Test (ASST): Intradermal injection of patient's own serum — positive whealing response in autoimmune CSU. Available at dermatology centres. Not routine at PHC.
Dermographometer Testing: Calibrated pressure applied to skin to confirm symptomatic dermographism.
2. Basic Investigations (Chronic Urticaria >6 Weeks)
CBC with Differential: Eosinophilia suggests parasitic infestation or drug reaction. Leukocytosis may indicate infection-triggered urticaria.
ESR & CRP: Elevated in urticarial vasculitis and systemic disease-associated urticaria. Differentiates from simple CSU.
Thyroid Function Tests (TSH, T3, T4) + Anti-TPO Antibodies: Mandatory in all CSU patients — autoimmune thyroid disease is a major reversible cause in India.
Total IgE Level: Elevated in allergic/atopic urticaria. Baseline before Omalizumab (anti-IgE biologic) therapy.
generic_medicines: 1. First-Line: Second-Generation Antihistamines (Non-Sedating)
Cetirizine 10mg once daily oral: First-choice. Onset 1 hour, 24-hour duration. Minimal sedation. Safe in pregnancy (avoid first trimester). Syrup available for children.
Levocetirizine 5mg once daily oral: More potent than Cetirizine. Preferred for severe pruritus. Once daily — high compliance.
Loratadine 10mg once daily oral: Non-sedating. Safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding. First-line for daytime use.
Fexofenadine 120–180mg once daily oral: Most non-sedating antihistamine. Ideal for patients requiring alertness (drivers, machine operators).
Desloratadine 5mg once daily oral: Active metabolite of Loratadine; more potent. Once daily.
2. Up-Dosing (IADVL Step 2)
Cetirizine/Levocetirizine Up to 4x Standard Dose: IADVL/EAACI guidelines recommend up-dosing second-generation H1 antihistamines up to 4x daily dose before step-up to add-ons or biologic.
3. Add-On Therapies (Partial Responders)
Montelukast 10mg/day oral: H1-leukotriene receptor antagonist; add-on for aspirin/NSAID-exacerbated urticaria and chronic urticaria partially responding to antihistamines.
Ranitidine/Famotidine (H2 blockers): Add-on to H1 antihistamine; modest benefit in some CSU patients.
4. Short-Course Steroids (Acute Severe / Anaphylaxis)
Prednisolone 0.5–1mg/kg/day oral (3–5 days): For acute severe urticaria flares or urticaria with early angioedema. Not for chronic use.
Hydrocortisone 200mg IV + Chlorphenamine 10mg IV: For anaphylaxis with urticaria — hospital emergency protocol.
5. Adrenaline (Anaphylaxis Emergency)
janaushadhi_medicines: Cetirizine Hydrochloride 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): First-line affordable antihistamine for urticaria. Available at all PMBJP stores.
Levocetirizine 5mg Tablets (10 tabs): Second-generation antihistamine, once daily.
Loratadine 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): Non-sedating antihistamine for daytime use.
Prednisolone 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): Short-course steroids for acute urticaria flare.
Montelukast 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): Add-on therapy for chronic urticaria.
Albendazole 400mg Tablets (single dose): Deworming for parasite-associated urticaria.
Ranitidine 150mg Tablets (10 tabs): H2 blocker add-on therapy.
ayurvedic_medicines: Haridra (Turmeric) Khand: Classical anti-allergic and antihistaminic Ayurvedic formulation. Haridra 3–5g with warm milk twice daily — traditional treatment for Sheetapitta (urticaria). Potent mast cell stabilizing activity.
Haridra/Turmeric Capsules (Himalaya/Organic India): Standardized curcumin extract. Clinical evidence for anti-inflammatory and anti-allergic activity. 500mg twice daily after meals.
Giloy (Guduchi) Tablets (Himalaya): Immunomodulatory herb clinically studied for chronic urticaria in CCRAS trials. Reduces frequency and severity of CSU flares. 1–2 tablets twice daily.
Khadirarishta 450ml (AVP/Dabur): Classical fermented formulation with Khadira (Acacia catechu). Blood purifier and anti-allergic. 15–20ml with equal water twice daily after meals.
Haritaki (Terminalia chebula) Powder: Mild laxative and detoxifying herb; removes Ama (toxins) that trigger Pitta-associated urticaria. 3g at bedtime with warm water.
Mahamanjisthadi Kashaya: Classical decoction with Manjistha, Neem, and Guduchi for blood purification in allergic skin diseases. 15ml twice daily before meals.
Panchatikta Ghrita: Classical medicated ghee for Pitta-dominant allergic conditions. Anti-inflammatory and immune-regulatory.
Shirisha (Albizia lebbeck) Bark Powder: Clinically studied Ayurvedic anti-allergic herb; demonstrated efficacy in urticaria and allergic rhinitis. 3–5g twice daily with honey.
Neem Capsules (Himalaya/Organic India): Blood purifier and antihistaminic properties for recurring urticaria associated with parasitic infestation and toxin accumulation.
Trikatu Churna: Digestive spice combination (Ginger, Black Pepper, Long Pepper) — improves Agni, reduces Ama, anti-allergic in Kapha-Pitta urticaria. 1–3g with honey before meals.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Food and Drug Triggers: Common Indian dietary triggers include prawns, crabs, shellfish, groundnuts, and sesame. NSAIDs (widely self-purchased in India) are the most common drug trigger for chronic urticaria exacerbations. Parasitic Infestation Co-Association: Intestinal parasites (Ascaris, Giardia, Strongyloides) are a significant cause of chronic urticaria in India. Deworming resolves urticaria in ~20% of cases in endemic regions — mandatory exclusio
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Anaphylaxis is a Life-Threatening Emergency: Urticaria with throat tightening, difficulty breathing, dizziness, or low blood pressure = ANAPHYLAXIS. Immediate Adrenaline (Epinephrine) 0.5mg IM is the ONLY first-line treatment. Adrenaline auto-injectors or vials should be ava

diseases.name: Acne Vulgaris
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS
Acne is diagnosed clinically; lesion type counting and grading (GEA/IGA scale) at each visit for treatment monitoring
HORMONAL WORKUP (female patients with hormonal/PCOS pattern acne)
Serum testosterone (total and free) — elevated in hyperandrogenism
DHEAS (dehydroepiandrosterone sulphate) — adrenal androgen; elevated in congenital adrenal hyperplasia
LH/FSH ratio — LH:FSH >2:1 in PCOS
Fasting glucose, insulin, HOMA-IR — insulin resistance in PCOS
Prolactin — hyperprolactinaemia can contribute to hormonal acne
Pelvic ultrasound — polycystic ovarian morphology (PCOM)
TSH — thyroid dysfunction skin changes
generic_medicines: TOPICAL THERAPY

Benzoyl Peroxide (BPO) 2.5–5% gel/wash — bactericidal (kills C. acnes); anti-comedonal; generates NO resistance; first-line foundation of ALL acne treatment regardless of severity; prevents antibiotic resistance when combined with antibiotics
Adapalene 0.1–0.3% gel — retinoid; normalises follicular keratinisation; anti-comedonal; anti-inflammatory; safest retinoid in pregnancy (Category B); apply at night; combine with BPO
Tretinoin 0.025–0.05–0.1% cream/gel — strongest topical retinoid; highly comedolytic; photolabile — nighttime only; sun protection mandatory; initial purging (worsening at 2–4 weeks) is expected
Clindamycin 1% gel/solution — ALWAYS combine with BPO to prevent resistance; NO antibiotic monotherapy
Clindamycin 1% + Benzoyl Peroxide 5% combination gel — most prescribed topical combination in India; superior to either alone
Azelaic acid 15–20% cream/gel — mild-moderate acne + PIH; anti-comedogenic + depigmenting; SAFE in pregnancy
Salicylic acid 2% — beta-hydroxy acid; comedolytic; chemical exfoliant; comedonal acne
SYSTEMIC ANTIBIOTICS (max 3 months; always co-prescribe topical BPO)
Doxycycline 100 mg BD (4–6 weeks) then OD — first-choice tetracycline; anti-inflammatory properties at subantimicrobial dose too
Lymecycline 408 mg OD — tetracycline; once daily; compliance advantage
Azithromycin 500 mg pulse (3 days/week × 12 weeks) — macrolide; use only if tetracycline-intolerant
HORMONAL THERAPY (FEMALES ONLY)
Combined OCP: Ethinyl Estradiol 35 mcg + Cyproterone acetate 2 mg (Diane-35) — anti-androgenic; reduces sebum and hormonal acne
Combined OCP: EE 30 mcg + Drospirenone 3 mg (Yasmin) — anti-androgenic progestogen benefiting acne
ayurvedic_medicines: TOPICAL

Neem (Azadirachta indica) — most evidence-based Ayurvedic topical for acne; antibacterial against C. acnes; anti-inflammatory; sebum reducing; Neem leaf paste or Neem face wash; standard in Indian Ayurvedic skin care
Haridra (Turmeric / Curcuma longa) — topical turmeric + sandalwood paste; anti-inflammatory; antiseborrhoeal
Lodhra (Symplocos racemosa) — astringent; reduces seborrhoea; Lodhradi Churna face mask
Multani Mitti (Fuller's Earth) — sebum-absorbing clay; face pack; reduces comedonal acne
Chandan (Sandalwood) — anti-inflammatory; bacteriostatic; cooling Pitta-reducing; paste for inflamed pustular acne
SYSTEMIC

Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia) — premier Raktashodhaka (blood purifier) in Ayurveda for skin; anti-inflammatory; anti-androgenic properties in animal studies; 250–500 mg extract OD-BD; multiple Indian clinical trials for acne
Sariva (Hemidesmus indicus) — blood purifier; cooling; reduces Pitta-type inflammatory acne; combined with Manjistha
Khadira (Acacia catechu) — strong antimicrobial skin agent; classical Kushtha (skin disease) herb; Khadirarista formulation
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors POST-INFLAMMATORY HYPERPIGMENTATION (PIH) IN INDIAN SKIN Fitzpatrick III-V Indian skin produces excessive melanin after ANY inflammatory insult; PIH from acne can be more distressing than acne itself; persists months-years; requires separate treatment (topical depigmenting agents) HOT HUMID CLIMATE Tropical heat and humidity increase sebum production, sweat-induced comedogenesis, and Malassezia co-infection worsening back acne; seasonal peaks in summer
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations ISOTRETINOIN TERATOGENICITY — PREGNANCY PREVENTION IS NON-NEGOTIABLE: Isotretinoin causes severe, characteristic birth defects (craniofacial, cardiac, CNS malformations) in 25–30% of exposed pregnancies with no safe exposure threshold. ALL female patients of childbearing pot

diseases.name: Pemphigus Vulgaris
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis
Nikolsky Sign Test: Firm lateral pressure on perilesional normal skin — positive if new bulla forms or epidermis peels. Highly suggestive of pemphigus; simple bedside test.
Asboe-Hansen Test: Pressure on intact bulla extends it laterally. Confirms intraepidermal location of split.
Oral Cavity Examination: Full assessment of extent of oral erosions — grading severity and anatomical involvement guides treatment intensity.
2. Histopathology (Gold Standard)

Skin Biopsy (H&E Staining): Peri-lesional or early lesional skin — shows suprabasal acantholysis (separation of epidermal cells), Tzanck cells (rounded acantholytic cells), and basal layer 'tombstoning'.
3. Immunofluorescence (Diagnostic Confirmation — Mandatory)

Direct Immunofluorescence (DIF): Biopsy of perilesional NORMAL-appearing skin. IgG (and often C3) deposition in intercellular spaces of epidermis — fishnet/chicken-wire pattern. Pathognomonic. Sensitivity 95–100%.

Indirect Immunofluorescence (IDIF): Patient serum tested on monkey oesophagus substrate. Detects circulating anti-desmoglein antibodies; titre correlates with disease activity. Available at dermatology teaching hospitals.
4. Serological Tests
generic_medicines: 1. First-Line Immunosuppression

Prednisolone 1–1.5mg/kg/day oral: Traditional high-dose corticosteroid for pemphigus. Taper once new blister formation stops. Prolonged high-dose causes significant steroid complications — move to steroid-sparing agents early.

Dexamethasone Pulse (IV Cyclophosphamide Pulse Therapy — DCP): IV Dexamethasone 100mg in 500ml NS over 8 hours x 3 days monthly + oral Cyclophosphamide 50mg daily in between. IADVL-recommended Indian protocol for cost-effective pemphigus management where Rituximab unavailable.

Rituximab 1000mg IV (2 doses 14 days apart) or 500mg x4 weekly: Anti-CD20 B-cell depleting biologic. Now first-line for moderate-severe pemphigus per international and IADVL 2020 guidelines. Long-term remission in 70–90% after single course.
2. Steroid-Sparing Agents (Adjuvant Immunosuppressants)
Azathioprine 1–2.5mg/kg/day oral: Most widely used steroid-sparing agent in India (affordability). TPMT enzyme testing before initiation ideal but not always available. Monitor CBC monthly.
Mycophenolate Mofetil 1.5–2g/day oral (divided doses): Effective steroid-sparing agent; better tolerated than Azathioprine. Increasingly available in India.
Cyclophosphamide 1–2mg/kg/day oral: For refractory pemphigus. Risk of haemorrhagic cystitis, infertility, and malignancy. Use with mesna if IV.
3. Wound Care and Supportive Treatment
Normal Saline Soaks / Potassium Permanganate 1:10000 Soaks: Twice daily wound soaks for denuded erosions — removes debris, prevents secondary infection.
Mupirocin 2% Ointment or Framycetin Gauze: Topical antibiotic dressing for infected erosions.
Oral Hygiene Protocol: Chlorhexidine 0.2% mouthwash every 4–6 hours for oral erosions — reduces secondary bacterial/fungal colonization.
4. Prophylaxis During Immunosuppression
Cotrimoxazole 480mg alternate days: PCP (Pneumocystis pneumonia) prophylaxis during high-dose steroid + immunosuppressant — mandatory.
Calcium 500mg + Vitamin D3 400IU twice daily: Osteoporosis prophylaxis with long-term corticosteroids — mandatory in all Indian pemphigus patients.
janaushadhi_medicines: Prednisolone 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): Systemic corticosteroid for pemphigus. Core PMBJP medicine.
Azathioprine 50mg Tablets (10 tabs): Steroid-sparing immunosuppressant. Available at Jan Aushadhi Kendras.
Pantoprazole 40mg Tablets (10 tabs): Gastroprotection during steroid therapy. PMBJP stores.
Calcium + Vitamin D3 Tablets (10 tabs): Osteoporosis prophylaxis. Jan Aushadhi stores.
Cotrimoxazole 480mg Tablets (10 tabs): PCP prophylaxis. PMBJP stores.
Fluconazole 150mg Capsules (1 cap): Antifungal cover during immunosuppression.
Chlorhexidine 0.2% Mouthwash 100ml: Oral hygiene for oral erosions.
Mupirocin 2% Ointment 5g: Topical antibiotic for infected skin erosions.
Potassium Permanganate Crystals: Wound soaks for denuded erosions. District hospital pharmacy.
ayurvedic_medicines: Gandhaka Rasayana: Classical Ayurvedic formulation with purified Sulphur (Shuddha Gandhaka). Used as blood purifier and for chronic skin diseases including bullous dermatoses. Anti-inflammatory and antipruritic. 500mg–1g twice daily under physician supervision.

Mahatikta Ghrita: Classical medicated ghee with Tikta (bitter) herbs including Neem, Guduchi, Patola, and Kiratatikta. Comprehensive Panchakarma preparatory and oral formulation for severe chronic skin diseases. 10–20ml on empty stomach under supervision.
Panchatikta Ghrita Guggulu: Anti-inflammatory Pitta-Vata formulation for inflammatory skin diseases. Guduchi, Neem, Patola, and Guggulu combination. 2 tablets twice daily.
Kaishore Guggulu: Tridoshic blood purifier for deep-seated inflammatory skin conditions with systemic involvement. 2 tablets twice daily with warm water.
Neem Kashaya: Internal Neem bark decoction — blood purifier and anti-inflammatory for Pitta-dominant severe skin diseases. Supportive adjunct only.
Sariva (Hemidesmus indicus) Decoction: Classical blood cooler and purifier for Pitta diseases. Anti-inflammatory and antitoxic. 30ml twice daily before meals.
Triphala Guggulu: Wound-healing, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant classical formulation. Supportive role in erosion healing.
Jatyadi Tailam: Classical topical medicated oil with Jasminum grandiflorum, Neem, and wound-healing herbs. Apply to non-infected erosions for wound healing and pain relief.
Chandanadi Tailam: Cooling, anti-inflammatory topical oil for burning, painful erosions.
Virechana (Panchakarma): Therapeutic purgation under qualified Vaidya for Pitta removal in severe pemphigus. Only when patient is stable and able to tolerate Panchakarma.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Highest Global Incidence in India: India has the highest incidence of pemphigus vulgaris globally — 4.4 per 100,000 in some studies. Particularly prevalent in Ashkenazi Jewish, Sephardic Jewish, and Indian (especially Jewish community in India) populations with specific HLA-DRB1 haplotypes. Delayed Diagnosis — Oral Ulcer Misdiagnosis: In rural India, oral pemphigus erosions are frequently misdiagnosed as aphthous stomatitis or oral Herpes for 6–18 mont
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations EMERGENCY — Pemphigus is LIFE-THREATENING Without Treatment: Untreated pemphigus vulgaris has >90% mortality from sepsis, protein loss, and fluid/electrolyte imbalance from widespread denuded skin. Any patient with widespread blisters/erosions especially in mouth must be urg

diseases.name: Lichen Planus
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis

6 Ps Assessment: Classic flat-topped violaceous papules with Wickham's striae — clinical diagnosis in typical cases. Wickham's striae with dermatoscopy (white crossing lines) confirms LP without biopsy in classic presentations.

Oral Mucosal Examination: Reticular white bilateral buccal patterns = classic oral LP. Erosive or atrophic patterns need biopsy. Unilateral lesions or irregular white patches in tobacco users → mandatory biopsy to exclude SCC.
Nail Assessment: Longitudinal ridging + pterygium formation — clinical diagnosis of nail LP.
2. Histopathology (Gold Standard for Atypical/Oral/Erosive Cases)

Skin Biopsy (H&E): Saw-tooth rete ridges, dense band-like lymphocytic infiltrate at dermo-epidermal junction (lichenoid infiltrate), basal cell vacuolar degeneration, civatte bodies (colloid bodies — apoptotic keratinocytes). Pathognomonic histology.
Oral Mucosal Biopsy: Essential for erosive oral LP, leukoplakia differentiation, and malignancy exclusion. Shows same lichenoid histology as skin LP.
3. Immunofluorescence

Direct Immunofluorescence (DIF): Fluorescent fibrinogen deposits at dermo-epidermal junction (shaggy fibrin band); IgM + Civatte bodies. Differentiates from lupus (IgG+IgM continuous band), pemphigoid, and dermatitis herpetiformis.
4. Investigations for Underlying Triggers
generic_medicines: 1. Topical Therapy (First-Line for Localised Cutaneous LP)
Clobetasol Propionate 0.05% Ointment/Cream: Potent topical corticosteroid — first-line for cutaneous LP. Apply twice daily for 4–6 weeks. Restrict to small areas; avoid face and flexures long-term.
Betamethasone Valerate 0.1% Ointment: Moderately potent topical steroid; second-line or maintenance. Suitable for flexural and facial LP.
Tacrolimus 0.1% Ointment: Topical calcineurin inhibitor — highly effective for oral LP (erosive) and genital LP. Steroid-sparing option. Apply twice daily. Mild burning initially common.
2. Oral Topical (Oral LP)
Triamcinolone Acetonide 0.1% Orabase Paste: Topical corticosteroid paste for oral LP erosions — apply to lesion 3–4 times daily after meals.
Dexamethasone 0.1mg/ml Mouthwash (Elixir): For widespread oral erosive LP — swish 5ml for 3 minutes and spit. Three times daily.
3. Systemic Therapy (Generalised/Severe/Oral Erosive LP)
Prednisolone 0.5–1mg/kg/day oral (4–6 weeks, taper): For widespread cutaneous LP, severe nail LP, or erosive oral/genital LP causing pain and disability. Short courses to control flares.

Hydroxychloroquine 200–400mg/day oral: Effective for generalised cutaneous LP — antimalarial drug with immunomodulatory activity. Safe long-term (eye exam annually for retinopathy). NOT a trigger drug at therapeutic doses.
Acitretin 25–35mg/day oral (6 months): Systemic retinoid. Effective for hypertrophic lichen planus and LP unresponsive to steroids. Teratogenic — strict contraception in females.
Methotrexate 10–15mg weekly oral: For erosive oral LP and generalised LP unresponsive to above therapies. Monitor CBC + LFT monthly.
4. Antipruritic Adjuncts
Hydroxyzine 25mg at night oral: Antihistamine for nocturnal pruritus — sedating, useful at night to improve sleep.
Cetirizine 10mg once daily oral: Non-sedating antihistamine for daytime pruritus control.
janaushadhi_medicines: Clobetasol 0.05% Ointment 15g: Potent topical steroid for cutaneous LP. PMBJP stores.
Betamethasone Valerate 0.1% Cream 15g: Moderate potency topical steroid.
Tacrolimus 0.1% Ointment 10g: Topical calcineurin inhibitor for oral and genital LP.
Prednisolone 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): Systemic steroid for severe generalised LP.
Hydroxychloroquine 200mg Tablets (10 tabs): For generalised cutaneous LP.
Hydroxyzine 25mg Tablets (10 tabs): Antihistamine for nocturnal pruritus.
Methotrexate 2.5mg Tablets (10 tabs): For refractory oral/generalised LP.
ayurvedic_medicines: Khadirarishta 450ml (AVP/Dabur): Classical fermented formulation with Khadira (Acacia catechu). Blood purifier for chronic lichenoid skin diseases; anti-inflammatory and antibacterial. 15–20ml twice daily with water after meals.
Mahamanjisthadi Kashaya: Multi-herb decoction with Manjistha, Neem, Guduchi — Pitta and Rakta purification. For chronic inflammatory skin diseases with PIH. 15ml twice daily before meals.
Manjistha Powder (Rubia cordifolia): Blood purifier, anti-inflammatory, and hyperpigmentation-reducing herb. Useful for LP-associated PIH in Indian skin. 3–5g twice daily with warm water or honey.
Arogyavardhini Vati: Classical mercury-herbomineral formulation; Pitta detoxifier for chronic skin diseases. Under qualified Vaidya supervision only. 250mg twice daily.
Neem Capsules (Himalaya): Anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory for chronic LP. 1–2 capsules twice daily after meals.
Haridra (Turmeric) Khand: Curcumin-rich preparation; anti-inflammatory and anti-pruritic. 3–5g with warm milk twice daily.
Triphala Churna: Systemic antioxidant and mild detoxifier. Supports gut health underlying immune dysregulation in LP. 5g at bedtime.
Chandanasavam: Cooling Pitta-pacifying fermented formulation with Sandalwood. For LP with burning sensation in oral or genital involvement. 15–20ml twice daily with water.
Sariva (Hemidesmus indicus) Decoction: Classical Rakta-shodhaka (blood purifier) for Pitta-dominant skin conditions. Anti-inflammatory. 30ml twice daily before meals.
Trikatu Churna: Digestive formula — Agni improvement and Ama removal for lichenoid dermatitis with oral involvement. 1–2g with honey before meals.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors High Prevalence Oral Lichen Planus: India has one of the highest global rates of oral lichen planus — partly linked to tobacco chewing (gutka, pan masala, khaini) which coexists with and may trigger oral LP. Difficult to distinguish from leukoplakia — biopsy mandatory in tobacco users. Oral LP Malignant Transformation: Erosive oral lichen planus carries 0.5–2% lifetime risk of oral squamous cell carcinoma — significantly higher in India with tobacco ch
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Oral LP Requires Regular Malignant Transformation Surveillance: Erosive oral lichen planus has 0.5–2% lifetime risk of squamous cell carcinoma, especially in Indian patients who also use tobacco. Every oral LP patient must have clinical oral examination every 6–12 months. An

diseases.name: Seborrheic Dermatitis
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (Primary — No Routine Tests Needed)
Clinical Distribution Assessment: Greasy yellowish scales in seborrheic areas (nasolabial folds, eyebrows, scalp, ears, chest) — clinical diagnosis in typical presentations.

Response to Antifungal Treatment: Therapeutic trial of ketoconazole shampoo or cream — improvement confirms seborrheic dermatitis (Malassezia-driven). Non-response suggests alternative diagnosis (psoriasis, tinea).
2. Dermoscopy

Dermoscopy (Trichoscopy for Scalp): Scalp SD shows dotted/comma vessels, branching vessels, and diffuse yellowish scales with follicular plugs — distinguishes from scalp psoriasis (red dots/globules) and tinea capitis.
3. Mycological Tests (To Exclude Tinea/Ringworm)

KOH Mount of Scalp Scrapings: Excludes tinea capitis (dermatophyte infection) in scalp SD — especially in children. Dermatophytes show branching hyphae; Malassezia shows bottle-shaped yeast (not routinely visible on KOH).
Fungal Culture: When KOH positive or tinea suspected — Sabouraud dextrose agar culture.
4. For Atypical/Severe/Extensive SD
HIV Testing (4th Generation Ag/Ab): Mandatory for all patients with severe, extensive, or treatment-resistant seborrheic dermatitis — HIV is a major trigger in India.
generic_medicines: 1. Antifungal Treatments (First-Line — Targets Malassezia)
Ketoconazole 2% Shampoo: First-line for scalp SD. Apply on scalp 3 times weekly, leave 5 minutes, rinse. Effective for both dandruff and seborrheic dermatitis. Also available as cream for facial SD.
Selenium Sulphide 2.5% Shampoo: Anti-Malassezia and anti-seborrheic; apply twice weekly. Available OTC in India. Effective for scalp SD and dandruff.
Zinc Pyrithione 1–2% Shampoo: Anti-Malassezia mechanism; milder option for dandruff/mild scalp SD. Use 3–4 times weekly.
Ciclopirox 1% Shampoo/Gel: Effective antifungal for seborrheic dermatitis; broad-spectrum anti-Malassezia activ ity.
Ketoconazole 2% Cream: For facial SD on nasolabial folds, eyebrows, and ears. Apply twice daily for 2–4 weeks.
2. Anti-Inflammatory Treatments
Hydrocortisone 1% Cream: Mild topical steroid; for short-term use (1–2 weeks) on face to control inflammation. Do NOT use higher-potency steroids on face.
Desonide 0.05% Cream/Lotion: Low-potency topical steroid suitable for facial and flexural SD. Safer for face than stronger steroids.
Tacrolimus 0.1%/Pimecrolimus 1% Cream: Steroid-sparing calcineurin inhibitors; ideal for facial SD and seborrheic blepharitis requiring long-term management. No skin atrophy risk.
3. Scalp-Specific
Fluocinolone Acetonide 0.01% in Coconut Oil (Scalp Solution): Anti-inflammatory and emollient scalp preparation. Useful for thick, crusted scalp SD to loosen scales and reduce inflammation.
Coal Tar Shampoo 1–2%: For thick crusted scalp SD. Anti-proliferative and anti-inflammatory. Use 2–3 times weekly.
Salicylic Acid 2–6% Shampoo/Scalp Solution: Keratolytic; loosens and removes thick adherent scales in severe scalp SD before antifungal shampoo application.
4. Systemic (Severe/Resistant SD)
janaushadhi_medicines: Ketoconazole 2% Shampoo 75ml: First-line antifungal shampoo for scalp seborrheic dermatitis. PMBJP stores.
Ketoconazole 2% Cream 15g: For facial and body SD. Jan Aushadhi Kendras.
Selenium Sulphide 2.5% Shampoo 75ml: Anti-Malassezia scalp shampoo. PMBJP.
Zinc Pyrithione 2% Shampoo 75ml: OTC anti-dandruff shampoo; mild SD.
Hydrocortisone 1% Cream 15g: Short-term anti-inflammatory for facial SD.
Salicylic Acid 2% Shampoo 75ml: Keratolytic for thick scalp scales.
Itraconazole 100mg Capsules (10 caps): For severe systemic antifungal treatment.
Fluconazole 150mg Capsules (1 cap): Weekly systemic antifungal for SD.
ayurvedic_medicines: Neem Oil (Pure/pressed): Scalp application — antifungal (nimbidin inhibits Malassezia), anti-inflammatory. Dilute 1:4 with coconut-free oil; apply on scalp 30 minutes before washing. Traditional Indian scalp care.
Tea Tree Oil (5% in carrier oil): Potent anti-Malassezia essential oil. Clinically studied; reduces dandruff by 40% compared to placebo. Mix 5 drops in 10ml carrier oil; scalp massage before washing.

Bhringraj (Eclipta alba) Hair Oil (Kesh King/Dabur Vatika): Traditionally used for dandruff and scalp inflammation. Contains anti-inflammatory and antifungal phytochemicals. Apply to scalp 1–2 hours before washing.
Amla (Indian Gooseberry) Hair Oil: Rich in Vitamin C and antioxidants; reduces scalp inflammation and dandruff. Used in Dabur Amla Oil formulations — widely available and trusted.
Neem + Tulsi Anti-Dandruff Shampoo (Himalaya): Clinically-tested Ayurvedic shampoo with Neem and Holy Basil. Anti-Malassezia and scalp anti-inflammatory. Use 2–3 times weekly.

Panchanimbadi Churna: Internal formulation with five bitter Neem-family herbs — anti-inflammatory, antifungal, and blood purifier for recurrent fungal and seborrheic skin conditions. 3–5g twice daily with honey.

Haridra (Turmeric) Paste: Fresh turmeric paste applied to affected facial areas — curcumin anti-inflammatory and anti-Malassezia activity. Apply thinly, leave 20 minutes, wash off. Traditional remedy.
Kumkumadi Tailam: Anti-inflammatory brightening facial oil for seborrheic dermatitis with hyperpigmentation on face. Applied as overnight face treatment (caution: avoid if oily/acne-prone).
Khadirarishta (Dabur/AVP): Internal blood purifier for chronic skin diseases with Malassezia-fungal component. 15–20ml twice daily with equal water after meals.
Bakuchi (Psoralea corylifolia) Oil — low concentration: Traditionally used for skin conditions; anti-inflammatory. Use cautiously — concentrated Bakuchi is photosensitizing.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Malassezia Colonization in Indian Climate: Hot, humid Indian climate (especially coastal and tropical India) promotes excessive Malassezia yeast proliferation on scalp and skin — the primary pathogenic driver of seborrheic dermatitis. Monsoon season typically triggers flares. HIV-Associated Severe Seborrheic Dermatitis: Severe, extensive, and treatment-resistant seborrheic dermatitis — particularly affecting face, trunk, and skin folds — is a common ea
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Seborrheic Dermatitis is Chronic and Relapsing — Counsel Expectations: Patients must understand seborrheic dermatitis cannot be permanently cured — only controlled. Stopping antifungal treatment leads to recurrence within weeks to months. Long-term maintenance therapy (antif

diseases.name: Contact Dermatitis
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis
Pattern and Distribution Analysis: Critical — geometric, patterned, or anatomically restricted distribution matching contactant location is most diagnostic. Examine entire body for clues.
Detailed Exposure History: Occupational exposure, cosmetics, jewellery, topical medications, plants, and clothing materials — systematic detailed history is the most important diagnostic tool.

Differentiation ICD vs ACD: ICD — onset rapid after first contact; burning/stinging; affects hands predominantly; no prior sensitization needed. ACD — delayed 24–72h; pruritic; requires sensitization; can affect any site.
2. Patch Testing (Gold Standard for ACD)

Patch Test (European Baseline Series): Standardized allergen panels (metals, fragrances, preservatives, rubber chemicals, PPD, epoxy resins) applied to back under occlusion for 48 hours; read at 48h and 96h. Identifies specific allergen in ACD. Available at IADVL-registered dermatology centres.

Indian Patch Test Series: Special series includes India-specific allergens — turmeric (curcumin), henna/PPD, kumkum, coconut oil, sindoor, jasmine oil, and bindi adhesives. Available at selected dermatology departments.
Photopatch Test: For suspected photoallergic contact dermatitis (sunscreens, fragrances) — patch test + UV irradiation.
3. Confirmatory Tests (Atypical Cases)
Skin Biopsy: Spongiotic dermatitis with variable eosinophilic infiltrate — not distinguishing between ACD and ICD histologically but excludes psoriasis and other diagnoses.
generic_medicines: 1. Topical Treatments (First-Line)
Clobetasol Propionate 0.05% Ointment: Potent topical steroid for severe acute ACD — hands, body. Apply twice daily for 1–2 weeks. Rapid symptom control.
Betamethasone Valerate 0.1% Cream: Moderate-potency topical steroid for moderate ACD/ICD. Apply twice daily for 2–4 weeks.
Hydrocortisone 1% Cream: Mild topical steroid for face and flexure contact dermatitis. Short-term only.
Tacrolimus 0.1% Ointment / Pimecrolimus 1% Cream: Steroid-sparing calcineurin inhibitors for chronic contact eczema on face, eyelids, and sensitive skin areas. Avoid long-term steroid complications.
2. Emollients and Barrier Repair
White Soft Paraffin (Petroleum Jelly): Barrier restoration in ICD — apply after washing, multiple times daily. Essential for hand ICD in housewives and healthcare workers.
Liquid Paraffin 10% + White Soft Paraffin 45% Cream (Emulsifying Ointment): Standard emollient for chronic contact eczema. Apply after every handwash.
3. Systemic Treatments (Severe/Widespread ACD)
Prednisolone 0.5–1mg/kg/day oral (7–14 days, taper): For severe, widespread ACD — including PPD hair dye face/scalp reaction, poison ivy type extensive ACD. Rapid relief; short course.
4. Antipruritic Adjuncts
Hydroxyzine 25mg at night oral: Sedating antihistamine; controls nocturnal pruritus and helps sleep.
Cetirizine 10mg once daily oral: Non-sedating daytime antipruritic.
5. Wet Dressings (Acute Oozing ACD)

Potassium Permanganate 1:10000 Solution Soaks: For oozing, weeping ACD — antiseptic, astringent, drying. Use twice daily; dilute properly (stains purple/brown if concentrated). Available at district hospital pharmacies.
janaushadhi_medicines: Clobetasol 0.05% Ointment 15g: Potent topical steroid for severe ACD. PMBJP stores.
Betamethasone 0.1% Cream 15g: Moderate potency topical steroid for contact dermatitis.
Hydrocortisone 1% Cream 15g: Mild steroid for facial contact dermatitis.
White Soft Paraffin 50g: Barrier emollient for ICD hand eczema. Jan Aushadhi.
Prednisolone 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): For severe widespread ACD short course.
