Hydroxyzine 25mg Tablets (10 tabs): Sedating antihistamine for nocturnal itch.
Mupirocin 2% Ointment 5g: Topical antibiotic for secondary infection.
Potassium Permanganate Crystals: Wet soaks for oozing ACD. District hospital.
ayurvedic_medicines: Coconut Oil (Pure, Virgin): Barrier emollient for dry, irritated ICD hands — anti-inflammatory lauric acid content. Apply multiple times daily after handwashing. Widely available and affordable in India. Recommended by IADVL as affordable emollient.
Aloe Vera Gel (Pure): Anti-inflammatory, soothing, and wound-healing for acute ACD. Reduces erythema and burning. Apply cool gel 3–4 times daily on affected areas.
Neem Oil (diluted 1:5): Antifungal, antibacterial, and anti-inflammatory for mildly infected or Pitta-dominant contact dermatitis. Apply topically on limited areas.

Haridra (Turmeric) Paste (topical): Fresh turmeric in coconut oil — traditional anti-inflammatory application for contact dermatitis. Curcumin inhibits NF-kB inflammatory pathway. Apply for 20 minutes before washing.
Chandanadi Tailam: Cooling, Pitta-reducing medicated oil for burning, erythematous contact dermatitis. Apply twice daily.
Dashanga Lepa: Classical Ayurvedic paste with ten cooling and astringent ingredients for inflammatory skin conditions. Applied topically.
Mahamarichyadi Tailam: Traditional medicated oil for contact-type inflammatory skin diseases.
Mahamanjisthadi Kashaya (oral): Blood purifier for systemic management of recurrent or widespread contact dermatitis — Pitta-Rakta etiological framework. 15ml twice daily before meals.
Neem Capsules (Himalaya): Internal blood purifier and anti-allergic for systemic background management of recurrent ACD with hypersensitivity mechanism.
Triphala Churna: Antioxidant, mild immunomodulatory — systemic support for chronic recurrent contact dermatitis. 5g at bedtime with warm water.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Paraphenylenediamine (PPD) Hair Dye Allergy: India has one of the highest global rates of PPD (para-phenylenediamine) contact allergy from black chemical hair dye and henna. Severe ACD to PPD causes dramatic facial swelling, eyelid edema, severe pruritus, and may progress to PPD anaphylaxis. PPD cross-reacts with many medicines (sulphonamides, Dapsone, thiazides). Cement Dermatitis in Construction Workers: Hexavalent chromate in cement is a major cause
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations IDENTIFY AND ELIMINATE THE CAUSE: Contact dermatitis cannot be cured without permanent identification and removal of the causative contactant. Medical treatment manages symptoms temporarily — the disease recurs if re-exposure continues. Detailed history including ALL cosmeti

diseases.name: Cellulitis
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (Primary)

ALT-70 Score for Differentiating Cellulitis from Pseudocellulitis: Asymmetry (1pt) + Leukocytosis >10 (1pt) + Tachycardia >90 (1pt) + Age >70 (1pt). Score 0–1 = 83% cellulitis. Score 2–4 = more likely pseudocellulitis (lipodermatosclerosis, stasis dermatitis). Misdiagnosis of pseudocellulitis as cellulitis leads to unnecessary antibiotics in ~30% of admissions.

Pen Marking Test: Mark the advancing border of erythema with indelible ink — reassess hourly. Spreading beyond mark within 12–24 hours despite antibiotics = progression/MRSA/necrotising fasciitis — escalate urgently.
2. Laboratory Tests

CBC with Differential: Leukocytosis (WBC >11,000) with neutrophilia confirms bacterial infection. Severe leukocytosis (>20,000) or leukopenia = systemic sepsis. Serial CBC monitors treatment response.
CRP / ESR / Procalcitonin: Elevated in active bacterial infection. CRP serial monitoring guides antibiotic duration and confirms resolution. Procalcitonin >2 ng/ml indicates bacteraemia.

Blood Culture (2 sets): For all patients with systemic sepsis, fever >38.5°C, or immunocompromised state. Positive in ~5% of cellulitis. Essential for MRSA/unusual organism detection and antibiotic guidance.
Random Blood Glucose + HbA1c: Mandatory for all cellulitis patients — undiagnosed diabetes is common presenting factor in recurrent cellulitis in India.
3. Imaging
Ultrasound Soft Tissue: Differentiates cellulitis (diffuse echogenicity) from abscess (defined fluid collection requiring drainage). Essential if fluctuance palpable.
generic_medicines: 1. Mild Non-Purulent Cellulitis (Outpatient)
Cefalexin 500mg four times daily oral (7–10 days): First-line for uncomplicated non-purulent cellulitis (Streptococcal aetiology). Well tolerated, widely available in India.
Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 625mg (500/125mg) twice/thrice daily oral (7 days): For mild-moderate cellulitis — broader spectrum covering Staphylococcus + MSSA + anaerobes. First-line for animal bites.
Dicloxacillin 500mg four times daily oral: For Staphylococcus-suspected cellulitis (penicillinase-resistant penicillin).
2. Moderate Cellulitis (With Systemic Features — Consider Hospitalisation)
Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 1.2g IV three times daily: IV step-up for moderate cellulitis when oral treatment failing.
Benzylpenicillin 2.4g IV four times daily + Cloxacillin 2g IV four times daily: IADVL protocol for hospitalised non-MRSA cellulitis — covers Streptococcal + MSSA dual coverage.
3. Severe/MRSA-Suspected Cellulitis (Hospital)
Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV twice daily (dose adjusted to renal function and VCM trough levels): For MRSA cellulitis. Requires ICU/ward setting with IV access and renal monitoring.
Linezolid 600mg twice daily IV/oral: MRSA alternative — oral bioavailability enables step-down. Available in India.
Cotrimoxazole (TMP-SMX) 160/800mg twice daily oral: For community MRSA step-down oral therapy in mild-moderate MRSA skin infections.
4. Necrotising Fasciitis (Surgical Emergency)

Meropenem 1g IV eight-hourly + Vancomycin + Metronidazole 500mg IV three times daily: Empirical combination for necrotising fasciitis pending culture. PLUS — urgent surgical debridement is the ONLY definitive treatment.
5. Recurrence Prevention
Clotrimazole 1% Cream (Feet): Treat tinea pedis to eliminate recurrent cellulitis entry point. Apply between toes twice daily for 4 weeks.
janaushadhi_medicines: Cefalexin 500mg Capsules (10 caps): First-line oral antibiotic for mild cellulitis. PMBJP stores.
Amoxicillin + Clavulanate 625mg Tablets (10 tabs): Broad spectrum for moderate cellulitis/animal bites.
Cotrimoxazole 480mg Tablets (10 tabs): MRSA oral step-down therapy. Jan Aushadhi Kendras.
Clotrimazole 1% Cream 15g: Treats tinea pedis entry point for cellulitis prevention.
Metronidazole 400mg Tablets (10 tabs): For anaerobic component in mixed infections.
Paracetamol 500mg Tablets (10 tabs): Fever and pain management. PMBJP.
Diethylcarbamazine (DEC) 100mg Tablets (10 tabs): For filarial lymphoedema-associated recurrent cellulitis.
ayurvedic_medicines: Gandhaka Rasayana: Classical purified Sulphur formulation — antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and immunostimulatory. For recurrent bacterial skin infections and furunculosis. 500mg–1g twice daily under Vaidya supervision.

Mahatikta Ghrita: Tikta (bitter) medicated ghee with strong anti-inflammatory, antibacterial (Neem, Guduchi, Patola) activity. For severe inflammatory skin infections. 10–15ml on empty stomach under supervision.

Kaishore Guggulu: Tridoshic blood purifier with Guggulu — effective for deep-seated bacterial skin infections, abscesses, and cellulitis. Reduces Ama and clears Rakta (blood) toxicity. 2 tablets twice daily.
Neem (Azadirachta indica) Kashaya: Internal Neem bark decoction — potent antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and immunostimulatory. 30ml twice daily before meals for active skin infections.
Panchanimbadi Churna: Five bitter herbs including Neem — classical antibacterial and anti-Pitta formulation for inflammatory skin infections. 3–5g twice daily with honey.
Neem Paste (External): Fresh Neem leaf paste applied as cold poultice on cellulitis — haematoma reduction, anti-inflammatory, and antibacterial. Apply and wrap; change twice daily. Adjunctive only.
Haridra (Turmeric) Milk: Warm milk with 1 teaspoon turmeric — anti-inflammatory curcumin for systemic support in skin infections. Pain and fever reduction role.
Eranda (Castor Oil) Warm Compress: Warm castor oil fomentation over the affected area — traditional Indian poultice for cellulitis. Anti-inflammatory and promotes drainage. Apply warm pad over oil.
Triphala Churna: Systemic antioxidant and mild immunostimulatory; general detoxifier supporting healing from infection. 5g at bedtime.

CRITICAL: Ayurveda as Supportive ONLY in Cellulitis: Cellulitis is a potentially life-threatening bacterial skin infection. Systemic antibiotics are mandatory — Ayurvedic treatment CANNOT replace antibiotics. Topical poultices may be used as comfort measures supplementing antibiotics but must NEVER delay medical care. Necrotising fasciitis warrants immediate surgical referral.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Diabetic Foot Cellulitis: Diabetes mellitus affects ~100 million Indians. Peripheral neuropathy + micro/macrovascular disease + immune dysfunction makes diabetic patients highly susceptible to rapidly progressing, treatment-resistant lower limb cellulitis and necrotising fasciitis. Diabetic foot cellulitis is a major cause of amputation in India. Tinea Pedis (Athlete's Foot) as Entry Point: Untreated interdigital tinea pedis (fungal infection between t
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Rapidly Spreading Cellulitis is a Medical Emergency — ACT FAST: If erythema continues to advance beyond marked borders despite 48–72 hours of oral antibiotics, or if patient develops high fever, severe pain disproportionate to appearance, skin blistering, skin darkening, or

diseases.name: Impetigo
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (Primary)

Lesion Morphology Assessment: Honey-coloured golden crusts on erythematous base (non-bullous) or flaccid bullae (bullous impetigo) — classic clinical diagnosis. No investigations required for typical uncomplicated impetigo.
Nikolsky Sign: Negative in impetigo/SSSS localized; positive widespread in SSSS and pemphigus.
2. Microbiological Tests (Moderate-Severe / Recurrent / Treatment-Failure Cases)

Wound/Crust Swab Culture and Sensitivity: Most important investigation for impetigo not responding to empirical antibiotics within 3–5 days. Identifies causative organism (GAS vs MSSA vs MRSA) and antibiotic sensitivities. Available at PHC bacteriology labs.
Gram Stain of Vesicle Fluid: Gram-positive cocci in clusters (S. aureus) or chains (Streptococcus) — rapid orientation but culture is definitive.
3. Investigations for PSGN Complication Screening

Urine Routine Microscopy + Protein: MANDATORY 3–6 weeks after streptococcal impetigo in children — detects haematuria and proteinuria marking PSGN onset. PHC-level urine dipstick test is sufficient for screening.

Serum ASO (Anti-Streptolysin O) Titre: Elevated in streptococcal skin infection — however ASO rise is LESS reliable after skin impetigo than throat infection. Anti-DNase B titre is a better serological PSGN marker.
Anti-DNase B Titres: More reliably elevated after streptococcal skin infection (impetigo) than ASO. Useful for retrospective confirmation of GAS skin infection if PSGN develops.
generic_medicines: 1. Topical Antibiotics (First-Line for Localised Impetigo)
Mupirocin 2% Ointment: First-line for localised non-bullous impetigo (<5 lesions). Apply three times daily for 5–7 days. Excellent activity against S. aureus and GAS. Avoid mucocutaneous membranes.
Fusidic Acid 2% Cream: Alternative topical antibiotic — comparable efficacy to mupirocin. Apply three times daily for 7 days. Anti-staphylococcal.
Retapamulin 1% Ointment: Newer topical pleuromutilin antibiotic — approved for impetigo. Apply twice daily for 5 days. Effective against MRSA topically.
2. Crust Removal Before Topical Antibiotic
Normal Saline or Povidone Iodine 5% Wash: Gentle soaking and removal of crusts before topical antibiotic application — improves penetration and reduces bacterial load. Done twice daily.
3. Systemic Antibiotics (Widespread / Bullous / Systemic Features)
Cefalexin 25–50mg/kg/day (children) or 500mg four times daily (adults) oral — 7 days: First-line systemic antibiotic for widespread non-bullous impetigo. Covers GAS + MSSA.
Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 25–45mg/kg/day (children) or 625mg twice daily (adults) oral — 7 days: For moderate-severe impetigo with mixed flora or bite-associated.
Cloxacillin 12.5–25mg/kg/day (children) or 500mg four times daily (adults) oral — 7 days: Penicillinase-resistant; for MSSA impetigo.
4. MRSA-Suspected Impetigo
Cotrimoxazole (TMP-SMX) 8/40mg/kg/day (children) or 160/800mg twice daily (adults) oral — 7 days: Oral MRSA treatment for community-acquired MRSA impetigo.
Linezolid 10mg/kg twice daily (children <5y) or 600mg twice daily (adults) oral: For MRSA impetigo not responding to Cotrimoxazole.
5. SSSS (Neonatal — Hospital Treatment)
Cloxacillin 50–100mg/kg/day IV divided every 6 hours: IV anti-staphylococcal antibiotic for SSSS. Wound care + IV fluids + temperature maintenance.
janaushadhi_medicines: Mupirocin 2% Ointment 5g: First-line topical antibiotic for localised impetigo. PMBJP stores.
Fusidic Acid 2% Cream 5g: Alternative topical antibiotic for impetigo.
Cefalexin 250mg Capsules (10 caps): Systemic antibiotic for widespread impetigo (paediatric + adult).
Amoxicillin + Clavulanate 375mg Tablets (10 tabs): Paediatric-dose broad spectrum for moderate impetigo.
Cotrimoxazole 480mg Tablets (10 tabs): For MRSA-suspected impetigo. Jan Aushadhi.
Povidone Iodine 5% Solution 100ml: Wound cleaning and crust softening before antibiotic application.
Paracetamol 125mg/5ml Syrup 60ml: Fever management in children with bullous impetigo.
Clotrimazole 1% Cream 15g: Co-existing tinea management if secondary fungal infection.
ayurvedic_medicines: Neem Paste (External): Fresh Neem leaf paste applied on impetigo lesions — potent antibacterial against S. aureus and Streptococcus. Traditional Indian first-response. Apply twice daily; wash off with clean water.

Haridra (Turmeric) + Neem Paste: Combined anti-inflammatory and antibacterial topical paste. Curcumin + nimbidin synergistic antibacterial action. Mix turmeric powder in Neem water; apply on lesions twice daily.

Jatyadi Tailam (External): Classical wound-healing medicated oil with Jasminum oil base and multiple antibacterial herbs. Applied on post-rupture impetigo erosions to promote wound healing and prevent scar formation.

Gandhaka Rasayana: Internal purified Sulphur formulation — antibacterial, immunostimulatory. Used under Vaidya supervision for recurrent impetigo in malnourished or immunocompromised children after acute phase managed medically.
Nimbidin Ointment (commercial Neem): External antibacterial application derived from Neem bark extract. Applied twice daily as topical antibacterial adjunct.
Haridra Khand (Turmeric preparation): Internal anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory. Supports immune response in recurrent childhood impetigo. 3g with warm milk twice daily for children.
Triphala Kashaya Wash: Triphala decoction used to clean and irrigate impetigo lesions — astringent, antibacterial wound wash. Apply warm decoction twice daily.

Coconut Oil (Virgin): Lauric acid in coconut oil has documented S. aureus antibacterial activity. Apply as softening and antibacterial agent on crusts before mechanical removal. Widely available and accepted.
Panchanimbadi Churna: Five bitter Neem-family herbs — internal antibacterial and blood purifier for recurrent childhood skin infections. 1–2g twice daily in children with honey.

Chyawanprash: Immunostimulatory nutritional Ayurvedic supplement with Amla, Ashwagandha, and 40+ herbs. Recommended for malnourished children with recurrent impetigo — improves nutritional status and immune function. 1–2 teaspoons daily.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Post-Streptococcal Glomerulonephritis (PSGN): CRITICAL COMPLICATION. Group A Streptococcus (GAS) impetigo — particularly nephritogenic strains (M types 2, 49, 55, 57, 60) — causes acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis 3–6 weeks after skin infection. India has one of the highest PSGN burdens globally. Presents with haematuria, proteinuria, hypertension, and oedema. PSGN cannot be prevented by antibiotics once established — prevention requires EARL
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations MONITOR FOR POST-STREPTOCOCCAL GLOMERULONEPHRITIS: All children with streptococcal impetigo must be monitored for PSGN 3–6 weeks later. Key warning signs: tea-coloured/smoky urine (haematuria), facial puffiness in the morning (periorbital oedema), reduced urine output, high

diseases.name: Pityriasis Versicolor
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (Primary)
Clinical Morphology: Fine powdery-scaled hypo/hyperpigmented macules on upper trunk in a young adult in tropical India = clinical diagnosis in >90% of cases. Investigations confirmatory only.
Darier's Sign: Gentle scratching reveals fine white bran-like scale — simple PHC-level confirmation test.
2. KOH Microscopy (Gold Standard Bedside Confirmation)

KOH Mount of Skin Scraping: Scale scraped from lesion surface into KOH 10–20% on microscope slide. Shows classic 'spaghetti and meatballs' (or 'banana and grapes') pattern — short curved hyphae (spaghetti) with round spores (meatballs) of Malassezia. Pathognomonic. Available at all district hospital labs.
Modified KOH (DMSO + Calcofluor White): Fluorescent stain improves hyphae visibility under fluorescence microscope. More sensitive than simple KOH.
3. Wood's Lamp Examination

Wood's Lamp: UV light examination in darkened room — Malassezia fluoresces golden-yellow/copper-orange. Confirms diagnosis and helps delineate extent of involvement including subclinical lesions. Available at dermatology OPD.
4. Dermoscopy
Dermoscopy: Pityriasis versicolor shows yellow-white diffuse pigmentation with white reticular pattern and follicular plugging without the blood vessel patterns of psoriasis or lichen planus.
generic_medicines: 1. Topical Antifungals (First-Line)

Ketoconazole 2% Shampoo: Apply to entire affected area (trunk, shoulders) as lotion — leave for 10–15 minutes before rinsing. Once daily for 3–4 weeks. Most effective, widely available, and affordable in India.
Selenium Sulphide 2.5% Shampoo/Lotion: Apply to affected skin, leave 15–30 minutes, rinse. Use daily for 2 weeks then weekly for 1–2 months maintenance. Very cost-effective option.
Zinc Pyrithione 1–2% Shampoo: Anti-Malassezia shampoo applied to affected skin. Leave 5–10 minutes and rinse. Daily for 4 weeks.
Clotrimazole 1% Cream: Apply twice daily for 4–6 weeks on affected patches. Broadspectrum antifungal.
Miconazole 2% Cream: Apply twice daily for 4–6 weeks. Azole antifungal targeting Malassezia.
Terbinafine 1% Cream: Apply once daily for 2 weeks on affected areas. Anti-Malassezia activity — caution: oral terbinafine is NOT effective for PV (Malassezia needs different mechanism).
2. Systemic Antifungals (Extensive / Recurrent PV)

Itraconazole 200mg once daily for 5–7 days oral: Most effective systemic treatment for extensive or recurrent PV. Pulse: 200mg/day for 7 days per month for 3–6 months maintenance for recurrence prevention.

Fluconazole 300–400mg single dose or weekly for 4 weeks oral: Alternative systemic treatment. Single high-dose (300–400mg) simplest regimen — exercise immediately after to promote sweating and skin delivery.
Ketoconazole 200mg once daily for 10 days oral: Older systemic option. Hepatotoxicity risk limits long-term use — prefer Itraconazole or Fluconazole.
3. Maintenance Prophylaxis (Highly Recommended in India — High Recurrence)
Ketoconazole 2% Shampoo once weekly (body): Long-term monthly/weekly application during hot season — prevents recurrence. Selenium Sulphide or Zinc Pyrithione weekly shampoo acceptable alternatives.
Itraconazole 200mg monthly (pulse): Monthly pulse systemic prophylaxis during summer months for patients with frequent recurrence.
4. Adjunctive
janaushadhi_medicines: Ketoconazole 2% Shampoo 75ml: First-line topical antifungal applied to skin. PMBJP stores.
Selenium Sulphide 2.5% Shampoo 75ml: Anti-Malassezia topical treatment for PV.
Clotrimazole 1% Cream 15g: Topical antifungal for PV patches. Jan Aushadhi.
Miconazole 2% Cream 15g: Topical azole antifungal for pityriasis versicolor.
Itraconazole 100mg Capsules (10 caps): Systemic treatment for extensive or recurrent PV.
Fluconazole 150mg Capsules (2 caps single dose): Single-dose or weekly systemic treatment.
Zinc Pyrithione 2% Shampoo 75ml: Anti-Malassezia topical OTC shampoo.
Ketoconazole 200mg Tablets (10 tabs): Systemic option for recurrent PV (short course).
ayurvedic_medicines: Tea Tree Oil (5% in carrier oil): Strong topical anti-Malassezia activity. Clinical studies show comparable efficacy to topical antifungals for PV. Mix 5 drops pure tea tree oil in 10ml coconut-free carrier oil; apply to lesions twice daily.

Neem Oil (diluted 1:5 in sesame oil): Anti-Malassezia and anti-inflammatory. Neem's nimbidin has antifungal properties. Apply to affected patches daily; wash after 30 minutes. Traditional Indian antifungal remedy.
Turmeric Paste (External Low Concentration): Fresh turmeric in coconut-free oil — anti-Malassezia properties from curcumin. Apply on patches for 20 minutes daily. Note: may temporarily yellow skin.
Haridra (Turmeric) Capsules (Himalaya/Organic India — Internal): Internal curcumin for systemic anti-fungal and anti-inflammatory support. 500mg twice daily after meals.
Neem Capsules (Himalaya): Internal blood purifier with antifungal activity. For recurrent, widespread pityriasis versicolor. 1–2 capsules twice daily after meals.
Khadirarishta (AVP/Dabur): Classical fermented Khadira decoction — antifungal, anti-inflammatory blood purifier. For recurrent/constitutional predisposition. 15–20ml with equal water twice daily.

Gandhaka Rasayana: Purified sulphur internal formulation — potent antifungal activity confirmed in Ayurvedic literature and some clinical studies. For recurrent fungal skin conditions. Under Vaidya supervision.
Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia) + Neem Combination Tablets: Anti-Pitta, anti-Malassezia internal formulation for recurrent pityriasis and seborrheic states. Reduces fungal recurrence.
Coconut Oil with Camphor (Kapur): Traditional Indian application — coconut oil base with camphor (antifungal) applied to PV patches. Camphor has demonstrated in-vitro anti-Malassezia activity.
Aloe Vera Gel: Anti-fungal and anti-inflammatory topical application. Supportive for mild PV and post-treatment skin soothing.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Tropical Climate and Hyperhidrosis: India's hot humid tropical climate with prolonged heat and monsoon humidity dramatically promotes Malassezia furfur overgrowth — India has one of the world's highest prevalences of pityriasis versicolor (15–20% population affected). Profuse sweating and sebum production are primary drivers. Misdiagnosis as Vitiligo or Leprosy: Hypopigmented PV is extremely commonly misdiagnosed as vitiligo or borderline leprosy (both
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Skin Colour MAY NOT Immediately Return After Treatment: This is the most critical counselling point for pityriasis versicolor in India. Even after successful antifungal treatment and negative KOH (mycological cure), hypopigmented patches can persist for 1–6 months until mela

diseases.name: Alopecia Areata
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (Primary)
Clinical Pattern Recognition: Round/oval smooth non-scarring patches of hair loss with exclamation mark hairs at margins = clinical diagnosis. Dermoscopy highly useful for confirmation.
Pull Test: Gentle traction of 60 hairs proximal to patch margin — >6 hairs extracted = positive active disease. Simple PHC-level test.
2. Trichoscopy (Dermoscopy of Scalp)

Dermoscopy Findings: Yellow dots (empty follicles — good prognosis), black dots (broken hairs — active disease), exclamation mark hairs (pathognomonic), short vellus hairs (regrowth sign). Dermoscopy distinguishes AA from trichotillomania and tinea capitis without biopsy.
3. Laboratory Investigations
TSH + Free T4 + Anti-TPO Antibodies: Mandatory screen for autoimmune thyroid disease — common treatable association in Indian patients with alopecia areata.
CBC (Complete Blood Count): Anaemia (iron deficiency), lymphocytosis (autoimmune pattern).
Serum Ferritin: Iron deficiency (ferritin <30 ng/ml) worsens hair shedding and should be corrected — though it is a co-factor not the primary cause of alopecia areata.
Serum Vitamin D (25-OH): Deficiency very common in India despite sun exposure (due to skin pigmentation, dietary patterns); associated with autoimmune hair diseases. Correct if <30 ng/ml.
generic_medicines: 1. Topical Treatments (Mild-Moderate AA — <50% Scalp Involvement)
Clobetasol Propionate 0.05% Solution/Foam: Potent topical corticosteroid applied to alopecia patches once daily for 3 months. Monitor for local side effects (atrophy, folliculitis).

Minoxidil 5% Solution or Foam (Males) / 2% Solution (Females): Promotes hair regrowth via vasodilation and direct follicle stimulation. Apply twice daily to patches. NOT disease-modifying but improves regrowth speed alongside immunosuppression.

Anthralin (Dithranol) 0.5–1% Cream (Contact Sensitisation Therapy): Applied to patches for 20–60 minutes daily — contact sensitisation mechanism stimulates non-specific immune response. Effective for patchy AA. Short contact, rinse thoroughly.
2. Intralesional Steroids (Small Patches — Dermatology Procedure)

Triamcinolone Acetonide 5–10mg/ml Intralesional Injections: Most effective treatment for limited alopecia areata with <5 patches. Injected into patch every 4–6 weeks. Regrowth in 70% within 3 months. Specialist/dermatologist procedure.
3. Systemic Immunosuppressants (Extensive AA — >50%)

Prednisolone 0.5–1mg/kg/day oral (4–6 weeks, taper): For rapidly progressive, widespread alopecia areata. Induces remission but high relapse rate on tapering. Short-course; not for long-term maintenance.
Oral minipulse Betamethasone 5mg on 2 consecutive days/week: IADVL/Indian protocol for extensive alopecia areata. Effective and reduces steroid total dose compared to daily prednisolone.
Methotrexate 10–25mg weekly oral + Folic Acid 5mg weekly: Steroid-sparing immunosuppressant for extensive alopecia areata. Effective; takes 3–6 months. Monitor LFT + CBC.
Cyclosporine 2.5–5mg/kg/day oral: For extensive rapidly progressive AA. Monitor blood pressure and creatinine. Not first-line in India due to cost and monitoring requirements.
4. JAK Inhibitors (Severe Refractory AA — CDSCO Approved)
Baricitinib 2–4mg/day oral: JAK1/2 inhibitor approved by CDSCO for severe AA. Significant scalp hair regrowth in clinical trials. Monthly CBC + LFT monitoring.
Ritlecitinib 50mg/day oral: Selective JAK3/TEC inhibitor approved for severe AA. Well tolerated.
5. Adjunctive
janaushadhi_medicines: Minoxidil 5% Solution 60ml: First-line topical hair regrowth promoter. PMBJP stores.
Clobetasol 0.05% Scalp Solution 30ml: Potent topical steroid for alopecia patches.
Prednisolone 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): Systemic steroid for extensive AA.
Betamethasone 0.5mg Tablets (10 tabs): Oral mini-pulse protocol for AA.
Folic Acid 5mg Tablets (10 tabs): Methotrexate companion supplement.
Vitamin D3 60000 IU Capsules (4 caps): Vitamin D deficiency correction.
Zinc Sulphate 220mg Capsules (10 caps): Zinc deficiency correction for hair loss.
ayurvedic_medicines: Bhringraj (Eclipta alba) Oil (Dabur Mahabhringraj / Kesh King): Most widely used Ayurvedic hair oil in clinical Ayurvedic practice. Bhringaraja is the primary Kesha-vardhana (hair-promoting) herb. Scalp massage twice weekly — anti-inflammatory on scalp, promotes follicle stimulation. Multiple RCT evidence for hair growth promotion.

Babchi (Bakuchi / Psoralea corylifolia) Oil (diluted): Traditionally used for alopecia areata — psoralen content causes photosensitisation and contact immunostimulation. Apply to patches, expose to morning sunlight for 10–15 minutes. USE DILUTED — concentrated Bakuchi oil causes severe burns. Under Vaidya supervision.

Amla (Amalaki / Emblica officinalis) Oil + Brahmi Oil: Combined Amla-Brahmi hair oils — antioxidant (Vitamin C in Amla), adaptogenic (Brahmi reduces cortisol). Traditional scalp nourishment for stress-triggered hair loss.

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) Capsules (Himalaya/Dabur): Adaptogenic herb — reduces cortisol and psychological stress, which are key AA triggers in India. 300–600mg standardized extract twice daily. Clinical evidence for stress reduction.
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) Tablets: Adaptogen and anxiolytic — reduces stress-induced alopecia areata triggers. 300mg twice daily. Supports sleep quality.
Narasimha Rasayana: Classical Ayurvedic immunomodulatory and Rasayana formulation for Khalitya (alopecia). Contains Ashwagandha, Bhallataka, and Amalaki — systemic tonic and hair-promoting.

Shatavari Churna (Female Patients): Phytoestrogenic and immunomodulatory adaptogen for female alopecia areata — particularly effective for PCOS-related or hormonal-trigger AA in Indian women. 3–5g with warm milk.
Thikthaka Ghrita: Medicated Tikta (bitter) ghee for autoimmune skin and hair diseases — anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory. 5–10ml on empty stomach under Vaidya supervision.
Neem + Haridra Scalp Paste: Anti-inflammatory topical paste applied on alopecia patches. Haridra (curcumin) reduces local autoimmune inflammation; Neem reduces secondary scalp infection.

Sesame Oil (Tila Taila): Classical Ayurvedic scalp massage oil — warm sesame oil massage stimulates scalp circulation, reduces Vata-Pitta imbalance, nourishes follicles. Applied 1 hour before hair wash.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Thyroid Autoimmunity Association: Hypothyroidism (Hashimoto's thyroiditis) is strongly associated with alopecia areata — affecting up to 8–28% of alopecia areata patients. India has a high prevalence of both autoimmune thyroid disease and iodine deficiency. TSH + Anti-TPO screening mandatory in all alopecia areata patients. Atopic Triad Association: Alopecia areata is strongly associated with atopic dermatitis, allergic rhinitis, and asthma. India's ri
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Alopecia Areata is Autoimmune — NOT due to Shampoo, Stress Alone, or Nutrition: The single most important clarification in Indian practice is that alopecia areata is caused by the immune system attacking hair follicles — not by shampoo brands, hair colouring, specific dietar

diseases.name: Stevens-Johnson Syndrome
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (EMERGENCY — Do Not Delay Treatment for Investigations)

Clinical Criteria for SJS: (1) Epidermal detachment <10% BSA + (2) Mucosal involvement (≥2 sites) + (3) History of new drug within 8 weeks. Clinical diagnosis; investigations are confirmatory and prognostic only.
Nikolsky Sign: Positive — gentle lateral pressure on apparently normal perilesional skin causes epidermal detachment. Perform carefully — causes pain.
SCORTEN Severity Scoring: Calculated on Day 1 of admission — 7 variables (see Col B). Guides ICU triage, burns unit transfer, and family counselling.
2. Histopathology (Optional — Confirmation if Uncertain)

Skin Biopsy (H&E): Full-thickness epidermal necrosis — necrotic keratinocytes extending from basal to superficial layers. Subepidermal cleavage below necrotic epidermis. Sparse dermal infiltrate. Distinguishes SJS from SSSS (subcorneal split) and pemphigus.
Frozen Section Biopsy: Available at tertiary centres — rapid (1 hour) histological diagnosis in atypical presentations.
3. Blood Investigations (Prognostic and Management Guidance)
CBC + Differential: Leukopenia indicates poor prognosis; eosinophilia suggests drug hypersensitivity; neutrophilia indicates secondary bacterial sepsis.
Serum Creatinine + BUN + Electrolytes: Renal failure (SCORTEN parameter); electrolyte monitoring for fluid replacement.
generic_medicines: 1. IMMEDIATE — STOP CAUSATIVE DRUG

Causative Drug Discontinuation: The SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT INTERVENTION — stopping the causative drug IMMEDIATELY reduces mortality significantly. Even 1 additional day of continued drug increases mortality. Identify and stop ALL potentially causative drugs. Do not restart.
2. ICU Supportive Care (Burns Unit Equivalent)
IV Fluid Resuscitation: Parkland formula-guided fluid replacement for denuded BSA — same as burns management. 2–4ml/kg per %BSA involved per 24 hours. Default: Hartmann's/Ringer's lactate.
IV Analgesia: Morphine 2–5mg IV PRN or Tramadol 50–100mg IV for pain management of denuded skin.
Nasogastric Tube + High-Protein Liquid Feeds: Oral feeding is impossible — NGT mandatory. 35–40 kcal/kg/day with high protein.
3. Wound Care (Non-Adherent Dressings)
Paraffin-Impregnated Non-Adherent Gauze (Jelonet/Mepitel): Non-adherent wound dressings on denuded areas — prevents dressing-wound adherence and further trauma. Changed daily in aseptic conditions.
Chlorhexidine 0.05% Solution Wound Irrigation: Gentle irrigation of denuded skin areas — antiseptic without cytotoxicity at 0.05% concentration.
4. Immunomodulatory Treatment (IADVL Recommended)

Cyclosporine 3mg/kg/day IV (then oral) for 7–10 days: Most evidence-based treatment for progressive SJS in India — inhibits T-cell activation causing keratinocyte death. Start EARLY (first 48–72 hours) for maximum benefit.
IVIG (IV Immunoglobulin) 1g/kg/day for 3 days: For patients contraindicated to cyclosporine (renal failure, hypertension). Evidence is moderate. High cost limits use in India.
5. Eye Care (Mandatory)
Preservative-Free Artificial Tears Drops (every 1–2 hours): Lubricates conjunctiva; prevents corneal drying and ulceration during acute SJS.
Topical Cyclosporine 0.1% Eye Drops: Anti-inflammatory eye drops to prevent chronic ocular sequelae (dry eye, symblepharon). Start in acute phase.
janaushadhi_medicines: Cyclosporine 50mg Capsules (10 caps): Most evidence-based immunomodulation for progressive SJS. PMBJP stores at tertiary level.
Morphine 10mg/ml Injection (1ml amp): IV analgesia for SJS pain. Hospital formulary.
Tramadol 100mg Injection (2ml amp): IV analgesia alternative for denuded skin pain.
Ringer's Lactate Solution 500ml: IV fluid resuscitation for denuded BSA loss.
Chlorhexidine 0.05% Solution 500ml: Wound irrigation antiseptic. PHC and district hospital.
Fluconazole 150mg Capsules (1 cap): Antifungal prophylaxis for immunosuppressed SJS patients.
Artificial Tears (Carmellose 0.5% Eye Drops 10ml): Lubricant eye drops for SJS ocular involvement.
Pantoprazole 40mg Injection: Stress ulcer prophylaxis during steroid/cyclosporine therapy.
ayurvedic_medicines: Jatyadi Tailam (External — Wound Healing): Classical medicated oil with wound-healing herbs (Jasmini, Neem, Sesame). Can be applied as a supportive wound dressing adjunct on actively healing denuded areas after acute phase — promotes re-epithelialisation and soothes raw surface.

Aloe Vera Gel (Pure, Preservative-Free): Anti-inflammatory, wound-healing, antimicrobial properties. Supportive topical application on healing SJS skin erosions in the recovery phase. Widely available and affordable.
Madhu (Honey — Medical Grade): Natural antiseptic and wound-healing agent. Applied to denuded skin as antibiotic-free wound dressing in recovery phase. Manuka honey has clinical burn-wound evidence.

Turmeric Milk (Internal — Recovery Phase): Anti-inflammatory curcumin via warm milk — supportive nutritional and anti-inflammatory role during SJS recovery when oral feeding resumes. NOT during acute phase.
Panchatikta Ghrita: Tikta-ghee for systemic immune modulation in autoimmune skin diseases. Adjunct in recovery phase to reduce post-inflammatory sequelae. Under Vaidya supervision only.

Triphala Eye Wash: Dilute Triphala decoction (cooled, filtered, sterile) used as Ayurvedic eye wash for conjunctival inflammation. Traditional anti-inflammatory eye care supporting conventional ophthalmological treatment.
Sariva Decoction: Cooling Rakta-shodhaka (blood purifier) for post-SJS inflammatory state in recovery. 30ml twice daily when oral feeding tolerated.

ABSOLUTE CRITICAL WARNING — SJS is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY: Stevens-Johnson Syndrome is a LIFE-THREATENING dermatological emergency with up to 30% mortality. Causative drug must be stopped IMMEDIATELY. Patient MUST be hospitalised — burns unit or dermatology ICU. Ayurvedic treatment has NO role in the ACUTE management of SJS. Topical Ayurvedic applications are supportive ONLY in the recovery phase (re-epithelialisation stage) and only as adjuncts. Any delay in medical referral for Ayurvedic treatment in acute SJS may be fatal.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Allopurinol — Most Common SJS Trigger in India: Allopurinol (used for gout and hyperuricaemia — extremely widely prescribed in India) is the single most common cause of SJS/TEN in India, particularly in patients with HLA-B*5801 allele (prevalent in Han Chinese, Thai, and Indian — especially South Indian — populations). ALL new allopurinol prescriptions should be preceded by HLA-B*5801 testing where available. Antiepileptic Drugs (Carbamazepine, Phenyto
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations MEDICAL EMERGENCY — IMMEDIATE DRUG STOP AND HOSPITAL REFERRAL: SJS is a dermatological emergency. Any patient with: new skin rash + fever + painful mouth/eye erosions + recently started new drug (especially Allopurinol, Carbamazepine, Cotrimoxazole, NSAIDs) must have the cau

diseases.name: Pressure Ulcers
tests: 1. Clinical Staging (Primary Tool — NPUAP/EPUAP Classification)

National Pressure Injury Advisory Panel (NPUAP) Staging: Stages 1–4 + Unstageable + Deep Tissue Injury — clinical examination at each PHC/ward visit. Complete staging documentation drives treatment intensity.

Wound Assessment (SIZE + TISSUE + INFECTION + MOISTURE + EDGE — TIME Framework): Wound size (length x width x depth in cm), tissue type (granulation/slough/eschar/bone), infection signs, exudate level, and wound edge undermining — documented at every assessment.

Braden Scale: Validated tool for pressure ulcer risk assessment — 6 domains: sensory perception (1–4), moisture (1–4), activity (1–4), mobility (1–4), nutrition (1–4), friction/shear (1–3). Score ≤18 = at risk; ≤12 = high risk. Guides preventive measures intensity.
2. Microbiological Investigations

Wound Swab Culture and Sensitivity (Qualitative): For infected PUs — identifies colonizing organisms (S. aureus, Pseudomonas, Enterococcus, Bacteroides). Guides targeted topical and systemic antibiotic selection. Do NOT culture all pressure ulcers — culture ONLY when clinical infection present.
Deep Tissue Biopsy/Quantitative Culture: For deep Stage 3–4 ulcers with suspected osteomyelitis — quantitative culture >10^5 organisms/gram tissue = true infection (not colonisation).
Blood Culture (2 sets): For systemic sepsis from infected pressure ulcer — leukocytosis, fever, hypotension. Mandatory before IV antibiotics.
3. Imaging
X-Ray of Underlying Bone: For Stage 4 ulcers with bone exposure — periosteal reaction, bony destruction = osteomyelitis.
generic_medicines: 1. Wound Bed Preparation and Debridement

Collagenase Ointment (Enzymatic Debridement): Selective enzymatic debridement of slough and necrotic tissue — applied once daily. Preserves viable granulation tissue better than sharp debridement in neuropathic/elderly patients.

Normal Saline Wound Irrigation: Most recommended wound cleansing solution — physiological, non-cytotoxic. Irrigate with 35ml syringe and 18-gauge needle (100–150ml/cm^2 wound area) at each dressing change.

Povidone Iodine 5% Solution (Short-Term): Antimicrobial wound wash for infected pressure ulcers — however cytotoxic with chronic use, impairs granulation. Use maximum 1–2 weeks for heavily infected wounds only; transition to NS wound irrigation.
2. Wound Dressings (Evidence-Based)
Hydrocolloid Dressings (e.g., Duoderm): For Stage 2 ulcers — moist wound healing environment, autolytic debridement, periwound protection. Change every 3–5 days.
Foam Dressings (e.g., Allevyn): For moderately-heavily exudating Stage 2–3 ulcers — absorb exudate while maintaining moist environment. Change every 2–4 days.
Silver-Impregnated Wound Dressings (e.g., Mepilex Ag): For infected pressure ulcers — sustained antimicrobial release. Use for limited 2-week periods to reduce biofilm load.

Negative Pressure Wound Therapy (NPWT/VAC): For Stage 3–4 refractory ulcers — continuous or intermittent suction (−125mmHg) promotes granulation, reduces wound size. Available at district/teaching hospitals.
3. Systemic Antibiotics (For Infected Ulcers with Systemic Features ONLY)
Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 625mg three times daily oral (Mild-Moderate PU Infection): For polymicrobial PU infection — covers Staph, Strep, coliforms, and anaerobes.
Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5g IV every 6–8 hours: For severe PU infection/sepsis with Gram-negative organisms.
Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV twice daily: For MRSA pressure ulcer infection.
Metronidazole 500mg three times daily (IV or oral): Mandatory anaerobic cover for deep sacral or perianal pressure ulcers.
4. Nutrition (Foundational — Non-Negotiable)
janaushadhi_medicines: Normal Saline 0.9% 500ml Irrigation Bags: Wound cleansing standard solution. PMBJP.
Povidone Iodine 5% Solution 100ml: Short-term antimicrobial wound wash.
Metronidazole 400mg Tablets (10 tabs): Anaerobic infection coverage for deep PUs.
Amoxicillin + Clavulanate 625mg Tablets (10 tabs): Oral polymicrobial infection cover.
Zinc Sulphate 220mg Capsules (10 caps): Wound healing micronutrient supplementation.
Vitamin C 500mg Tablets (10 tabs): Collagen synthesis for wound healing.
Paracetamol 500mg Tablets (10 tabs): Wound pain management.
Iron + Folic Acid Tablets (10 tabs): Correct anaemia impeding wound healing. PMBJP.
ayurvedic_medicines: Jatyadi Tailam (External — Classical Wound Healing Oil): Most widely used Ayurvedic wound oil — contains Jasmine (Jasminum), Neem, and Sesame base with multiple healing herbs. Applied to clean, granulating pressure ulcer wounds — promotes re-epithelialisation, reduces inflammation, and provides antimicrobial action. Multiple traditional studies support wound healing. Apply after wound cleansing, cover with dressing.

Madhu (Honey — Medical Grade/Unprocessed): Applied topically as wound dressing on shallow pressure ulcers — potent antibacterial (hydrogen peroxide + methylglyoxal), osmotic debrider, and wound-healing promoter. Cover with gauze dressing. Change daily. Most accessible natural wound treatment in rural India.

Neem Paste Poultice: Fresh Neem leaf paste applied on infected pressure ulcer surface — antibacterial against MRSA and Pseudomonas in in-vitro studies. Applied as cool poultice, wrapped lightly; change twice daily. Supportive antimicrobial adjunct.

Haridra (Turmeric) + Coconut Oil Wound Application: Curcumin anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. Applied as thin paste on clean Stage 1–2 pressure ulcer areas — reduces inflammation and secondary infection risk.

Brahmi Ghrita (Oral): Medicated Ghee with Brahmi (Bacopa) — anti-inflammatory and Vata-reducing properties. Internal support for bedridden patients; reduces Vata imbalance causing tissue wasting and skin fragility. 5–10ml twice daily under Vaidya supervision.

Chyawanprash (Oral): Comprehensive Rasayana — Amla-based antioxidant, immunostimulatory, and Vitamin C-rich nutritional supplement. Addresses micronutrient deficiencies common in malnourished bedridden patients at risk of pressure ulcers. 1–2 teaspoons twice daily.

Ashwagandha Churna (Oral): Anabolic and adaptogenic herb — promotes protein synthesis and muscle mass retention in debilitated bedridden patients. 3–5g with warm milk twice daily. Supports tissue healing and prevents further muscle wasting.
Eladi Keram (External): Classical Ayurvedic cooling oil for inflammatory, Stage 1–2 pressure areas on bony prominences — anti-inflammatory and skin-protective.
Nagaradi Tailam: Classical Ayurvedic oil with wound-healing and anti-Vata properties for bedridden patients. Apply on at-risk bony prominences as preventive pressure area care.
Triphala Kashaya Wound Wash: Triphala decoction (cooled, filtered) used as antiseptic/astringent wound wash for granulating pressure ulcers before dressing application.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Spinal Cord Injuries (Road Traffic Accidents): India has the world's second-highest road traffic accident mortality. Spinal cord injury patients (paraplegics, quadriplegics) are at extreme risk of pressure ulcers — sacrum, ischium, and heels under constant pressure without protective sensation. SCI patients in India often lack access to pressure-relieving equipment (air mattresses, wheelchair cushions) due to cost — dramatically increasing ulcer incide
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations PREVENTION IS THE ONLY CURE — 2-Hourly Repositioning is Non-Negotiable: Pressure ulcers are almost entirely preventable with timely repositioning. ALL bedridden patients (post-stroke, spinal injury, post-surgery, unconscious) MUST be repositioned every 2 hours — day and nigh

diseases.name: Oral Candidiasis
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (Primary)

Scrape Test: Gentle scraping of white plaque with tongue depressor — pseudomembranous candidiasis scrapes off (leaving red base); leukoplakia does NOT scrape off. Simple bedside diagnosis at PHC level.
2. Microscopy (Rapid Confirmation)

KOH Mount (10% KOH) of Scraped Plaque: Scrape plaque onto glass slide, add 10% KOH, heat gently, examine under microscope — pseudohyphae + budding yeasts = positive for Candida. Available at all district hospital labs. Rapid 15-minute test.
Gram Stain of Oral Swab: Gram-positive budding yeasts and pseudohyphae on Gram stain — alternative to KOH at PHC level. Simple, cheap.
3. Culture and Sensitivity

Fungal Culture on Sabouraud Dextrose Agar (SDA): Swab on SDA — Candida colonies grow within 48–72 hours. Species identification by Germ Tube Test and CHROMagar. Mandatory for: recurrent, treatment-refractory, immunosuppressed patients, or HIV-positive patients (where resistant species likely).

Antifungal Susceptibility Testing (AFST) — Broth Microdilution: MIC values for Fluconazole, Amphotericin B, Caspofungin, Voriconazole. Essential in ICU candidemia, treatment failure, or non-albicans species isolated.
4. Mandatory Systemic Investigations (Adults with Unexplained Oral Candidiasis)
HIV 1+2 ELISA / Rapid HIV Test: Mandatory for all adults with oral candidiasis without obvious predisposing cause. HIV counselling and testing (ICTC) available at all government hospitals under NACO.
generic_medicines: 1. Topical Antifungals (Mild-Moderate Oral Candidiasis)

Clotrimazole 10mg Troches/Lozenges: Dissolve slowly in mouth 5 times daily for 14 days — high local mucosal drug concentration with minimal systemic absorption. First-line for mild pseudomembranous candidiasis. Not commonly available in India; Nystatin is the local alternative.

Nystatin Oral Suspension 100,000 units/ml: 4–6ml four times daily swish and swallow for 7–14 days — fungicidal against Candida in oral cavity. Poorly absorbed systemically — safe for neonates, pregnant patients, and patients on multiple medications.

Miconazole Oral Gel 2%: Apply 2.5ml four times daily directly to oral lesions — good contact action. Available in India. Safe for infants and pregnant women. Useful for denture-associated candidiasis (also apply under denture).
2. Systemic Antifungals (Moderate-Severe, HIV, Refractory)

Fluconazole 150mg on Day 1, then 100mg daily for 7–14 days (oral/oesophageal): First-line systemic antifungal for oral candidiasis in immunosuppressed patients, HIV, and oesophageal extension. Well tolerated. Check for DDI with ARVs (Efavirenz/Nevirapine interaction).

Itraconazole 100mg BD for 7–14 days: Alternative for Fluconazole-refractory candidiasis — useful for non-albicans species. Take with food (solution preferred for oral bioavailability). Significant drug interactions.
Voriconazole 200mg BD (Refractory/Invasive): For Fluconazole-resistant Candida krusei and C. glabrata. Tertiary centre use.
3. Angular Cheilitis

Clotrimazole 1% Cream + Hydrocortisone 1% Cream (Combination): Apply BD to commissures — combined antifungal and anti-inflammatory for Candida+Staph angular cheilitis. Available as Candid-HC or Canesten-HC.
4. Neonatal Oral Thrush
Nystatin Oral Drops 100,000 units/ml: 1ml QID applied with cotton swab to oral lesions in neonates. Safe and effective. Standard protocol under IMNCI.
5. Predisposing Factor Management

Correct Inhaler Technique (Mouth Rinse after ICS use): Rinse mouth thoroughly with water and spit after every use of beclomethasone/fluticasone/budesonide inhaler — prevents oropharyngeal steroid deposition and Candida risk. No drug required — education only.
Denture Hygiene: Remove dentures at night; soak in Chlorhexidine or Nystatin solution; clean before replacement — prevents denture-associated erythematous candidiasis recurrence.
janaushadhi_medicines: Fluconazole 150mg Capsules (1 cap): Systemic first-line antifungal for oral/oesophageal candidiasis. PMBJP widely available.
Fluconazole 50mg Tablets (10 tabs): Low-dose daily fluconazole for prophylaxis in HIV patients with CD4 <200.
Itraconazole 100mg Capsules (4 caps): Alternative systemic antifungal for fluconazole-refractory cases.
Clotrimazole 1% Cream 20g: Topical antifungal for angular cheilitis at commissures.
Nystatin Oral Suspension 30ml: Topical oral antifungal for neonatal thrush and mild adult candidiasis.
Miconazole Oral Gel 2% 15g: Apply directly to oral lesions 4x daily.
Chlorhexidine Gluconate 0.2% Mouthwash 100ml: Oral antiseptic — adjunct to reduce secondary bacterial infection in denuded oral mucosa.
ayurvedic_medicines: Irimedadi Taila (Oil Pulling / Kavala Graha): Classical Ayurvedic medicated oil for oral disease (Mukha Roga) — 10ml held in mouth for 10–15 minutes, then spat out. Contains Vidanga, Triphala, and other antimicrobial herbs. Traditional oil pulling reduces oral Candida counts (supported by recent studies). Perform on empty stomach.

Triphala Decoction Gargle (Kavala): Warm Triphala decoction (Amala + Haritaki + Bibhitaki) gargled and held in mouth for 2–3 minutes, twice daily — astringent, antimicrobial, and anti-inflammatory. Reduces oral plaque biofilm. Widely available as Triphala Churna — boil 1 teaspoon in 200ml water, cool, filter, use as mouth rinse.

Haridra + Honey Oral Application: Fresh turmeric paste (Haridra) mixed with honey — direct application to oral Candida lesions. Curcumin (anti-Candida), honey (osmotic/antimicrobial) — evidence-based combination. Apply with cotton, hold 5 minutes, spit. Twice daily.

Yashtimadhu (Licorice / Glycyrrhiza glabra) Decoction Gargle: Glycyrrhizin demonstrates direct anti-Candida activity. 5g Yashtimadhu in 200ml water boiled, cooled, filtered — used as gargle twice daily. Also soothes mucosal inflammation. Available as tongue / mouth formulations.

Nimba (Neem / Azadirachta indica) Twig/Mouth Wash: Traditional Neem twig use for oral hygiene — contains azadirachtin which has documented anti-Candida properties. Neem leaf decoction rinse twice daily reduces oral Candida colonisation.

Trikatu Churna (Internal): Combination of Ginger + Black Pepper + Long Pepper — used in Kapha-dominant oral conditions causing excess mucus and white coating. 1–2g with honey twice daily. Reduces Kapha which in Ayurvedic concept underlies mucosal overgrowth.

Vidanga (Embelia ribes) Powder: Directly anti-parasitic and antimicrobial herb — traditional prescription for Mukha Roga (oral disease). 1–2g with warm water twice daily for oral Candida in ayurvedic practice.

Khadirarishta (Oral): Classical Ayurvedic fermentation formulation containing Khadira (Acacia catechu) — astringent, antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory for oral and skin conditions. 15–25ml twice daily after meals. Useful for recurrent oral candidiasis.

Dashamoola Kashaya (Low-Immunity Background Cases): Immunostimulatory Ayurvedic decoction of 10 roots — for recurrent oral candidiasis associated with immunocompromised state. 20–30ml twice daily under Vaidya guidance.
Shatavari Powder (Female Patients / HIV-related Candidiasis Recovery): Adaptogen and immunomodulatory herb — supports mucosal immunity. 3–5g with warm milk daily during recovery phase.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors HIV/AIDS: India's 2.4 million HIV population — oral candidiasis is the most common OI in HIV, present in >90% of patients with CD4 count <200. A CD4 count <200 cells/µl predisposes to oesophageal extension. NACO guidelines: ALL adult patients with oral candidiasis must be offered HIV testing. Uncontrolled Diabetes Mellitus: India's 100 million+ diabetic population — hyperglycaemia creates a glucose-rich mucosal environment perfect for Candida prolifera
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Oral Candidiasis in Adults is a Red Flag for HIV — Test Mandatory: Any adult presenting with oral candidiasis without obvious predisposing cause (antibiotics, dentures, steroids) should be offered HIV testing per NACO guidelines. In India, oral candidiasis is frequently the

diseases.name: Vulvovaginal Candidiasis
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (First Presentation)

Symptom Triad: Vulval pruritus + white curdy odourless discharge + vulval erythema/satellite lesions = clinical diagnosis sufficient for first uncomplicated episode in non-pregnant woman. Treat empirically; no microscopy required initially.
2. Microscopy (Recurrent, Atypical, or Refractory Cases)

Vaginal Swab KOH Wet Mount (10%): Most important diagnostic test — vaginal discharge applied to slide with 10% KOH, examined at 40x. Pseudohyphae and budding yeasts = VVC confirmed. 50–70% sensitivity (only moderate; negative does not exclude).
Vaginal pH Testing: pH <4.5 = VVC (confirms Candida, which grows in acidic environment). pH >4.5 = Bacterial Vaginosis or Trichomoniasis. Simple, cheap, rapid test using pH indicator strips.
Amine (Whiff) Test: Add KOH drop to discharge — fishy amine odour = BV or T. vaginalis positive. VVC = NO fishy odour. Simple bedside discriminator.
3. Culture and Sensitivity

HVS (High Vaginal Swab) on Sabouraud Dextrose Agar: Essential for: (a) recurrent VVC (≥4/year), (b) fluconazole treatment failure, (c) severe VVC in HIV/DM. Species identification distinguishes C. albicans (fluconazole-sensitive) from C. glabrata/C. krusei (resistant). Report MIC values.
4. Systemic Investigations (Recurrent VVC)
Fasting Blood Sugar + HbA1c: Mandatory for all women with recurrent VVC to exclude undiagnosed DM.
generic_medicines: 1. Uncomplicated VVC (First episode, non-pregnant, non-diabetic)

Fluconazole 150mg single oral dose: Single-pill treatment — most convenient, highly effective (85–90% cure rate for C. albicans). First-line for uncomplicated VVC in non-pregnant women. Avoid in first trimester pregnancy.

Clotrimazole 500mg Single Pessary (Intravaginal): Single intravaginal tablet — 85% efficacy. First-line in pregnancy (safe; not systemically absorbed). Alternative for Fluconazole-intolerant patients.
Clotrimazole 1% Cream Intravaginal (7 days): 5g applicator at night for 7 days. For patients who prefer cream formulations. Less convenient than single-dose but equally effective.
Clotrimazole 2% External Cream: Applied to vulva and perianal area twice daily for 7 days — treats external vulval dermatitis component of VVC alongside intravaginal treatment.
2. Severe VVC (extensive erythema, fissuring, immunosuppressed)
Fluconazole 150mg Day 1 + Day 4 (Two doses): Two doses 72 hours apart for severe VVC. Preferred over single dose in severe disease.
Itraconazole 200mg BD for 1 day (oral): Two-dose oral regimen — alternative to Fluconazole.
3. Recurrent VVC (≥4 episodes/year) — Suppressive Therapy

Fluconazole 150mg weekly for 6 months (after induction): Induction — Fluconazole 150mg every 72 hours x 3 doses; then maintenance 150mg weekly for 6 months. Standard suppressive protocol (FOGSI-endorsed).

Boric Acid 600mg Vaginal Capsules: For C. glabrata or Fluconazole-refractory VVC. Intravaginal 600mg capsule daily for 14 days — evidence-based rescue therapy. IMPORTANT: ORAL USE IS FATAL — only intravaginal.
Flucytosine 1g Intravaginal Cream + Amphotericin B 3% Cream: Compounded intravaginal preparation for C. glabrata refractory to boric acid. Specialist compounding pharmacy required.
4. VVC in Pregnancy (Safe Topical Agents Only)
Clotrimazole 100mg Pessary for 7 nights: Safe, effective, and approved for all trimesters. Manual insertion only (avoid applicator in late pregnancy).
Nystatin Intravaginal (100,000 units daily x 14 days): Alternative topical safe in pregnancy — less effective than azoles but acceptable. Available as Candid gel.
janaushadhi_medicines: Fluconazole 150mg Capsule (1 cap): Single-dose treatment for uncomplicated VVC. PMBJP widely available.
Clotrimazole 500mg Vaginal Pessary (1 tab): Single intravaginal tablet. First-line in pregnancy.
Clotrimazole 1% Cream 20g + Vaginal Applicator: 7-night intravaginal course.
Clotrimazole 2% External Cream 20g: For external vulval application.
Itraconazole 100mg Capsules (4 caps): For Fluconazole-refractory or recurrent VVC.
Nystatin Vaginal Pessary 100,000 units (6 pessaries): Alternative safe in pregnancy.
Folic Acid 5mg Tablets (10 tabs): For pregnant patients with VVC — standard antenatal supplement.
ayurvedic_medicines: Yoni Prakshalana (Vaginal Wash) with Triphala Kashaya: Classical Ayurvedic Yoni Prakshalana — douching with cooled, filtered Triphala decoction. Triphala's astringent, antimicrobial properties reduce vaginal Candida symptoms and restore acidic environment. Prepare fresh; use at body temperature. NOT more frequently than once daily — over-washing disrupts natural flora.

Neem Leaf Decoction Sitz Bath: Warm Neem leaf decoction sitz bath (pan ghalana / avagaha) for vulval pruritus and erythema — 10 minutes twice daily. Neem's anti-Candida and anti-inflammatory properties reduce vulval symptoms significantly. Traditional gynaecological practice widely used by Indian women.

Khadira (Acacia catechu) Kashaya Yoni Wash: Khadira decoction vaginal wash — strongly astringent (tannins), antifungal, anti-inflammatory. Traditional prescription for Shveta Pradara (white discharge) in Ayurvedic gynaecology. Applied as external vulval wash and gentle introital wash twice daily.

Dashamoolarishta (Internal): Classical fermented Ayurvedic tonic containing 10 roots — anti-inflammatory, immunostimulatory, and balances Vata-Pitta-Kapha imbalances underlying Shveta Pradara. 15–25ml twice daily after meals. Specific to recurrent VVC in Vata-Kapha constitution.

Chandraprabha Vati: Multi-ingredient classical tablet formula — for Mutrakriccha (urinary disorders) and Shveta Pradara — contains Shilajit, Guggulu, and antimicrobial herbs. 2 tablets twice daily. Anti-inflammatory and reduces dysuria.

Shatavari Ghrita or Churna: Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus) — primary Ayurvedic herb for female reproductive tract — phytoestrogenic, immunomodulatory, and demulcent for vaginal mucosa. 3–5g Shatavari Churna with warm milk twice daily. For recurrent VVC associated with hormonal imbalance.

Haridra (Turmeric) Vaginal Application (Diluted): Turmeric paste (Haridra + coconut oil, 1:4 dilution) external vulval application — anti-Candida, anti-inflammatory for vulval erythema and itch relief. External use only; not for intravaginal application. Warn patient about mild yellow staining.

Lodhra (Symplocos racemosa) Decoction Vaginal Wash: Classical Yoni Dhoopana / Prakshalana herb — astringent, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial for Shveta Pradara. 5g in 200ml water, boil, cool, filter, use as external wash.

Probiotic Curd / Dahi Application (External and Oral): Traditional Indian practice of applying cold unsweetened curd (Dahi — contains Lactobacillus cultures) externally to vulva for itch relief. Also consuming plain curd 1–2 times daily replenishes gut Lactobacillus — particularly important during and after antibiotic courses. Supported by limited clinical evidence.
Avipattikar Churna: For VVC associated with constipation, and Pitta-dominant heat symptoms — reduces Pitta imbalance underlying genital inflammation. 3–5g with warm water before bedtime.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Diabetes Mellitus: India's massive diabetic population — uncontrolled hyperglycaemia creates an ideal vaginal glycogen-rich environment for Candida proliferation. Recurrent VVC is a diagnostic red flag for undiagnosed or poorly controlled DM. HbA1c must be tested in any woman with ≥2 episodes/year. Antibiotic Usage Pattern in India: India's high antibiotic consumption (often without prescription) disrupts vaginal Lactobacillus-dominant flora, allowing
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Single-Dose Fluconazole is NOT Safe in First-Trimester Pregnancy: Oral fluconazole (all doses) is avoided in the first trimester due to teratogenicity concerns from cohort data. Topical Clotrimazole vaginal pessary is safe in all trimesters. Counsel all women of reproductive

diseases.name: Invasive Aspergillosis
tests: 1. Radiological Investigations

CT Chest (High Resolution) — MOST IMPORTANT: More sensitive than CXR for IPA — detects halo sign, cavitating nodules, air-crescent sign, wedge-shaped infarcts. CT chest is MANDATORY for any febrile neutropenic patient not responding to antibiotics.
Chest X-Ray: Detects pulmonary infiltrates and cavities but has poor sensitivity in early IPA; insensitive during severe neutropaenia (inflammatory response reduced). CT always preferred.
CT Sinuses: For suspected invasive rhinosinusitis — mucosal thickening, bone erosion, orbital/intracranial extension.
MRI Brain: For suspected CNS aspergillosis — haemorrhagic infarcts, ring-enhancing lesions.
2. Biomarkers (Non-Culture Rapid Tests)

Serum Galactomannan (GM) ELISA (Aspergillus Cell Wall Mannan — BioRad Platelia): Sensitivity 70–80% in haematology patients. Optical density index (ODI) >0.5 positive. Twice-weekly monitoring in high-risk neutropenic patients. False positives with Piperacillin-Tazobactam (common in India) — interpret with caution.
BAL (Bronchoalveolar Lavage) Galactomannan: More sensitive than serum GM for pulmonary aspergillosis. ODI >1.0 positive. Requires bronchoscopy.
Serum Beta-D-Glucan (1,3-β-D-Glucan Test): Panfungal marker — positive in Aspergillus AND Candida AND PCP. More sensitive than GM but less specific. Useful in combination.
3. Microbiological Investigations
generic_medicines: 1. First-Line Treatment — Voriconazole (IDSA/Indian Guidelines)

Voriconazole 6mg/kg IV loading dose every 12 hours on Day 1, then 4mg/kg every 12 hours IV: Gold standard for IPA — superior to Amphotericin B in clinical trials. IV loading then transition to oral. Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) recommended.

Voriconazole 200mg oral twice daily (after IV stabilisation): Excellent oral bioavailability (96%) — transition from IV to oral Voriconazole at 200mg BD when patient clinically stable. Monitor LFT, visual symptoms.
2. Alternative First-Line / Salvage

Isavuconazole 200mg TID loading for 6 doses then 200mg daily IV/oral: Non-inferior to Voriconazole with better tolerability — fewer visual side effects. Licensed in India for IPA. Standard treatment duration ≥6–12 weeks.

Liposomal Amphotericin B (L-AmB) 3–5mg/kg/day IV: For Voriconazole-refractory or intolerant cases. Less nephrotoxic than conventional AmB. Standard first-line in mucormycosis (NOT Aspergillus) — avoid conventional AmB formulations.
Posaconazole 300mg IV or 300mg oral (tablet) daily after loading: For refractory IPA or prophylaxis in very high-risk patients. Mould-active. IV formulation available in India.
3. Combination Therapy (Refractory IPA)
Voriconazole + Caspofungin 70mg loading then 50mg daily: Combination evidence mixed but used in refractory life-threatening IPA. Caspofungin inhibits beta-glucan synthesis; synergistic mechanism.
4. Prophylaxis (High-Risk Patients)
Posaconazole 300mg oral/IV daily: Standard prophylaxis for AML induction, allogeneic HSCT (IADVL/Indian Haematology guidelines). Reduces IPA incidence by 50% in high-risk haematology patients.
Voriconazole 200mg oral BD: Alternative prophylaxis if Posaconazole not available.
5. Supportive Management

G-CSF (Granulocyte Colony Stimulating Factor) — Filgrastim/Lenograstim: Accelerates neutrophil recovery in IPA setting — adjunct to antifungal therapy. Standard supportive treatment in neutropenic IPA.
Reduction of Immunosuppression (Where Possible): Reducing corticosteroid dose in steroid-induced IPA is critical — must balance against underlying disease activity.
janaushadhi_medicines: Voriconazole 200mg Tablets (4 tabs): Oral IPA treatment. Available at PMBJP state stores and cancer hospital pharmacies.
Itraconazole 100mg Capsules (4 caps): Alternative azole for chronic pulmonary aspergillosis/aspergilloma management.
Fluconazole 150mg Capsules (1 cap): For prophylaxis in moderate-risk only (NOT active IPA — Fluconazole lacks mould activity against Aspergillus).
Amphotericin B Injection 50mg (1 vial): Conventional AmB for IPA where Voriconazole unavailable — monitor nephrotoxicity closely.
Caspofungin 50mg Injection (1 vial): Available at select Jan Aushadhi specialty counters and government hospital formularies.
ayurvedic_medicines: CRITICAL WARNING — Invasive Aspergillosis is a Life-Threatening Emergency: Invasive Aspergillosis in immunosuppressed patients requires immediate medical treatment with systemic antifungals (Voriconazole/Liposomal AmB) in a hospital setting. There is NO Ayurvedic replacement for antifungal therapy in established invasive aspergillosis. Delay for Ayurvedic treatment is directly life-threatening.

Turmeric (Haridra / Curcumin): Curcumin has demonstrated anti-Aspergillus activity in laboratory studies — inhibits spore germination and mycelial growth. As an adjunctive supportive supplement in recovering, stable patients on antifungals, curcumin supplementation (500mg standardised extract three times daily) may provide modest immune and anti-inflammatory benefit. NOT a substitute for antifungal therapy.

Tulsi (Ocimum sanctum) Leaf Extract: Anti-fungal and immunostimulatory properties of eugenol and ursolic acid in Tulsi — traditional immunomodulation. During recovery from IPA, Tulsi kadha (decoction) as a supportive immunostimulant. 2–3g dry leaf in 200ml water, boil, filter, sip warm twice daily.

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) Standardised Extract: Adaptogenic and immunostimulatory — supports immune recovery during and after prolonged immunosuppression in post-transplant and oncology patients. 300–600mg extract twice daily during late recovery phase. NOT during acute active aspergillosis.

Chyawanprash: Amla-based comprehensive immune tonic — as nutritional and immunostimulatory support AFTER clinical stabilisation and discharge from hospital. Addresses underlying immune deficit in outpatient setting.

Trikatu (Ginger + Pippali + Maricha): Kaphahara herbal combination — traditional for chronic respiratory Kapha manifestations including productive cough, sputum retention. Supportive adjunct for chronic pulmonary aspergillosis (aspergilloma) in stable patients. 2g with honey twice daily.

Vasaka (Adhatoda vasica) Leaf Decoction: Bronchodilatory, expectorant, and antimicrobial — traditional treatment for haemoptysis and pulmonary infections. Useful supportive adjunct for haemoptysis in post-TB aspergilloma patients. 30ml twice daily.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Haematological Malignancies and Bone Marrow Transplants: AML induction chemotherapy (profound neutropaenia), allogeneic HSCT, and haematological transplants are the highest-risk scenarios. India's growing oncology centres report increasing IPA incidence in AML/ALL patients during induction. Prophylactic Posaconazole is now standard in high-risk centres. Post-COVID Immunosuppression (CAPA): India had uniquely high rates of COVID-Associated Pulmonary Asp
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Febrile Neutropaenic Patient Not Responding to Antibiotics in 72–96 Hours Requires Immediate Fungal Investigation: The critical rule in haematology and oncology wards: persistent or recurrent fever in neutropenic patient after 72–96 hours of adequate antibiotics = fungal inf

diseases.name: Cryptococcal Meningitis
tests: 1. Lumbar Puncture and CSF Analysis (Mandatory — Diagnostic and Therapeutic)

CSF Opening Pressure Measurement: Most critical first measurement at LP — record pressure in mmH2O. Normal <200; CM typically 250–400+. Therapeutic LP (drain to <200mmH2O or remove 20–30ml CSF) performed at same sitting if pressure elevated.

CSF India Ink Preparation: Drop CSF on slide, add ink, coverslip — encapsulated yeast cells appear as clear halos against dark background under microscope. Sensitivity 60–80% in HIV-related CM. Simple, available at all district hospital labs.

CSF Cryptococcal Antigen (CrAg) Latex Agglutination or LFA: Sensitivity 99%, specificity 97% — the gold standard CSF test for cryptococcal meningitis. Titre also guides treatment response (declining titre = response). Lateral flow assay (LFA) versions available cheaply and at POC level.

CSF Cell Count, Protein, Glucose: Classic CM picture — lymphocytic pleocytosis (typically <50 cells; sometimes near-normal in severe immunosuppression), elevated protein (50–150mg/dl), low glucose. Markedly elevated protein and near-normal cells = poor immune response = poor prognosis.

CSF Fungal Culture on SDA: Gold standard for definitive diagnosis and antifungal susceptibility. Cryptococcus grows as mucoid white creamy colonies in 3–5 days. Determines azole MIC for fluconazole resistance testing.
2. Blood Tests

Serum/Blood CrAg LFA: Point-of-care finger-prick test — 99% sensitivity for CM in HIV patients. NACO mandates serum CrAg testing in all HIV patients with CD4 <100. Positive serum CrAg in symptomatic patient = proceed to LP immediately.
Blood Fungal Culture (2 sets): Separate from bacteraemia screen — in disseminated cryptococcosis blood cultures positive in 50–70%.
HIV Rapid Test + CD4 Count: Mandatory in all CM — guides prognosis and timing of ART initiation.
generic_medicines: 1. Induction Phase (Weeks 1–2) — FUNGICIDAL

Liposomal Amphotericin B (L-AmB) 3–4mg/kg/day IV for 14 days: WHO-preferred induction therapy for cryptococcal meningitis (superior to conventional AmB — less nephrotoxicity, equivalent efficacy). EXPENSIVE in India — available through government HIV programme procurement at major ART centres.

Amphotericin B Deoxycholate (Conventional AmB) 0.7–1mg/kg/day IV for 14 days: Less expensive alternative when L-AmB unavailable. Monitor renal function daily, potassium/magnesium supplementation mandatory. Pre-hydrate with 500ml NS before each dose. Daily creatinine monitoring.

Flucytosine (5-FC) 100mg/kg/day oral in 4 divided doses for 14 days: Given IN COMBINATION with AmB (AmB+5FC combination = most effective induction; superior to AmB alone in ACTA trial). Unfortunately 5-FC is not available in India at most centres — its absence significantly impacts outcomes.
Fluconazole 800mg/day oral: Alternative induction if AmB unavailable or intolerable — less effective but lifeboat option. Standard NACO fallback in resource-limited settings.
2. Consolidation Phase (Weeks 3–10)
Fluconazole 400mg/day oral for 8 weeks: After 2-week AmB-based induction — consolidation therapy. Oral outpatient treatment. Ensure CSF culture negative before switching.
3. Maintenance (Secondary Prophylaxis) — 12 Months Minimum
Fluconazole 200mg/day oral: Long-term secondary prophylaxis to prevent relapse. Continue until CD4 >200 for ≥3 months on ART. NACO provides free Fluconazole at ART centres.
4. ICP Management (CRITICAL — Prevents Blindness and Death)

Therapeutic Lumbar Puncture (Daily CSF Drainage): Remove 20–30ml CSF or drain until opening pressure halved — performed DAILY for the first 2 weeks or until ICP normalised. This intervention is as life-saving as antifungal therapy. Does NOT require acetazolamide or dexamethasone (both ineffective/harmful in CM).
Avoid Corticosteroids: Dexamethasone does NOT benefit CM and increases mortality in clinical trials — CONTRAINDICATED in cryptococcal meningitis (unlike bacterial meningitis).
5. ART Timing

Defer ART for 4–6 Weeks After CM Diagnosis: Starting ART immediately causes IRIS — which can be fatal. NACO 2020 guidelines: defer ART 4–6 weeks in patients with confirmed CM. Reduces IRIS-related mortality significantly.
janaushadhi_medicines: Fluconazole 200mg Tablets (10 tabs): Secondary prophylaxis and consolidation phase. PMBJP available, free at ART centres.
Fluconazole 150mg Capsules (6 caps): Alternative pack size for consolidation (use two 150mg = 300mg for consolidation step-down).
Amphotericin B Injection 50mg (1 vial): Government HIV programme procurement — induction phase.
Potassium Chloride Injection 15% (10ml amp): Mandatory electrolyte replacement during AmB therapy (AmB causes renal potassium wasting).
Magnesium Sulphate Injection: AmB-induced hypomagnesaemia management.
Normal Saline 500ml (pre-hydration): Pre-infusion hydration before every AmB dose to reduce nephrotoxicity.
Paracetamol 500mg Tablets (10 tabs): Fever and headache symptom relief.
ayurvedic_medicines: ABSOLUTE WARNING — Cryptococcal Meningitis is a Neurological Emergency: Cryptococcal meningitis has 30–70% in-hospital mortality in India without prompt antifungal therapy and ICP management. There is NO Ayurvedic treatment that can substitute for systemic Amphotericin B and daily therapeutic lumbar punctures. Any delay in medical treatment for Ayurvedic therapy in acute cryptococcal meningitis is likely fatal. The following supportive measures apply ONLY in the recovery/rehabilitation phase.

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) Capsules — Recovery Phase: Neuroprotective and cognitive-enhancing adaptogen — supports neurological recovery in post-CM survivors with residual cognitive deficits, memory impairment, and attention difficulties. 300mg standardised extract twice daily. Clinical trial evidence for cognitive enhancement. Under neurologist/Vaidya guidance post-discharge.

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — Recovery Phase: Adaptogenic and neuro-restorative — supports recovery from chronic neurological illness. 600mg standardised root extract daily. Also helps post-CM fatigue, immune restoration, and weight recovery.

Saraswatarishta: Classical Ayurvedic fermented tonic for neurological conditions (Unmada, Apasmara) — contains Brahmi, Shatavari, and multiple nerve-nourishing herbs. 15–25ml twice daily after food during outpatient recovery phase. For cognitive sequelae post-CM.
Chyawanprash: Comprehensive immune tonic — Amla-based antioxidant and immunomodulatory formula. Critical for immune restoration in HIV-positive post-CM patients on ART. 2 teaspoons daily.

Triphala Churna (Oral): Post-CM constipation management (common with prolonged AmB/Fluconazole and hospitalisation). 5g with warm water at bedtime promotes bowel regularity without gut flora disruption.

Warm Sesame Oil Scalp and Foot Massage (Abhyanga): Vata-pacifying practice for post-CM altered sensorium, headache, and neuropathy. Daily warm sesame oil massage promotes relaxation, reduces residual post-CM neuropathic symptoms, and supports general convalescence in ayurvedic recovery protocols.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors HIV-Associated Cryptococcal Meningitis: CD4 <100 cells/µl is the dominant risk factor. India's HIV burden — particularly in high-prevalence states (Maharashtra, Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Telangana, Manipur, Nagaland) — means cryptococcal meningitis is a significant cause of preventable hospital mortality in HIV patients. NACO's CrAg LFA rollout across ICTC/ART centres is saving lives. ART Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome (IRIS):
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations CrAg Lateral Flow Assay Before ART is Life-Saving Population-Level Intervention: NACO 2020 mandates Cryptococcal Antigen (CrAg) LFA testing in ALL HIV-positive patients with CD4 <100 cells/µl BEFORE starting ART. This cheap, simple blood test (Rs.200–500) detects asymptomati

diseases.name: Onychomycosis
tests: 1. Clinical Diagnosis (Preliminary)

Clinical Algorithm (SCIO Score): Subungual hyperkeratosis + onycholysis + nail discolouration. Clinical diagnosis acceptable for treatment initiation in uncomplicated toenail onychomycosis — but laboratory confirmation recommended before systemic antifungal treatment.
2. KOH Preparation (Nail Clipping or Subungual Scraping)

KOH 20–30% Nail Clipping Mount: Nail clipping ground or scraped, dissolved in 20–30% KOH, heated gently, examined at 40x. Septate branching hyphae = dermatophyte. Budding yeasts = Candida. 80% sensitivity when adequate specimen taken. MUST include subungual debris (most fungal material here), not just nail plate.
KOH with Fluorescent Blankophor/Calcofluor White Stain: Dramatically improves sensitivity of KOH over plain mount — fluorescent chitin staining of fungal cell walls. Requires fluorescence microscope.
3. Nail Fungal Culture (Definitive — Species Identification)

Nail Culture on SDA + Dermasel Agar at 28°C: Grows dermatophytes in 2–3 weeks; moulds in 5–7 days. Species identification (T. rubrum vs T. interdigitale vs Candida vs NDM) guides appropriate antifungal choice. Mandatory for: treatment failure, atypical presentation, immunosuppression, Terbinafine-resistant cases.
Antifungal Susceptibility Testing (AFST): For T. rubrum with prior Terbinafine failure — Terbinafine resistance (MIC >4µg/ml) increasingly described. Voriconazole/Itraconazole may be required.
4. Histopathology (Atypical/Certainty)

PAS-Stained Nail Section (Nail Plate and Bed Biopsy): For definitive diagnosis when KOH negative but clinical suspicion high. Periodic Acid-Schiff stain highlights fungal elements in nail keratin. 90%+ sensitivity.
generic_medicines: 1. Systemic Antifungals (Standard of Care for Onychomycosis — Topical Alone Insufficient)

Terbinafine 250mg once daily for 12 weeks (toenails) / 6 weeks (fingernails): First-line oral antifungal for dermatophyte onychomycosis — highest clinical and mycological cure rates (60–80%). Fungicidal; deposits in nail keratin. Monitor LFT at baseline and 6 weeks. Avoid in liver disease.

Itraconazole 200mg twice daily for 1 week per month (Pulse Therapy) — 3 pulses toenails, 2 pulses fingernails: Standard pulse regimen — cost-effective, fewer side effects than continuous. Effective for Candida and dermatophyte onychomycosis. Take with fatty meal (highest absorption). Monitor LFT.
Itraconazole 200mg once daily for 12 weeks (Continuous): Alternative to pulse therapy — higher total dose but better for extensive NDM or refractory toenail disease.

Fluconazole 150–300mg once weekly for 9–12 months (fingernails), 12–18 months (toenails): Weekly oral Fluconazole for patients intolerant to Terbinafine/Itraconazole or with Candida onychomycosis. Longer duration = lower cost per dose but longer treatment course.
2. Topical Antifungals (Adjunctive — Limited Efficacy Alone for Nail Disease)
