Ciclopirox 8% Nail Lacquer (daily for 48 weeks): Only monotherapy-indicated topical for mild DLSO without matrix involvement. Apply daily; file nail weekly. 30% mycological cure — less effective than oral but suitable when oral agents contraindicated (pregnancy, liver disease).
Amorolfine 5% Nail Lacquer: Once or twice weekly — better nail penetration than Ciclopirox. Used as adjunct to oral therapy for faster response.
Clotrimazole 1% Cream (Periungual): For Candida perionychia (paronychia) — apply to nail fold BD. NOT effective for nail plate invasion.
3. Adjunctive Measures

Nail Avulsion (Chemical/Surgical): 40–50% urea cream under occlusive dressing for 7–14 days (chemical avulsion) or surgical nail avulsion — used for very thick nails where antifungal penetration is inadequate. Avulsion followed by topical/oral antifungal.
janaushadhi_medicines: Terbinafine 250mg Tablets (10 tabs): First-line oral dermatophyte onychomycosis treatment. PMBJP widely available.
Itraconazole 100mg Capsules (4 caps): Pulse therapy or continuous for Candida/mixed onychomycosis.
Fluconazole 150mg Capsules (1 cap): Weekly oral pulse for Candida onychomycosis.
Urea 40% Cream 25g: Chemical nail avulsion preparation — nail softening before mechanical debridement.
Ibuprofen 400mg Tablets (10 tabs): Analgesic for nail pain during debridement or ingrown nail complication.
ayurvedic_medicines: Tea Tree Oil (Melaleuca alternifolia) 100% Pure — Topical: Evidence-based natural antifungal for onychomycosis nail application — terpinen-4-ol inhibits dermatophyte growth. Apply undiluted (small area, nail) with cotton bud to infected nail twice daily. RCTs show comparable to Clotrimazole for mild onychomycosis. Widely available at Indian pharmacies. Safe and affordable.

Neem Oil (Cold Pressed) Nail Application: Anti-dermatophyte properties of nimbidin and azadirachtin. Apply Neem oil with cotton directly to infected nail twice daily — traditional and increasingly evidence-supported antifungal for mild-moderate toenail onychomycosis.

Coconut Oil (Caprylic Acid) Application: Medium chain triglycerides (caprylic, capric acid) in virgin coconut oil have antifungal membrane-disrupting action. Apply warm VCO to infected nails and nail folds twice daily — antimicrobial and also prevents dry nail cracking.

Garlic (Allium sativum) Juice / Paste Application: Allicin (organosulfur compound) — potent proven anti-fungal. Crushed fresh garlic juice applied directly to nail — or garlic paste under occlusion for 30 minutes. Pungent; caution for sensitive skin. Traditional and pharmacologically supported.

Haridra (Turmeric) Curcumin Paste: Curcumin anti-Candida and anti-dermatophyte in laboratory studies. Fresh turmeric paste or curcumin cream applied to nail and periungual skin twice daily. Mild yellow discolouration of skin — cosmetically limiting.

Triphala Kashaya Foot Soak: Warm Triphala decoction foot soak (20 minutes daily) — astringent, antimicrobial, antifungal. Traditional Ayurvedic foot care. Particularly beneficial for comorbid tinea pedis associated with toe-web onychomycosis.

Panchvalkal Kashaya (External Wash): Classical five-bark decoction — each bark has significant astringent and antimicrobial properties. Foot soak and nail wash — traditional for recurrent dermatophytosis and onychomycosis.

Marichadi Taila (Nail Application): Maricha (Black Pepper), Haritaki, sesame oil base — Ayurvedic medicated oil with anti-Kapha (anti-fungal, anti-inflammatory) action for Krimija nail diseases (fungal nail infections). Apply twice daily to infected nails.

Nimba + Haridra Churna Oral: Neem and turmeric powder oral combination — 2g of each with warm water twice daily. Systemic antimicrobial and blood-purifying support for chronic recurrent onychomycosis.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Tinea Pedis as Initiating Factor: India's warm, humid climate, use of shared bathrooms, open sandal wear in public spaces (temples, community gyms, public showers), and barefoot walking creates extremely high tinea pedis transmission. Dermatophytes from interdigital tinea pedis subsequently invade toenails → onychomycosis. Diabetes Mellitus: India's diabetic population has dramatically higher onychomycosis prevalence (up to 50% vs 15–20% in non-diabeti
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Topical Treatment Alone is Insufficient for Most Nail Onychomycosis — Systemic Treatment Required: A critical education point for Indian patients (and many practitioners using only Clotrimazole cream on nails): topical antifungals cannot penetrate the nail plate to reach the

diseases.name: Tinea Capitis
tests: 1. Clinical Examination

Scalp Hair Examination: Pattern recognition — grey patch (ectothrix), black dot (endothrix), kerion (inflammatory). Posterior cervical lymphadenopathy + scalp scaling + patchy alopecia = tinea capitis until proven otherwise in a child.
2. Wood's Lamp (UV Fluorescence)

Wood's Lamp Examination in Dark Room: Ectothrix Microsporum species (M. canis, M. audouinii) fluoresce bright green. However — T. violaceum and T. tonsurans (most common Indian species) do NOT fluoresce — Wood's lamp is unreliable in India as a standalone test. Use for screening but always proceed to microscopy.
3. Microscopy and Culture (Definitive)

Hair KOH Mount (Broken Hairs + Scale Scraping): Pull affected hairs with forceps, mount on slide with 10–20% KOH. Endothrix = arthroconidia INSIDE hair shaft. Ectothrix = spore chains OUTSIDE hair shaft. Microscopy in 15 minutes — rapid bedside test at dermatology clinic.

DTM (Dermatophyte Test Medium) or SDA Fungal Culture of Broken Hairs: Inoculate broken hairs and scale; incubate at 28°C for 2–4 weeks. Species identification by colony morphology and microscopy. T. violaceum grows slowly, pigmented colonies. DTM changes colour red = dermatophyte growth indicator.
4. Additional Investigations
CBC (in Kerion): Neutrophilia + elevated ESR during kerion. Distinguish from furuncle/scalp abscess requiring surgical drainage vs antifungal-alone kerion.
Serum Ferritin: Iron deficiency in children with tinea capitis — treat if <20ng/ml to improve immune response.
generic_medicines: 1. Systemic Oral Antifungals (Mandatory — Topical Alone Is Insufficient for Scalp)

Terbinafine 3–6mg/kg/day oral for 6 weeks: Preferred for endothrix T. violaceum and T. tonsurans (most common in India). Weight-based dosing in children: <20kg = 62.5mg; 20–40kg = 125mg; >40kg = 250mg daily. Fungicidal; concentrates in hair keratin.

Griseofulvin Microsize 20–25mg/kg/day oral for 8–12 weeks: Older but effective for Microsporum species. Take with fatty meal (highest absorption). Still widely used in India due to availability and cost. Lower effectiveness for T. violaceum — use Terbinafine instead.
Itraconazole 3–5mg/kg/day oral for 4–6 weeks: Alternative for Griseofulvin/Terbinafine-refractory tinea capitis. Weight-based; available as oral solution for children. Take with food.
Fluconazole 3–6mg/kg/week oral for 8–16 weeks: Weekly pulse — less commonly used. For refractory or Candida tinea capitis. Oral suspension available.
2. Adjunctive Antifungal Shampoo (Reduces Transmission — Not Curative Alone)

Selenium Sulphide 2.5% Shampoo (twice weekly): Reduces viable spores on scalp surface — reduces household and school transmission. Apply, leave 5 minutes, rinse. Use during entire treatment course. Does NOT cure tinea capitis but dramatically reduces spore shedding.
Ketoconazole 2% Shampoo (twice weekly): Alternative antifungal shampoo for reducing scalp fungal load. Also controls seborrhoeic dermatitis if co-present.
3. Kerion Management
Systemic Antifungal (Terbinafine or Griseofulvin) — Primary Treatment: Kerion is treated with systemic antifungal alone — NOT incision and drainage (I&D). I&D is not indicated.

Prednisolone 0.5mg/kg/day for 7–14 days (Adjunct for Severe Kerion): Short-course oral steroid reduces the inflammatory scarring response in severe kerion — may reduce risk of permanent alopecia. Use WITH antifungal, never alone.
4. Household Transmission Control
Treat All Symptomatic Household Members: Examine and treat with antifungal shampoo all siblings and household contacts showing any scalp abnormality — prevents reinfection of treated patient.
janaushadhi_medicines: Terbinafine 250mg Tablets (10 tabs): First-line oral antifungal for T. violaceum tinea capitis. Scored for paediatric dosing. PMBJP widely available.
Griseofulvin 125mg Tablets (10 tabs): Traditional first-line for Microsporum tinea capitis. Widely available at government hospitals.
Itraconazole 100mg Capsules (4 caps): Paediatric alternative for refractory cases.
Ketoconazole 2% Shampoo 100ml: Antifungal shampoo adjunct — reduces scalp spore load.
Selenium Sulphide 2.5% Lotion 100ml: Scalp shampoo adjunct to reduce transmission. PMBJP or government hospital pharmacy.
Prednisolone 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): For severe kerion adjunctive anti-inflammatory use.
Ferrous Sulphate + Folic Acid Tablet (10 tabs): Iron supplementation for children with co-existing iron deficiency anaemia.
Ferrous Sulphate + Folic Acid (10 tablets): Rs.6
ayurvedic_medicines: Karanja (Pongamia pinnata) Oil — Scalp Application: Traditional Indian antimicrobial scalp oil — anti-dermatophyte activity of karanjin and pongamol. Apply to scalp patches twice daily; leave 30 minutes before washing. Widely used in rural India for scalp fungal infections.

Neem (Nimba) Oil Scalp Application: Direct anti-dermatophyte activity of nimbidin. Apply warm Neem oil to affected scalp areas, cover with cloth, leave overnight. Wash with Neem-based shampoo. Reduces scaling and itch while systemic antifungal takes effect.

Haridra (Turmeric) + Neem Paste (External): Fresh paste of equal parts turmeric and Neem leaf — applied to scalp patches and covered with cloth for 30–60 minutes. Traditional anti-ringworm scalp remedy with antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory actions. Use as adjunct.

Bhringaraj (Eclipta alba) Oil Scalp Massage: Anti-inflammatory and hair growth-promoting — key Ayurvedic trichological herb. Bhringaraj oil massage supports hair follicle recovery during and after tinea capitis treatment, reducing post-inflammatory alopecia.

Vidanga (Embelia ribes) Paste — External: Krimighna (antiparasitic/antifungal) herb — Vidanga paste applied directly to scalp lesions. Traditional treatment for Dadroo (ringworm) and Krimija Khalitya (fungal hair loss).

Triphala Churna Oral: Internal rasayana and immune-support — blood purifying Tridosha balancer. 5g Triphala with warm water at bedtime during tinea capitis treatment — supports systemic antifungal effect and immune response.

Mahamanjishtadi Kashayam (Oral): Classical blood-purifying Ayurvedic decoction — for recurrent or refractory tinea capitis associated with blood Dosha (Rakta Dushti). 15ml twice daily diluted with warm water.

Arogyavardhini Vati: Liver-protecting and immunostimulatory tablet used during long oral antifungal therapy — may provide hepatoprotective support during 6–8 week Griseofulvin or Terbinafine courses. 250–500mg twice daily under Vaidya guidance.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors T. violaceum and T. tonsurans Dominance: Unlike Western countries (M. canis predominant), India predominantly harbours endothrix species T. violaceum and T. tonsurans — which do NOT fluoresce under Wood's lamp (UV) and have smaller ectothrix spores — making rapid screening by Wood's lamp unreliable in Indian practice. Direct microscopy of hair and culture are mandatory. Urban Migrant Worker Household Transmission: Dense urban migrant worker housing in
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Topical Antifungal Alone is Completely Ineffective for Tinea Capitis — Oral Treatment is Mandatory: The most critical education point: topical creams (Clotrimazole, Terbinafine, Ketoconazole) applied to the scalp DO NOT CURE tinea capitis. They may temporarily reduce scaling

diseases.name: Histoplasmosis
tests: 1. Fungal Culture (Gold Standard — Slow)

Culture of BAL, Bone Marrow, Blood, or Tissue on SDA at 25°C and 37°C: Definitive — Histoplasma grows as white-brown mould at 25°C (tuberculate macroconidia = identifying feature). Blood cultures positive in 50–70% of PDH. Takes 2–6 weeks. Biosafety Level 3 handling required — not available at many Indian centres.
Bone Marrow Trephine (PDH): Most sensitive single specimen for PDH culture AND histopathology simultaneously — intracellular yeasts visible in macrophages on H&E/PAS/GMS stain.
2. Histopathology (Rapid Diagnosis from Tissue)

Biopsy of Accessible Lesion (Oral Ulcer, Lymph Node, Skin, Liver): H&E + PAS/GMS stain — intracellular small (2–4µm) oval yeasts within macrophage vacuoles ('negative staining halo'). Should be requested in ALL unexplained oral ulcers in immunosuppressed Indian patients.

Buffy Coat Peripheral Blood Smear (Severe PDH): Wright/Giemsa stained buffy coat — intracellular yeasts seen in neutrophils and monocytes. Rapid, cheap 15-minute test — positive in severe PDH with fungaemia. Can diagnose histoplasmosis at district lab level.
3. Antigen Detection (Fastest Clinical Test)

Urine Histoplasma Antigen (MVista EIA / LFA): Sensitivity 90%+ in PDH and 80% in acute severe pulmonary histoplasmosis. Point-of-care lateral flow assay available. Test cross-reacts with Paracoccidioides, Blastomyces — consider endemic geography. Urine is optimal specimen (more concentrated antigen than serum). Available at AIIMS, CMC Vellore, PGIMER.
Serum Histoplasma Antigen EIA: 80–85% sensitivity in PDH. Useful for monitoring treatment response — antigen titre falls with successful therapy.
4. Serology (Diagnosis in Subacute/Chronic Disease)
generic_medicines: 1. Moderate-Severe PDH (Including HIV — Induction)

Liposomal Amphotericin B 3mg/kg/day IV for 2 weeks: Most effective induction for PDH — superior to conventional AmB, significantly less nephrotoxic. Standard of care at major Indian centres. Expensive; government HIV programme may provide.
Amphotericin B Deoxycholate 0.7–1mg/kg/day IV for 2 weeks: Alternative when L-AmB unavailable — effective but nephrotoxic. Pre-hydrate, replace potassium daily. Monitor creatinine every 2 days.
2. Mild-Moderate PDH and Chronic Pulmonary Histoplasmosis

Itraconazole 200mg three times daily for 3 days (loading), then 200mg twice daily for 12 months: Standard maintenance and mild PDH treatment. Take with fatty meal and acidic beverage (Coke/orange juice) for maximum absorption — critical for itraconazole capsule bioavailability.
3. Step-Down Therapy (After AmB Induction — Consolidation + Maintenance)

Itraconazole 200mg twice daily for minimum 12 months total duration: Step-down after 2 weeks AmB induction. Minimum 12 months for PDH; longer if immunosuppression continues. Serum Itraconazole levels >1µg/ml required for therapeutic response monitoring.
4. Acute Self-Limiting Pulmonary Histoplasmosis
Itraconazole 200mg once daily for 6–12 weeks: For symptomatic acute pulmonary histoplasmosis lasting >4 weeks. Mild acute cases need only supportive treatment and monitoring.
5. Secondary Prophylaxis (HIV PDH — CD4 <150)
Itraconazole 200mg/day indefinitely (until CD4 >150 on ART for ≥6 months): Secondary prophylaxis to prevent PDH relapse in HIV. NACO/WHO recommend continuation until immune reconstitution confirmed.
6. Adrenal Insufficiency Management

Hydrocortisone 20mg morning + 10mg afternoon oral daily: Replacement for adrenal histoplasmosis-associated adrenal insufficiency. Taper as Histoplasma treatment takes effect and adrenal function recovers (monitor ACTH stimulation test).
janaushadhi_medicines: Itraconazole 100mg Capsules (4 caps): Step-down and maintenance histoplasmosis treatment. PMBJP available.
Amphotericin B 50mg Injection (1 vial): Induction therapy where L-AmB unavailable.
Hydrocortisone 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): Adrenal insufficiency replacement.
Potassium Chloride SR 600mg Tablets (10 tabs): Electrolyte replacement during AmB therapy.
Normal Saline 500ml: Pre-hydration before AmB infusion.
Vitamin D3 60000 IU Capsules (4 caps): Bone health and immune support during prolonged antifungal therapy.
ayurvedic_medicines: IMPORTANT: Histoplasmosis PDH is a Potentially Fatal Systemic Fungal Infection Requiring Medical Antifungal Treatment: Systemic Amphotericin B / Itraconazole is the life-saving treatment — Ayurvedic medicines serve only as supportive adjuncts in the recovery phase.

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) Standardised Extract: Immunoadaptogen — supports immune recovery during and after prolonged antifungal therapy. 600mg extract twice daily. Particularly useful in HIV-positive post-PDH patients on ART to counter fatigue and immune restoration.

Guduchi / Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia) Tablets or Kadha: Leading Ayurvedic immunomodulatory herb — Guduchi Ghana has documented immunostimulatory activity (lymphocyte and macrophage activation). 500mg standardised tablets twice daily or 30ml decoction twice daily. Supports non-specific immunity post-PDH.

Chyawanprash (Dabur / Himalaya): Comprehensive Rasayana for immune rebuilding — particularly during 12-month Itraconazole maintenance therapy. Vitamin C and antioxidant content supports mucosal immunity. 2 teaspoons daily.
Shatavari Churna: Female immunomodulatory Rasayana — supportive for female PDH patients recovering under prolonged antifungal therapy. 5g with warm milk twice daily.

Trikatu Churna: Digestive stimulant to enhance Itraconazole absorption in patients complaining of poor appetite or gastric intolerance. 1g before each Itraconazole dose with ginger tea for Kapha-dominant gut type.

Vasaka (Adhatoda vasica) Decoction: For chronic pulmonary histoplasmosis with productive cough and haemoptysis — bronchodilatory, expectorant, and haemostatic properties. 30ml twice daily as adjunct to Itraconazole therapy.
Punarnava (Boerhavia diffusa) Decoction: For hepatomegaly and oedema associated with PDH — traditional Ayurvedic hepatoprotective and diuretic. 30ml decoction twice daily under Vaidya guidance.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Endemic Zones in India: Histoplasma capsulatum is endemic in the Gangetic plains (West Bengal, Bihar, Uttar Pradesh, Assam), parts of Maharashtra, and Andhra Pradesh. Bats (roosting in old buildings and caves), bird-dropping-contaminated soil, and aerosol during construction/earth disturbance are the primary sources of India exposure. HIV Co-infection: PDH is the defining AIDS OI in Histoplasma-endemic Indian states — analogous to Cryptococcal meningit
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Histoplasmosis is the Most Under-Diagnosed Systemic Fungal Infection in India — Think Beyond TB: Any HIV-positive or immunosuppressed patient from the Gangetic plains, Bengal, or Maharashtra presenting with fever + weight loss + hepatosplenomegaly + pancytopenia who is NOT r

diseases.name: Amoebiasis
tests: 1. Stool Examination (Intestinal Amoebiasis)

Stool Microscopy — Wet Mount: Fresh stool examined within 30 minutes of collection — Entamoeba histolytica trophozoites seen as motile organisms with ingested red blood cells (erythrophagocytosis = diagnostic of E. histolytica pathogen vs non-pathogenic E. dispar). Cysts visible in formed stools. Low sensitivity — 3 stool samples increase yield.

Stool Antigen Detection (ELISA or RDT): Entamoeba histolytica-specific antigen test on stool — distinguishes pathogenic E. histolytica from non-pathogenic E. dispar (morphologically identical on microscopy). Sensitivity 86–93%. Available at reference labs.
Stool PCR: Most sensitive and specific — gold standard for research/reference. Identifies E. histolytica vs E. dispar vs E. moshkovskii genotypically. Not routinely available at district level.
2. Serology (Amoebic Liver Abscess — Most Useful)

Entamoeba histolytica IgG ELISA (Serum): Sensitivity 95–99% for ALA. Positive >1:128 titre = strong evidence for amoebic aetiology. May be negative in first week. Most important diagnostic test for ALA — available at AIIMS, PGIMER, major reference labs. Rapid IHA (Indirect Haemagglutination Assay) also widely used in India.
3. Imaging (ALA)

Ultrasound Abdomen: Most rapid and accessible first-line — hypoechoic or anechoic hepatic lesion in right lobe (85–90% right lobe), rounded, well circumscribed, close to hepatic capsule — typically single large abscess. Guides aspiration if needed.

CT Abdomen with Contrast: For atypical, multiple, or ruptured abscesses. Low-density, non-enhancing cavity with peripheral rim enhancement. Assesses complications (rupture into peritoneum, pleura, pericardium).
4. Diagnostic Aspiration (When Diagnosis Uncertain or No Drug Response)
generic_medicines: 1. Tissue Amebicide (Active Against Invasive Disease)

Metronidazole 400mg three times daily oral for 10 days (Intestinal Amoebiasis): First-line for amoebic dysentery. Highly effective against invasive trophozoites. GI side effects (nausea, metallic taste) — take with food. AVOID alcohol during and 48 hours after treatment.

Metronidazole 800mg three times daily oral for 10 days (ALA): Standard high-dose oral regimen for amoebic liver abscess — 90% efficacy. Start BEFORE aspiration and imaging confirmation when clinically confident.
Metronidazole 500mg IV every 8 hours (Severe/Fulminant No Oral Route): IV Metronidazole for severe amoebic colitis, fulminant disease, or patients unable to take orals.

Tinidazole 2g once daily for 3–5 days (Intestinal and ALA): Single daily dose — better compliance than TID Metronidazole; fewer GI side effects; equivalent efficacy. ALA: 5 days; intestinal amoebiasis: 3 days.
Ornidazole 500mg twice daily for 5 days: Well tolerated; twice-daily dosing. Equivalent to Metronidazole for amoebiasis. Less metallic taste.
2. Luminal Amebicide (Mandatory After Tissue Treatment)

Diloxanide Furoate 500mg three times daily for 10 days: ESSENTIAL follow-up after every course of tissue amebicide — eradicates cysts in intestinal lumen (Metronidazole has poor luminal cyst activity). Without luminal amebicide clearance, relapse rate approaches 50%. Available as Furamide in India.
3. Adjunctive
Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS) — WHO Formula: For dehydration from amoebic dysentery — maintain hydration with ORS throughout treatment. Freely available at all government health centres.

Aspiration (Therapeutic — Not Routinely Required): Ultrasound-guided percutaneous needle aspiration for: ALA >5cm, left-lobe abscess (risk of pericardial rupture), rupture impending, no response in 72 hours of Metronidazole, or diagnostic uncertainty. NOT routine first-line — Metronidazole alone cures 90% of ALA.
janaushadhi_medicines: Metronidazole 400mg Tablets (10 tabs): First-line amoebiasis treatment. PMBJP widely available.
Tinidazole 500mg Tablets (4 tabs): Single-dose equivalent alternative — better compliance.
Diloxanide Furoate 500mg Tablets (10 tabs): Mandatory luminal clearance after tissue treatment.
ORS Sachet (Oral Rehydration Salts): Dehydration management. Available at all PMBJP outlets and government health centres.
Paracetamol 500mg Tablets (10 tabs): Fever management in ALA.
Metronidazole 500mg/100ml IV Infusion Bag: For severe amoebiasis or fulminant colitis. Government hospital formulary.
ayurvedic_medicines: Kutajarishta (Holarrhena antidysenterica Fermented Decoction): Most important classical Ayurvedic treatment for Atisar (dysentery) — particularly for Raktatisara (blood dysentery = amoebic). Kutaja (Holarrhena) alkaloids (conessine, kurchicine) have direct anti-amoebic activity confirmed in laboratory studies. 15–25ml twice daily after food. Widely available and affordable. Complementary to Metronidazole; do NOT use as replacement in severe disease.

Bilwadi Churna: Bilwa (Aegle marmelos / Bael fruit) — dried unripe Bael fruit has tannins and marmelosin with anti-amoebic and antidiarrhoeal properties. 3–5g twice daily with warm water or Kutajarishta. Traditional first-aid for amoebic dysentery in Indian households. Unripe Bael tablet/powder widely available.

Nagarmotha (Cyperus rotundus) Decoction: Kapha-Pitta balancing herb with carminative and antidiarrhoeal action — reduces tenesmus and colicky pain in amoebic dysentery. 30ml decoction twice daily. Anti-amoebic and anti-inflammatory.

Musta (Cyperus rotundus) + Dhataki (Woodfordia fruticosa) Compound: Components of Kutaja parpati and other classical formulations for Pravahika (dysentery with tenesmus). Astringent and anti-induction of intestinal mucosa.

Haritaki (Terminalia chebula) Churna: Tri-doshic herbomineral purifier — mild laxative and anthelmintic properties support elimination of amoebic organisms from gut in mild, sub-acute intestinal amoebiasis. 5g with warm water at bedtime.
Guduchi Kwatha (Tinospora cordifolia Decoction): Immunostimulatory — supports systemic immunity and hepatic function during ALA recovery. 30ml twice daily.

Chandanasava: Classical fermented Ayurvedic preparation — for pyrexia associated with ALA, particularly the burning and Pitta-excess constitution. Cooling, anti-inflammatory, antipyretic. 15ml after meals.
Panchatikta Ghrita: Tikta (bitter) ghee for systemic anti-inflammatory support in invasive amoebiasis — hepatoprotective and immunomodulatory. 5–10ml on empty stomach under Vaidya guidance.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Contaminated Municipal Water Supply: India's urban water supply — despite chlorination — frequently has post-chlorination contamination through broken sewage-crossing distribution pipes in dense urban areas. Entamoeba cysts are relatively resistant to chlorine at standard doses. Boiling drinking water eliminates Entamoeba risk. Street Food Consumption: Raw vegetables, uncooked salads, cut fruit, sugarcane juice, and water at street stalls — contaminate
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Always Follow Tissue Amebicide with Luminal Amebicide — Critical for Preventing Relapse: The single most common management error in amoebiasis treatment: prescribing Metronidazole or Tinidazole alone without following with Diloxanide Furoate. Metronidazole is a tissue amebic

diseases.name: Neurocysticercosis
tests: 1. Neuroimaging (Primary Diagnostic Tool)

MRI Brain with Gadolinium (Preferred): Most sensitive modality — detects all stages: (Stage 1) Vesicular: clear cyst with scolex (dot-in-hole sign = pathognomonic — bright scolex within cyst); (Stage 2) Colloidal vesicular: ring enhancement + perilesional oedema; (Stage 3) Granular nodular: solid nodule + enhancement; (Stage 4) Calcified nodule. MRI critical for ventricular/subarachnoid/spinal NCC.

CT Brain with Contrast: Detects ring-enhancing lesion with perilesional oedema (active colloidal stage) and calcified granulomata (old lesions). Less sensitive than MRI for small lesions, posterior fossa, ventricular, and subarachnoid NCC. Most commonly used in Indian district hospitals due to availability and cost.
2. Serology

EITB (Enzyme-Linked Immunoelectrotransfer Blot) — Serum/CSF: Gold standard serological test — 98% specificity; 50–94% sensitivity (lower for single lesions). Available at AIIMS, PGIMER, NIMHANS, CMC Vellore. Positive in multiple cysts; may be negative in SCG (single cyst).
Taenia solium Antibody ELISA (Serum): More widely available but lower specificity than EITB. Cross-reactivity with other helminths limits specificity in India's highly helmintic population.
3. CSF Analysis (When CNS Infection Suspected)

Lumbar Puncture + CSF Cell Count/Biochemistry/Cytology: For meningeal NCC — eosinophilic pleocytosis, elevated protein. CSF EITB highly sensitive for subarachnoid/meningeal NCC. LP only after CT/MRI to exclude raised ICP.
4. Stool Examination

Stool Microscopy for Taenia solium Eggs/Proglottids: Screen household members for active intestinal tapeworm carriage — the human taeniasis carrier is the transmission source. Three stool samples increase sensitivity. Positive = treat immediately with Praziquantel.
generic_medicines: 1. Antiepileptic Drugs (Seizure Control — Priority)

Phenytoin 300mg/day oral (divided doses): Classical first-line AED in India for focal-onset seizures. Narrow therapeutic index — monitor levels (target 10–20µg/ml). Gingival hyperplasia, phenytoin facies with long-term use.
Carbamazepine 400–800mg/day oral (divided doses): Preferred for focal seizures — better tolerability than Phenytoin. Start 100–200mg BD, titrate. Monthly liver function.

Levetiracetam 500–1500mg twice daily: Newer, well-tolerated AED for NCC seizures — minimal drug interactions (important given concurrent Albendazole and corticosteroids). Replace Phenytoin with Levetiracetam where feasible.
2. Antiparasitic Treatment (Active/Viable Lesions — NOT Calcified)

Albendazole 400mg twice daily for 8–30 days (with fatty meal): First-line antiparasitic — disrupts Taenia solium cyst metabolism. MUST be combined with corticosteroids (steroid started 2–3 days before Albendazole and continued throughout) to manage pericystic inflammation from dying cysts. Do NOT treat calcified NCC with antiparasitic.

Praziquantel 50mg/kg/day in 3 divided doses for 14 days: Alternative or co-treatment antiparasitic — synergistic with Albendazole for heavy burden NCC. NOTE: Praziquantel is CONTRAINDICATED in ocular/spinal NCC. Drug interaction with steroids (reduces Praziquantel levels — use Cimetidine to increase levels).
Combination Albendazole + Praziquantel: For heavy burden (>2 cysts) NCC — recommended by current international guidelines as superior to either alone. 28-day course.
3. Corticosteroids (Anti-inflammatory — Mandatory with Antiparasitic)

Dexamethasone 0.1mg/kg/day for 1–2 weeks: Started 2–3 days before antiparasitic and continued throughout treatment — prevents pericystic inflammation (from dying cysts causing perilesional oedema and seizure exacerbation).
Prednisolone 1mg/kg/day (alternative oral steroid): For outpatient NCC management where IV Dexamethasone not needed.
4. Hydrocephalus Management

Ventriculoperitoneal (VP) Shunt (Neurosurgical): For obstructive hydrocephalus from ventricular NCC — requires neurosurgical referral. Shunt + antiparasitic + steroid together. Neuroendoscopic cyst removal preferred where available.
5. Household Tapeworm Treatment

Praziquantel 10mg/kg single oral dose (Household T. solium Carrier): Single-dose treatment for intestinal Taenia solium tapeworm in household carrier — eliminates ongoing transmission source. Critical public health intervention.
janaushadhi_medicines: Albendazole 400mg Tablets (10 tabs): Antiparasitic treatment for viable NCC. PMBJP widely available.
Praziquantel 600mg Tablets (6 tabs): Antiparasitic for NCC and household tapeworm carrier treatment.
Phenytoin 100mg Tablets (10 tabs): First-line antiepileptic for NCC seizures. Government health programme widely supplied.
Carbamazepine 200mg Tablets (10 tabs): Alternative AED — focal seizures from NCC.
Dexamethasone 0.5mg Tablets (10 tabs): Anti-inflammatory for perilesional oedema during antiparasitic therapy.
Levetiracetam 500mg Tablets (10 tabs): Newer well-tolerated AED. PMBJP list.
Prednisolone 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): Outpatient corticosteroid alternative to Dexamethasone.
Omeprazole 20mg Capsules (10 caps): Gastric protection during prolonged steroid therapy.
ayurvedic_medicines: Vidanga (Embelia ribes) Powder (Oral): Primary Krimighna (antiparasitic) herb in Ayurveda — used for intestinal worms and parasitic infections. 2–3g powder with honey twice daily. Clinical studies demonstrate activity against cestodes (tapeworms). Supportive antiparasitic role; most relevant for household intestinal Taenia clearance.

Pippali (Piper longum) + Vidanga Combination: Classical Krimighna combination from Charaka Samhita — Pippali+Vidanga for intestinal parasite elimination. Frequently prescribed together in compound formulations.

Vacha (Acorus calamus) Root Powder: Medhya Rasayana (brain tonic) — nootropic and antiepileptic properties described in Charaka. Traditionally used for epilepsy (Apasmara). 1–2g with honey twice daily as adjunct alongside AEDs during NCC seizure management.

Brahmi Ghrita or Brahmi Vati: Classical Medhya Rasayana for brain-related conditions — cognitive support and anti-convulsant adjunct. 500mg twice daily or 5–10ml Brahmi Ghrita. Supports cortical recovery from pericystic inflammation and seizure-related brain stress.

Saraswatarishta: Compound fermented brain tonic (Brahmi + Shatavari + multiple rasayanas) — for post-NCC cognitive deficits, memory impairment, and neurological rehabilitation. 15–25ml twice daily after food.

Ashwagandha Churna: Adaptogenic and neuroprotective — supports long-term neurorecovery from NCC inflammation. 5g with warm milk twice daily. May also reduce epilepsy anxiety and improve sleep quality.

Panchagavya Ghrita: Traditional preparation used in Apasmara (epilepsy) — under classical Ayurvedic Vaidya prescription only. Contains bovine products with neuroprotective role in Ayurvedic neurological practice.

Shankhapushpi (Convolvulus pluricaulis) Syrup: Leading Ayurvedic nervine tonic and mild antiepileptic — reduces anxiety, supports memory, mild anti-convulsant effect on animal models. 10–20ml syrup twice daily. Widely available commercially (Himalaya, Dabur, Patanjali brands).
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Pig-Rearing Without Sanitation: NCC transmission cycle requires: (1) Human consumes undercooked pork containing Taenia solium cysticerci → adult tapeworm in intestine; (2) Human tapeworm-carrier contaminates environment with proglottid/eggs; (3) Pigs (or humans — through faecal-oral ingestion of eggs) ingest eggs → cysticerci form in muscle/brain. India's significant pig-rearing communities — particularly in Bihar, UP, Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, Northeas
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Neuroimaging at Every New-Onset Seizure in India is Not Optional — It is Essential: India has the world's highest burden of NCC-related epilepsy. The single most impactful clinical action is ensuring CT or MRI brain is performed for every new-onset seizure at any age — inclu

diseases.name: Toxoplasmosis
tests: 1. Serology (Primary Diagnostic Approach)

Toxoplasma IgM ELISA (Serum): Marks acute/recent primary infection — rises within 1–2 weeks of infection, persists 6–9 months. Positive IgM in a pregnant woman/immunocompetent adult = acute infection diagnosis requiring confirmation.

Toxoplasma IgG ELISA (Serum): Marks past infection/chronic carriage — positive in ≥90% of cerebral Tox cases (reactivation). Negative IgG in a suspected cerebral Tox case strongly argues against the diagnosis (consider alternative).

IgG Avidity Test: Distinguishes recent from remote primary infection in pregnant women — high avidity = infection >3 months ago (low foetal risk); low avidity = recent primary infection (high foetal transmission risk). Essential for antenatal Toxoplasma IgG+/IgM equivocal interpretation.
2. Neuroimaging (Cerebral Toxoplasmosis)

MRI Brain with Gadolinium: Multiple (or single) ring-enhancing lesions — predilection for basal ganglia, thalamus, and corticomedullary junction. Concentric/target ring pattern ('target lesion') on MRI = characteristic but not pathognomonic. Perilesional oedema and mass effect. Start empirical treatment — do not delay for definitive diagnosis.
CT Brain with Contrast: Ring-enhancing hypodense lesions with mass effect. Less sensitive than MRI for small or brainstem lesions. First-line in Indian settings.
3. PCR (Molecular Diagnosis)
Toxoplasma PCR — CSF: For CNS Tox — sensitivity 50–60% in CSF; specificity >97%. Positive CSF PCR is highly specific for active cerebral Tox.
Toxoplasma PCR — Blood/BAL: Blood PCR for systemic disease; BAL PCR for pulmonary Toxoplasma.
generic_medicines: 1. Standard Treatment — Cerebral and Systemic Toxoplasmosis (Induction)

Pyrimethamine 200mg oral loading dose Day 1, then 50–75mg/day: First-line anti-Toxoplasma — inhibits dihydrofolate reductase. MUST be combined with Leucovorin (Folinic Acid) to prevent bone marrow suppression (Pyrimethamine-induced folate antagonism). Available as branded Daraprim in India.

Sulfadiazine 1–1.5g orally four times daily: Synergistic with Pyrimethamine — combined = classic 'Gold standard' cerebral Tox regimen. Maintain high fluid intake (≥2L/day) to prevent sulfa crystalluria and nephrotoxicity. Unavailable at many Indian centres — Clindamycin is the substitute.
Leucovorin (Folinic Acid) 10–20mg/day orally: MANDATORY with Pyrimethamine — prevents haematological toxicity (neutropaenia, thrombocytopaenia). Continue throughout Pyrimethamine course.
2. Alternative Therapy (When Sulfadiazine Unavailable — Common in India)

Pyrimethamine 50–75mg/day + Clindamycin 600mg IV/oral every 6 hours: Second-line alternative when Sulfadiazine unavailable — widely used in Indian ART centres. Clindamycin + Pyrimethamine has comparable efficacy. Continue Leucovorin.

Co-trimoxazole (Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole) 5/25mg/kg twice daily: Alternative for resource-limited settings — slightly inferior to Pyr+Sulfa but widely available and inexpensive. Also used as primary and secondary prophylaxis.
3. Anti-Inflammatory (Cerebral Tox with Raised ICP/Mass Effect)
Dexamethasone 4mg IV/oral every 6 hours: Used for severe cerebral oedema and mass effect from Toxoplasma abscesses — taper once lesion responds. Avoid prolonged unnecessary steroids in HIV patients.
4. Maintenance Therapy (After Induction — Secondary Prophylaxis)
Pyrimethamine 25mg/day + Sulfadiazine 500mg–1g twice daily: Lifelong secondary prophylaxis until CD4 >200 for ≥6 months on ART. Prevents relapse.
Co-trimoxazole DS 960mg (Septran DS) once daily: Primary prophylaxis for Tox in HIV at CD4 <200; also prevents PJP and Isospora.
5. Ocular Toxoplasmosis

Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Leucovorin for 4–6 weeks + topical/systemic corticosteroid: Standard ocular Tox treatment. Ophthalmologist-supervised. Systemic Prednisolone 1mg/kg/day for active inflammation — never steroids alone without antiparasitic cover.
6. Congenital Toxoplasmosis
janaushadhi_medicines: Co-trimoxazole (Septran DS) 960mg Tablets (10 tabs): Primary and secondary prophylaxis; alternative treatment. Core PMBJP HIV OI formulary.
Pyrimethamine 25mg Tablets (10 tabs): Standard induction and maintenance. Available at ART centres.
Leucovorin (Folinic Acid) 5mg Tablets (10 tabs): Mandatory bone marrow protection with Pyrimethamine.
Clindamycin 300mg Capsules (10 caps): Alternative combination partner for Pyrimethamine.
Dexamethasone 0.5mg Tablets (10 tabs): Cerebral oedema management.
Prednisolone 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): Ocular Tox anti-inflammatory.
Normal Saline 1000ml: Hydration during Sulfadiazine therapy to prevent crystalluria.
ayurvedic_medicines: IMPORTANT — Cerebral and Congenital Toxoplasmosis are Medical Emergencies Requiring Immediate Antifungal/Antiparasitic Treatment: Ayurvedic medicines are SUPPORTIVE ONLY — no classical or modern Ayurvedic formulation replaces Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine in cerebral Tox or congenital Tox. These conditions are life-threatening and sight-threatening.

Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) Tablets/Decoction: Immunomodulatory — macrophage and lymphocyte activation helps systemic immune response to T. gondii. 500mg standardised extract twice daily or 30ml kwatha twice daily. Most useful for immune support in HIV patients on ART maintenance prophylaxis.

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) Extract: Adaptogenic — reduces treatment fatigue, supports adrenal axis during prolonged Pyrimethamine therapy and HIV immune reconstitution. 600mg extract twice daily.

Amalaki (Phyllanthus emblica / Indian Gooseberry): High natural Vitamin C and antioxidant — immune potentiation, reduction of oxidative stress from cerebral Tox inflammation. 500mg standardised extract twice daily or Triphala Churna 5g nightly.

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) Tablets: Medhya Rasayana — cognitive rehabilitation after cerebral Tox neurological injury. Supports memory, concentration, and cortical recovery. 300mg standardised extract twice daily; Syrup 10ml twice daily for children with congenital Tox neurological sequelae.

Shatavari Granules: Female reproductive and immune tonic — supportive for pregnant women at elevated Toxoplasma risk, particularly for immune priming and foetal nutrition support. 5g with warm milk twice daily. NOT a prevention strategy — proper food hygiene and antenatal care are essential.
Triphala Churna: Gentle bowel tonic and antioxidant — supports GI health during prolonged antiparasitic drug therapy and prevents Pyrimethamine-induced constipation. 5g at bedtime.

Punarnava Mandura: Classical iron-containing Ayurvedic preparation — for anaemia from Pyrimethamine-induced bone marrow suppression after Leucovorin supplementation. Use under Vaidya guidance; monitor Hb.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors HIV Co-Infection and Cerebral Toxoplasmosis Epidemic: Cerebral toxoplasmosis is one of the top three CNS OIs in HIV-positive Indians (alongside TB meningitis and Cryptococcal meningitis) — particularly in South India where HIV prevalence is higher. NACO ART guidelines mandate Co-trimoxazole prophylaxis at CD4 <200 to prevent all three infections simultaneously. Undercooked Meat and Maternal Risk in India: Mutton (goat meat), pork, and bushmeat from Nor
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Co-trimoxazole (Septran DS) Must Be Given to All HIV Patients at CD4 <200 — It Prevents Three Killers at Once: Co-trimoxazole prophylaxis at CD4 <200 simultaneously prevents (1) Cerebral Toxoplasmosis, (2) Pneumocystis jirovecii Pneumonia, and (3) Isospora/Cystoisospora inte

diseases.name: Hydatid Disease
tests: 1. Ultrasound Abdomen (Primary Diagnostic Tool)

Abdominal Ultrasound — WHO-IWGE Classification: Most important first-line investigation. Characteristic findings: (CE1) Simple unilocular cyst with 'snowstorm' sign (cyst sand); (CE2) Multivesicular — 'honeycomb' appearance (daughter cysts); (CE3) Daughter cysts + detached membrane; (CE4) Heterogeneous with calcification; (CE5) Heavily calcified inactive cyst. Cyst stage guides treatment. Pathognomonic findings: 'water lily sign' (detached membranes floating in cyst fluid) and 'double-arch sign'.
2. CT Scan Abdomen/Chest

CT Abdomen with Contrast: Defines cyst number, size, location, relationship to biliary/vascular structures — essential pre-operative planning. Detects calcification and daughter cysts. CT chest for pulmonary hydatid.
3. Serology

Echinococcus IgG ELISA (Serum): Most widely used — sensitivity 64–90% for hepatic hydatid; lower (50–60%) for pulmonary and other locations. Cross-reactive with Taenia, Toxocara. Positive ELISA + compatible US/CT = confirms diagnosis.
Indirect Haemagglutination Assay (IHA): Widely available in India — operational test for field/district settings. IHA titre ≥1:256 = significantfor hydatid diagnosis.
Western Blot / Immunoblot (Arc5 and B epitopes): Most specific confirmatory test — Arc5 band = pathognomonic for Echinococcus granulosus. Available at AIIMS, PGIMER.
4. Intradermal Casoni Test: Historical — no longer recommended due to marked false-positive rate. Replaced by ELISA/IHA.
5. Histopathology (Surgical or PAIR Specimen)
generic_medicines: 1. Antiparasitic (Pericidal Drug — Mandatory Pre- and Post-Operatively)

Albendazole 400mg twice daily with fatty meal (PAIR / Surgery): Perioperative use: Started 3–7 days before PAIR/surgery (renders protoscolices non-viable) and continued 28 days post-procedure. Also used as sole therapy for CE4/CE5 inactive cysts (observation + Albendazole) and in inoperable cases.

Albendazole 400mg twice daily in 3-month cycles (4-week on, 2-week off): Primary medical treatment for small CE1 cysts <5cm or inoperable hydatid — 3 cycle minimum; up to 12 months. Monitor LFTs every 2 weeks — hepatotoxicity is a major side effect especially for long treatment cycles.
2. PAIR Procedure (Minimally Invasive — First-Line for Hepatic CE1/CE3a)

PAIR Protocol: Percutaneous puncture (US-guided) → Aspiration of cyst content → Injection of scolicidal agent (20% hypertonic saline or 95% ethanol) for 15 minutes → Reaspiration. Performed with Albendazole cover. WHO-endorsed as first-line for uncomplicated hepatic hydatid. Less morbidity and shorter hospital stay than surgery.

Hypertonic Saline 20% (Scolicidal Agent): Intraoperative injection into cyst cavity to kill protoscolices before incision and prevent peritoneal seeding. Also used in PAIR procedures. MUST avoid spillage into bile ducts (hyperosmolar cholangitis risk).
3. Surgical (Complex/Pulmonary/Complicated Cysts)

Surgical Cystectomy / Subtotal Pericystectomy / Liver Resection: For CE2 (multivesicular), CE3b, complicated (biliary communication, pulmonary, cardiac, bone) cysts. Surgical techniques: total cystectomy (removes entire cyst + pericyst), subtotal pericystectomy (leaves part of pericyst), liver resection (for deeply embedded cysts). Pulmonary hydatid: cystotomy + capitonnage or pulmonary resection.
4. Anaphylaxis Management (Cyst Rupture Emergency)
Adrenaline (Epinephrine) 0.5mg IM: Immediate first-line for anaphylaxis from cyst rupture — IM antero-lateral thigh. Life-saving.
Chlorpheniramine 10mg IV + Hydrocortisone 200mg IV: Adjuncts to Adrenaline for anaphylaxis management.
5. Post-Procedure Monitoring
LFT Monitoring every 2 weeks during Albendazole: Hepatotoxicity is the major Albendazole complication — elevated transaminases (>5x ULN) requires drug interruption. Restart after normalisation.
janaushadhi_medicines: Albendazole 400mg Tablets (10 tabs): Primary antiparasitic for medical treatment and perioperative prophylaxis. PMBJP widely available.
Adrenaline 1mg/ml Injection (1ml): Emergency anaphylaxis management. All hospitals must stock.
Chlorpheniramine 4mg Tablets (10 tabs): Antihistamine for mild allergic reactions and post-PAIR prophylaxis.
Hydrocortisone 100mg Injection (1 vial): Adjunct IV steroid for anaphylaxis and post-PAIR inflammatory response.
Omeprazole 20mg Capsules (10 caps): Gastric acid protection during cycle Albendazole therapy.
Ursodeoxycholic Acid 300mg Tablets (10 tabs): Biliary protection and flow improvement in biliary communication cases.
ayurvedic_medicines: IMPORTANT — Hydatid Disease Requires Medical and/or Surgical Treatment: Echinococcal cysts do not regress spontaneously and carry life-threatening anaphylaxis rupture risk. Ayurvedic medicines are supportive adjuncts — primarily for immune strengthening and hepatoprotection during Albendazole therapy. No Ayurvedic formulation kills Echinococcus or substitutes for Albendazole/surgery.

Arogyavardhini Vati: Classical Ayurvedic hepatoprotective — supports liver function and hepatocyte regeneration during prolonged Albendazole therapy and post-surgery liver regeneration. 250–500mg twice daily after food under Vaidya guidance. Well-studied hepatoprotective Ayurvedic formulation.

Kutki (Picrorhiza kurroa) Tablets: Most potent Ayurvedic hepatoprotective herb — documented hepatocyte protective and regenerative activity. 250–500mg twice daily. Supports liver during long-cycle Albendazole hepatotoxicity risk. Available as Himalaya Livfit/Picrorhiza.

Phyllanthus niruri (Bhumyamalaki) Extract: Hepatoprotective and antioxidant — used for liver disease of all aetiologies in Ayurveda. 500mg standardised extract twice daily. Evidence-based hepatoprotection supporting LFT normalisation.
Punarnava (Boerhavia diffusa): Hepatomegaly and oedema reduction — Punarnava decoction 30ml twice daily for hepatic hydatid post-PAIR/surgery oedema management. Traditional hepatoprotective.
Guduchi Satva (Tinospora cordifolia Extracted Starch): Immunomodulatory rasayana — supports immune defence against Echinococcus and recovery post-surgery. 500mg twice daily.
Ashwagandha Extract: Adaptogenic — post-surgical recovery, fatigue management, immune support. 600mg extract twice daily.
Triphala Churna: Gentle bowel regulariser and antioxidant — during prolonged Albendazole therapy. 5g at bedtime with warm water.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors India's Epidemiology — Rajasthan, Gujarat, Himachal Pradesh, J&K, Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh: Cystic echinococcosis is hyperendemic in pastoral states of India — particularly Rajasthan, Gujarat, Himachal Pradesh, Jammu & Kashmir (sheep-rearing), and some parts of South India (Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh). Hospital series from Rajasthan show hydatid as the most common surgical hepatic cystic lesion. Dog-Sheep Lifecycle and Rural Transmission: Echinococcu
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Never Aspirate a Liver Cyst Without Excluding Hydatid First — Anaphylaxis Risk: The most dangerous clinical error in managing unknown liver cysts in India: blind aspiration of a hepatic cyst without first excluding Echinococcal (hydatid) aetiology by serology and careful ult

diseases.name: Strongyloidiasis
tests: 1. Stool Examination (Microscopy — Limited Sensitivity)

Stool Wet Mount Microscopy — Rhabditiform Larvae (L1): Fresh stool (must examine within 30 minutes — larvae actively motile) — rhabditiform L1 larvae with short buccal cavity. Low sensitivity (25–50% for single stool) — 3 concentrated stool samples with repeat testing required.

Baermann Technique: Concentrates Strongyloides larvae by migration through warm water from fresh stool — sensitivity higher than direct smear. Stool placed in warm water funnel; larvae migrate to bottom and sediment for microscopy.

Koga Agar Plate Culture: Semi-quantitative culture method — stool on agar plate, Strongyloides larvae migrate creating characteristic tracks visible after 2–3 days. More sensitive than direct microscopy. Available at reference labs.
2. Serology (Most Sensitive for Chronic Infection)

Strongyloides stercoralis IgG ELISA (Serum): Most sensitive screening test for chronic strongyloidiasis — sensitivity 85–95%. Cross-reactive with Filaria and Toxocara in India (common co-endemic areas). Positive ELISA in India = strongly indicates Strongyloides infection even in absence of larvae on stool.
3. Duodenal Aspirate / Jejunal Biopsy

Duodenal Aspirate or Enteric Capsule (Enterotest): For detection when stool negative — rhabditiform larvae in duodenal aspirate. Jejunal biopsy shows larvae in mucosa with eosinophilic enteritis. Most sensitive but invasive.
4. Blood Investigations

CBC — Peripheral Eosinophilia: Eosinophilia (500–3000 cells/µl) present in 60–70% of strongyloidiasis. Absence of eosinophilia in immunosuppressed patients with hyperinfection (steroids suppress eosinophilia) is a poor prognostic sign.
generic_medicines: 1. First-Line Treatment

Ivermectin 200mcg/kg/day orally for 2 days (on empty stomach): First-line and most effective treatment for all forms of strongyloidiasis. Single/2-day course achieves cure in 85–95% of uncomplicated cases. Must be taken on empty stomach (30 min before food) for maximum absorption. Repeat after 2 weeks for immunocompromised patients.

Ivermectin 200mcg/kg/day for 7 days or longer (Hyperinfection/Disseminated): Extended course for hyperinfection — continue until 2 consecutive negative stool examinations. Some guidelines recommend 2-weekly courses until stool-negative for immunosuppressed patients.
2. Alternative / Second-Line Treatment
Albendazole 400mg twice daily for 7 days: Less effective than Ivermectin for Strongyloides (70–80% cure rate) — prescribed where Ivermectin unavailable. Extend to 14 days in hyperinfection.
3. Prophylaxis (Before Immunosuppression)

Ivermectin 200mcg/kg single dose (Pre-Steroid / Pre-Transplant Screening Protocol): For patients from endemic areas or positive Strongyloides IgG starting long-term corticosteroids, immunosuppressants, or organ transplant — single pre-treatment course clears Strongyloides and prevents hyperinfection.
4. Adjunctive (Hyperinfection)

Broad-Spectrum Antibiotics (Pipercillin-Tazobactam / Meropenem / Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole): For Gram-negative sepsis complicating hyperinfection — larvae carry enteric bacteria through gut wall. IV antibiotics mandatory in hyperinfection with fever/sepsis features.
ORS / IV Fluids: Electrolyte replacement for diarrhoea and paralytic ileus in heavy infection and hyperinfection.
5. HTLV-1 Co-Infected Patients

Ivermectin — Extended and Repeated Cycles (Monthly, Long-Term): HTLV-1 co-infected patients cannot control Strongyloides autoinfection immunologically — require long-term intermittent Ivermectin maintenance. Monthly single dose Ivermectin indefinitely in HTLV-1 + Strongyloides co-infection.
janaushadhi_medicines: Ivermectin 6mg Tablets (4 tabs): First-line treatment for strongyloidiasis. PMBJP supplies; Government NTD programme.
Albendazole 400mg Tablets (10 tabs): Second-line alternative where Ivermectin unavailable. Widely available PMBJP.
ORS Sachets: Symptomatic hydration management. Available at all health centres.
Metronidazole 400mg Tablets (10 tabs): Adjunct antibacterial for enteric bacterial co-infection in hyperinfection.
Ceftriaxone 1g Injection (1 vial): IV sepsis management in hyperinfection syndrome.
Vitamin B Complex Tablets (10 tabs): Nutritional support during malabsorption from intestinal strongyloidiasis.
ayurvedic_medicines: Vidanga (Embelia ribes) Powder: Most important classical Krimighna (antiparasitic) herb in Ayurveda for intestinal worm infections — activity against nematodes confirmed in laboratory studies. 2–3g powder with honey on empty stomach twice daily. Supportive antiparasitic role alongside Ivermectin for chronic strongyloidiasis.

Krimikuthar Ras: Classical Ayurvedic herbomineral antiparasitic Rasa formulation — Vidanga, Trikatu, Tankana (borax) combination — for Krimi (intestinal parasite) infections. 250mg twice daily under Vaidya guidance. Traditional broad-spectrum antiparasitic for helminthic infestations.

Nimba (Neem / Azadirachta indica) Oil or Leaf Decoction: Neem extract has documented in-vitro anthelmintic activity — Nimba decoction (30ml twice daily) or neem leaf capsules (500mg twice daily). Traditional Ayurvedic first-line intestinal antiseptic and antiparasitic.

Bilwadi Churna (Bael Compound): Antidiarrhoeal, astringent, and intestinal mucosal healing — bael/bilwa (Aegle marmelos) for diarrhoea management in intestinal strongyloidiasis. 3–5g twice daily with warm water.

Trikatu Churna: Digestive stimulant — Pippali (Piper longum), Maricha (black pepper), Shunthi (ginger). Enhances bioavailability of Ivermectin and improves intestinal absorption. 1g before meals twice daily in Kapha-dominant individuals with sluggish gut.

Kutaja Ghana Vati (Holarrhena antidysenterica Extract): Classical anti-dysenteric and intestinal antiparasitic — reduces diarrhoea and intestinal inflammation from Strongyloides enteritis. 500mg twice daily after food.
Guduchi Churna or Satva: Immunostimulatory rasayana — supports cellular immune response regulation in chronic strongyloidiasis and prevention of autoinfection exacerbation. 500mg twice daily.

Ashwagandha Granules: Adaptogenic and immunomodulatory — supports adrenal and immune axis during heavy Strongyloides infection with systemic symptoms; post-hyperinfection recovery support. 5g twice daily with warm milk.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Soil-Transmitted Helminth Endemic Zone — Tropical India: Strongyloides is prevalent throughout India's tropical belt — particularly Assam, West Bengal, Bihar, Jharkhand, Odisha, Chhattisgarh, and Tamil Nadu. High-risk activities: barefoot farming, walking on contaminated soil, agricultural workers, and children playing in soil contaminated with human faeces (open defecation). HTLV-1 Co-Infection in Northeast India: Human T-cell Lymphotropic Virus type
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations SCREEN FOR STRONGYLOIDES BEFORE PRESCRIBING SYSTEMIC CORTICOSTEROIDS — Non-Negotiable in Endemic India: The most preventable cause of death from Strongyloides is hyperinfection precipitated by corticosteroid prescription. Any patient from endemic rural tropical India who is

diseases.name: Enterobiasis
tests: 1. Scotch Tape / Cellophane Tape Test (Primary Diagnostic Test — Extremely Simple)

Perianal Scotch Tape Test (Graham's Test): Clear adhesive tape pressed firmly on perianal skin immediately on waking (BEFORE bathing or defecation) — mounted on glass slide — examined under low-power microscopy. Enterobius eggs = ovoid, flattened on one side, 50–60µm. Single test sensitivity 50–60%; 3 consecutive morning tests = 90%+ sensitivity. This is one of the easiest diagnostic tests in medicine — should be performed in any child with nocturnal perianal pruritus.
2. Stool Examination (Low Sensitivity — Not Recommended as Primary Test)

Stool Microscopy: Eggs found in <5–15% of infected patients. Not recommended as the primary test because female worms deposit eggs on perianal skin, NOT inside the colon. Stool exam may detect eggs only incidentally.
3. Direct Worm Visualisation (Often Diagnostic Without Laboratory Test)

Inspection of Perianal Skin at Night (9–11 PM) with Torchlight: Parent inspects perianal region of sleeping child with torch 2–3 hours after sleep onset — white, actively moving 8–13mm threadworms visible. Pathognomonic diagnosis without any laboratory test.
4. Blood Count

CBC — Eosinophilia: Mild or absent eosinophilia — Enterobius does NOT typically cause significant blood eosinophilia (unlike tissue-invasive helminths). Presence of significant eosinophilia with Enterobius = consider additional helminths.
5. Clinical Diagnosis (In Practice — Most Common Approach)

Clinical Diagnosis: In children with classic nocturnal perianal pruritus + sleep disturbance in an Indian urban or mixed urban-rural setting — clinical diagnosis is entirely appropriate, and empirical family-wide treatment is both safe and cost-effective. Diagnostic testing reserved for atypical presentations or treatment failure.
generic_medicines: 1. First-Line Treatment (Virtually 100% Effective)

Mebendazole 100mg single oral dose — repeat after 2 weeks: First-line treatment; chewable tablet available. Single dose kills adult worms — repeat dose at 2 weeks kills worms from eggs that hatched after initial treatment (eggs unaffected by Mebendazole). TREAT ALL HOUSEHOLD MEMBERS SIMULTANEOUSLY on both dose days.

Albendazole 400mg single oral dose — repeat after 2 weeks: Equally effective alternative — same 2-dose protocol with 2-week interval. ENTIRE HOUSEHOLD simultaneously. Takes with or without food. Both doses mandatory for complete eradication.
2. Repeat Treatment Course (For Persistent Perianal Pruritus After Initial Treatment)

Mebendazole 100mg — third dose 2 weeks after second dose: If symptoms persist after 2-dose course, re-examine with tape test; if positive, third dose and enhanced hygiene measures. Consider re-infection from school or household member who was not treated.
3. Topical Symptomatic Relief

Zinc Oxide Ointment / Calamine Lotion — Perianal Application: Topical antipruritic for overnight perianal application — reduces scratching and secondary bacterial infection from excoriation. Non-medicated approach to break the scratch → egg-transfer → autoinfection cycle.

Mild Antihistamine (Cetirizine 5–10mg at Bedtime): For severe nocturnal pruritus disrupting sleep — short-course antihistamine allows undisturbed sleep while drug treatment is working. Not a treatment for Enterobius — symptomatic adjunct only.
4. No IV/Hospital Treatment Required: Enterobiasis is entirely managed in the community. No hospital admission, parenteral treatment, or specialist referral required in uncomplicated cases.
janaushadhi_medicines: Mebendazole 100mg Tablets (6 tabs): First-line treatment — chewable. PMBJP widely available at all outlets.
Albendazole 400mg Tablets (10 tabs): Alternative first-line. PMBJP standard listing.
Cetirizine 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): Symptomatic antihistamine for nocturnal pruritus.
Zinc Oxide Ointment 10% (1 tube): Topical perianal antipruritic/barrier cream.
ayurvedic_medicines: Vidanga (Embelia ribes) Powder: Classical primary Krimighna (antiparasitic/anthelmintic) herb — Vidanga for Krimi (intestinal worms) in children is described extensively in Charaka and Sushruta Samhita. 1–2g powder with honey on empty stomach twice daily for 7 days. Activity against Enterobius documented in preliminary studies. Well-tolerated in children — primary Ayurvedic antiparasitic for childhood enterobiasis.

Krimikuthar Ras: Classical Ayurvedic compound rasa formulation for intestinal parasitic infections — Vidanga base with Trikatu, Tankana (borax), and metallic-herbal combination. 125–250mg twice daily under Vaidya guidance for children >5 years. Traditional staple for school-age Krimi management in Ayurvedic practice.

Palasha Beeja (Butea monosperma / Flame of Forest Seeds) Powder: Traditional anthelmintic for roundworm and pinworm — 2–3g powder with warm water on empty stomach. Available at Ayurvedic pharmacies and traditional Vaidyas.
Shatpushpa + Vidanga Churna Compound: Combined carminative and antiparasitic — reduces intestinal cramping and expels worms. Traditional household preparation for children in Western India.

Nimba (Neem) Leaf Powder: Internal antiparasitic and antimicrobial — 1–2g neem leaf powder with honey in children twice daily for 5 days. External use: dilute neem oil for perianal application to reduce egg viability on skin.

Haritaki (Terminalia chebula) Churna: Mild laxative and bowel-cleansing agent — helps eliminate worms from large intestine. 3g with warm water at bedtime for adults; 1g with honey for children. Tridoshic bowel regulator.
Hingwashtak Churna: Carminative formula (Hing/Asafoetida based) — reduces abdominal colic, flatulence, and bowel cramping associated with enterobiasis. 3–5g with warm water before meals.

Bala (Sida cordifolia) Syrup or Gripe Water Compound: Paediatric gut tonic used in children — reduces night restlessness and improves sleep quality as symptomatic adjunct. Available as commercial paediatric Ayurvedic syrup.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Urban Prevalence Despite Better Sanitation: Unlike most soil-transmitted helminths (Ascaris, hookworm) which are Rural predominant, Enterobius thrives in URBAN India — transmission does not require soil contact. Crowded apartments, shared bathrooms, domestic helpers, and school environments sustain urban transmission. Enterobius is India's most democratic parasite — infects children across all socioeconomic classes. Boarding Schools and Madrasas: India
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Treat the ENTIRE Household Simultaneously — Treating One Person is Never Enough: The single most important management principle for enterobiasis: Enterobius spreads so efficiently within a household that treating one infected child while leaving household members untreated r

diseases.name: Cryptosporidiosis
tests: 1. Stool Microscopy — Modified Ziehl-Neelsen (Kinyoun) Stain (Primary Diagnostic Test)

Modified AFB (Cold Kinyoun) Staining of Stool: Brilliant red/pink 4–6µm round oocysts against blue/green background — PATHOGNOMONIC appearance. Most cost-effective and widely available test for Cryptosporidiosis. Should be requested as 'modified ZN / Kinyoun stain for Cryptosporidium' — NOT routine stool AFB (which uses hot Ziehl-Neelsen and is too hot for oocysts). Available at all medical college labs.

Auramine-Rhodamine Fluorescence Microscopy: More sensitive than Kinyoun for oocyst detection — fluorescent yellow-green oocysts. Available at reference labs; higher throughput for large-volume screening.
2. Antigen Detection (ELISA / RDT — More Sensitive than Microscopy)

Cryptosporidium Stool Antigen ELISA or Lateral Flow RDT: Sensitivity 90–99%; specificity 99%. Superior to microscopy for low-burden infections. Detects both C. parvum and C. hominis. Commercially available rapid test kits (TechLab, IDEXX, others). Available at AIIMS, CMC, PGIMER, and increasingly major private labs.
3. Molecular Diagnosis (PCR — Reference Standard)

Stool Cryptosporidium PCR: Most sensitive and typing-capable method — differentiates C. parvum (zoonotic) from C. hominis (human-only) for outbreak investigation. Not routinely required clinically but valuable for epidemiological investigation.
4. Intestinal Biopsy (For Atypical/Refractory Cases)

Duodenal/Jejunal Biopsy Histopathology: Comma-shaped intracellular parasites on luminal surface of enterocytes on H&E/PAS — typically at the brush border. For refractory or atypical cases where diagnosis remains uncertain.
5. Blood Tests
generic_medicines: 1. Treatment in Immunocompetent Adults and Children
Nitazoxanide 500mg twice daily for 3 days (Adults): Most effective anti-Cryptosporidium drug for immunocompetent individuals — 80–90% clinical cure rate. Take with food for maximum absorption.

Nitazoxanide 100mg twice daily for 3 days (Children 1–3 years); 200mg twice daily (4–11 years): Paediatric-dosed Nitazoxanide oral suspension — effective for childhood cryptosporidiosis including malnourished children in Indian endemic settings.
2. Oral Rehydration — Most Important Intervention at All Severity Levels

ORS (WHO Low-Osmolarity Formula) — Maintain Hydration: ORS is the cornerstone of management across all severity levels — prevents dehydration-related mortality in children. IV access and IV fluids when unable to maintain ORS. Zinc 20mg/day for 14 days (children) alongside ORS per IMNCI.
3. HIV-Associated Cryptosporidiosis — ART is the Only Definitive Treatment

Antiretroviral Therapy (ART) — Immediate Optimisation: In HIV-positive patients with Cryptosporidiosis, raising CD4 above 100 cells/µl is the ONLY reliable treatment. Nitazoxanide has limited/no efficacy in those with CD4 <50. ART optimisation is the primary intervention — clinical cure of cryptosporidiosis follows immune reconstitution.
Nitazoxanide 500mg twice daily (adjunct in HIV — CD4 50–200): Modest benefit as adjunct to ART in HIV patients with moderate immune function. Not effective as monotherapy with CD4 <50.
4. Nutritional Support (Critical for Children)
Zinc Sulphate 20mg/day for 14 days (Children — IMNCI Protocol): Reduces diarrhoea severity and duration — essential co-prescription with ORS in all under-5 cryptosporidiosis cases.
High-Calorie Nutritional Rehabilitation: Protein-calorie nutritional support during recovery — RTUF (Ready to Use Therapeutic Food) for severe acute malnutrition with cryptosporidiosis.
5. Biliary Cryptosporidiosis
ERCP + Biliary Stenting: For biliary strictures with obstructive jaundice — endoscopic biliary drainage. Definitive treatment remains ART-driven immune reconstitution.
Ursodeoxycholic Acid 300mg twice daily: Supportive biliary agent for HIV cholangiopathy management.
janaushadhi_medicines: Nitazoxanide 500mg Tablets (6 tabs): Primary treatment for cryptosporidiosis in immunocompetent patients. PMBJP listed.
Nitazoxanide 200mg/5ml Oral Suspension (30ml): Paediatric formulation for childhood cryptosporidiosis.
ORS Sachets (WHO Formula): Core hydration management — widely available at all government health centres.
Zinc Sulphate 20mg Dispersible Tablets (14 tabs): Mandatory co-therapy with ORS for under-5 diarrhoea per IMNCI.
Metronidazole 400mg Tablets (10 tabs): For co-infection with Giardia or amoeba (commonly co-detected in Indian childhood diarrhoea).
IV Normal Saline 500ml: For moderate-severe dehydration where ORS cannot be used.
ayurvedic_medicines: Kutajarishta (Holarrhena antidysenterica Fermented Decoction): Most relevant classical Ayurvedic formulation for Atisara (diarrhoea) and intestinal infections — Kutaja alkaloids have direct in-vitro antiprotozoal and antibacterial activity. 15–25ml twice daily after food. Complementary to ORS and Nitazoxanide; particularly suitable for convalescent phase diarrhoea management and reducing relapse risk.

Bilwadi Churna (Bael / Aegle marmelos Compound): Antidiarrhoeal astringent — tannins in unripe bael fruit reduce intestinal hypersecretion and mucosal permeability. Particularly useful for watery diarrhoea and intestinal mucosal protection. 3–5g twice daily with warm water.

Ativisha (Aconitum heterophyllum) Churna: Classical paediatric Ayurvedic herb for childhood diarrhoea — Ativisha is the most important Ayurvedic herb for Bal Atisara (childhood diarrhoea) in classical texts. 125–250mg with honey for children. Under Vaidya prescription — significant activity for diarrhoeal conditions in children.

Nagarmotha (Cyperus rotundus) Decoction: Antiprotozoal, antidiarrhoeal, and carminative — reduces cramping, frequency, and gut motility dysregulation. 30ml decoction twice daily as intestinal mucosal supportive therapy.

Dadimadi Ghrita: Digestive and mucosal healing ghee-based formulation with Dadima (pomegranate) — supports intestinal epithelial repair after Cryptosporidium-induced villous atrophy. 5–10ml on empty stomach.

Ashwagandha + Shatavari Combination: Immune and nutritional rebuilding — for post-cryptosporidiosis malnutrition recovery and immune rehabilitation, particularly in HIV-positive patients on ART. 5g each with warm milk twice daily.
Triphala Churna: Gentle intestinal tonic and antioxidant — regularises bowel function post-cryptosporidiosis gastroenteritis recovery. 5g at bedtime with warm water.

Punarnava (Boerhavia diffusa) Decoction: Diuretic and anti-oedema — for nutritional oedema and protein-energy malnutrition recovery state post-prolonged Cryptosporidium diarrhoea. 30ml twice daily under Vaidya guidance.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Chlorine-Resistant Oocysts in Municipal Water: Unlike most waterborne pathogens, Cryptosporidium parvum oocysts resist standard chlorine disinfection concentrations used in Indian municipal water treatment. The only reliable household-level decontamination is boiling or WHO-certified UV water purifiers. Most Indian economic water filters (basic ceramic, sand) do NOT reliably remove Cryptosporidium — only 1-micron absolute filter or reverse osmosis is e
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Cryptosporidium Oocysts are Chlorine-Resistant — Boiling is the Only Reliable Household Water Protection: This is the most clinically important public health fact about Cryptosporidiosis. India's entire municipal water chlorination infrastructure — which adequately controls

diseases.name: Toxocariasis
tests: 1. Serology (Primary Diagnostic Test)

Toxocara canis IgG ELISA — Excretory/Secretory Antigen (TES-ELISA): Most important diagnostic test — sensitivity 78–91%, specificity 92–95% with TES antigen. Standard diagnostic test globally and at Indian reference labs. Available at AIIMS, CMC Vellore, PGIMER, NIMHANS, and increasingly private reference labs.
Western Blot Confirmation: For indeterminate ELISA results — 32-kDa band on WB is highly specific for Toxocara. Available at major reference labs.
2. Blood Count — Eosinophilia (Key Supportive Finding)
CBC with Differential: Marked eosinophilia (>1000 cells/µl; often 5000–30,000+ in active VLM) is a cardinal feature. Anaemia in young children with heavy worm burden.
3. Ophthalmological Examination (OLM)

Indirect Ophthalmoscopy (Dilated Fundus Examination by Ophthalmologist): Peripheral granuloma in vitreous or retina, vitreous bands/haze, dragged disc — in OLM. B-scan ultrasound for posterior pole lesion characterisation. Fluorescein angiography for activity.

Ocular Ultrasound (B-Scan): Useful for dense vitreous haze or media opacification — identifies posterior segment granuloma. Essential for differentiating OLM from retinoblastoma (calcification present in retinoblastoma, absent in OLM).
4. Imaging — VLM
Abdominal Ultrasound: Hepatic granulomata — multiple hypoechoic small lesions in liver parenchyma corresponding to Toxocara larval tracks. Characteristic honeycomb pattern of microabscesses.
generic_medicines: 1. Anti-Toxocara Treatment (VLM and Covert Toxocariasis)

Albendazole 400mg twice daily for 5 days (Adults/Children >2 years): Standard first-line treatment for VLM — kills migrating larvae by blocking microtubule polymerisation. Take with fatty meal. Some guidelines extend to 10–14 days for heavy VLM with systemic involvement.
Mebendazole 100–200mg twice daily for 5 days: Alternative to Albendazole — effective but variable GI absorption. Less commonly used for VLM in India.
2. Anti-Inflammatory (OLM and Severe VLM — Mandatory Adjunct)

Prednisolone 1–2mg/kg/day for 2–4 weeks (VLM with Severe Eosinophilia or OLM): Corticosteroids suppress the eosinophilic inflammatory response to migrating larvae — reduces hepatic and pulmonary granuloma formation severity and prevents retinal scarring in OLM. In OLM: ALWAYS start Prednisolone WITH Albendazole (not steroids alone which may worsen larval migration).

Periocular Steroid Injection (OLM with Active Vitritis): Intravitreal or sub-Tenon Triamcinolone for active OLM vitreous inflammation — under ophthalmologist supervision. Suppresses vitreous inflammation and prevents tractional retinal detachment.
3. Ophthalmological Interventions (OLM Complications)
Laser Photocoagulation (Retinal Toxocara Granuloma): To destroy accessible retinal Toxocara larvae directly and prevent retinal extension. Under retinal specialist evaluation.
Vitrectomy (OLM with Tractional Retinal Detachment): For OLM causing vitreous membranes and tractional detachment — surgical vitreoretinal intervention.
4. Bronchodilators (Pulmonary VLM with Wheezing)

Salbutamol MDI 100mcg (2 puffs as needed) + Montelukast 5–10mg/day: Symptomatic bronchodilation for Loeffler syndrome wheezing — NOT the definitive treatment (Albendazole + Prednisolone are). Withdraw once Toxocara treatment complete and eosinophilia resolves.
janaushadhi_medicines: Albendazole 400mg Tablets (10 tabs): First-line anti-Toxocara treatment. PMBJP widely available.
Prednisolone 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): Anti-inflammatory adjunct — VLM and OLM.
Cetirizine 10mg Tablets (10 tabs): Antihistamine for urticarial and eosinophilic hypersensitivity symptoms.
Salbutamol 100mcg MDI (Inhaler): Bronchodilator for pulmonary Loeffler syndrome wheezing.
Montelukast 5mg Chewable Tablets (10 tabs): Anti-leukotriene for eosinophilic asthma/VLM pulmonary component in children.
Omeprazole 20mg Capsules (10 caps): Gastric protection during steroid course.
ayurvedic_medicines: Vidanga (Embelia ribes) Churna: Primary Krimighna herb — traditional for systemic intestinal and extra-intestinal worm infections. 2–3g twice daily with honey on empty stomach for children. Supportive antiparasitic alongside Albendazole in visceral larva migrans.

Krimikuthar Ras: Compound classical rasa antiparasitic — for Krimi in systemic organs as well as intestinal worms. 250mg twice daily under Vaidya guidance. Traditional for multi-organ helminthic invasion.

Triphala Ghrita: Internal administration — ghee infused with Triphala for systemic infection with hepatic involvement. Hepatoprotective and antioxidant; supports hepatic granuloma resolution during VLM. 5–10ml on empty stomach.
Nimba (Neem) Leaf Capsules: Antiparasitic and anti-inflammatory — neem extract has activity against multiple helminths and reduces systemic inflammation. 500mg capsules twice daily.

Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) Extract: Immunomodulatory and anti-inflammatory — reduces excessive eosinophilic inflammatory response in VLM and supports reticuloendothelial system. 500mg standardised extract twice daily.

Saptamruta Lauha: Classical Ayurvedic ophthalmic and rasayana formulation — used in eye diseases caused by internal infections and for liver-eye axis conditions (Netra Roga from Yakrit Vikar). Under Vaidya guidance for OLM supportive management.
Traiphala Ghrita Eye Drops: Ayurvedic ophthalmic preparation for vitreous clarity and retinal support in OLM — traditional Netra Tarpana with medicated ghee under Vaidya supervision. Supportive only.

Amalaki (Indian Gooseberry) Powder: High antioxidant and immune-supportive — reduces systemic oxidative stress from migrating Toxocara larvae and reduces eosinophilic tissue injury. 5g twice daily with warm water.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors Stray Dog Density and Soil Contamination: India's massive stray dog population contaminates urban and rural soils ubiquitously with Toxocara canis eggs. Unlike in countries with comprehensive stray dog management and deworming programmes, India has limited systematic stray dog anthelmintic treatment — leading to continuous environmental egg contamination. Sandpit and Park Contamination: Indian urban parks, school sandpits, playgrounds, and roadsides ar
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations Deworm Your Pets Regularly — The Most Important Prevention for Toxocariasis in India: Toxocariasis is 100% preventable at source by regular anthelmintic treatment of all dogs and cats. Pet dogs should receive Pyrantel Pamoate or Fenbendazole deworming at 2, 4, 6, and 8 weeks

diseases.name: Schizophrenia
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (DSM-5 / ICD-11)
DSM-5: ≥2 of 5 symptoms (hallucinations, delusions, disorganised speech, disorganised behaviour, negative symptoms) for ≥1 month; social/occupational dysfunction ≥6 months
ICD-11: Persistent delusions, hallucinations, disorganised thinking, passivity experiences, or negative symptoms for ≥1 month
Structured clinical interviews: MINI International Neuropsychiatric Interview (MINI), SCID-5
RATING SCALES
PANSS (Positive and Negative Syndrome Scale) — gold standard for symptom severity
BPRS (Brief Psychiatric Rating Scale) — widely used in Indian settings
CDSS (Calgary Depression Scale for Schizophrenia) — comorbid depression
GAF (Global Assessment of Functioning)
COGNITIVE TESTING
generic_medicines: TYPICAL (FIRST-GENERATION) ANTIPSYCHOTICS
Haloperidol 1.5–20 mg/day oral (decanoate depot IM monthly for adherence)
Trifluoperazine 5–20 mg/day oral
Chlorpromazine 75–300 mg/day oral (rarely used — sedation, photosensitivity)
Fluphenazine decanoate 12.5–25 mg IM every 2–4 weeks
ATYPICAL (SECOND-GENERATION) ANTIPSYCHOTICS
Risperidone 2–8 mg/day oral (first-line in India — affordable, effective)
Olanzapine 5–20 mg/day oral (good broad-spectrum; weight gain risk)
Quetiapine 150–800 mg/day (useful when sedation or sleep benefit needed)
Aripiprazole 10–30 mg/day (weight-neutral, dopamine partial agonist)
Amisulpride 300–800 mg/day (good for negative symptoms)
Ziprasidone 40–160 mg/day (QTc monitoring required)
REFRACTORY SCHIZOPHRENIA
Clozapine 150–900 mg/day — gold standard for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (WBC monitoring mandatory)
ANTIDEPRESSANTS (augmentation for negative symptoms or comorbid depression)
ayurvedic_medicines: CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — cognitive support, calming adaptogen; 300–600 mg extract/day
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — adaptogenic, anxiolytic, neuroprotective; 300–600 mg/day
Shankhapushpi (Convolvulus pluricaulis) — nervine tonic, cognitive enhancement
Jatamansi (Nardostachys jatamansi) — Ayurvedic sedative-anxiolytic; 250–500 mg/day
Vacha (Acorus calamus) — medhya (intellect-promoting), used carefully (mild anticonvulsant)
Saraswatarishta (polyherbal tonic) — nervine, cognitive; 15–20 mL BD after meals
Manasamitra Vatakam — traditional psychosis formulation (contains gold; used in Kerala tradition)
BRANDED AYURVEDIC
Mentat (Himalaya) — Brahmi + Ashwagandha + Shankhapushpi combination
