diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors TREATMENT GAP Treatment gap estimated at 70–80% — majority never receive psychiatric care First contact with traditional / faith healers before psychiatry in most rural areas SOCIOCULTURAL FACTORS Severe stigma — patients hidden, chained (Erwadi tragedy, 2001) or abandoned Caregiver burnout — primary caregivers are family members with no support Marriage and employment discrimination severely impact recovery HEALTHCARE SYSTEM Limited district mental he
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations CLOZAPINE SAFETY: Mandatory weekly white blood cell (WBC/ANC) count for 18 weeks, then fortnightly; clozapine must be STOPPED if ANC <1500/mm³ (severe neutropenia risk — agranulocytosis in 0.5–2%) NEVER ABRUPT DISCONTINUATION: Stopping antipsychotics without tapering leads t

diseases.name: Bipolar Disorder
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS
DSM-5 Bipolar I: Manic episode ≥7 days (or any duration if hospitalisation required); not attributable to substances or medical condition
DSM-5 Bipolar II: Hypomanic episodes + major depressive episodes; no full manic episode
STRUCTURED INTERVIEWS
MINI International Neuropsychiatric Interview (MINI) — standard in India
SCID-5 — research standard
RATING SCALES
Young Mania Rating Scale (YMRS) — 11-item clinician-administered; gold standard for mania
HAMD (Hamilton Depression Rating Scale) or MADRS for depressive episodes
MDQ (Mood Disorder Questionnaire) — screening tool; sensitivity 73%, specificity 90%
generic_medicines: MOOD STABILISERS — FIRST-LINE
Lithium carbonate 900–2400 mg/day in divided doses (most proven agent; monitor levels)
Sodium Valproate (Divalproex) 750–2000 mg/day oral (acute mania; avoid in pregnancy)
Lamotrigine 25–200 mg/day — bipolar depression and maintenance (titrate slowly to avoid Stevens-Johnson syndrome)
Carbamazepine 400–1200 mg/day — acute mania and maintenance (enzyme inducer, drug interactions)
ANTIPSYCHOTICS (acute mania and maintenance)
Olanzapine 10–20 mg/day — effective for acute mania; FDA-approved maintenance
Risperidone 2–6 mg/day — acute mania
Quetiapine 300–800 mg/day — acute mania AND bipolar depression (only antipsychotic with level 1 evidence for bipolar depression)
Aripiprazole 15–30 mg/day — acute mania, weight neutral
Haloperidol 5–10 mg/day — acute severe mania with agitation (adjunct)
BENZODIAZEPINES (ACUTE MANIA — SHORT-TERM ONLY)
Lorazepam 1–4 mg IM/oral — acute agitation in mania
Clonazepam 1–4 mg/day — adjunct for agitation, sleep
ANTIDEPRESSANTS (use with CAUTION — only with mood stabiliser)
ayurvedic_medicines: CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — nervine tonic, reduces anxiety, supports mood regulation; 300–600 mg/day
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — adaptogenic, mood-stabilising properties; 300–600 mg/day
Jatamansi (Nardostachys jatamansi) — calming, anti-manic, sleep promoting; 250–500 mg/day
Brahmi Vati — classical tablet for psychosis and mania; contains Brahmi, Vacha, Suvarna bhasma
Saraswatarishta — polyherbal fermented tonic for nervine and mental health; 15–20 mL BD
Mandukaparni (Centella asiatica) — neuroprotective, cognitive support
SLEEP AND MOOD SUPPORT
Ashwagandharishtam — stress, fatigue, depression adjunct; 15–20 mL BD
Tagara (Valeriana wallichii) — mild sedative for sleep in depressive phase; 250–500 mg/day

diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations LITHIUM TOXICITY RISK: Narrow therapeutic window (0.8–1.2 mEq/L therapeutic; toxic >1.5 mEq/L). Early signs: coarse tremor, GI upset, polyuria, polydipsia. Severe: ataxia, confusion, seizures, cardiac arrhythmias. Common precipitants in India: NSAIDs, dehydration (fever, sum

diseases.name: Major Depressive Disorder
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (DSM-5)
≥5 symptoms for ≥2 weeks; must include depressed mood or anhedonia; not attributable to substances or medical condition; causes significant distress or functional impairment
STRUCTURED INTERVIEWS
MINI International Neuropsychiatric Interview (MINI) — validated in India
SCID-5 — research standard
PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9) — validated primary care screening tool; score ≥10 = moderately severe; widely used in India
RATING SCALES
HAM-D (Hamilton Depression Rating Scale) — 17-item; gold standard for severity
MADRS (Montgomery-Asberg Depression Rating Scale) — more sensitive to change
BDI-II (Beck Depression Inventory) — self-rated; widely used
generic_medicines: SSRI — FIRST-LINE (preferred for most patients; safest in overdose)
Escitalopram 10–20 mg/day (most well-tolerated SSRI; minimal drug interactions)
Sertraline 50–200 mg/day (first-line in post-MI depression, postpartum; safest in pregnancy)
Fluoxetine 20–60 mg/day (long half-life; useful for adherence issues; PMDD)
Paroxetine 20–50 mg/day (useful for comorbid anxiety; discontinuation syndrome risk)
Fluvoxamine 50–300 mg/day (comorbid OCD)
SNRI
Venlafaxine 75–225 mg/day (effective for depression + anxiety; blood pressure monitoring)
Duloxetine 60–120 mg/day (comorbid pain, diabetic neuropathy)
TCAs (LOW COST — useful at Jan Aushadhi; higher toxicity in overdose)
Amitriptyline 25–150 mg/day (also for neuropathic pain, insomnia; sedating)
Imipramine 75–200 mg/day
Clomipramine 75–250 mg/day (also for OCD)
NDRI
Bupropion 150–450 mg/day (activating; no sexual side effects; smoking cessation)
janaushadhi_medicines: Clonazepam 0.5 mg tablet (adjunct for anxiety and sleep)
ayurvedic_medicines: CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — neuroprotective, antidepressant, anxiolytic properties; 300–600 mg extract/day
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — adaptogen, anti-cortisol, mood-lifting; 300–600 mg/day
Saffron (Crocus sativus / Kesar) — Level 1b evidence for mild-moderate depression; 30 mg/day saffron extract (equivalent to Fluoxetine 20 mg in some RCTs)
Shankhapushpi (Convolvulus pluricaulis) — nervine tonic, anti-anxiety; syrup 10–20 mL BD
Jatamansi (Nardostachys jatamansi) — sedative, anti-depressant, reduces cortisol; 250–500 mg/day
Giloy / Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulator, anti-fatigue, anti-stress
Tagara (Valeriana wallichii) — sleep promoting in insomnia-predominant depression; 0.5–2 g/day
CLASSICAL COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Saraswatarishta 15–20 mL BD — nervine rejuvenator for depression with cognitive slowing
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors EPIDEMIOLOGY India has the highest absolute number of depression cases globally (approximately 56 million) Suicide rate in India is 12.9 per 100,000 — predominantly from depression in productive age group GENDER AND SOCIAL FACTORS Women: Domestic violence, dowry harassment, restricted autonomy, early marriage, postpartum depression — significant risk amplifiers Men: Occupational stress, societal expectation against help-seeking ('real men don't cry'),
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations SUICIDAL RISK ASSESSMENT IS MANDATORY: All patients with MDD must be assessed for suicidal ideation at every visit using a structured tool (C-SSRS, PHQ item 9). Patients with active suicidal ideation with plan should be hospitalised. Prescribe limited quantities of medicatio

diseases.name: Generalised Anxiety Disorder
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (DSM-5)
Excessive anxiety and worry ≥6 months; ≥3 of 6 symptoms; causing significant distress/impairment; not attributable to substances or medical condition
SCREENING TOOLS
GAD-7 (Generalised Anxiety Disorder 7-item scale) — score ≥10 = moderate-severe; widely validated in India
GAD-2 — ultra-brief 2-item screen for primary care settings
STRUCTURED INTERVIEWS
MINI International Neuropsychiatric Interview — validates GAD diagnosis
RATING SCALES
HAMA (Hamilton Anxiety Rating Scale) — 14-item; psychic and somatic subscales
BAI (Beck Anxiety Inventory) — somatic-focused, self-rated
generic_medicines: FIRST-LINE — SSRIs AND SNRIs
Escitalopram 10–20 mg/day (preferred SSRI for GAD; best tolerability profile)
Sertraline 50–200 mg/day
Paroxetine 20–50 mg/day (strong anti-anxiety; high discontinuation syndrome risk)
Venlafaxine XR 75–225 mg/day (SNRI; approved for GAD; monitor blood pressure)
Duloxetine 60–120 mg/day (comorbid painful somatic symptoms)
SECOND-LINE
Buspirone 15–60 mg/day in divided doses (non-addictive; slow onset 2–4 weeks; useful adjunct)
Mirtazapine 15–45 mg/day (insomnia-predominant GAD with low appetite)
Pregabalin 150–600 mg/day (good anxiolytic; approved for GAD in Europe; dependence potential)
ADJUNCTIVE / SHORT-TERM
Benzodiazepines (Lorazepam 1–2 mg, Clonazepam 0.5–2 mg) — SHORT-TERM ONLY (2–4 weeks); dependency and cognitive side effects risk
Beta-blockers (Propranolol 10–40 mg PRN) — performance anxiety, somatic symptoms (palpitations, tremor)
Hydroxyzine 25–50 mg as needed — antihistamine with anxiolytic properties; no dependency
PSYCHOTHERAPY (FIRST-LINE — combined with pharmacotherapy)
ayurvedic_medicines: CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — adaptogen, cortisol-reducing, anti-anxiety; 300–600 mg/day
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — reduces anxiety, improves cognitive performance; 300 mg/day
Jatamansi (Nardostachys jatamansi) — nervine sedative for anxiety and insomnia; 250–500 mg/day
Shankhapushpi (Convolvulus pluricaulis) — nervine tonic, anti-anxiety, cognitive support
Tagara (Valeriana wallichii) — sleep-promoting and anxiolytic (sleep-onset anxiety); 0.5–2 g/day
Sarpagandha (Rauvolfia serpentina) — classical antihypertensive and anxiolytic (reserpine source; use cautiously)
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Saraswatarishta 15–20 mL BD — nervine, memory, and anxiety support
Ashwagandharishtam 15 mL BD — stress resilience and anti-anxiety

diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations BENZODIAZEPINE DEPENDENCY RISK: Benzodiazepines (Diazepam, Clonazepam, Lorazepam) are widely overprescribed for anxiety in India. They provide rapid relief but cause dependence within 4–6 weeks of daily use. Tolerance develops requiring dose escalation. Withdrawal can cause

diseases.name: Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (DSM-5)
Criterion A–H: Exposure to traumatic event + ≥1 intrusion + ≥1 avoidance + ≥2 negative cognitions/mood + ≥2 arousal/reactivity; duration >1 month; causes significant distress/impairment
SCREENING TOOLS
PCL-5 (PTSD Checklist for DSM-5) — 20-item self-report; score ≥33 = PTSD likely; most widely used
IES-R (Impact of Event Scale — Revised) — 22-item; intrusion, avoidance, hyperarousal subscales
Primary Care PTSD Screen (PC-PTSD-5) — 5-item ultra-brief screen for primary care
STRUCTURED INTERVIEWS
CAPS-5 (Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale for DSM-5) — gold standard for diagnosis and severity
MINI International Neuropsychiatric Interview (PTSD module)
RATING SCALES
generic_medicines: FIRST-LINE (FDA and NICE approved)
Sertraline 50–200 mg/day (FDA-approved for PTSD; most widely used in India)
Paroxetine 20–60 mg/day (FDA-approved for PTSD; discontinuation syndrome with abrupt stop)
Venlafaxine XR 75–225 mg/day (strong evidence; particularly for hyperarousal and sleep)
SECOND-LINE
Fluoxetine 20–60 mg/day (good evidence; useful when depression comorbid)
Mirtazapine 15–45 mg/day (nightmares, insomnia, and depressive symptoms)
Prazosin 1–15 mg at bedtime — alpha-1 blocker; specifically for PTSD nightmares (strong RCT evidence)
ADJUNCTIVE / SPECIFIC SYMPTOM TREATMENT
Propranolol 40–80 mg/day — hyperarousal, reactivity, palpitations
Clonidine 0.1–0.4 mg/day — hyperarousal, sleep, nightmares (alpha-2 agonist)
Quetiapine 25–200 mg — insomnia, nightmares, hyperarousal (adjunct)
AVOID IN PTSD
Benzodiazepines — no evidence for core PTSD symptoms; may worsen dissociation and increase substance use risk
PSYCHOTHERAPY — FIRST-LINE (superior to medication alone)
ayurvedic_medicines: CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — reduces cortisol, adaptogenic; helps with hyperarousal; 300–600 mg/day
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — neuroprotective, reduces anxiety and stress; 300 mg daily
Jatamansi (Nardostachys jatamansi) — calming, anti-stress; reduces nightmares and arousal; 250–500 mg/day
Tagara (Valeriana wallichii) — sedative for nightmares and insomnia; 0.5–2 g/day
Mandukaparni (Centella asiatica) — nervine tonic, trauma-related cognitive impairment
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Saraswatarishta — nervine tonic, reduces stress and anxiety; 15–20 mL BD after meals
Ashwagandharishtam — adaptogenic; general stress and PTSD adjunct
Brahmi Ghrita — classical ghee preparation; neuroprotective, trauma recovery

diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations PSYCHOTHERAPY IS THE PRIMARY TREATMENT: For PTSD, trauma-focused psychotherapy (TF-CBT, EMDR, Prolonged Exposure) is superior to medication alone. Medication addresses symptom severity but does not process the traumatic memory. Both should be combined when possible. In India

diseases.name: Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (DSM-5)

Presence of obsessions, compulsions, or both; time-consuming (>1 hour/day) or causing distress or impairment; not attributable to substances or medical condition; not better explained by another mental disorder
RATING SCALES
Y-BOCS (Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale) — gold standard; 10-item; 0–40 score; severe ≥24
OCI-R (OC Inventory — Revised) — 18-item self-report; screen and dimensional profile
DOCS (Dimensional Obsessive-Compulsive Scale) — 20-item; symptom dimensions
STRUCTURED INTERVIEWS
SCID-5 OCD module — diagnostic confirmation
MINI (OCD and related disorders module)
BIOLOGICAL (exclude neurological and organic causes)
generic_medicines: FIRST-LINE — SSRIs (at higher doses than for depression)
Fluvoxamine 100–300 mg/day (first SSRI approved for OCD; particularly for religious/aggressive obsessions)
Sertraline 100–250 mg/day (well-tolerated; good for paediatric OCD as well)
Fluoxetine 40–120 mg/day (long half-life; forgiving for missed doses)
Paroxetine 40–60 mg/day (approved for OCD)
Escitalopram 20–40 mg/day (evidence mounting; good tolerability)
FIRST-LINE — TCA
Clomipramine 150–300 mg/day (most potent anti-OCD agent; first-line alongside SSRIs; serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor; cardiac side effects — ECG monitoring)
AUGMENTATION STRATEGIES (for partial response)
Add Risperidone 0.5–3 mg/day to SSRI — best evidence for augmentation in OCD
Add Quetiapine 25–200 mg/day — insomnia + anxiety + OCD
Add Aripiprazole 5–15 mg/day — activating; weight neutral
Add Mirtazapine 15–30 mg — OCD with prominent depression/insomnia
REFRACTORY OCD
Clomipramine IV infusion (off-label) — for severe, refractory cases
ayurvedic_medicines: CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — reduces repetitive cognitive patterns, anxiolytic; 300–600 mg/day
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — anti-anxiety, reduces hyperactivation of OCD circuit; 300–600 mg/day
Jatamansi (Nardostachys jatamansi) — calming, reduces compulsive urge states; 250–500 mg/day
Mandukaparni / Gotu Kola (Centella asiatica) — frontal lobe function support, neuroprotective
Shankhapushpi — nervine tonic; traditional use for obsessive mental states
Sarpagandha (Rauvolfia serpentina) — classical sedative for agitation and thought-racing (OCD intrusion)
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Saraswatarishta 15–20 mL BD — nervine rejuvenator for mental clarity and obsessive patterns
Brahmi Vati — classical tablet for mental disorders with compulsive and agitated components
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors RELIGIOUS SCRUPULOSITY Religious OCD is extremely prevalent and culturally amplified in India — excessive temple rituals, purification rites, fear of committing religious transgressions, excessive pilgrimage compulsions Difficult to distinguish from devout religious practice — requires clinical judgement and disability assessment CULTURAL FACTORS Purity-pollution concepts in Hindu caste system amplify contamination OCD Gender pressures — women's OCD of
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations HIGH-DOSE SSRIs REQUIRED: OCD responds to higher SSRI doses than depression. Sertraline up to 200–250 mg, Fluoxetine up to 80–120 mg, Fluvoxamine up to 300 mg. Adequate trial = 12 weeks at maximum tolerated dose. Premature dose escalation plateau or early switching is a comm

diseases.name: Parkinsons Disease
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (UK Brain Bank Criteria / MDS Criteria)
Bradykinesia + ≥1 of rigidity, rest tremor, or postural instability; asymmetric onset; L-DOPA responsive
RATING SCALES
MDS-UPDRS (Movement Disorder Society Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale) — standard comprehensive assessment
Hoehn and Yahr Scale — staging I–V
Parkinson's Disease Questionnaire (PDQ-39) — quality of life
MoCA (Montreal Cognitive Assessment) — cognitive screening; <26 indicates impairment
NEUROIMAGING
MRI brain — rule out vascular parkinsonism, PSP, MSA, normal pressure hydrocephalus; PD = normal MRI
DaTscan (FP-CIT SPECT) — dopamine transporter imaging; confirms nigrostriatal degeneration; distinguishes PD from essential tremor and drug-induced Parkinsonism
generic_medicines: DOPAMINERGIC — FIRST-LINE
Levodopa + Carbidopa 25/100 mg tablets — cornerstone of PD motor treatment; multiple doses/day; protein interactions
Levodopa + Carbidopa + Entacapone (Triple combination) — extended ON time; reduces motor fluctuations
Pramipexole 0.375–4.5 mg/day (agonist; first-line young-onset; impulse control disorder risk)
Ropinirole 3–24 mg/day (agonist)
Cabergoline 1–6 mg/day (agonist; once-daily dosing)
ADJUNCTS (to Levodopa; reduce wearing-off)
Entacapone 200 mg with each Levodopa dose (COMT inhibitor; prolongs Levodopa effect)
Rasagiline 1 mg/day (MAO-B inhibitor; neuroprotective; early or adjunct)
Selegiline 5–10 mg/day (MAO-B inhibitor)
Amantadine 100–300 mg/day (anti-dyskinesia; antiviral with dopaminergic activity; reduces L-DOPA dyskinesia)
ANTICHOLINERGIC (tremor — younger patients without cognitive impairment)
Trihexyphenidyl 2–6 mg/day — tremor; AVOID in elderly (confusion, urinary retention, glaucoma)
Benztropine 0.5–6 mg/day
NON-MOTOR SYMPTOM TREATMENT
ayurvedic_medicines: CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS

Mucuna pruriens (Kapikacchu / Velvet Bean) Seed Powder — natural L-DOPA source; 5–30 g defatted powder/day. Significant clinical evidence — multiple RCTs vs Levodopa showing comparable motor benefit. The most important Ayurvedic contribution to Parkinsonism treatment.
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — neuroprotective; reduces neuroinflammation; adaptogen; 300–600 mg/day
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — cognitive support in PDD; neuroprotective, cholinergic enhancement; 300 mg/day
Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulator, neuroprotective, anti-inflammatory; 500 mg/day
Gotu Kola (Centella asiatica) — neuroprotective, antioxidant, cognitive preservation
Shilajit (Asphaltum punjabianum) — antioxidant, mitochondrial support, mineral-rich; 250–500 mg/day
Vacha (Acorus calamus) — CNS tonic; mild dopaminergic-like effect; traditional Indian PD adjunct
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Ksheerabala Taila — classical medicated oil for neuromuscular conditions; Nasya (nasal oil) use
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors EPIDEMIOLOGY India — approximately 5.8 million PD patients; 2nd most common neurodegenerative disease Onset typically 55–65 years; Young-Onset PD (<50 years) more common in India than Western populations Genetic — LRRK2, PARKIN2, PINK1 mutations; Parkin mutation most common in young-onset Indian PD TREATMENT ACCESS Levodopa/Carbidopa widely available and affordable at Jan Aushadhi Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS) available at NIMHANS, AIIMS, CMC Vellore —
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations NEVER USE TYPICAL ANTIPSYCHOTICS IN PARKINSON'S PSYCHOSIS: Haloperidol, Risperidone, Olanzapine (strongly avoid), and most typical antipsychotics block D2 receptors and will catastrophically worsen Parkinsonism — leading to rigidity, akinesia, falls, and aspiration pneumonia

diseases.name: Alzheimers Disease
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (NIA-AA 2011 / DSM-5)
Cognitive decline significantly interfering with daily functioning; gradual onset and progressive decline; not explained by delirium or other psychiatric disorder; memory + ≥1 other cognitive domain
COGNITIVE SCREENING
MMSE (Mini-Mental State Examination) — 30-point scale; <24 = dementia; widely used in India
MoCA (Montreal Cognitive Assessment) — more sensitive for MCI (mild cognitive impairment); <26 = impairment
HMSE (Hindi Mental State Examination) — validated Hindi-language version; used in Indian population
ACE-III (Addenbrooke's Cognitive Examination) — more detailed; available in Hindi
CDR (Clinical Dementia Rating) — staging: 0.5 = MCI; 1 = mild; 2 = moderate; 3 = severe
NEUROIMAGING
MRI brain (structural) — hippocampal atrophy (medial temporal), parietal/frontal atrophy; rules out other causes (tumour, SDH, NPH, vascular)
generic_medicines: CHOLINESTERASE INHIBITORS (symptomatic; delay progression — not curative)
Donepezil 5–10 mg/day at bedtime (mild to severe Alzheimer's; most widely used; GI side effects)
Rivastigmine 3–12 mg/day oral (capsules); 4.6–13.3 mg/24 hours patch (Parkinson's Disease Dementia + Alzheimer's)
Galantamine 8–24 mg/day (allosteric nicotinic modulator additionally; GI side effects)
NMDA RECEPTOR ANTAGONIST
Memantine 5–20 mg/day (moderate to severe Alzheimer's; reduces glutamate excitotoxicity; used as monotherapy or combined with Donepezil)
COMBINATION THERAPY
Donepezil 10 mg + Memantine 20 mg — superior to either alone in moderate-severe Alzheimer's
BPSD (Behavioural and Psychological Symptoms) — specific management
Depression comorbid with Alzheimer's: Sertraline 50 mg, Escitalopram 10 mg (first-line SSRIs — mild but acceptable evidence)
Agitation/aggression: Quetiapine 12.5–100 mg (avoid typical antipsychotics in elderly — high stroke, mortality risk — 'Black Box' warning)
Sleep disturbance: Melatonin 3–9 mg at bedtime (first-line for Alzheimer's sleep disruption; safe)
Mirtazapine 15–30 mg (sleep + depression + agitation; appetite stimulation)
Wandering and severe agitation: Very low-dose Haloperidol 0.5–1 mg (only when other options exhausted — short-term due to mortality and stroke risk in elderly dementia)
VASCULAR DEMENTIA RISK FACTOR MANAGEMENT
ayurvedic_medicines: CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — most extensively studied Ayurvedic herb for cognitive decline; 300–600 mg standardised extract/day; RCTs show improvement in memory, attention, information processing speed
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — reduces beta-amyloid plaque deposition in animal models; anti-inflammatory neuroprotective; 300–600 mg/day
Shankhapushpi (Convolvulus pluricaulis) — cognitive enhancement, memory support; traditional Medhya Rasayana
Guduchi / Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulator, anti-neuroinflammatory; antioxidant; 500 mg/day
Gotu Kola / Mandukaparni (Centella asiatica) — nerve growth factor stimulation; cognitive preservation; 500 mg/day
Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus) — neuroprotective; antioxidant; supports memory and cognitive vitality
MEDHYA RASAYANA (classical group of four cognitive tonics in Charaka Samhita)
Brahmi, Shankhapushpi, Guduchi, Mandukaparni — traditionally used for Smriti Bhramsha (cognitive decline and memory disorder)
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors EPIDEMIOLOGY India has approximately 8.8 million dementia patients (Alzheimer's & Related Disorders Society of India, ARDSI) Dementia prevalence 7.4% age ≥65, rising sharply with age Vascular dementia more prevalent in India compared to Alzheimer's — hypertension, diabetes, stroke (most common dementia aetiologies in India may be vascular) RISK FACTORS IN INDIA Hypertension, diabetes, obesity — major modifiable vascular risk factors Cardiovascular risk
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations EXCLUDE REVERSIBLE CAUSES BEFORE LABELLING ALZHEIMER'S: India's high prevalence of B12 deficiency (common in vegetarians), hypothyroidism, and neurosyphilis (still endemic in some areas) means that some dementia cases are from treatable metabolic/infectious causes. Every dem

diseases.name: Migraine
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (ICHD-3 Criteria)
Migraine without aura: ≥5 attacks; 4–72 hours; unilateral, pulsating, moderate-severe, aggravated by activity; nausea/vomiting or photo/phonophobia
Migraine with aura: ≥2 attacks; ≥1 aura symptom (visual, sensory, speech, motor, cerebellar, retinal) fully reversible; spreading over ≥5 min; lasting 5–60 min; headache within 60 min of aura
WHEN TO IMAGE (RED FLAGS — SNNOOP10)

Sudden onset thunderclap headache (worst-ever), New headache >50 years, Progressive worsening headache, Neurological signs, Meningism, Positional headache, Triggered by Valsalva, HIV/Cancer, or fever → MRI/CT brain mandatory
MRI brain — normal in typical migraine; rules out structural causes (tumour, AVM, aneurysm, Chiari)
MRI with MRV — if cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST) suspected (headache + visual + papilloedema + young woman on OCP)
NO ROUTINE IMAGING needed for typical migraine meeting ICHD-3 criteria with normal neurological examination
BLOOD TESTS (acute attack in ER)
CRP, ESR — temporal arteritis (>50 years, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness)
generic_medicines: ACUTE ATTACK TREATMENT (stratified by severity)
MILD-MODERATE ATTACKS
Aspirin 900–1000 mg + Metoclopramide 10 mg (evidence-based; highly effective; Metoclopramide reduces nausea and improves absorption)
Ibuprofen 400–800 mg (first-line NSAID for acute migraine)
Naproxen 500–1000 mg
Paracetamol 1000 mg (if NSAIDs contraindicated — less effective)
MODERATE-SEVERE ATTACKS (Triptans — 5-HT1B/1D agonists)
Sumatriptan 50–100 mg oral (first triptan; gold standard) / 6 mg SC injection (fastest onset) / 20 mg nasal spray
Rizatriptan 5–10 mg oral (faster absorption; ODT wafer for vomiting)
Zolmitriptan 2.5–5 mg oral or nasal
Almotriptan 12.5 mg (fewer AEs, good tolerability)
ANTI-EMETICS (adjunct)
Metoclopramide 10 mg IV/IM/oral
Domperidone 10 mg oral
Prochlorperazine 12.5 mg IM (effective acute treatment in ER)
ayurvedic_medicines: CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — reduces headache frequency; anti-stress; neuroprotective
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — stress-reduction (major migraine trigger); adaptogen; 300–600 mg/day
Butterbur (Petasites hybridus) — strong clinical trial evidence for migraine prevention (comparable to pharmacotherapy); uncommon in India but relevant
Feverfew (Tanacetum parthenium) — clinical evidence for migraine prevention; inhibits platelet aggregation and 5-HT release; 50–150 mg/day dried leaf
Shatavari — hormonal balance (beneficial for menstrual migraine); adaptogen
Triphala — digestive and liver support; reduces inflammatory triggers
Peppermint oil (Mentha piperita) — topical 10% peppermint oil on forehead: RCT evidence comparable to Paracetamol for tension-type and mild migraine
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Shirashooladi Vajra Ras — classical Ayurvedic preparation specifically for Shiro Shoola (headache disorders)
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors PREVALENCE Migraine prevalence 14.2% in India (approximately 175 million people) — one of world's highest burdens Women 3x more common than men (hormonal triggers — menstrual migraine in 50% of female migraineurs) TRIGGERS (common in India) Skipped meals — fasting triggers are common in India (religious fasting — Navratri, Ekadashi) Sleep irregularity — common with irregular shift work, late-night social events Strong sunlight and heat — significant tr
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations MEDICATION OVERUSE HEADACHE — DIAGNOSE AND BREAK THE CYCLE: MOH is the most common cause of chronic daily headache in India and the most common reason for preventive therapy failure. ANY patient using analgesics (aspirin, ibuprofen, paracetamol) ≥15 days/month or triptans ≥1

diseases.name: Multiple Sclerosis
tests: REVISED McDONALD CRITERIA 2017 (space + time dissemination)
DIS: ≥2 different CNS locations on MRI (periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal) OR ≥2 clinical attacks involving different CNS regions
DIT: ≥2 attacks OR simultaneous gadolinium-enhancing and non-enhancing lesions OR new T2 lesion on follow-up MRI
MRI BRAIN AND SPINE — CORNERSTONE
T2/FLAIR: Periventricular (ovoid; perpendicularly oriented = Dawson's fingers), juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal lesions
Gadolinium-enhancing: Active lesions (blood-brain barrier breakdown; new/acute lesions enhance; activity marker)
Spinal cord MRI: Focal lesion ≤2 vertebral segments (partial cord); myelitis
MRI spine T2 criterion: Long-segment cord lesion >3 levels suggests NMOSD not MS
VISUAL EVOKED POTENTIALS (VEP)
Prolonged P100 latency with preserved waveform — demyelinated optic nerve; confirms subclinical optic neuritis; helps DIS
generic_medicines: ACUTE RELAPSE TREATMENT
IV Methylprednisolone 1 g/day for 3–5 days — shortens relapse duration (does NOT alter long-term outcome)
Oral Methylprednisolone 500 mg/day equivalent — non-inferior to IV in PROMISE trial
DISEASE MODIFYING THERAPIES (DMT) — prevent relapse and slow disability accumulation
FIRST-LINE (injectable)
Interferon beta-1a (Avonex/Rebif) 30 mcg IM weekly / 44 mcg SC 3x/week — reduce relapse rate 30%
Interferon beta-1b (Betaferon) 250 mcg SC alternate days
Glatiramer acetate 20 mg SC daily — immune modulation; safe in pregnancy
FIRST-LINE (oral)
Dimethyl fumarate (Tecfidera) 240 mg BD — moderate efficacy; flushing, GI AEs; PML risk (rare)
Teriflunomide 14 mg/day — oral; moderate efficacy; teratogenic
HIGH-EFFICACY DMTs (2nd line / aggressive RRMS)
Natalizumab 300 mg IV monthly (anti-VLA-4; 68% relapse reduction; PML risk with JC virus positivity — requires JC antibody index monitoring)
Ocrelizumab 600 mg IV 6-monthly (anti-CD20; also first-line in Primary Progressive MS; hepatitis B reactivation screen mandatory)
Cladribine (Mavenclad) 3.5 mg/kg total oral — 2-year pulse treatment; high efficacy
ayurvedic_medicines: CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — immunomodulator (Th1/Th2 balancing); neuroprotective; anti-inflammatory; adaptogen; 300–600 mg/day
Guduchi / Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulatory; anti-neuroinflammatory; IMPORTANT — Guduchi can theoretically stimulate immunity; caution in autoimmune conditions; use under supervision
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — cognitive and neuroprotective support; reduces MS-associated cognitive decline; 300 mg/day
Shallaki (Boswellia serrata) — potent anti-inflammatory; inhibits 5-LOX (leukotriene pathway); reduces neuroinflammation; 300–600 mg/day BD

Turmeric (Curcumin from Curcuma longa) — anti-inflammatory, antioxidant; curcumin has demonstrable anti-inflammatory effects on Th17 and cytokine pathways; piperine enhances bioavailability; 500–1000 mg/day
Gotu Kola (Centella asiatica) — remyelination-promoting (animal studies); neuroprotective; myelin-associated protein expression
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Ksheerabala Taila — traditionally used for Vata-vyadhi (neuromuscular conditions); Nasya + Abhyanga
Dhanwantara Taila — external use for spasticity, weakness; warm oil massage
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors EPIDEMIOLOGY MS prevalence in India 7–30 per 100,000 — lower than Europe/North America but rising rapidly Younger age of onset in India (peak 25–35 years) — neurologically active reproductive years most affected OPTICOSPINAL MS IN INDIA Neuromyelitis Optica Spectrum Disorder (NMOSD) — anti-AQP4 antibody disease — more common in Asia and India. Mimics MS but different pathology, much more severe relapses (transverse myelitis + optic neuritis), different
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations DISTINGUISH MS FROM NMOSD BEFORE STARTING DMTs: Neuromyelitis Optica Spectrum Disorder (NMOSD) and MOGAD are far more common in India than in Western populations and present very similarly to MS (optic neuritis + myelitis). CRITICAL — many MS DMTs (Interferon-beta, Natalizum

diseases.name: Guillain-Barre Syndrome
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (Brighton Collaboration criteria / Asbury criteria)
Progressive bilateral weakness of limbs (more legs than arms) + ↓/absent deep tendon reflexes
Onset to nadir: days to 4 weeks
NERVE CONDUCTION STUDIES (NCS) / ELECTROMYOGRAPHY (EMG) — CRITICAL
AIDP (demyelinating): Reduced conduction velocity, prolonged distal latencies, conduction block, temporal dispersion, prolonged F-waves
AMAN/AMSAN (axonal): Reduced/absent CMAP with relatively preserved conduction velocity (axon intact in AMAN initially before axonal degeneration)
NCS helps determine subtype and prognosis (AIDP recovers well; AMSAN slower recovery)
CSF ANALYSIS — ALBUMINOCYTOLOGICAL DISSOCIATION
Elevated CSF protein (50–400 mg/dL) with <10 cells/mm3 (WBC) — hallmark of GBS after 1–2 weeks
Normal CSF protein in first week (does NOT rule out GBS early)
generic_medicines: ACUTE IMMUNOTHERAPY — EQUALLY EFFECTIVE (use one, not both)
Intravenous Immunoglobulin (IVIg) 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days (total 2 g/kg) — enhances recovery; neutralises pathogenic antibodies; first-line in India when available
Plasma Exchange (PE / Plasmapheresis) 5 exchanges over 1–2 weeks — removes pathogenic anti-ganglioside antibodies; equally effective as IVIg; requires specialised equipment
NO BENEFIT FROM STEROIDS: Prednisolone / Methylprednisolone has been tested in multiple trials and does NOT improve GBS outcomes — may be marginally harmful. Do NOT give corticosteroids for GBS.
SUPPORTIVE CARE (CRUCIAL)
Heparin 5000 IU SC BD or Low Molecular Weight Heparin — DVT prophylaxis (bed-bound + paralysed patients extremely high DVT/PE risk)
Bladder catheterisation — if urinary retention
NG tube — if bulbar weakness and aspiration risk
Morphine / Gabapentin / Carbamazepine — pain management (GBS pain severe and undertreated)
MECHANICAL VENTILATION
Intubation and ICU mechanical ventilation for respiratory failure — supportive; no specific drugs
AUTONOMIC
Short-acting antihypertensives (IV Labetalol, Esmolol) — hypertension
IV fluids + Noradrenaline — hypotension
Atropine — symptomatic bradycardia
janaushadhi_medicines: Methylprednisolone injection (NOT for GBS; included for completeness — NOT indicated)
NOTE: IVIg and Plasmapheresis are not available at Jan Aushadhi — these are hospital-level expensive treatments
IVIg 5 g vial (private market estimate): Rs.4500–6000 per vial (10 vials required)
ayurvedic_medicines: SUPPORT IN RECOVERY PHASE ONLY (not acute treatment)
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — nerve regeneration support; neroprotective; promotes axonal regrowth; anti-inflammatory; 300–600 mg/day
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — neuroprotective in recovery phase; 300 mg/day
Bala (Sida cordifolia) — traditional Ayurvedic herb for Vatashula (nerve pain) and muscle weakness; nervine tonic; 9–18 g powder/day
Ashwagandha + Bala combination — classical Vatavyadhi (neurological weakness) treatment
Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulator in recovery phase; general health restoration
OILS FOR NERVE REHABILITATION
Ksheerabala Taila — Abhyanga daily oil massage; classical neurological recovery oil; reduces stiffness and promotes limb movement
Karpasasthyadi Taila — traditional formulation for nerve weakness and paralysis
Dhanwantara Taila — classical Vata-vyadhi oil; neuroprotective massage
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors PRECEDING INFECTIONS (Antecedent event in 70% of GBS) Campylobacter jejuni — most common trigger globally and in India (25–40%); particularly for axonal subtypes (AMAN/AMSAN); preceding diarrhoea 1–3 weeks; contaminated water and chicken, both extremely common in India CMV (Cytomegalovirus) — young adults EBV (Epstein-Barr virus) Influenza, SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19) — well-documented GBS trigger Zika virus — epidemic-associated GBS outbreak (Brazil 2015–16
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations DO NOT GIVE STEROIDS IN GBS: Multiple RCTs (Dutch Guillain-Barré trial, others) have demonstrated that corticosteroids (Prednisolone, Methylprednisolone) provide no benefit in GBS and may slightly worsen outcomes. In India, where GI and systemic steroid complications are com

diseases.name: Myasthenia Gravis
tests: CLINICAL TESTS
Ice pack test — apply ice to ptotic eye for 2 minutes; ptosis improves in MG by ≥2 mm (sensitivity 80%); myasthenia-specific; easy, bedside, immediate
Fatigability test — sustained upgaze for 1 minute; ptosis increases; repeated deltoid testing — weakness worsens

Tensilon (Edrophonium) test — IV edrophonium 2 mg test dose then 8 mg; dramatic improvement in ptosis/diplopia within 30–60 seconds; SHORT-ACTING (90 seconds); CARDIAC MONITORING required (bradycardia/asystole risk); keep atropine 0.6 mg IV ready
NEUROPHYSIOLOGY
Repetitive Nerve Stimulation (RNS) — 3 Hz; decremental response >10% in CMAP = abnormal; sensitivity 50–80% generalised MG
Single Fibre EMG (SFEMG) — increased jitter; most sensitive test (95%); specialist investigation
ANTIBODY TESTS — CONFIRMATORY
Anti-AChR antibody (anti-acetylcholine receptor) — positive in 85% generalised MG; 50% ocular MG; GOLD STANDARD
Anti-MuSK antibody — positive in 40% of seronegative generalised MG; associated with severe bulbar/respiratory disease; female-predominant; different treatment response
generic_medicines: SYMPTOMATIC TREATMENT

Pyridostigmine (Neostigmine methyl sulphate analog) 30–120 mg every 4–6 hours orally — acetylcholinesterase inhibitor; first-line symptomatic; dose titrated to symptoms; max ~480 mg/day to avoid cholinergic crisis
IMMUNOSUPPRESSIVE THERAPY
Prednisolone 10–60 mg/day or alternate day dosing — most widely used; start LOW and increase slowly to avoid paradoxical worsening; long-term use with bone protection (Calcium + Vit D, Alendronate)
Azathioprine 2–3 mg/kg/day — first-line steroid-sparing agent; onset 6–18 months; check TPMT before starting; monitor CBC, LFT
Mycophenolate mofetil 1000–2000 mg/day — alternative to Azathioprine
Cyclosporine 3–5 mg/kg/day — second-line; renal toxicity monitoring
MYASTHENIC CRISIS — ACUTE
IVIg 2 g/kg over 5 days — short-term rapid improvement
Plasma exchange (PE) 5 exchanges — equivalent to IVIg; faster onset; preferred if anti-MuSK positive
Mechanical ventilation if VC <20 mL/kg
AVOID PRECIPITATING DRUGS (see India-Specific section)
THYMECTOMY
Surgical — for thymoma (mandatory) and AChR+ generalised MG <65 years (MGTX trial): improves 3-year remission rate
Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) preferred
janaushadhi_medicines: Calcium + Vitamin D3 tablet (steroid bone protection)
ayurvedic_medicines: CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — neuroprotective, anti-neuroinflammatory; adaptogen; may modulate neuromuscular junction; 300–600 mg/day
Bala (Sida cordifolia) — classical Ayurvedic nerve and muscle tonic; specifically indicated for vatavyadhi (neuromuscular disease); improves muscle strength; 9–18 g powder/day or extract
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — neurotransmitter modulation; neuroprotective; cognitive support; 300 mg/day
Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus) — immune modulation; adaptogen; helpful as adjunct in autoimmune myasthenic states
Guduchi / Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulator (CAUTION: in autoimmune conditions, stimulating immunity may theoretically worsen disease — careful use at low dose under supervision)
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Ashwagandha Churna + Bala combination — Vatashaman (nerve-muscle restoration); classical Rasayana pairing for neuromuscular weakness
Ksheerabala Taila — daily abhyanga (oil massage) and oral; neuroprotective Ksheerabala number 7, 21, 101 preparations (progressively processed)
Dhanwantara Taila — traditional oil for all neurological and musculoskeletal conditions
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors PREVALENCE Estimated 60,000–100,000 cases in India; likely significant underdiagnosis Mean age of onset bimodal: young women 20–30 years, older men 60–70 years DRUGS TO AVOID IN MG — EXTREMELY IMPORTANT IN INDIA Aminoglycosides (Gentamicin, Amikacin, Streptomycin, Kanamycin) — widely used in India for TB, UTI, sepsis; precipitate myasthenic crisis Fluoroquinolones (Ciprofloxacin, Moxifloxacin) — very widely prescribed in India; can worsen NMJ transmiss
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations DRUG SAFETY — MEDICATIONS TO ABSOLUTELY AVOID: Every MG patient must receive a written list of drugs to avoid. Most dangerous in India: Aminoglycosides (Gentamicin, Amikacin) — widely used for TB (Streptomycin), UTI, and sepsis; may precipitate fatal myasthenic crisis. Cipro

diseases.name: Huntingtons Disease
tests: GENETIC TESTING — DEFINITIVE DIAGNOSIS
CAG Repeat Analysis (HTT gene, chromosome 4): Normal ≤26 repeats; Intermediate 27–35; Reduced Penetrance 36–39; Full Penetrance ≥40 (virtually 100% develop HD if live long enough)
Sensitivity 100% for full penetrance mutation; specificity 100% — no further confirmation needed with positive genetic test
CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS
Chorea + unequivocal family history (autosomal dominant) = clinical HD
Unified Huntington's Disease Rating Scale (UHDRS) — motor, cognitive, behavioural, functional capacity subscales
PSYCHOMETRIC TESTING
MMSE, MoCA — cognitive screening; neuropsychological battery (Symbol Digit Modalities Test — most sensitive early cognitive marker)
NEUROIMAGING
MRI brain — progressive bicaudate ratio increase; caudate nucleus atrophy on coronal T1; putamen atrophy; cortical atrophy
generic_medicines: NO DISEASE-MODIFYING TREATMENT EXISTS for HD. All current pharmacotherapy is SYMPTOMATIC.
CHOREA SUPPRESSION

Tetrabenazine 12.5–100 mg/day (VMAT2 inhibitor; most effective anti-choreic agent; FDA/EMA approved; available in India; AE: depression, parkinsonism, sedation, QTc; CONTRAINDICATED with MAO-inhibitors)
Deutetrabenazine 12–48 mg/day (improved tolerability; less available in India)
Haloperidol 0.5–5 mg/day (dopamine antagonist; effective for chorea; also treats psychosis; extrapyramidal side effects)
Clonazepam 0.5–2 mg (mild chorea; anxiety; dystonia)
Amantadine 100–300 mg/day (modest anti-choreic; augments tetrabenazine)
PSYCHIATRIC SYMPTOMS
Depression: Sertraline 50–200 mg, Escitalopram 10–20 mg, Mirtazapine 15–45 mg (avoid SNRI/bupropion = seizure risk; avoid TCA in chorea)
Anxiety: SSRIs; Buspirone 15–60 mg/day (anxiolytic without sedation)
Irritability/aggression: SSRIs, Clonazepam, Quetiapine
Psychosis: Low-dose antipsychotics (Quetiapine, Haloperidol — also treats chorea)
Apathy: No reliably effective treatment; Methylphenidate may help
SLEEP
Mirtazapine, Melatonin, Clonazepam — sleep disturbance in HD
janaushadhi_medicines: High-protein dietary supplements (from Jan Aushadhi or PMJAY nutritional schemes)
ayurvedic_medicines: CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — cognitive neuroprotector; anti-stress; modest evidence for slowing cognitive decline in neurodegeneration; 300–600 mg/day
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — potent neuroprotective (mitochondrial support), anti-neuroinflammatory; reduces oxidative stress which contributes to HD striatal neurotoxicity; 300–600 mg/day
Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — antioxidant, anti-inflammatory; general neuroprotective
Shankhapushpi — nervine tonic; reduces psychiatric symtom burden adjunctively
Shatavari — adaptogen; general vitality; nutritional support
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Brahmi Ghrita — neuroprotective ghee; classical Medhya Rasayana
Saraswatarishta — nervine tonic; cognitive and mood support
Manasamitra Vatakam — classical compound for psychiatric and neurological conditions
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors EPIDEMIOLOGY HD prevalence estimated 1–5 per 100,000 in India; lower than European populations (CAG repeat length and founder effects differ) Pan-ethnic — all Indian communities affected AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT — 50% inheritance risk Each child of affected parent has 50% risk; equal sex distribution New (de novo) mutations rare — almost all cases familial ANTICIPATION CAG repeat expansions tend to increase in subsequent generations (anticipation) — earlier
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations GENETIC TESTING REQUIRES PRE- AND POST-TEST COUNSELLING PROTOCOL: Predictive genetic testing for Huntington's in at-risk individuals (parent affected) must follow the International Huntington Association and European HD Network protocol — minimum 2–3 counselling sessions bef

diseases.name: Autism Spectrum Disorder
tests: ASD IS A CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS — no blood test, brain scan, or genetic test definitively diagnoses ASD
DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA (DSM-5 / ICD-11)

DSM-5: Social communication deficits (Domain 1) across multiple contexts + restricted/repetitive behaviours (≥2 of Domain 2) + symptoms present in early developmental period + cause clinically significant impairment
STANDARDISED DIAGNOSTIC TOOLS
ADOS-2 (Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule, Second Edition) — gold standard semi-structured observational assessment; 5 modules across ages; requires trained examiner
ADI-R (Autism Diagnostic Interview-Revised) — structured parent interview; covers developmental history; gold standard with ADOS-2
CARS-2 (Childhood Autism Rating Scale) — widely used, practical clinical tool; available Indian versions
M-CHAT-R/F (Modified Checklist for Autism in Toddlers) — 16–30 month screening tool; widely used in India
Developmental Behaviour Checklist (DBC) — co-occurring mental health
DEVELOPMENTAL AND COGNITIVE ASSESSMENT
generic_medicines: THERE IS NO MEDICATION THAT TREATS THE CORE FEATURES OF ASD (social communication, repetitive behaviours). Pharmacotherapy is for ASSOCIATED/CO-OCCURRING CONDITIONS ONLY.
IRRITABILITY, AGGRESSION, SELF-INJURY
Risperidone 0.25–3 mg/day — FDA-approved for ASD-related irritability in children 5–16 years; efficacy well-established; metabolic side effects (weight gain, hyperprolactinaemia), EPS
Aripiprazole 2–15 mg/day — FDA-approved for ASD irritability; less metabolic and prolactin AEs than Risperidone
ADHD CO-OCCURRING
Methylphenidate 0.3–1 mg/kg/day — effective for ADHD in ASD; increases tics and irritability at higher rates than neurotypical ADHD; start low
Atomoxetine 1.2 mg/kg/day — NRI; alternative to methylphenidate; fewer tic issues; QTc monitoring
ANXIETY
Fluoxetine 5–20 mg/day (SSRIs — modest evidence for anxiety in ASD; some RCT evidence; gastrointestinal activation at initiation)
Sertraline 25–100 mg/day
Buspirone 5–30 mg/day
SLEEP
Melatonin 0.5–10 mg at bedtime — first-line; excellent evidence in ASD; safe; improves sleep onset latency
REPETITIVE BEHAVIOURS / OCD-LIKE
Fluoxetine / Fluvoxamine — some RCT evidence for reducing repetitive behaviours at higher doses; not robustly replicated
ayurvedic_medicines: CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — most clinically studied herb for neurodevelopmental conditions; multiple RCTs show improvement in attention, memory, behaviour in children; 150–300 mg/day in children
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — reduces anxiety, improves adaptive functioning; neuroprotective; 125–300 mg/day in children
Mandukaparni / Gotu Kola (Centella asiatica) — traditionally prescribed for intellectual development in children; Medhya Rasayana; nerve growth factor stimulation; cognitive support
Shankhapushpi (Convolvulus pluricaulis) — classical Medhya Rasayana for intellectual development; memory enhancement
Yashtimadhu (Glycyrrhiza glabra / Licorice) — anti-inflammatory, neuroprotective; used in pediatric formulations
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Mentat syrup/tablet (Himalaya) — Brahmi + Bacopa + Mandukaparni combination; commonly used in India for ASD and developmental conditions; RCT evidence for ADHD symptoms
Saraswatarishta or Brahmi syrup — sweet traditional formulations; suitable for children
Manasamitra Vatakam — classical compound; psychiatry and neurology conditions
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors EPIDEMIOLOGY Prevalence estimates: 1 in 68–100 children in India based on limited population surveys (Goa study, AIIMS data) — likely underestimate Recognition improving but vast diagnosis gap persists DIAGNOSIS DELAY IN INDIA Average age of ASD diagnosis in India: 4–6 years; vs 2–3 years in high-income countries Causes of delay: familial attribution to late talking, maternal dietary/health explanations, pressure to NOT label child, lack of development
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations EARLY IDENTIFICATION AND INTERVENTION IS THE ONLY PROVEN INTERVENTION FOR CORE ASD: The single most impactful intervention for ASD is EARLY intensive behavioural and developmental intervention — ABA (Applied Behaviour Analysis), ESDM (Early Start Denver Model), speech therap

diseases.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
tests: SLICC 2012 CRITERIA (need ≥4 of 11 clinical + immunological criteria OR biopsy-proven lupus nephritis WITH ANA or anti-dsDNA)
ACR/EULAR 2019 CRITERIA — more accurate; entry criterion ANA, then additive scoring across domains
ANA (ANTINUCLEAR ANTIBODY) — SCREENING TEST
ANA IF (Indirect Immunofluorescence) ≥1:80 — entry criterion; highly sensitive (95%) but NOT specific
Homogeneous nuclear pattern = most common in SLE
SPECIFIC SLE ANTIBODIES
Anti-dsDNA (double-stranded DNA) — highly specific (95%) for SLE; titres correlate with disease activity (especially nephritis); useful for monitoring
Anti-Sm (Smith) — pathognomonic for SLE; specific (99%) but less sensitive (25%); does not correlate with activity
Anti-Ro/SSA, Anti-La/SSB — Sjogren's overlap, neonatal lupus, SCLE, congenital heart block
Antiphospholipid antibodies (aCL, anti-beta2GP1, Lupus Anticoagulant) — 30% SLE; thrombosis and obstetric risk
generic_medicines: BACKGROUND THERAPY — ALL SLE PATIENTS

Hydroxychloroquine (HCQ) 200–400 mg/day (max 5 mg/kg/day) — mandatory unless contraindicated; reduces flares, organ damage, mortality; requires annual ophthalmology screening (retinopathy risk at >5 years or >200g cumulative dose)
MILD-MODERATE SLE
NSAIDs (Naproxen, Ibuprofen) — arthralgia, serositis, fever; use cautiously in renal disease, APS
Topical sunscreens (SPF ≥50) — photosensitivity management; lifestyle cornerstone
Prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day — acute flares; taper over weeks to months
Azathioprine 1.5–2.5 mg/kg/day — mild-moderate SLE maintenance; skin, joints, haem, serositis
Methotrexate 15–25 mg/week — skin and joint manifestations; folate supplementation
LUPUS NEPHRITIS (class III/IV/V)
INDUCTION (first 3–6 months)
Pulse Methylprednisolone 500–1000 mg IV ×3 days then oral Prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day
Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) 2–3 g/day — first-line induction (especially Black, Hispanic, Asian patients — superior to CYC in ALMS study)
IV Cyclophosphamide (Euro-Lupus regimen: 500 mg every 2 weeks ×6 — less toxic) or NIH regimen (0.75–1 g/m2 monthly ×6) — alternative
MAINTENANCE (after remission)
MMF 1–2 g/day or Azathioprine 1.5–2 mg/kg/day for ≥3 years
ayurvedic_medicines: CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS

Guduchi / Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulator; anti-inflammatory; reduces ESR, CRP; traditionally used for Jwara (autoimmune fever); CAUTION: may stimulate immunity — use at physiological doses only; 500 mg/day
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — immunomodulatory (bidirectional); reduces cortisol; reduces autoimmune inflammation; 300–600 mg/day
Turmeric / Curcumin (Curcuma longa) — potent NF-kB inhibitor; anti-inflammatory in SLE animal models; inhibits IL-6, TNF-alpha; 500 mg BD with piperine
Shallaki (Boswellia serrata) — anti-inflammatory (5-LOX inhibitor); reduces joint pain, ESR; 300–500 mg BD
Neem (Azadirachta indica) — anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory; skin disease application; 250–500 mg/day
Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia) — Rakta-shodhana (blood purification); skin conditions; anti-inflammatory; 500 mg BD
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Kaisora Guggulu — Rakta-dosha and skin/joint conditions; anti-inflammatory
Aarogyavardhini Vati — liver-protective, anti-inflammatory; hepatic health during immunosuppression
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors EPIDEMIOLOGY SLE prevalence in India approximately 3–4 per 100,000; 9:1 female-to-male ratio; onset 15–45 years Darker skin = higher SLE risk and more severe presentation (anti-dsDNA titres higher, renal involvement more common) INFECTIONS Indian SLE patients have higher infection risk — Urinary tract infections (frequent), pneumonia, TB (TB reactivation on immunosuppression — critically important) Infections are the most common cause of death in India
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations HYDROXYCHLOROQUINE IS MANDATORY IN ALL SLE: HCQ 200–400 mg/day must be prescribed to virtually every SLE patient unless there is a specific contraindication (retinopathy, G6PD deficiency causing haemolysis). It reduces overall mortality by 30–40%, flare frequency, organ dama

diseases.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
tests: ACR/EULAR 2010 CLASSIFICATION CRITERIA (score ≥6 = RA)
Joint involvement (score 0–5) + Serology (RF/ACPA; 0–3) + Acute phase reactants (ESR/CRP; 0–1) + Duration ≥6 weeks (0–1)
SEROLOGY — KEY TESTS
Rheumatoid Factor (RF) IgM — positive in 70–80% of RA; also positive in many other conditions (low specificity); high titre RF = more erosive, extra-articular disease
Anti-CCP (anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide) / ACPA — high specificity (95-98%) for RA; present years before clinical onset; predicts erosive disease; Gold standard serology for RA
ANA — ANA positive in 30% RA; if strongly positive consider SLE/MCTD overlap
INFLAMMATORY MARKERS
ESR (erythrocyte sedimentation rate) — elevated; correlates with activity
CRP (C-reactive protein) — more sensitive, faster response to activity changes; monitors treatment response
HAEMATOLOGY
generic_medicines: TREAT TO TARGET: Start csDMARD immediately at diagnosis. Assess every 4–8 weeks. Escalate if not at target (remission/LDA) by 3–6 months.
FIRST-LINE csDMARDs
Methotrexate 7.5–25 mg/week (single first-line DMARD; folic acid 5 mg/week mandatory; monitor CBC, LFT; hold in infection, surgery; contraindicated in pregnancy)
Hydroxychloroquine 200–400 mg/day (mild RA; combination partner; cardioprotective benefits)
Sulfasalazine 500 mg–3 g/day (moderate RA; useful in combination)
Leflunomide 10–20 mg/day (equally effective to MTX; teratogenic; washout with cholestyramine if pregnancy needed)
COMBINATION csDMARD (for moderate-severe RA or inadequate MTX response)
MTX + HCQ + SSZ (triple therapy) — RCT-proven efficacy comparable to biologic in MTX-naive RA (O'Dell trial)
BIOLOGIC DMARDs (after csDMARD failure)
TNF inhibitors — Etanercept, Adalimumab, Infliximab, Golimumab, Certolizumab; reduce joint destruction, CV risk; TB screen mandatory
Abatacept (CTLA4-Ig) — T-cell co-stimulation blocker; lower TB risk than TNF-i
Rituximab (anti-CD20) — second-line biologics; cost-effective; Indian biosimilar available; monthly infusions ×2
Tocilizumab (IL-6 receptor inhibitor) — effective for RA and systemic features; can mask fever of infection
JAK INHIBITORS (targeted synthetic DMARDs)
Baricitinib 2–4 mg/day, Tofacitinib 5 mg BD, Upadacitinib 15 mg/day — oral; effective; thrombosis, HZV reactivation, lipid risk; not first-line
janaushadhi_medicines: Pantoprazole 40 mg tablet (GI protection with NSAIDs/steroids)
ayurvedic_medicines: CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS

Shallaki (Boswellia serrata) — potent anti-inflammatory (5-LOX inhibitor, inhibits leukotriene synthesis); multiple RCTs in RA showing pain and swelling reduction; 400–750 mg extract BD; Sallaki branded tablet widely available
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — anti-arthritic properties in multiple animal models; reduces RF, ESR; adaptogen for RA-related fatigue; 300–600 mg/day
Guggulu (Commiphora wightii / Mukul Myrrh) — anti-inflammatory, anti-arthritic; multiple clinical trials; the cornered of Ayurvedic arthritis management
Turmeric / Curcumin — anti-inflammatory; curcumin 500–1500 mg/day with piperine; pain and swelling reduction RCTs in RA; inhibits TNF-alpha, IL-1, IL-6
Nirgundi (Vitex negundo) — external application as leaf pack and oil for joint pain; anti-inflammatory
Eranda (Ricinus communis / Castor oil) — traditional Vatavyadhi (joint disease) treatment; Eranda Paka
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Yogaraj Guggulu — widely used tablet for all forms of joint disease; anti-inflammatory, Vata-pacifying
Mahayograja Guggulu — stronger; severe arthritis
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors PREVALENCE RA prevalence 0.5–1% in India; approximately 5–7 million patients Women 3–4x more common; onset 35–55 years TREATMENT GAP Huge treatment gap — early RA often diagnosed late in India; rural patients present with established deformities Treat-to-target (T2T) strategy underimplemented; rheumatology workforce limited TUBERCULOSIS AND DMARDS Methotrexate — mild increase in infection risk; pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia risk (rare) Leflunomide —
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations EARLY AGGRESSIVE TREATMENT IS JOINT-SAVING: Joint damage in RA begins within weeks of onset and is irreversible. The 'window of opportunity' for treatment response is the first 3–6 months. Every month of diagnostic delay = permanent joint damage. Any patient in India with sy

diseases.name: Ankylosing Spondylitis
tests: ASAS CLASSIFICATION CRITERIA (axial SpA)
Age <45 + chronic back pain ≥3 months + EITHER: (sacroiliitis on imaging + ≥1 SpA feature) OR (HLA-B27 positive + ≥2 SpA features)
ASSESSMET TOOLS
BASDAI (Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Disease Activity Index) — patient-reported; 0–10; ≥4 = active disease
BASFI (Bath AS Functional Index) — functional impairment
Modified Schober test — lumbar flexion assessment
Chest expansion measurement — costovertebral joint involvement
IMAGING
X-ray pelvis (SIJ views) — sacroiliitis Grading I–IV; grade ≥II bilateral or ≥III unilateral = radiographic criterion; normal X-ray does NOT exclude early AS
MRI sacroiliac joints (STIR sequence) — bone marrow oedema = active inflammation; detects AS years before X-ray changes; crucial for nr-axSpA diagnosis
generic_medicines: NSAIDs — FIRST-LINE (for pain and inflammation)
Naproxen 250–500 mg BD, Ibuprofen 400–800 mg TDS, Diclofenac 75 mg BD — continuous use (not just PRN) reduces bone formation/progression in AS
Aceclofenac 100 mg BD (popular in India)
Celecoxib 200 mg OD-BD (COX-2 inhibitor; better GI safety; evidence for slowing radiographic progression in AS)
ALL NSAIDs: Gastroprotection with PPI mandatory; assess CV and renal risk
BIOLOGICAL DMARDs — TNF INHIBITORS (after failure of ≥2 NSAIDs at adequate dose)
Adalimumab 40 mg SC every 2 weeks (widely used; Indian biosimilar available)
Etanercept 25 mg SC twice/week or 50 mg SC once/week
Infliximab 5 mg/kg IV at 0, 2, 6 weeks then every 6–8 weeks
Golimumab 50 mg SC monthly
Certolizumab pegol 200 mg SC every 2 weeks
IL-17A INHIBITORS (better for skin psoriasis; comparable spine efficacy to TNF-i; effective in TNF-failure)
Secukinumab 150–300 mg SC every 4 weeks (after loading)
Ixekizumab 80 mg SC every 4 weeks (after loading)
JAK INHIBITORS
ayurvedic_medicines: CLASSICAL FORMULATIONS
Shallaki (Boswellia serrata) — strong evidence for ankylosing spondylitis pain and stiffness; 5-LOX anti-inflammatory; 400–800 mg BD; Shallaki tablets widely available
Guggulu (Commiphora wightii) — anti-inflammatory for Amavata and Vata-Rakta; joint conditions; Yogaraj Guggulu / Mahayograja Guggulu
Eranda (Castor oil / Ricinus communis) — Eranda Paka or castor oil basti specifically for lumbosacral Vata disorders including sacral back pain; 10–20 mL castor oil in warm milk at night
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — anti-inflammatory; adaptogenic; reduces morning stiffness; 300–600 mg/day
Nirgundi (Vitex negundo) — anti-inflammatory; leaf poultice externally for spine and SIJ pain; Nirgundi oil for Abhyanga
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Yogaraj Guggulu — standard preparation for all arthritis; Trikatu (digestive) + Guggulu combination
Simhanada Guggulu — Ama pachana; inflammatory arthritis
Rasnasaptaka Kwatha — decoction formulation; anti-inflammatory; lumbosacral disease classical use
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors EPIDEMIOLOGY AS prevalence India approximately 0.1–0.5%; predominantly men (3:1); onset 20–30 years HLA-B27 frequency: 6–8% in Indian general population vs 8–10% in white Europeans HLA-B27 in AS patients in India: 75–88% DIAGNOSIS DELAY Average delay from symptom onset to AS diagnosis in India: 8–10 years Chronic back pain extremely common in India; almost always attributed to muscular or disc disease; IBP criteria almost never applied in primary care
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations EXERCISE IS NOT OPTIONAL — IT IS TREATMENT: In AS, physical therapy and daily spinal exercises are as evidence-based as NSAIDs for maintaining spinal mobility and preventing fusion. Unlike RA where rest during flares is appropriate, in AS patients must exercise even through

diseases.name: Sjogrens Syndrome
tests: ACR/EULAR 2016 CRITERIA — CLASSIFICATION
Entry criteria: dry eye or dry mouth symptoms
Lip biopsy (minor salivary gland biopsy) focal lymphocytic sialadenitis, focus score ≥1 = 3 points (weight 3)
Anti-Ro/SSA positive = 3 points
Ocular Staining Score ≥5 = 1 point
Schirmer test ≤5 mm/5 min = 1 point
Unstimulated salivary flow ≤0.1 mL/min = 1 point
Score ≥4 = classifiable as primary Sjogren's
AUTOANTIBODIES
Anti-Ro/SSA — 60–70% positive (major Sjogren's autoantibody)
generic_medicines: SICCA MANAGEMENT (LOCAL)
Artificial tears (carboxymethylcellulose 0.5–1%, hydroxypropyl methylcellulose) — multiple times daily; preservative-free preferred for frequent use
Sodium Hyaluronate eye drops — superior viscosity and retention; preferred for severe dry eye
Punctal plugs — ophthalmological procedure; blocks tear drainage to retain moisture
Lubricant eye ointment (Paraffin base) — night-time use
Saliva substitutes / Oral gels — xylitol-based mouth-wash, carboxymethylcellulose oral gel
Pilocarpine 5 mg TDS — muscarinic agonist; stimulates residual salivary and lacrimal function; SE=sweating, bradycardia, nausea; contraindicated in asthma, narrow angle glaucoma
Cevimeline — alternative muscarinic agonist; better tolerated; not widely available India
SYSTEMIC DISEASE
Hydroxychloroquine 200–400 mg/day — reduces arthralgias, fatigue, constitutional symptoms; may reduce lymphoma risk; background Sjogren's therapy
Prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day — acute extraglandular flares (ILD, vasculitis, glomerulonephritis)
Azathioprine 1.5–2.5 mg/kg/day — steroid-sparing; ILD, systemic disease maintenance
Mycophenolate mofetil 1.5–2 g/day — ILD, renal tubular disease
Rituximab — refractory extraglandular Sjogren's (severe ILD, vasculitis, neuropathy, MALT lymphoma); improving evidence
ORAL CARE
ayurvedic_medicines: EXTERNAL EYE CARE
Rose water (Gulab Jal) — organic, preservative-free; eye wash and drops; immediate symptomatic relief for dry eyes in India; widely available and culturally accepted
Triphala Ghrita eye drops / Triphala wash — reduces ocular surface inflammation; Triphala decoction as eye wash — traditional Akshi-tarpana base

Akshi Tarpana (medicated ghee eye pooling) — Panchakarma procedure; ghee (clarified butter = base fat for Ayurvedic eye preparation) pooled around orbit using dough ring; classical treatment for dry eyes, refractory to drops
Saindhavadi Taila for nasal and oral cavity lubrication
INTERNAL
Triphala churna 1–3 g at night — Tridosha balancing; particularly Pitta-Kapha in Srotas (channels); traditional for eye health and mucous membrane health
Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus) — mucosal tonic; reduces dryness of all mucous membranes (Rasayana property); 500 mg–1 g BD; particularly indicated in women
Yashtimadhu (Glycyrrhiza glabra / Licorice) — mucosal moisture; anti-inflammatory; throat, GI, and oral mucosa; 500 mg BD; CAUTION: avoid in hypertension (licorice increases BP)
Guduchi / Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulatory; anti-inflammatory; useful adjunct at physiological doses
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors DIAGNOSIS GAP Sjogren's is significantly under-diagnosed in India; symptoms attributed to ageing, vitamin deficiency, or menopause; delayed diagnosis 5–10 years typical Dry eye extremely common complaint in Indian practice — most attributed to screen use, environment, allergy; Sjogren's not consistently excluded HEAT AND ENVIRONMENT Indian climate (heat, dust, pollution, ceiling fans) dramatically worsens xerophthalmia and xerostomia; patients need ext
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations LYMPHOMA SURVEILLANCE IS ESSENTIAL: Sjogren's carries a 5–10x elevated lifetime risk of MALT lymphoma compared to the general population. High-risk features that must trigger urgent haematological workup: rapidly enlarging salivary gland (especially unilateral), generalised

diseases.name: Systemic Sclerosis
tests: ACR/EULAR 2013 CRITERIA — Total score ≥9 = SSc
Skin thickening of fingers of both hands extending proximal to MCPs (sufficient alone — score 9)
Skin thickening of fingers (sclerodactyly, puffy fingers), digital tip lesions (ulcers, pitting scars), telangiectasia, abnormal nailfold capillaries, PAH/ILD, Raynaud's, SSc-related autoantibody
AUTOANTIBODIES (highly specific SSc antibodies)
Anti-Scl-70 (anti-topoisomerase I) — diffuse SSc; ILD risk; poor prognosis
Anti-centromere antibody (ACA) — limited SSc (CREST); PAH risk; better skin prognosis
Anti-RNA polymerase III — diffuse SSc; SRC risk; malignancy association
ANA — 95%+ positive (speckled or centromere pattern)
NAILFOLD CAPILLAROSCOPY
SSc pattern: dilated, avascular, haemorrhagic capillaries — early, active, late SSc patterns
generic_medicines: RAYNAUD'S AND DIGITAL ULCERS
Nifedipine LA 30–60 mg/day (calcium channel blocker) — first-line vasodilator for Raynaud's; reduces frequency and severity of attacks
Amlodipine 5–10 mg/day — alternative CCB
Sildenafil 25–50 mg BD-TDS — PDE-5 inhibitor; vasodilation; digital ulcer healing; also PAH
Iloprost IV infusion — prostacyclin analogue; severe Raynaud's and digital ulcers; hospitalisation required
Bosentan (endothelin receptor antagonist) — digital ulcer prevention in severe SSc; PAH; teratogenic; monthly LFT required
Mycophenolate mofetil 1.5–3 g/day — first-line SSc-ILD (SLS II trial equivalent to CYC with better tolerance)
Nintedanib 150 mg BD — antifibrotic; slows ILD progression in SSc-ILD (SENSCIS trial); nausea, diarrhoea; hepatotoxicity
Cyclophosphamide IV 600 mg/m2 monthly ×6 — alternative/resource-limited option
Sildenafil 20–50 mg TDS (WHO Group I PAH)
Bosentan 62.5–125 mg BD (endothelin antagonist)
Macitentan 10 mg/day, Riociguat 1–2.5 mg TDS — more potent options
Ambrisentan 5–10 mg/day
GERD AND GI
High-dose PPI (Omeprazole 40 mg BD, Pantoprazole 40 mg BD) — oesophageal disease; continuous long-term
ayurvedic_medicines: RAYNAUD'S AND SKIN — VASCULAR
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — circulatory enhancement; vasodilatory properties at clinical doses; 300 mg/day
Ginkgo biloba — vasodilatory; improves peripheral circulation; small evidence in Raynaud's; 120 mg/day
Arjuna (Terminalia arjuna) — cardiovascular tonic; Hrit Dharaniya (heart-supporting); 500 mg BD
SKIN AND FIBROSIS
Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia) — Rakta-shodhana (blood purification); skin health; anti-fibrotic properties in animal models; 500 mg BD

Kanchanar Guggulu — classically indicated for Gandamala (glandular/nodular conditions), fibrotic conditions, thyroid; anti-fibrotic in traditional use; widely used in India for scleroderma-like skin conditions
Guggulu (Commiphora wightii) — anti-inflammatory and anti-fibrotic; Triphala Guggulu; reduces fibrotic tissue formation
Shilajit (Asphaltum punjabianum) — Rasayana (rejuvenative); mitochondrial support; connective tissue conditions; 250–500 mg/day
INTERNAL MUCOSAL AND GI
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors HEAT AND RAYNAUD'S Indian climate generally protective for severe cold-induced Raynaud's episodes — however air conditioning, cold food from refrigerators, ice water, cold bathing → triggers in urban India Digital ulcers still occur in Indian SSc despite ambient warmth — suggest more prominent vasculopathy component DIAGNOSIS SSc is diagnosed late in India — skin thickening attributed to 'tight skin' or traditional explanations; specialised Rheumatolog
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations SCLERODERMA RENAL CRISIS IS A LIFE-SAVING EMERGENCY: SRC occurs mostly in early diffuse SSc (first 4 years) and presents as sudden severe hypertension (often >200/120 mmHg) + acute kidney injury. Common misconception: steroid doses (>15 mg/day prednisolone) are a major trigg

diseases.name: Polymyositis Dermatomyositis
tests: BOHAN AND PETER CRITERIA (classic; still used)
1. Symmetric proximal muscle weakness
2. Elevated muscle enzymes (CK, LDH, AST, ALT, aldolase)
3. EMG myopathic pattern
4. Muscle biopsy — necrosis, regeneration, inflammatory infiltrate
5. Dermatological features of DM (Gottron's, heliotrope, other rashes)
Definite: 3–4 criteria (+ rash for DM); Probable: 2 criteria (+ rash for DM)
MUSCLE ENZYMES
CK (creatine kinase) — most sensitive; 5–50x normal in active PM; may be normal in DM (especially amyopathic DM)
Aldolase, LDH, AST, ALT — all can be elevated
generic_medicines: FIRST-LINE
Prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day (up to 80 mg/day) — major induction; taper over months as CK normalises and strength improves
Methotrexate 15–25 mg/week (+Folic acid 5 mg/week) — steroid-sparing; skin and muscle
Azathioprine 1.5–2.5 mg/kg/day — steroid-sparing; equally effective to MTX; preferred if ILD present
SEVERE / REFRACTORY PM/DM
IV Methylprednisolone pulse 1 g/day ×3 days — severe acute presentations, rapid respiratory compromise
Mycophenolate mofetil 1.5–2 g/day — ILD-associated DM; refractory muscle disease; better GI tolerance
IV Cyclophosphamide — anti-MDA5 DM-ILD; calcinosis; rapidly progressive ILD
IV Immunoglobulin (IVIG) 2 g/kg over 5 days — effective for muscle and skin; JDM; resistant PM; ProDERM trial data
Rituximab (anti-CD20) — refractory PM/DM; anti-SRP necrotising myopathy; anti-HMGCR statin myopathy; RIM trial
Tacrolimus 2–4 mg/day — particularly for DM-ILD; Japan/India data
ILD TREATMENT (rapidly progressive — especially anti-MDA5)
High-dose steroids + Cyclophosphamide + Tacrolimus triple therapy — anti-MDA5 DM-ILD standard
Tofacitinib — emerging evidence for anti-MDA5 refractory rapidly progressive ILD
CALCINOSIS (limited options)
ayurvedic_medicines: MUSCLE STRENGTHENING (RASAYANA)

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — Balya (strength-promoting); 'Mamsa dhatu' (muscle tissue) strengthener; anti-inflammatory and anti-fatigue; 300–600 mg/day; multiple clinical trials for muscle mass and strength
Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus) — Balya, Rasayana; muscle strength and endurance; 500 mg–1 g BD
Bala (Sida cordifolia) — Balya for muscular weakness; 500 mg BD
Vidarikanda (Pueraria tuberosa) — Rasayana herb; muscle mass; 3–6 g/day
Kapikacchu / Mucuna pruriens — natural source of L-DOPA precursor; muscle and neuromuscular function; 5 g/day
ANTI-INFLAMMATORY
Shallaki (Boswellia serrata) — anti-inflammatory; muscle and joint pain in inflammatory myopathy adjunctive; 400–750 mg BD
Turmeric/Curcumin — anti-inflammatory; NF-kB inhibition; 500–1500 mg/day
Guggulu (Commiphora wightii) — Mamsa-vaha srotas (muscle channel) anti-inflammatory; Yogaraj Guggulu; Mahayograja Guggulu
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors TUBERCULOSIS DM/PM treatment requires high-dose steroids + steroid-sparing immunosuppressants → TB reactivation extremely common in India Mandatory baseline TB workup (IGRA/Mantoux + CXR) before initiating immunosuppression On cyclophosphamide/azathioprine — annual review for TB signs; prophylaxis if IGRA positive MALIGNANCY SCREENING IN INDIA Cancer screening resources limited in India; cost of CT + PET limits comprehensive screening Priority India-sp
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations MALIGNANCY SCREENING IS MANDATORY IN DERMATOMYOSITIS: DM is a paraneoplastic condition in approximately 25% of adult cases. Cancer may precede, coincide with, or follow DM diagnosis. Anti-TIF1-gamma (anti-p155) and anti-NXP2 antibodies are particularly associated with malign

diseases.name: Vasculitis Anca
tests: ANCA TESTING
cANCA (cytoplasmic ANCA) pattern IF → anti-PR3 (anti-proteinase-3) by ELISA — GPA (85% sensitivity)
pANCA (perinuclear ANCA) pattern IF → anti-MPO (anti-myeloperoxidase) by ELISA — MPA (60–75% sensitivity); EGPA (40–50%)
ANCA IF + specific ELISA (PR3, MPO) required together — IF alone insufficient
Sensitivity: GPA 85–90%, MPA 70–80%, EGPA 40–50%
RENAL
Urine dipstick, spot PCR, urine microscopy (RBC casts = nephritis)
Serum creatinine, eGFR — trajectory monitoring
Renal biopsy — crescentic GN with pauci-immune pattern (negative/scanty immunofluorescence) = strongly supports AAV; distinguishes from lupus (full-house IF) and anti-GBM
RESPIRATORY
generic_medicines: INDUCTION OF REMISSION
Rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly ×4 or 1 g ×2 (2 weeks apart) — equivalent to CYC; preferred in relapsing AAV, young women (fertility), anti-PR3 GPA; biosimilar available in India
IV Cyclophosphamide 15 mg/kg every 3 weeks ×6 pulses (Euro-Lupus-like) — standard induction; AAV without severe renal (creatinine < 500 micromol/L)
High-dose Prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day + IV Methylprednisolone 1 g/day ×3 (severe/RPGN/diffuse alveolar haemorrhage)
Plasma exchange — DAH (diffuse alveolar haemorrhage) or anti-GBM overlap (controversial for pure RPGN-AAV: PEXIVAS trial negative for plasma exchange in AAV-RPGN)
MAINTENANCE OF REMISSION (12–24+ months)
Rituximab 500 mg every 6 months — superior to Azathioprine for maintenance in GPA/MPA (MAINRITSAN study)
Azathioprine 2 mg/kg/day — standard maintenance alternative; less expensive
Low-dose Prednisolone 5 mg/day — taper over 12–18 months
EGPA-SPECIFIC
Mepolizumab (anti-IL-5 monoclonal) 300 mg SC monthly — approved for EGPA; reduces eosinophil-driven disease; expensive but may permit dose reduction of steroids
RESISTANT/REFRACTORY
IV Immunoglobulin — refractory GPA without nephritis; adjunctive
Mycophenolate mofetil — maintenance alternative (less evidence)
PNEUMOCYSTIS PROPHYLAXIS
ayurvedic_medicines: ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AND VASCULAR

Guduchi / Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulator; anti-inflammatory; particularly useful in reducing systemic inflammation; 500 mg–1 g/day (at physiological dose — not large immunostimulant doses in active vasculitis)
Turmeric / Curcumin — potent NF-kB inhibitor; anti-inflammatory; 500–1500 mg/day with piperine
Punarnava (Boerhavia diffusa) — anti-inflammatory; renal protective; diuretic; extensively studied for renal conditions; 500 mg–1 g BD
Gokshura (Tribulus terrestris) — renal supportive; anti-inflammatory for Mutraghata (urinary tract disorders); 500 mg BD
Varuna (Crataeva nurvala) — renal and urinary tract conditions; 500 mg BD
Shallaki (Boswellia serrata) — anti-inflammatory; reduces systemic vasculitis-related inflammation; 400–750 mg BD
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Punarnavadi Kwatha / Punarnavadi Mandura — renal anti-inflammatory decoction; iron for anaemia of vasculitis; classical for renal conditions (Mutrakrichra)
Gokshuradi Guggulu — renal and urinary anti-inflammatory
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors INFECTIONS MIMICKING AAV TB granulomatosis — pulmonary and ENT TB can mimic GPA perfectly (granulomatous lung disease + sinusitis + haematuria from renal TB); rigorous TB exclusion before diagnosing AAV and starting immunosuppression is critical ANCA may be positive in active TB (false positive AAV serology) Treatment of TB as AAV = missed TB + potentially fatal immunosuppression of active infection Treatment of AAV as TB = untreated vasculitis → renal
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations RPGN IS A NEPHROLOGICAL EMERGENCY — HOURS MATTER: Rapidly Progressive Glomerulonephritis from ANCA vasculitis can progress from normal creatinine to dialysis-requiring renal failure within 2–4 weeks. The key clinical indicator is a rising creatinine with active urine sedimen

diseases.name: Antiphospholipid Syndrome
tests: UPDATED SAPPORO/SYDNEY CRITERIA (2006) — REVISED 2023 ACR/EULAR
Clinical: 1+ thrombosis or obstetric morbidity
Laboratory: 1+ of (Lupus Anticoagulant, aCL IgG/IgM medium-high titre, anti-beta2GP1 IgG/IgM) PRESENT ON 2 OCCASIONS ≥12 WEEKS APART
LABORATORY — ANTIPHOSPHOLIPID ANTIBODIES

Lupus Anticoagulant (LA) — most thrombogenic; paradoxically prolongs clotting tests (APTT) in vitro but causes thrombosis in vivo; tested by DRVVT (dilute Russell viper venom time) + APTT screen + mixing studies + confirm step
Anticardiolipin antibodies (aCL) — IgG/IgM; medium titre (>40 GPL/MPL) or high titre (>80) on ELISA
Anti-beta2-Glycoprotein I antibodies (anti-beta2GP1) — IgG/IgM; ELISA
TRIPLE POSITIVITY (positive for all three) = highest thrombosis risk; DOAC absolutely contraindicated; warfarin mandatory
THROMBOSIS WORKUP
Doppler ultrasound — DVT diagnosis (limbs, portal vein)
generic_medicines: ACUTE THROMBOSIS (venous or arterial)
IV Unfractionated Heparin (UFH) — loading 80 units/kg IV then infusion; monitor APTT 60–100 sec; acute treatment bridge to warfarin
LMWH (Enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SC BD or 1.5 mg/kg once daily) — DVT treatment; bridge to warfarin; preferred in pregnancy
LONG-TERM ANTICOAGULATION
Warfarin (Sodium Warfarin) — MAINSTAY of APS anticoagulation
Venous thrombosis APS: Target INR 2–3
Arterial thrombosis APS: Target INR 2.5–3.5 (or 2–3 with aspirin 100 mg/day)
Triple positive APS: INR 3–4 or INR 2–3 + Aspirin (debate; maintain strict monitoring)
Duration: Lifelong in confirmed APS with thrombosis (high recurrence risk if stopped)

DOAC NOTE: Rivaroxaban, Apixaban, Dabigatran — DO NOT USE in high-risk (triple-positive) APS; inferior outcomes in TRAPS and other trials. May be used only in single-positive, low-risk, venous-only if warfarin not tolerated.
OBSTETRIC APS
Aspirin 75–100 mg/day from pre-conception or first trimester + LMWH (Enoxaparin 40 mg/day prophylactic dose) throughout pregnancy and 6 weeks postpartum
Refractory obstetric APS: HCQ (hydroxychloroquine) add-on may improve outcomes; Prednisolone controversial
CATASTROPHIC APS (CAPS) — EMERGENCY
IV Heparin (immediate, full anticoagulation) + High-dose IV Methylprednisolone 1 g/day ×3 + Plasma exchange ×5 + IVIG 2 g/kg
ayurvedic_medicines: ANTICOAGULANT-SUPPORTIVE HERBS

Ginkgo biloba — antiplatelet properties; improves peripheral circulation; evidence in vascular conditions; 120 mg/day; CAUTION: may increase bleeding if combined with anticoagulants — use under supervision
Arjuna (Terminalia arjuna) — cardioprotective; reduces platelet hyperreactivity in animal models; 500 mg BD of bark powder

Garlic (Allium sativum) — antiplatelet and anti-thrombotic properties; ajoene (component) inhibits platelet aggregation; 600–1200 mg standardised extract/day; CAUTION: anticoagulant interaction — increased bleeding risk with Warfarin
ANTI-INFLAMMATORY (for inflammatory component of APS)
Turmeric / Curcumin — anti-inflammatory; inhibits platelet aggregation at high doses; 500 mg/day; CAUTION: additive antiplatelet effect with anticoagulants
Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulatory; anti-inflammatory; 500 mg/day
FOR LIVEDO RETICULARIS AND SKIN
Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia) — Rakta shodhana; vascular skin conditions; 500 mg BD
IMPORTANT CAUTIONS FOR APS
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors MISDIAGNOSIS AND TREATMENT GAP Recurrent miscarriages and obstetric morbidity in India often attributed to nutritional deficiency, trauma, or divine fate — APS not routinely excluded Young stroke in India — APS workup routinely done by tertiary neurologists but often missed in secondary care (only OCP/drug misuse searched for) ORAL CONTRACEPTIVE PILL + APS Combined OCP dramatically increases thrombosis risk in APS-positive women; OCP screening for APS
