diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations WARFARIN IS LIFELONG IN CONFIRMED APS THROMBOSIS: Unlike most DVTs where 3–6 months of anticoagulation suffices, confirmed APS with thrombosis requires LIFELONG anticoagulation because the underlying thrombotic risk does not resolve. The recurrence rate after stopping antico

diseases.name: Crohns Disease
tests: ENDOSCOPY
Ileocolonoscopy (gold standard initial investigation) — visualisation + biopsies
Crohn's endoscopic features: cobblestone mucosa, aphthous ulcers, deep serpiginous ulcers, skip lesions, rectal sparing (unlike UC), perianal findings
Upper GI endoscopy (EGD) — oral, oesophageal, gastric, proximal small bowel Crohn's
HISTOLOGY
Non-caseating granulomas (40–60% of Crohn's biopsies) — supportive but not pathognomonic
Full-thickness transmural inflammation, lymphoid aggregates, normal or near-normal rectum in some
TB special stains (ZN stain, AFB culture, MTB PCR) — must be done on ALL biopsies in India
IMAGING
MRI Enterography (MRE) — gold standard for small bowel Crohn's extent, activity, complications (fistulae, abscesses, strictures); no radiation
generic_medicines: MILD-MODERATE CROHN'S
Mesalazine (5-ASA) 3–4 g/day (limited efficacy in small bowel Crohn's; works better in colonic Crohn's; NOT as effective as in UC)
Budesonide 9 mg/day — controlled ileal release; first-line for ileocaecal Crohn's induction; less systemic SE than Prednisolone; taper 3–3 months
MODERATE-SEVERE CROHN'S
Prednisolone 40–60 mg/day — acute flares; taper over 8–12 weeks; NOT for maintenance
IV Methylprednisolone — severe hospitalised Crohn's
Azathioprine 2–2.5 mg/kg/day (Thiopurine) — maintenance; steroid-sparing; requires TPMT testing (enzyme — homozygous TPMT deficient = fatal toxicity)
Mercaptopurine 0.75–1.5 mg/kg/day — active metabolite of AZA; TPMT enzyme test mandatory
Methotrexate 25 mg SC/IM weekly — Crohn's maintenance (small bowel and steroid-dependent); folic acid mandatory
BIOLOGIC THERAPY (moderate-severe or steroid-dependent/refractory)
Infliximab 5 mg/kg IV at 0, 2, 6 weeks then every 8 weeks — first-line biologic; fistulising Crohn's; Indian biosimilar available
Adalimumab 160/80/40 mg SC — first-line biologic; self-administered; Indian biosimilar available
Vedolizumab 300 mg IV at 0, 2, 6 weeks then every 8 weeks — gut-selective; safer infection profile; less effective than TNF-i in extraintestinal manifestations
Ustekinumab 90 mg SC every 8–12 weeks (after IV loading) — IL-12/23 inhibitor; effective in TNF-failure
SURGICAL INDICATIONS
ayurvedic_medicines: GUT ANTI-INFLAMMATORY

Boswellia serrata (Shallaki) — extensive RCTs in Crohn's disease showing clinical remission, reduced mucosal inflammation; H15 extract 3400 mg/day in clinical trials; anti-inflammatory via 5-LOX inhibition; gut specifically active; considered comparable to Mesalazine in some Crohn's studies
Turmeric / Curcumin — potent gut anti-inflammatory; inhibits NF-kB; clinical trials in IBD showing remission maintenance; 1–3 g/day with piperine
Kutaja (Holarrhena antidysenterica) — classical anti-dysenteric; intestinal anti-inflammatory; reduces diarrhoea and mucosal inflammation in IBD; 250–500 mg BD
Bilva (Aegle marmelos) — excellent anti-diarrhoeal; mucosal healing; astringent; reduces cramping; 500 mg–1 g of ripe fruit powder
Musta (Cyperus rotundus) — digestive; anti-inflammatory; carminative; reduces GI spasm; 250–500 mg BD
Aloe vera — mucosal healing; anti-inflammatory; trials in Crohn's; 100 mL juice BD; avoid in obstruction
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Kutajarishta — classical fermented Ayurvedic compound; Kutaja as main ingredient; diarrhoea and dysentery; 15–30 mL BD
Bilvadi Churna — anti-diarrhoeal combination
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors DIAGNOSIS DELAY IBD diagnosis delay in India: 3–7 years typical; Crohn's misdiagnosed as: intestinal TB (most common confusion), IBS, chronic amoebic colitis, enteric fever sequelae INTESTINAL TB vs CROHN'S — THE INDIA DIAGNOSTIC DILEMMA Intestinal TB (ITB) and Crohn's disease can be clinically, endoscopically, radiologically, and histologically virtually identical Both: ileocaecal location, skip lesions, granulomas on biopsy, obstruction, fistulae Key
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations TB vs CROHN'S — THE SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT INDIA-SPECIFIC ISSUE IN IBD: Intestinal tuberculosis (ITB) and Crohn's disease are clinically, endoscopically, radiologically, and histologically indistinguishable in a significant proportion of patients. Both affect the ileocaecal r

diseases.name: Ulcerative Colitis
tests: COLONOSCOPY WITH BIOPSIES (GOLD STANDARD)
UC endoscopic features: continuous mucosal inflammation from rectum proximally; erythema, granularity, friability, ulceration, loss of vascular pattern, pseudopolyps (in chronic disease)
Mayo endoscopic subscore: 0 (normal) to 3 (severe)
HISTOLOGY
Crypt architectural distortion, crypt abscesses, mucin depletion, basal plasmacytosis (chronic mucosal damage features) — distinguish IBD from acute self-limiting colitis
STOOL TESTS
Stool culture (C/S) — Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, E. coli O157
Stool microscopy for O&P (ova and parasites) — amoeba cysts/trophozoites
C. difficile toxin PCR
Faecal calprotectin — elevated in active IBD; >250 mcg/g = mucosal inflammation; monitoring remission/relapse
generic_medicines: MILD-MODERATE UC

Mesalazine (5-ASA) 2–4.8 g/day oral + Mesalazine 1 g enema/suppository (rectal) — cornerstone of UC induction and maintenance; oral and topical combination superior to either alone; lifelong maintenance in most UC
Sulfasalazine 3–4 g/day (older, cheaper alternative; more SE — sulfa-related; folate supplementation required)
Prednisolone 40 mg/day — moderate flares; taper over 8–12 weeks; NOT for maintenance
Budesonide MMX (Cortiment) 9 mg/day — mild-moderate extensive UC; limited systemic absorption
MODERATE-SEVERE UC
IV Methylprednisolone 60 mg/day (or Hydrocortisone 400 mg/day) — acute severe UC; hospitalisation
Azathioprine 2–2.5 mg/kg/day — steroid-sparing maintenance; onset 3–6 months
Infliximab 5 mg/kg IV at 0, 2, 6 weeks then every 8 weeks — biologic induction; acute severe UC rescue (3 mg/kg accelerated dosing); Indian biosimilar available
Adalimumab 160/80/40 mg SC — induction and maintenance
Vedolizumab 300 mg IV — gut-selective; preferred if previous TB or infections; induction + maintenance
NEWER BIOLOGICS AND SMALL MOLECULES
Ustekinumab (IL-12/23i) — moderate-severe UC; TNF-failure
Tofacitinib 10 mg BD (induction) → 5 mg BD (maintenance) — JAK1/3 inhibitor; oral; rapid onset; HZV prophylaxis recommended
Ozanimod, Etrasimod (S1P modulators) — newer oral agents
ayurvedic_medicines: MUCOSAL HEALING
Boswellia serrata (Shallaki) — RCTs comparing H15 Boswellia extract to Sulfasalazine in UC show comparable remission rates; anti-inflammatory via 5-LOX; mucosa healing; 1200 mg TDS of H15 extract
Turmeric / Curcumin — multiple RCTs in UC maintenance; curcumin 1 g BD + mesalazine significantly better than mesalazine alone for maintaining remission; gut-specific anti-inflammatory
Aloe vera gel — Phase I/II RCTs in mild-moderate UC; 100 mL BD for 4 weeks; anti-inflammatory, mucosal healing
Kutaja (Holarrhena antidysenterica) — classical Ayurvedic anti-dysenteric; direct mucosal anti-inflammatory; Kutajarishta fermented formulation; reduces bloody diarrhoea frequency
Bilva (Aegle marmelos) — anti-diarrhoeal; astringent; mucosal protective; 500 mg fruit powder
Amalaki (Emblica officinalis/Amla) — mucosal health; antioxidant; 500 mg–1 g/day
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Kutajarishta 15–30 mL BD — Kutaja-based classical formulation; anti-dysenteric and GI anti-inflammatory
Mustarishta — anti-inflammatory digestive; Musta base
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors PREVALENCE AND TREND UC more common than Crohn's in India (unlike West where Crohn's ≈ UC); UC:CD ratio approximately 7:1 in India vs 1:1 in West India IBD incidence rising — Chandigarh longitudinal data shows doubling over 20 years (hygiene hypothesis, dietary westernisation) INFECTION vs IBD DISTINCTION Acute bloody diarrhoea in India — must distinguish UC from: acute amoebic dysentery (specific treatment), Campylobacter, Shigella, Salmonella, CMV co
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations AMOEBIASIS MUST BE EXCLUDED BEFORE TREATING UC WITH STEROIDS: Acute bloody diarrhoea in India can be amoebic dysentery, bacterial dysentery, or UC — they can appear clinically and endoscopically identical. Treating active amoebic colitis with steroids causes fulminant amoebi

diseases.name: Primary Biliary Cholangitis
tests: DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA (2 of 3 required)
1. Elevated ALP (at least 1.5x ULN) persisting for >6 months
2. AMA titre ≥1:40 (anti-mitochondrial antibody) — sensitivity 95%, specificity 98%; near-pathognomonic
3. Liver biopsy showing non-suppurative destructive cholangitis and interlobular bile duct destruction
AUTOANTIBODIES
AMA (anti-mitochondrial antibody) — anti-M2 subtype (anti-PDC-E2 = pyruvate dehydrogenase complex E2 subunit); 90–95% sensitivity; near-pathognomonic for PBC
AMA-negative PBC (5–10%) — ANA with PBC-specific patterns: sp100 (nuclear dots), gp210 (nuclear rim); same prognosis
ANA — 35% PBC positive; sp100 and gp210 are PBC-specific ANA patterns
LIVER FUNCTION TESTS
ALP and GGT — cholestatic pattern; disproportionately elevated vs transaminases
generic_medicines: FIRST-LINE — ALL PBC

Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) 13–15 mg/kg/day (in divided doses) — normalises ALP/GGT; slows progression; improves symptoms; reduces risk of cirrhosis and liver transplantation; start immediately at diagnosis; lifelong
INADEQUATE UDCA RESPONSE (at 1 year, assessed by Paris/GLOBE criteria)

Obeticholic acid (OCA) 5–10 mg/day — FXR (farnesoid X receptor) agonist; 45% achieve biochemical response with UDCA+OCA; SE: worsening pruritus, hypercholesterolaemia; CONTRAINDICATED in decompensated cirrhosis
Bezafibrate 400 mg/day — fibrate; potent ALP reducer in PBC; good data from BEZURSO trial; widely available India; cheap; reasonable alternative 2nd line
Fenofibrate — alternative fibrate
PRURITUS MANAGEMENT

Cholestyramine 4–16 g/day (bile acid sequestrant) — first-line for cholestatic pruritus; binds bile acids in gut; SE: constipation, steatorrhoea, reduced drug absorption (give 4 hours before UDCA); unpleasant taste

Rifampicin 150–300 mg/day — induces hepatic microsomal enzymes, reduces bile acid synthesis; highly effective for cholestatic pruritus; CAUTION: hepatotoxicity (monitor LFT); check for TB exposure (rifampicin is TB drug — resistance issues if TB present)
Naltrexone 12.5–50 mg/day — opioid antagonist; reduces centrally-mediated pruritus; opioid withdrawal-like SE at initiation
Sertraline 50–100 mg/day — SSRI; pruritus; colestyramine-refractory
OSTEOPOROSIS PREVENTION
Calcium 1000–1200 mg/day + Vitamin D3 800–2000 IU/day — fat-soluble vitamin deficiency prevention
Alendronate 70 mg/week — if T-score < -2.5 or fragility fracture
FAT-SOLUBLE VITAMIN SUPPLEMENTATION
ayurvedic_medicines: HEPATOPROTECTIVE HERBS

Bhumyamalaki (Phyllanthus niruri / Phyllanthus amarus) — potent hepatoprotective; anti-fibrotic properties in animal models; reduces ALP, transaminases; anti-cholestatic; 500 mg–1 g BD; multiple Indian clinical trials
Kalmegh (Andrographis paniculata) — anti-inflammatory and hepatoprotective; classic Yakrit (liver) herb; reduces hepatic inflammation; 250–500 mg BD
Punarnava (Boerhavia diffusa) — hepatic and renal anti-inflammatory; reduces ascites and hepatic oedema; 500 mg BD

Katuki (Picrorhiza kurroa) — hepatoprotective; bile flow stimulant (cholagogue); anti-cholestatic historically used; CAUTION: can be hepatotoxic at high doses — use quality-assured standard preparations only
Kutki / Katuki is an endangered plant — use certified Himalayan sources only
Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — liver protection during cholestasis; immunomodulatory; anti-inflammatory; 500 mg/day
Amalaki (Emblica officinalis / Amla) — antioxidant; hepatoprotective; Vitamin C source; reduces oxidative stress in cholestasis; 500 mg–1 g/day
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Arogyavardhini Vati — classical Ayurvedic liver tonic; hepatoprotective; anti-icteric (anti-jaundice); widely used in India for liver disorders; contains Guggulu, Kutaki, Chitrak, Shilajit components
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors EPIDEMIOLOGY PBC incidence in India underestimated — largely under-diagnosed; predominantly women 40–60 years Elevated ALP/GGT in young-middle aged woman with no obvious hepatic cause = AMA should be checked OVERLAP WITH VIRAL HEPATITIS PBC in the context of HBV or HCV — may complicate AMA interpretation Elevated ALP in the presence of known HCV — dual pathology (viral hepatitis + PBC) possible; AMA testing important HERBAL HEPATOTOXICITY Ayurvedic and
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations UDCA MUST BE TAKEN FOR LIFE — STOPPING IS DANGEROUS: Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) is not a treatment for 'acute episodes' — it is a continuous disease-modifying therapy that must be taken lifelong at 13–15 mg/kg/day. UDCA works by replacing toxic hydrophobic bile acids, impro

diseases.name: Autoimmune Hepatitis
tests: REVISED IAIHG SCORE / SIMPLIFIED SCORE

Simplified score: ANA or SMA ≥1:40 = 1 pt; ANA/SMA ≥1:80 or anti-LKM1 ≥1:40 or anti-SLA = 2 pt; IgG ≥ULN = 1 pt; >1.1x ULN = 2 pt; compatible histology = 1 pt; typical histology = 2 pt; ANA/SMA titre ≥1:40 = 1 pt; Absence of viral hepatitis = 2 pt. Score ≥6 = probable AIH; ≥7 = definite AIH
AUTOANTIBODIES
ANA (antinuclear antibody) — positive in Type 1 AIH (up to 80%); homogeneous or speckled
Anti-SMA (anti-smooth muscle antibody) — F-actin anti-SMA most specific Type 1 AIH; 70–80%
Anti-LKM1 (anti-liver kidney microsomal type 1) — Type 2 AIH; specific; anti-CYP450 2D6
Anti-SLA (anti-soluble liver antigen) — specific for AIH; associated with poor prognosis; available in reference labs
SEROLOGY
IgG (immunoglobulin G) — elevated in AIH (often markedly >2x normal); distinguishes from PBC (IgM elevated)
LIVER FUNCTION TESTS
generic_medicines: INDUCTION OF REMISSION
Prednisolone 40–60 mg/day (1 mg/kg) — start immediately; reduces ALT rapidly (50% within 4 weeks in responders); taper over 4–6 weeks to maintenance dose
IV Methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg/day — acute severe AIH (INR >1.5, or significant encephalopathy) in hospital; switch to oral when responding
MAINTENANCE AND STEROID-SPARING
Azathioprine 1–2 mg/kg/day — add after initial steroid induction; permits steroid dose reduction and eventual steroid withdrawal; check TPMT before starting; monitor CBC
Target: biochemical remission (ALT/AST normal + IgG normal) then sustained for ≥2 years before attempting withdrawal
DURATION

Total treatment 2–3 years minimum; 40–50% relapse on withdrawal — if relapse: restart and continue longer; many patients require lifelong low-dose therapy (AZA 50–75 mg/day + low-dose Prednisolone 2.5–5 mg/day)
STEROID-REFRACTORY OR STEROID-INTOLERANT
Mycophenolate mofetil 2 g/day — AZA intolerant or myelosuppression;  similarly effective
Tacrolimus 2–4 mg/day — refractory AIH; some evidence
Ciclosporin — refractory cases; specifically in acute severe AIH to avoid transplantation
Rituximab — emerging evidence in refractory AIH
PORTAL HYPERTENSION IF CIRRHOSIS
Propranolol — variceal prevention
ayurvedic_medicines: HEPATOPROTECTIVE

Bhumyamalaki (Phyllanthus niruri) — most extensively studied Ayurvedic agent for hepatitis; anti-viral (HBV), hepatoprotective; anti-inflammatory action in liver; 500 mg–1 g BD; reduces transaminases in multiple clinical trials
Kalmegh (Andrographis paniculata) — hepatoprotective; anti-inflammatory; stimulates immune regulation via NF-kB modulation; 250–500 mg BD
Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — hepatoprotective and immunomodulatory; reduces hepatic inflammation; 500 mg/day
Amalaki (Emblica officinalis) — antioxidant; Vitamin C; hepatoprotective; 500 mg–1 g/day

Yashtimadhu (Glycyrrhiza glabra / Licorice) — anti-inflammatory; hepatoprotective; glycyrrhizin (IV preparation used in Japan for chronic hepatitis); oral Yashtimadhu 500 mg BD; SIGNIFICANT CAUTION: licorice raises blood pressure and causes pseudoaldosteronism at higher doses; avoid in hypertension and cirrhosis with sodium retention
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Arogyavardhini Vati — Ayurvedic classical liver tonic; compound anti-inflammatory; anti-cholestatic; widely used in India for chronic liver disease
Liv.52 (Himalaya Drug Company) — most widely used hepatoprotective supplement in India; proprietary blend; reasonable safety profile; may reduce DILI risk when on Isoniazid
Punarnavasava — liver and splenic disorders; anti-ascitic
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors DIAGNOSIS AND EXCLUSION Viral hepatitis (HBsAg, HCV RNA) must be excluded — HBV and HCV are common in India and can mimic AIH serology (false positive ANA/SMA in viral hepatitis; DILI from anti-viral treatment) Wilson's disease — particularly in children and young adults; serum ceruloplasmin, urinary copper, slit-lamp — must exclude before steroids (steroids harmful in Wilson's crisis) ISONIAZID HEPATOTOXICITY Isoniazid (cornerstone of TB treatment in
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations EARLY TREATMENT IS LIVER-SAVING — RESPONSE IS DRAMATIC: AIH is one of the few autoimmune conditions where the primary treatment (prednisolone) is universally available, cheap, and produces dramatic biochemical response (ALT often falls 50% within 2–4 weeks). The tragedy in I

diseases.name: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus
tests: DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA (ADA/WHO)
Random plasma glucose ≥200 mg/dL + symptoms
Fasting plasma glucose ≥126 mg/dL
2-hour OGTT ≥200 mg/dL
HbA1c ≥6.5% (48 mmol/mol)
Two results on separate days (except if unequivocal hyperglycaemia with symptoms)
DISTINGUISHING T1DM FROM T2DM
C-peptide — low/undetectable in T1DM; normal/elevated in T2DM; done when fasting glucose >4 mmol/L; stimulated C-peptide (glucagon or mixed meal test) more informative
GAD65 antibody (anti-GAD) — most useful and widely tested; positive in 70–80% T1DM; also positive in LADA
IA-2 antibody (anti-islet tyrosine phosphatase) — positive in 50–60% T1DM
generic_medicines: INSULIN — LIFELONG AND MANDATORY
T1DM requires lifelong exogenous insulin — there is NO oral alternative; insulin is not optional
BASAL-BOLUS REGIMEN (Physiological replacement):
Insulin Glargine (Lantus / Toujeo) or Insulin Detemir — once or twice daily basal insulin; provides continuous background coverage; peak-free
Insulin Aspart (NovoRapid) / Insulin Lispro (Humalog) / Insulin Glulisine (Apidra) — rapid-acting analogue; given with each meal
CONVENTIONAL REGIMEN (Affordable — Jan Aushadhi)
NPH Insulin (Isophane / Humulin N / Wockhardt Insuman) — intermediate-acting basal; BD dosing
Regular Human Insulin (Actrapid / Humulin R) — short-acting bolus; give 30 minutes before meals
DOSE TITRATION
Total daily dose: ~ 0.5–0.7 units/kg/day; 50% basal + 50% bolus
Sick-day rules: never stop insulin even when not eating; increase monitoring frequency; check ketones
DKA TREATMENT (Emergency)
IV Normal Saline (0.9% NaCl) — aggressive fluid resuscitation
IV Regular Insulin infusion 0.1 units/kg/hour
Potassium replacement (critical — DKA = total body K+ depletion despite normal serum K+)
janaushadhi_medicines: Regular Insulin 40 IU/mL (10 mL vial): Rs.35
NPH Insulin 40 IU/mL (10 mL vial): Rs.35
ayurvedic_medicines: BLOOD GLUCOSE SUPPORTIVE

T1DM is an absolute insulin deficiency — no herbal preparation can replace insulin, and herbal therapy must NEVER be used as an alternative. However, certain herbs have adjunctive roles in glycaemic support.

Bitter Melon / Karela (Momordica charantia) — insulin-secretagogue and insulin sensitiser properties; multiple small RCTs; 50–100 mL fresh juice or 500 mg extract; modest FBS reduction; safe; does NOT replace insulin; can augment in T1DM as modest adjunct with close monitoring to avoid hypoglycaemia
Gymnema sylvestre (Gurmar) — 'sugar destroyer'; reduces glucose absorption; may support residual beta cell function in early LADA; 400 mg extract OD; published studies in T2DM
Fenugreek seeds (Methi) — soluble seed fibre reduces post-prandial glucose rise; 10–25 g soaked seeds before meals; good safety; common in Indian diet
Vijayasar (Pterocarpus marsupium) — beta-cell regeneration claims in animal models; clinical evidence limited; traditional use in India for diabetes
Turmeric / Curcumin — anti-inflammatory; may protect residual beta cells; reduces T2DM insulin resistance; 500 mg–1 g BD; safe adjunct
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Nishamalaki (Turmeric + Amla formulation) — glucose-modulating; anti-diabetic traditional combination

Vasant Kusumakar Rasa — classical Ayurvedic formulation for diabetes, debility, and urinary disorders; contains precious metals (gold, silver) — only quality-assured preparations from reputed Ayurvedic pharmacies
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors YOUNG-ONSET T1DM IN INDIA T1DM in India presents younger than global average; mean age of diagnosis 8–12 years in children; significant burden of childhood DKA as first presentation Misdiagnosis of T1DM as T2DM common in India — obese adolescent with new-onset diabetes may have T1DM; check C-peptide, GAD antibodies; wrong diagnosis leads to inadequate insulin therapy and repeated DKA INSULIN ACCESS AND AFFORDABILITY Insulin access in India: significant
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations NEVER STOP INSULIN IN T1DM — EVEN DURING ILLNESS OR FASTING: The commonest cause of preventable DKA hospitalisation in India is insulin omission during intercurrent illness, vomiting, or religious fasting. Patients mistakenly believe 'if I am not eating, I do not need insuli

diseases.name: Graves Disease
tests: THYROID FUNCTION TESTS (TFT)
TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone) — suppressed (<0.01 mU/L) in hyperthyroidism; most sensitive test
Free T4 (FT4) — elevated in overt hyperthyroidism
Free T3 (FT3) — elevated; sometimes disproportionately high in T3 toxicosis; FT3 more useful than T4 in some cases
AUTOANTIBODIES

TRAb (TSH receptor antibody) — pathognomonic of Graves; sensitivity 95%+; anti-TSH-receptor stimulating antibodies; positive titre confirms Graves; use for: confirming diagnosis, assessing remission, pregnancy monitoring (neonatal Graves risk from transplacental TRAb)
Anti-TPO (anti-thyroid peroxidase) — elevated in Graves and Hashimoto's; less specific for Graves
THYROID ULTRASOUND
Diffuse enlargement, hypervascularity ('thyroid inferno' on colour Doppler) — Graves pattern
Nodular goitre — toxic adenoma or toxic multinodular goitre (different from Graves — no TRAb)
generic_medicines: ANTITHYROID DRUGS (ATD)
Carbimazole 15–40 mg/day (starting dose) reducing to 5–15 mg/day maintenance — Block-and-replace or titration regimen; 18 months treatment; 50% remission rate; preferred in UK/India
Methimazole 15–40 mg/day — equivalent to carbimazole (CBZ is prodrug → methimazole); USA preferred

Propylthiouracil (PTU) 300–600 mg/day divided TDS — preferred in FIRST TRIMESTER PREGNANCY (CBZ teratogenic); liver toxicity risk (fatal hepatotoxicity reported) — avoid long-term use preferring carbimazole
Block-and-replace: Carbimazole 40 mg/day + T4 100 mcg/day — simpler titration; more medication; preferred in severe disease or unstable patients
SYMPTOMATIC
Propranolol 40–120 mg/day — beta-blocker; controls tachycardia, tremor, anxiety, heat intolerance RAPIDLY (within hours); bridge until ATD takes effect (6–8 weeks); NOT anti-thyroid
Atenolol 50–100 mg/day — alternative cardioselective beta-blocker
RADIOIODINE (RAI) I-131
Administered as single oral dose in nuclear medicine; ablates thyroid; permanent hypothyroidism ensues (planned)
Pre-treatment preparation: ATD to control thyroid function (stop CBZ 3–5 days before RAI); RAI in euthyroid state reduces thyroid storm risk
Contraindications: Pregnancy, breastfeeding, active GO
THYROID STORM (Emergency)
PTU 500 mg stat (inhibits synthesis + peripheral conversion T4→T3); Lugol's iodine (reduces thyroid hormone release — after PTU!); IV Propranolol; Dexamethasone 8 mg/day; Supportive (cooling, fluids)
GRAVES OPHTHALMOPATHY
ayurvedic_medicines: THYROID-MODULATING HERBS

Bugleweed (Lycopus virginicus / Lycopus europaeus) — European herbal medicine; mild antithyroid activity; reduces T3/T4 production; inhibits TSH binding; studied in mild hyperthyroidism; reduces palpitations and anxiety in thyrotoxicosis; 200–500 mg extract; NOT yet mainstream in Indian Ayurveda but available as supplement in India
Lemon Balm (Melissa officinalis) — anti-thyroid and anti-TSH receptor activity; reduces anxiety (thyrotoxicosis feature); available as standardised extract
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — central anxiolytic; reduces anxiety and insomnia secondary to thyrotoxicosis; well-studied in Indian Ayurveda; does not directly reduce thyroid hormones

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — IMPORTANT CAUTION: Ashwagandha can STIMULATE thyroid function and increase T3/T4 levels; HAS BEEN DOCUMENTED TO WORSEN HYPERTHYROIDISM; CONTRAINDICATED in Graves disease and hyperthyroidism; safe in HYPOTHYROIDISM (Hashimoto's)

Sarpagandha (Rauvolfia serpentina) — reduces tachycardia and anxiety in thyrotoxicosis; heart rate controlling Vata property; CAUTION: antihypertensive; sedative; psychosis association with Reserpine (active compound)
Jatamansi (Nardostachys jatamansi) — anxiolytic; reduces restlessness and insomnia of thyrotoxicosis; nerve tonic
Arjuna (Terminalia arjuna) — cardiac support; tachycardia and palpitations; reduces AF risk
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Sarpagandhaghana Vati — classical Ayurvedic anti-adrenergic; heart rate control in thyrotoxicosis
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors IODINE EXCESS Universal iodisation of salt in India — hypothyroidism has reduced; but Jod-Basedow effect: sudden iodine load (iodinated contrast) can precipitate thyrotoxicosis in nodular goitre (not Graves per se, but important differential) HIGH SELENIUM DEFICIENCY Selenium deficiency: suboptimal selenium in many Indian diets; selenium is cofactor for thyroid peroxidase and deiodinase enzymes; selenium supplementation (200 mcg/day) shown to reduce TR
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations AGRANULOCYTOSIS — STOP ATD IMMEDIATELY IF FEVER AND SORE THROAT: Carbimazole and Propylthiouracil can cause agranulocytosis (absolute neutrophil count < 500/microL) in 0.3–0.5% of patients. Agranulocytosis onset is unpredictable, can occur within days to months of starting t

diseases.name: Hashimotos Thyroiditis
tests: THYROID FUNCTION TESTS
TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone) — most sensitive test; elevated TSH = primary hypothyroidism; normal TSH range: 0.4–4.0 mU/L (lab-specific)
Subclinical hypothyroidism: TSH 4–10 mU/L + normal FT4 — treat if TSH >10 or symptomatic or anti-TPO positive or pregnant
Overt hypothyroidism: TSH >10 mU/L + reduced FT4
Free T4 (FT4) — reduced in overt hypothyroidism; reference range: 9–23 pmol/L
Free T3 (FT3) — low in severe hypothyroidism; T3 toxicosis screening
AUTOANTIBODIES

Anti-TPO (anti-thyroid peroxidase antibody) — positive in 90–95% Hashimoto's; confirms autoimmune aetiology; raised titres predict progression from subclinical to overt hypothyroidism; risk in pregnancy
Anti-thyroglobulin antibody (anti-TgAb) — positive in 60–70% Hashimoto's; often co-elevated with anti-TPO
TRAb (TSH receptor antibody) — needed to distinguish Hashimoto's from Graves in hashitoxicosis phase
generic_medicines: LEVOTHYROXINE (T4) — FIRST-LINE AND STANDARD
Levothyroxine sodium 25–200 mcg/day — once daily; taken fasted 30–45 minutes before food; half-life 7 days; dose titrated to TSH and symptom resolution
Starting dose: elderly and cardiac disease patients start 25–50 mcg/day; younger healthy adults start 50–75 mcg/day; full replacement dose 1.6 mcg/kg/day estimated
Titration: repeat TSH 6–8 weeks after each dose change; titrate in 25 mcg increments
Target TSH: 0.5–2.5 mU/L for most adults; lower targeting logical for symptoms; during pregnancy TSH <2.5 first trimester, <3.0 second/third
INTERACTIONS (reducing T4 absorption — give 4 hours apart):
Calcium carbonate, Iron supplements, Antacids (aluminium, magnesium), PPIs/H2 blockers, Cholestyramine, Soya products
T4/T3 COMBINATION THERAPY

Liothyronine (T3) 5–10 mcg/day added to T4 in persistently symptomatic patients with normal TSH — evidence-limited; T3 has short half-life; unstable TSH; some evidence for improved mood/wellbeing; specialist initiation
SUBCLINICAL HYPOTHYROIDISM — TREATMENT INDICATIONS
TSH >10 mU/L — treat regardless
TSH 4–10 + symptomatic (fatigue, depression, weight gain)
TSH 4–10 + anti-TPO positive (high risk of progression)
TSH 4–10 + PREGNANCY (mandatory)
TSH 4–10 + dyslipidaemia (cholesterol improvement with T4)
janaushadhi_medicines: Iron tablet (Ferrous Sulfate 150 mg) — anaemia co-treatment
Atorvastatin 20 mg tablet (secondary dyslipidaemia — if not resolved with T4)
ayurvedic_medicines: THYROID STIMULATING / SUPPORTING HERBS (for Hypothyroidism)

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — MOST EVIDENCE-BASED for hypothyroidism in Ayurveda; multiple clinical trials show Ashwagandha 600 mg/day increases T3/T4 levels in subclinical hypothyroidism; immunomodulatory; reduces anti-TPO antibody titres in some studies; adaptogenic — reduces cortisol (which suppresses thyroid function); 300–600 mg extract OD-BD

Guggul (Commiphora mukul) / Z-guggulsterone — guggulsterone stimulates thyroid hormone synthesis; multiple Indian studies showing reduced T4 supplementation requirement in hypothyroidism with Guggul; anti-inflammatory in AITD; 500 mg BD of standardised extract

Kanchnar Guggulu — classically indicated in Ayurveda for Gandamala (goitre/neck glandular swellings); contains Kanchnar (Bauhinia variegata), Triphala, Trikatu, Guggul; reduces goitre size and supports thyroid function; widely prescribed in India for all thyroid conditions
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — cognitive support; addresses brain fog and memory issues of hypothyroidism; well-tolerated; 300–450 mg standardised extract daily
Shankhpushpi (Convolvulus pluricaulis) — cognitive and thyroid-supportive; reduces anxiety and depression component of hypothyroidism
Triphala — constipation (common hypothyroid symptom); reduces cholesterol; antioxidant; 5–10 g at bedtime
Metabolic Herbs
Trikatu (Ginger, Black pepper, Pippali) — thermogenic; metabolic stimulant; counteracts metabolic slowing of hypothyroidism; digestive stimulant; 500 mg with meals
Vrikshamla / Garcinia cambogia — metabolic booster; lipid-lowering; weight support in hypothyroid obesity
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors IODINE DEFICIENCY AND HYPOTHYROIDISM COEXISTENCE Iodine deficiency goitre (DDG — Dietary Deficiency Goitre) — historically a major cause of thyroid dysfunction in mountain and hilly areas of India (Himalayas, Vindhyas, Sub-Himalayan) Universal salt iodisation improved this but iodine deficiency hypothyroidism still occurs alongside AITD in India PREVALENT UNDERDIAGNOSIS Hypothyroidism is the most under-diagnosed endocrine disorder in India — classic sy
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations TSH MUST BE NORMALISED IN PREGNANCY — FETAL BRAIN DEVELOPMENT IS AT STAKE: The fetal thyroid does not become functional until 12–14 weeks of gestation. During the first trimester, the developing fetal brain is entirely dependent on MATERNAL thyroid hormones crossing the plac

diseases.name: Melasma
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS
Melasma diagnosed clinically by distribution, symmetry, trigger history, and pigment appearance
WOOD'S LAMP EXAMINATION (365 nm UV)
Epidermal melasma — sharply enhanced, darker borders under Wood's lamp
Dermal melasma — no significant enhancement; bluish-grey
Mixed — partial enhancement
Less reliable in very dark skin (Fitzpatrick V-VI) — melanin saturation reduces differentiation
DERMOSCOPY
Regular pigment network, dark brown dots, telangiectasias; distinguishes melasma from: Lichen planus pigmentosus (LPP), drug-induced pigmentation, PIH, Riehl's melanosis, solar lentigos
mMASI SCORING
generic_medicines: SUNSCREEN — MANDATORY FOUNDATION OF ALL MELASMA TREATMENT

Broad-spectrum SPF50+ PA+++ sunscreen — daily application on all exposed skin; reapplication every 2 hours outdoors; TINTED sunscreen containing iron oxide or pigment mix = blocks visible HEV light — preferred in India; no topical agent works without strict photoprotection
TOPICAL DEPIGMENTING AGENTS
Hydroquinone (HQ) 2–4% cream — gold standard tyrosinase inhibitor; 8–12 week induction; nighttime use; NOT for >6 months continuous; monitor for ochronosis
Triple combination cream (Kligman's formula): HQ 4% + Tretinoin 0.05% + Fluocinolone 0.01% — most studied combination; 8–12 weeks induction; cannot be used long-term (steroid component)
Kojic acid 1–4% — tyrosinase inhibitor; safe alternative/adjunct to HQ; contact dermatitis risk in some patients
Azelaic acid 15–20% — mild depigmenting + anti-inflammatory; safe in PREGNANCY; well tolerated
Alpha-arbutin 1–4% — HQ precursor; milder; safer for long-term maintenance
Tretinoin 0.025–0.05% — epidermal turnover; melanin dispersal; nighttime maintenance; combine with SPF
Niacinamide 4–5% — inhibits melanosome transfer; anti-inflammatory; widely available in Indian skincare
ORAL AGENTS

Tranexamic acid (TXA) 250 mg BD — most important oral advance for melasma; competitively inhibits plasminogen activator → reduces UV-induced prostaglandin synthesis → reduces melanocyte stimulation; multiple Asian RCTs including India data; excellent response in Indian patients; combine with topicals
Glutathione 500 mg/day — antioxidant; inhibits pro-melanogenic eumelanin synthesis; popular in India; safety profile good at therapeutic doses
PROCEDURES
Chemical peels: Glycolic acid 35–70%, Salicylic acid 20–30%, Lactic acid — epidermal melanin dispersal; series of 4–6 peels every 2–3 weeks
ayurvedic_medicines: TOPICAL VARNYA (SKIN BRIGHTENING)

Kumkumadi Tailam — classical Ayurvedic facial oil; contains Kesar (Saffron), Manjistha, Chandan, Yashtimadhu, Usheer; documented tyrosinase-inhibiting components (Glabridin from Yashtimadhu, curcumin from Haridra, saffron crocin); 5–10 drops as facial oil at night; widely used in India; clinically studied in multiple trials

Yashtimadhu (Liquorice / Glycyrrhiza glabra) — Glabridin content is a potent tyrosinase inhibitor; anti-inflammatory; inhibits UVB-induced melanogenesis; 1–2% Glabridin-standardised cream; highly effective in epidermal melasma
Chandan (Sandalwood) — alpha-santalol inhibits tyrosinase; skin brightening; cooling Pitta-reducing; sandalwood paste + rose water topical
Usheer (Vetiver / Vetiveria zizanioides) — cooling; anti-melanogenic; Usheer-based face packs
Amalaki (Amla) paste — natural Vitamin C; inhibits DOPA oxidation step in melanogenesis; widely used as topical brightening agent
Rakta Chandan (Red Sandalwood / Pterocarpus santalinus) — anti-melanogenic; anti-inflammatory; combine with milk
Masoor Dal Ubtan (Red Lentil scrub) — traditional Indian exfoliant; gentle NAD-based lightening; widely used home remedy
SYSTEMIC
Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia) — premier Varnya (skin brightening) herb in Ayurveda; anti-melanogenic; reduces reactive melanocyte activity; 250–500 mg extract BD
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors FITZPATRICK III-V PHOTOTYPE PREVALENCE Indian skin (predominantly Fitzpatrick III-V) has higher melanocyte density and more reactive melanocytes; more prone to relapse; more likely to have mixed/dermal component; more prone to exogenous ochronosis from HQ overuse EXOGENOUS OCHRONOSIS — INDIA-SPECIFIC HQ COMPLICATION Prolonged Hydroquinone use (>6 months, >4% concentration, under intense tropical UV) in dark skin can paradoxically cause Exogenous Ochron
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations SUNSCREEN IS THE TREATMENT, NOT AN ADD-ON: Melasma is fundamentally UV and visible-light-driven. Without rigorous daily SPF50+ broad-spectrum sunscreen application (reapplied every 2 hours outdoors), no topical depigmenting agent — not HQ, not TXA, not retinoids — will produ

diseases.name: Androgenetic Alopecia
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS
AGA diagnosed clinically using Hamilton-Norwood (male) or Ludwig/Savin (female) classification and distribution pattern
TRICHOSCOPY (DERMOSCOPY)
Hair diameter diversity >20% — miniaturisation criterion (>20% of hairs on dermoscopy have reduced diameter relative to terminal hairs)
Peripilar sign — perifollicular brown discolouration (sebaceous gland hypertrophy)
Yellow dots, empty follicles — late AGA
HAIR PULL TEST
Pull 40–60 hairs gently; >10% anagen:telogen ratio change or >6 telogen hairs = active effluvium (TE component)
HORMONAL WORKUP (MANDATORY IN FEMALE AGA)
Serum testosterone (total and free) — elevated in hyperandrogenism
generic_medicines: TOPICAL — BOTH GENDERS

Minoxidil 2% solution (females) / 5% solution or foam (males) — vasodilator; prolongs anagen; promotes follicular vascularisation; apply OD-BD to dry scalp; results in 3–6 months; must continue lifelong — hair relost within 4–6 months of stopping; FDA approved for AGA since 1988

Minoxidil 0.25 mg oral (low-dose oral Minoxidil, LDOM) — increasingly used; 1 tablet OD; as effective as topical; better compliance; SE: hypertrichosis (body hair); very affordable in India; off-label but widely used
MALE AGA — SYSTEMIC

Finasteride 1 mg/day — 5-alpha reductase type II inhibitor; reduces scalp DHT 60–70%; approved for male AGA; 80% halt progression; partial regrowth in 65% at 2 years; take daily for life; SE: sexual side effects (2–3%) — erectile dysfunction, reduced libido, ejaculatory dysfunction (mostly reversible on stopping)

Dutasteride 0.5 mg/day — inhibits both 5-AR type I and II; more potent DHT reduction (>90%); approved for AGA in South Korea/Japan; off-label in India and West; more effective than Finasteride in some trials
FEMALE AGA — HORMONAL
Spironolactone 50–200 mg/day — anti-androgen; used off-label for female AGA; monitor potassium, BP; contraception needed (anti-androgenic effect on male fetus)
Combined OCP with anti-androgenic progestogen (Cyproterone acetate, Drospirenone) — reduces androgen-driven AGA in PCOS
Minoxidil 2% + Tretinoin 0.025% topical combination — enhances Minoxidil penetration and efficacy
PROCEDURES
Platelet-Rich Plasma (PRP) injection — autologous growth factor injection into scalp; 3–6 sessions monthly; evidence for halting progression and increasing density; widely available in India
Low-Level Laser Therapy (LLLT) — helmet/cap devices; FDA approved for AGA; photobiomodulation; 25 min 3x/week
Hair transplant (FUT/FUE) — permanent surgical solution; only for stable AGA; requires co-medical therapy
ayurvedic_medicines: HAIR GROWTH PROMOTERS

Bhringaraja (Eclipta alba) — most evidence-based Ayurvedic herb for AGA; inhibits 5-alpha reductase enzyme (same mechanism as Finasteride) in animal studies; promotes anagen extension; darkens and thickens hair; topical Bhringaraja oil scalp massage 3x/week; oral Bhringaraja churna 3–5 g/day with milk

Methi (Fenugreek / Trigonella foenum-graecum) — contains DHT-blocking compounds; steroidal saponins (diosgenin); traditional hair loss treatment; Methi seed paste scalp pack; oral Methi seeds 10 g/day; multiple Indian studies
Amla (Emblica officinalis) — antioxidant; Vitamin C; inhibits 5-AR; promotes follicular health; Amla oil scalp; oral Amla 500 mg–1 g/day
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — reduces cortisol (stress-driven AGA); hair follicle nourishing; scalp massage oil
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — reduces cortisol; reduces stress-driven hair loss; androgen-modulating properties; 300–600 mg extract BD
DIETARY AND NUTRITIONAL
Sesame seeds (Til) — high in zinc, copper, selenium, Vitamin E; direct hair nutrition; 2 tablespoons daily; traditional Indian hair diet
Flaxseed oil (omega-3) — anti-inflammatory; scalp health; reduces inflammatory-driven loss
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors EARLY ONSET AGA IN INDIA AGA increasingly presenting at younger ages (20–30s) in India; genetic predisposition in certain communities; premature AGA (<25 years) associated with higher insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome in Indian males HAIR OIL CULTURE AND MISCONCEPTIONS Widespread belief in India that hair oiling prevents or reverses AGA — oils do not address the androgen-driven follicular miniaturisation mechanism; oils maintain scalp moisture
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations FINASTERIDE SEXUAL SIDE EFFECTS — POST-FINASTERIDE SYNDROME: Finasteride 1 mg/day causes sexual adverse effects (reduced libido, erectile dysfunction, ejaculatory dysfunction) in 1.5–2.5% of males on prescribing trials. In most cases, side effects resolve within 3–12 months

diseases.name: Rosacea
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS
Rosacea diagnosed clinically: persistent central facial erythema/flushing, telangiectasias, inflammatory papules/pustules WITHOUT comedones, phymatous changes, ocular involvement
DERMOSCOPY

Telangiectasias (linear, branching vessels on nose, cheeks), polygonal vascular network, follicular plugging; confirms vascular nature; distinguishes from acne (BPO, salicylic acid would be wrong treatment)
REFLECTANCE CONFOCAL MICROSCOPY
Dilated vessels, perifollicular inflammation, Demodex mites in high density — research tool
DEMODEX DENSITY
Skin surface biopsy/tape stripping for Demodex density; >5 mites/cm² = elevated; Demodex-triggered rosacea responds to Ivermectin/Metronidazole
DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS INVESTIGATIONS
ANA, anti-Ro/La — SLE (malar rash of SLE vs rosacea erythema; SLE photosensitive)
generic_medicines: TOPICAL THERAPY
Metronidazole 0.75–1% gel/cream — first-line topical; anti-inflammatory; anti-Demodex; BD application; reduces papules/pustules in PPR subtype
Azelaic acid 15–20% gel/cream — anti-inflammatory; reduces erythema and papules; safe and effective; calcineurin-independent mechanism
Ivermectin 1% cream — most effective topical; anti-Demodex + anti-inflammatory; OD application; superior to Metronidazole in head-to-head trials; approved specifically for rosacea
Brimonidine 0.33% gel — alpha2-adrenergic agonist; reduces redness/flushing within 30 minutes; temporary effect (4–8 hours); rebound erythema with overuse
Oxymetazoline 1% cream — topical alpha-1 agonist; persistent erythema of ETR; approved USA 2017
TOPICAL CALCINEURIN INHIBITORS
Tacrolimus 0.1% — anti-inflammatory; useful when topical steroids cannot be used; post-steroid rosacea management
ORAL THERAPY

Doxycycline 40 mg MR (modified release) ONCE daily — anti-inflammatory dose (sub-antimicrobial); FDA approved specifically for rosacea PPR; reduces papules/pustules; does NOT cause antibiotic resistance at this sub-MIC dose; safest long-term oral treatment; first-line systemic
Doxycycline 100 mg BD (short-course 4–6 weeks) — if 40 mg MR not available; then tapering to maintenance
FLUSHING — SPECIFIC TREATMENT
Propranolol 20–40 mg BD — reduces vasomotor flushing; particularly if anxiety-triggered
Carvedilol 6.25 mg BD — alpha+beta blocker; flushing
Clonidine 50–75 mcg BD — central vasomotor inhibitor; flushing (also used for perimenopause flushes)
ayurvedic_medicines: ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AND ANTI-ERYTHEMA TOPICALS
Aloe vera gel — cooling; anti-inflammatory; reduces erythema and burning sensation of rosacea; soothing for sensitive reactive skin; apply fresh gel BD; widely available India
Chandan (Sandalwood) — anti-inflammatory; cooling (Sita Virya = cool energy); reduces Pitta-type erythema and burning; topical sandalwood paste in rose water
Usheer (Vetiver) — intensely cooling; reduces heat-triggered flushing; face mist with Usheer-infused water
Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia) — blood purifier; reduces capillary hyperreactivity; anti-inflammatory; reduces Rakta (blood) Pitta driving rosacea
Green tea extract — topical and oral; EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate) is anti-inflammatory; multiple rosacea RCTs; reduces papules, erythema, and UV-triggered flushing
Neem — anti-Demodex; anti-inflammatory; antimicrobial; reduces Demodex-driven rosacea component; neem leaf extract topical
SYSTEMIC

Haridra (Turmeric / Curcumin) — anti-inflammatory; reduces NF-kB activation; oral 500 mg BD curcumin; rosacea as Pitta-dominant skin disorder in Ayurveda; turmeric supports anti-inflammatory treatment
Triphala — internal cleanser; Pitta balancing; systemic anti-inflammatory; 5 g at bedtime
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors UNDER-RECOGNITION IN DARKER SKIN Rosacea classically described in fair-skinned Celtic/Northern European populations (Fitzpatrick I-II); in Fitzpatrick III-V Indian skin, erythema is less visible and may be masked by baseline melanin pigmentation; telangiectasias less apparent; most Indian rosacea presents as papulopustular type (Subtype 2) MISDIAGNOSIS AS ACNE Rosacea papulopustular type frequently misdiagnosed as acne in India; key difference: NO come
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations STEROID-INDUCED ROSACEA IS EPIDEMIC IN INDIA — AND REQUIRES A SPECIFIC WITHDRAWAL PROTOCOL: The most common cause of de novo rosacea-like dermatitis in India is prolonged application of topical steroids to the face — from OTC fairness creams (containing Clobetasol, Betametha

diseases.name: Seborrhoeic Dermatitis
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS
SD diagnosed clinically; characteristic site distribution, greasy scale, sebaceous area involvement
DERMOSCOPY (TRICHOSCOPY)
Yellowish or white structureless scaling areas; superficial blood vessels on scalp (contrasting with dry scale of psoriasis); 'branch-like dandruff vessels'
KOH PREPARATION

Skin scraping in 10–20% KOH — Malassezia globosa and restricta visible as short curved hyphae with round yeast forms ('meatballs and spaghetti' like tinea versicolor pattern); confirms fungal component
SKIN BIOPSY (if diagnostic uncertainty)
Spongiosis (epidermal oedema), perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate, serum in follicular infundibula with Malassezia; differentiates from psoriasis (parakeratosis, Munro's microabscesses)
DIFFERENTIAL INVESTIGATIONS
Skin swab — bacterial superinfection in infected SD
generic_medicines: SCALP SD
Ketoconazole 2% shampoo — first-line anti-Malassezia; leave on 3–5 minutes; 3x/week induction for 4 weeks; then 1–2x/week maintenance
Zinc pyrithione 1% shampoo — anti-Malassezia + anti-inflammatory; OTC; daily-use safe
Selenium sulfide 1–2.5% shampoo — anti-Malassezia; 2x/week; may discolour bleached or light hair
Ciclopirox 1% shampoo — antifungal with anti-inflammatory properties; 2–3x/week
Coal tar 2–5% shampoo — antiproliferative + antifungal; very effective; cosmetically less acceptable (smell); carcinogen warning with long-term overuse
Salicylic acid 2–3% shampoo — keratolytic; removes adherent scale; combine with antifungal shampoo
Topical corticosteroids for scalp — Betamethasone valerate 0.05–0.1% scalp lotion; Clobetasol propionate 0.05% scalp solution; short-course for acute flares
FACIAL SD
Ketoconazole 2% cream — apply to nasolabial folds, eyebrows, hairline BD for 2–4 weeks
Ciclopirox 0.77% gel/cream — very effective facial SD; anti-inflammatory + antifungal
Tacrolimus 0.03–0.1% — calcineurin inhibitor; anti-inflammatory without steroid atrophy risk; face-safe long-term; excellent facial SD maintenance
Low-potency topical steroids (Hydrocortisone 1%) — short-burst for acute flares on face; NOT potent steroids on face
SYSTEMIC FOR SEVERE/RECALCITRANT
Oral Fluconazole 150 mg once weekly × 4 weeks; then monthly maintenance — for severe scalp or facial SD
ayurvedic_medicines: TOPICAL ANTI-MALASSEZIA

Neem Oil (Azadirachta indica) — most clinically relevant Ayurvedic antifungal; azadirachtin, nimbidin — documented anti-Malassezia activity; neem oil scalp, diluted 1:5 in coconut oil; apply before wash 30 minutes; replaces commercial mineral oil for Indian oiling ritual
Tea Tree Oil (Melaleuca alternifolia) — terpinen-4-ol; potent anti-Malassezia (MIC studies); 5% tea tree oil shampoo comparable to 2% Ketoconazole in RCT; dilute 1–2% in carrier oil for scalp
Bhringaraja (Eclipta alba) — antifungal + anti-inflammatory scalp; Bhringaraja + Neem oil blend for dandruff; widely used Indian home remedy and Ayurvedic recommendation
Nimba Taila (Neem oil) scalp application — pure or blended; leave 30 min, wash with anti-Malassezia shampoo
INTERNAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY
Neem (oral) — Nimbarishta or Neem leaf extract tablets 500 mg BD; systemic antifungal + blood purifier
Haridra (Turmeric) — anti-inflammatory; reduces Pitta-driven sebaceous inflammation; 500 mg curcumin BD
Gandhak Rasayana — purified sulphur; antimicrobial; anti-inflammatory; classical for Kushtha (skin inflammation) including dandruff; 500 mg BD with ghee
Triphala — liver purifier; reduces Kapha-Pitta skin inflammation; 5 g at bedtime
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors HOT HUMID CLIMATE — YEAR-ROUND MALASSEZIA SUPPORT Malassezia (Pityrosporum ovale/globosa) thrives in warm, humid, lipid-rich environments; India's tropical climate provides year-round ideal conditions; SD prevalence higher and more severe year-round in India vs temperate climates HAIR OIL MISUSE Heavy daily oiling of scalp (coconut oil, parachute oil, mineral oil) — deeply entrenched in Indian hair care practice; however, excess scalp oil creates lipid
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations SD IS CHRONIC AND REQUIRES MAINTENANCE THERAPY — NOT COURSES: Seborrhoeic dermatitis is not cured by any available treatment. The Malassezia yeast is part of normal human skin flora and cannot be permanently eliminated. Treatment controls the abnormal inflammatory response a

diseases.name: Post-Inflam Hyperpigmentation
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS
PIH diagnosed clinically — match between lesion location/shape and history of preceding inflammation
WOOD'S LAMP
Epidermal PIH — enhanced (brighter) under 365 nm Wood's lamp = melanin in epidermis; more responsive to depigmenting treatment
Dermal PIH — no enhancement or bluish hue = melanin in dermis (melanophages); treatment-resistant; very slow natural resolution
DERMOSCOPY
Brown regular structureless areas (epidermal); grey pseudonetwork or dots (dermal); helps characterise treatment prognosis
REFLECTANCE CONFOCAL MICROSCOPY
Precise localisation of melanin to epidermis vs dermis; research tool for treatment planning
SKIN BIOPSY (rarely needed for diagnosis)
generic_medicines: PHOTOPROTECTION — FOUNDATION OF PIH TREATMENT (NON-NEGOTIABLE)

SPF50+ broad-spectrum sunscreen with PA+++ — daily application; tinted sunscreen with iron oxide blocks visible light (HEV); reapplication every 2 hours outdoors; without photoprotection, no topical depigmenting agent will produce lasting results
TOPICAL DEPIGMENTING FIRST-LINE

Topical Retinoids — Tretinoin 0.025–0.05% or Adapalene 0.1–0.3% — accelerates epidermal turnover; disperses melanin granules; key component of PIH treatment; nighttime application; mild purging initially
Hydroquinone 2–4% — gold standard depigmenting; inhibits tyrosinase; use maximum 6 months continuous; avoid >2 years (ochronosis risk in dark skin)
Azelaic Acid 15–20% — anti-tyrosinase + anti-inflammatory; safe long-term; dual action ideal for post-acne PIH (treats PIH + residual acne inflammation)
Kojic Acid 1–4% — tyrosinase inhibitor; alternative to or combined with HQ; contact sensitisation risk
Arbutin 2–4% — stable HQ precursor; milder; safer for extended use
Niacinamide 4–10% — inhibits melanosome transfer from melanocyte to keratinocyte; anti-inflammatory; barrier-repairing; extremely well tolerated; cosmetically elegant; excellent India option
COMBINATIONS (most effective)
Modified Kligman's Triple combination: Tretinoin 0.025% + HQ 4% + mild TCS (Fluocinolone 0.01%) — highly effective for PIH; use 8–12 weeks then switch to maintenance
Tranexamic acid topical 2–5% + Niacinamide 10% + SPF50 — excellent combination for Indian PIH; well tolerated
ORAL ADJUNCTS
Tranexamic acid oral 250 mg BD — particularly effective for PIH from acne and hormone-driven causes
Vitamin C (Ascorbic acid) oral 500 mg BD — antioxidant; reduces post-UV melanogenesis; adjunct
ayurvedic_medicines: TOPICAL VARNYA (COMPLEXION-BRIGHTENING)

Licorice root extract (Yashtimadhu / Glycyrrhiza glabra) — Glabridin inhibits tyrosinase; multiple in vitro and clinical studies for PIH; 1–2% glabridin topical formulation; very suitable Indian botanical for PIH

Kumkumadi Tailam — saffron, Manjistha, Sandalwood, Licorice, Vetiver — multiple tyrosinase-inhibiting compounds; classical face oil for complexion; especially suited for post-acne PIH; nighttime application
Masoor Dal (Red Lentil) ubtan — traditional Indian scrub; gentle exfoliant; removes superficial hyperpigmented layer; popular home remedy with genuine mild efficacy for epidermal PIH
Chandan (Sandalwood) + Rose water paste — anti-melanogenic; cooling; anti-inflammatory; suitable for reactive PIH

Niacinamide (Vitamin B3) — mechanistically blocks melanosome transfer; best evidence-base among skincare ingredients for PIH; 10% niacinamide serum; not traditional Ayurvedic but naturopathic; widely available India
SYSTEMIC

Manjistha (Rubia cordifolia) — Raktashodhaka; reduces hyper-reactive melanocyte response; 250–500 mg extract BD or Sarivadyasava; most consistently recommended systemic Ayurvedic herb for hyperpigmentation and PIH
Amalaki (Amla) — Vitamin C; antioxidant; anti-melanogenic; 500 mg–1 g/day significantly reduces post-UV pigmentation in skin cells; oral Amla + copper water synergy in Ayurvedic practice
Haridra (Curcumin) — anti-inflammatory; reduces UV-induced melanogenesis; 500 mg BD with piperine; reduces PIH formation from inflammatory lesions
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors HIGH UV INDEX YEAR-ROUND India's tropical location means high UV year-round; UV radiation is (1) the primary trigger that darkens PIH by stimulating melanocyte re-activation in affected areas, and (2) inhibits PIH resolution; patients not using SPF50+ sunscreen after acne treatment will develop PIH on virtually every healed lesion; PIH prevention = UV prevention FAIRNESS CREAM CULTURE — TOXIC REMEDIES FOR PIH India's billion-dollar fairness cream indus
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations PIH IN DARK SKIN IS NOT COSMETIC — IT IS A MAJOR DERMATOLOGICAL BURDEN: Dermatology training in India often under-emphasises PIH as a clinical priority because it is not 'active disease.' However, for patients with Fitzpatrick III-V skin (the majority of India's population),

diseases.name: Chronic Urticaria
tests: DIAGNOSIS IS CLINICAL — INVESTIGATIONS ARE TARGETED
Chronic urticaria diagnosis: wheals lasting <24 hours (each), pruritic, >6 weeks duration; history-taking for physical triggers (cold, exercise, pressure, dermographism)
UAS7 — Urticaria Activity Score 7 daily diary assessment — severity documentation
INITIAL BASIC SCREEN (Low yield; done for baseline & to identify reversible causes)
CBC + ESR — eosinophilia (parasitic trigger?); elevated CRP (infection, abscess, occult malignancy — rarely)
Thyroid function + anti-TPO antibodies — Hashimoto's association; 20% CU; subclinical hypothyroidism coexisting
Total IgE — elevated in atopic; does not change CSU treatment unless specific allergy suspected
H. pylori stool antigen test / urea breath test — eradication may resolve CU in positive patients
Stool microscopy — Blastocystis, Ascaris, hookworm — parasitic triggers
ANA, complement C3/C4 — if SLE suspected
generic_medicines: STEP 1 — SECOND-GENERATION NON-SEDATING H1-ANTIHISTAMINES (FIRST-LINE ALWAYS)
Cetirizine 10 mg OD — widely available; mild sedation; excellent for urticaria; well tolerated
Loratadine 10 mg OD — least sedating; good for daytime/working patients
Fexofenadine 120–180 mg OD — least CNS penetration; virtually non-sedating; preferred for driving/operating machinery
Levocetirizine 5 mg OD (evening) — highly potent H1 blocker; more sedating than Fexo or Loratadine
Bilastine 20 mg OD — newer; non-sedating; P-glycoprotein substrate (not taken with grapefruit juice)
STEP 2 — UPDOSING (4× STANDARD DOSE — LICENSED EAACI RECOMMENDATION)

If standard dose failed: increase to 4× standard dose (e.g., Cetirizine 10 mg TDS, Loratadine 10 mg BD–TDS, Fexofenadine 180 mg TDS) — proven in multiple RCTs to increase response rate in CSU without proportional increase in side effects; do this before escalating to biologics
STEP 3 — ADD-ON THERAPY IF HIGH-DOSE ANTIHISTAMINE INADEQUATE
Montelukast 10 mg OD — leukotriene receptor antagonist; add to H1-antihistamine; particularly useful in NSAID-sensitive CU
H2 blocker (Ranitidine 150 mg BD or Famotidine 20 mg BD) — add to H1; complementary antihistamine effect; modest benefit
Cyclosporin 2.5–5 mg/kg/day — immunosuppressive; severe refractory CSU; effective but toxicity monitoring (BP, renal function, CBC); short-medium term bridge
STEP 4 — OMALIZUMAB (THIRD-LINE — SEVERE REFRACTORY CSU)

Omalizumab 300 mg subcutaneous injection every 4 weeks — anti-IgE monoclonal antibody; FDA and European MA approved specifically for CU not controlled by antihistamines; 65–85% complete control; given 6–12 cycles then reassess; available India at specialist centres; expensive
ANGIOEDEMA/ACUTE URTICARIA RESCUE
janaushadhi_medicines: Hydroxyzine 25 mg tablet (sedating; sleep-disturbed CU, nighttime)
ayurvedic_medicines: ANTI-HISTAMINIC AND ANTI-PRURITIC

Haridra (Turmeric / Curcumin) — anti-histaminergic; inhibits mast cell degranulation; reduces histamine release; 500 mg curcumin BD is most evidence-based; Haridrakhand — classical Ayurvedic compound for Sheetapitta (Urticaria = Sheetapitta in Ayurveda; cold-triggered, pitta-vitiated blood condition)

Haridrakhand — classical compound formulation: Haridra + Milk + Ghee + sugar — direct Ayurvedic therapeutic reference for Sheetapitta (urticaria); traditionally given as warm preparation; anti-allergic activity
Neem (Azadirachta indica) — anti-histaminic; anti-inflammatory; blood purifier; reduces allergic hypersensitivity; Nimbarishta 10 mL BD
Triphala + Trikatu — Pitta-cleansing combination; reduces allergic inflammatory cascade
CHRONIC URTICARIA (SHEETAPITTA) SYSTEMIC
Gandhak Rasayana — purified sulphur; anti-allergic; anti-mast cell; classical chronic skin disease management; 500 mg BD
Kaishore Guggulu — anti-inflammatory; anti-allergic; Guggul-based; reduces Tridosha in chronic recurrent urticaria
Arogyavardhini Vati — liver and blood purifier; systemic anti-inflammatory; H. pylori / parasitic-trigger CU benefit through GI normalisation
Mustakarishta / Khadirarishta — Ayurvedic tonic formulations for chronic skin allergic conditions
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors HELICOBACTER PYLORI AND BLASTOCYSTIS H. pylori eradication shown to improve or resolve CU in several Indian studies and global meta-analyses; H. pylori prevalence India 50–70% (among highest globally) — H. pylori testing (UBT or stool antigen) and treatment in CU patients with GI symptoms or as empiric screening may significantly improve CU control Blastocystis hominis — common gut parasite in India; tropical prevalence; documented association with CSU
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations UPDOSING ANTIHISTAMINES TO 4× IS THE MOST UNDERUSED GUIDELINE IN INDIA: EAACI (European Academy of Allergy and Clinical Immunology), BSACI, and WAO guidelines all recommend increasing second-generation H1-antihistamines to 4× standard dose before escalating to biologics or i

diseases.name: Keloid And Hypertrophic Scars
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS
Diagnosis is clinical — visual appearance, extension beyond wound margin (keloid), site, patient history; no specific blood test
CLINICAL CRITERIA FOR KELOID vs HYPERTROPHIC SCAR
Keloid: extends BEYOND original wound; does NOT regress spontaneously; recurs after excision without adjuvant therapy; pruritic and painful
Hypertrophic: confined WITHIN wound margin; may spontaneously improve within 12–24 months; lower recurrence after excision
DERMOSCOPY
White/pink structureless areas; loss of normal skin markings; branching vessels; hyperpigmented margins in Indian skin; helps characterise activity
HISTOPATHOLOGY (rarely needed — only for diagnostic uncertainty or atypical features)
Keloid: thick hyalinised glassy collagen bundles (pathognomonic); fibroblast proliferation; myofibroblasts
HS: wavy, more ordered collagen fibres; less hyalinisation; distinguishes from keloid histologically
generic_medicines: FIRST-LINE — INTRALESIONAL CORTICOSTEROID

Intralesional Triamcinolone Acetonide (ILK) 10–40 mg/mL — gold standard single-modality treatment; injected directly into keloid/HS; every 4–6 weeks; reduces collagen synthesis and fibroblast proliferation; maximum clinical effect at 40 mg/mL; effective but 50% recurrence; multiple sessions (5–10) needed
Side effects of ILK: skin atrophy, depigmentation, telangiectasia if injected superficially or in excess
COMBINATION TREATMENT — MORE EFFECTIVE THAN MONOTHERAPY

ILK + Silicone gel sheet (SGS) — most evidence-based non-surgical combination; silicone sheet worn 12+ hours/day; begins immediately after wound healing; compresses scar, reduces water loss, alters cytokine environment
Post-excision + Immediate radiotherapy — most effective for large keloids; surgery excises bulk then RT prevents recurrence; RT within 24–48 hours of surgery; RT dose 12–15 Gy in 3–5 fractions
Cryotherapy — liquid nitrogen cryospray directly on keloid; 1–3 freeze-thaw cycles monthly; reduces bulk; combine with ILK
Pressure therapy — customised compression garments for post-burn and extensive HS; 20–25 mmHg continuous pressure; 6–12+ months
TOPICAL AGENTS
Silicone gel (topical) — alternative to sheets for small/facial scars; apply BD; improves hydration, flattens early HS/keloid
Onion extract gel (Mederma) — anti-inflammatory; anti-fibrotic; modest evidence; well marketed in India
Imiquimod 5% cream — TLR stimulant; anti-fibrotic; applied BD after excision to prevent recurrence; some RCT evidence
SYSTEMIC
Low-dose Isotretinoin (10–20 mg/day) + ILK — synergistic for acne keloid; reduces sebum and new acne lesion formation that would otherwise trigger new keloids
EMERGING
ayurvedic_medicines: ANTI-FIBROTIC TOPICAL
Aloe vera gel — anti-fibrotic; anti-inflammatory; MMP (matrix metalloproteinase) enhancing; reduces collagen over-deposition; topical fresh aloe gel BD; widely used India; well tolerated

Haldi (Turmeric/Curcumin) paste — anti-inflammatory; inhibits NF-kB; reduces TGF-beta1 fibrosis signalling; turmeric paste applied topically; oral curcumin 500 mg BD reduces systemic inflammation that drives keloid recurrence
Onion extract (Pyaz) — flavonoids quercetin and kaempferol; anti-fibrotic; reduces keloid fibroblast activity in vitro; topical application of onion extract BD; Mederma is commercially processed form
Castor oil (Arandi) — ricinoleic acid; anti-inflammatory; penetrating; traditional Indian scar massage oil; promote collagen remodelling
Neem leaf paste — anti-inflammatory; reduces Pitta-driven inflammatory scar formation; healing
SYSTEMIC ANTI-FIBROTIC

Kanchanar Guggulu — most relevant Ayurvedic formulation for fibrotic/hypertrophic conditions; Kanchanar bark with Guggul; anti-fibrotic; reduces excessive tissue growth (Granthi — tumor/growth in Ayurveda); 2 tablets BD with warm water; classical prescription for keloid and fibrotic lumps
Guggul (Commiphora mukul) — guggulipids; anti-inflammatory; anti-fibrotic; reduces TGF-beta activity
Varuna (Crataeva nurvala) — reduces Granthi; fibrotic tissue reduction
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors FITZPATRICK III-V SKIN — HIGHEST KELOID RISK Dark-skinned populations including Indians have 3–15x higher keloid incidence compared to Fitzpatrick I-II skin; genetic basis; higher TGF-beta1 activity and reduced matrix metalloproteinase activity in darker skin fibroblasts; BRCA pathway variants associated BCG VACCINATION KELOID India's universal BCG vaccination at birth creates deltoid scar; in keloid-prone individuals (especially those with positive fa
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations KELOID EXCISION WITHOUT ADJUVANT THERAPY HAS 50–100% RECURRENCE RATE: Simple surgical excision of a keloid without post-operative adjuvant treatment is not a treatment — it is the beginning of a new cycle. The very trauma of surgery stimulates further fibroproliferation, and

diseases.name: Hirsutism
tests: SERUM ANDROGENS
Total testosterone — elevated (>2 nmol/L or >0.6 ng/mL) indicates hyperandrogenism; normal in idiopathic hirsutism
Free testosterone — more sensitive; protein-bound portion doesn't explain symptoms
DHEAS (Dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate) — adrenal androgen; elevated suggests adrenal source (NC-CAH, adrenal tumour)
SHBG (Sex hormone binding globulin) — decreased in insulin resistance → more free testosterone available
Free Androgen Index (FAI) = Total T × 100 / SHBG — higher sensitivity for functional hyperandrogenism
PCOS WORKUP
LH/FSH ratio — LH:FSH >2:1 in PCOS (though not diagnostic criterion anymore)
Pelvic ultrasound — polycystic ovarian morphology (PCOM): ≥20 follicles 2–9 mm per ovary on modern US
Fasting glucose + 2-hr OGTT + fasting insulin — insulin resistance
generic_medicines: NON-PHARMACOLOGICAL (FIRST-LINE FOR COSMETIC CONCERN)
Physical hair removal: threading, waxing, shaving, bleaching — temporary; do NOT cause permanent increase in hair growth
Laser hair removal: Nd:YAG 1064 nm or Diode 810 nm — safe for dark Indian skin; 6–8 sessions monthly; permanent hair reduction; gold standard cosmetic treatment for Indian hirsutism
Eflornithine 13.9% cream — topically applied enzyme inhibitor (ODC ornithine decarboxylase); slows facial hair regrowth; not permanent; combined with laser; BD application
PHARMACOLOGICAL ANTI-ANDROGEN THERAPY

Combined oral contraceptive (COC) — first-line pharmacological; reduces LH → less ovarian androgen + increases SHBG → less free testosterone; use anti-androgenic progestogen: Cyproterone acetate (Diane-35/Dianette), Drospirenone (Yasmin/Yaz), Desogestrel

Spironolactone 50–200 mg/day — aldosterone antagonist/antiandrogen; directly blocks androgen receptor in hair follicle; very effective for hirsutism; requires contraception (anti-androgenic risk to male fetus); monitor potassium and BP; improves over 6–12 months
Flutamide 125–500 mg/day — pure androgen receptor antagonist; very effective; hepatotoxicity risk requires LFT monitoring; not widely used
Cyproterone acetate 10–25 mg/day (reversed sequential regimen) — powerful antiandrogen; with ethinyl oestradiol; available India
INSULIN SENSITISING (ESSENTIAL IN PCOS HIRSUTISM)
Metformin 500–1500 mg BD — improves insulin resistance → reduces ovarian androgen production; slow improvement in hirsutism; excellent for menstrual regulation and metabolic PCOS
Inositol (Myo-inositol 2 g + D-chiro-inositol 50 mg BD) — insulin sensitiser; emerging PCOS evidence; safe; improves hormone profile
ADRENAL NC-CAH
Prednisolone 2.5–5 mg at night — suppresses ACTH-driven adrenal androgen in NC-CAH
janaushadhi_medicines: Combined oral contraceptive (OCP with cyproterone acetate)
Fluconazole (for concurrent Candida from OCP use)
ayurvedic_medicines: ANTI-ANDROGENIC / HAIR-REDUCING

Spearmint Tea (Mentha spicata) — most clinically documented natural anti-androgen; two RCTs showed spearmint tea 240 mL BD for 30 days significantly reduced total testosterone and free testosterone in PCOS women; anti-androgenic (reduces LH-stimulated testosterone); grows naturally in India; available as herbal tea; excellent adjunct
Methi (Fenugreek / Trigonella foenum-graecum) — furostanolic saponins; anti-androgenic; improves insulin sensitivity; reduces testosterone in PCOS women (FenuSmart™ trials); 500 mg–1 g/day
Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus) — phytoestrogen; female hormonal balancer; reduces androgen dominance; regulates menstrual cycle; 500 mg BD
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — adaptogen; reduces cortisol-driven adrenal androgen excess; reduces testosterone in some trials; regulates PCOS hormonal axis; adrenal hirsutism adjunct
PCOS / INSULIN RESISTANCE
Cinnamon (Dalchini / Cinnamomum zeylanicum) — insulin sensitiser; lowers HOMA-IR and testosterone in PCOS trials; 1–3 g/day in warm water or as supplement
Vijaysar (Pterocarpus marsupium) — Ayurvedic hypoglycaemic; insulin sensitising; reduces androgen-driving hyperinsulinaemia; Vijaysar water from wooden tumbler — traditional India diabetic remedy
Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — immune modulator; anti-inflammatory; reduces adrenal stress-driven androgen
TOPICAL HAIR GROWTH REDUCTION
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors PCOS EPIDEMIC IN INDIA PCOS affects 10–22% of reproductive-age Indian women — among the highest global estimates; hirsutism is the most visible and distressing manifestation; PCOS in India has higher metabolic severity (greater insulin resistance, higher BMI, metabolic syndrome risk) compared to Western cohorts RACIAL HAIR DENSITY VARIATION Indian women having darker, thicker terminal hair and higher baseline follicular sensitivity to androgens; even m
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations HIRSUTISM IS A SYMPTOM REQUIRING CAUSE IDENTIFICATION — DO NOT JUMP TO HAIR REMOVAL ONLY: A woman presenting with hirsutism in India often wants 'treatment' meaning laser or anti-androgen cream. While addressing the cosmetic concern is valid, hirsutism is a clinical sign of

diseases.name: Hyperhidrosis
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS

Primary focal hyperhidrosis: bilateral, focal, symmetrical sweating; onset <25 years; at least 1 episode/week; no night sweating; family history; cessation during sleep; interference with daily activities
MINOR'S IODINE-STARCH TEST

Iodine solution painted on affected area; allowed to dry; starch powder applied; sweating areas turn dark blue/black (starch + iodine + sweat reaction); standard test for quantifying affected area for Botox injection planning
GRAVIMETRIC SWEAT MEASUREMENT
Filter paper weighed before and after 5-minute application to axilla/palm; >50 mg/5 min = significant hyperhidrosis; research and Botox planning
SKIN CONDUCTANCE TESTING
Sudometer; electrodermal activity; correlates with sweating rate
SECONDARY HYPERHIDROSIS INVESTIGATIONS (when generalised/nocturnal sweating)
FBC, ESR, CRP — infection, lymphoma
generic_medicines: TOPICAL ANTIPERSPIRANT (FIRST-LINE ALL TYPES)

Aluminium chloride hexahydrate 20–25% solution (Driclor) — first-line topical; apply before sleep on dry skin; occlusion optional; mechanism: aluminium salts plug eccrine duct; weekly maintenance after control; axillary and palmar; may irritate if applied to moist or recently shaved skin
Aluminium chloride 15% lotion — milder; useful when 20% causes irritation
IONTOPHORESIS (PALMAR/PLANTAR)

Direct galvanic current (15–20 mA) applied to hands/feet in water bath; mechanism: current + ions block eccrine sweat delivery; 3x/week for 3–4 weeks induction then maintenance; dedicated home devices available (Idromed, Hidrex); very effective for palmar/plantar (70–80%); no systemic side effects
BOTULINUM TOXIN (AXILLARY/PALMAR GOLD STANDARD)

Botulinum toxin A (Botox 50–100 units / axilla; Dysport equivalent) — subcutaneous injection at 1–2 cm intervals across affected area (Minor's starch test guides injection sites); axillary effect 4–6 months; palmar effect 3–4 months; palmar needs nerve block anaesthesia (painful); highly effective: 80–90% HDSS improvement
ORAL SYSTEMIC

Glycopyrrolate 1–2 mg BD — anticholinergic; reduces all eccrine sweating; helpful for craniofacial and generalised; SE: dry mouth, blurred vision, urinary retention, constipation, heat intolerance; use caution in hot Indian climate
Propranolol 10–40 mg BD — beta-blocker; reduces emotional/anxiety-triggered sweating; helps anxiety-associated hyperhidrosis; also reduces social performance anxiety component
Oxybutynin 2.5–5 mg BD — antimuscarinic; tolerable alternative to glycopyrrolate; similar side effect profile
SURGICAL

Endoscopic thoracic sympathectomy (ETS) — T2–T3 sympathetic chain ablation; permanent cure for palmar/axillary hyperhidrosis; RISK: compensatory hyperhidrosis (severe trunk/back sweating) occurs in 50–80%; Gustatory sweating; available at thoracic surgery centres India
Microwaving (MiraDry) — non-invasive microwave energy to axilla; destroys eccrine glands permanently; single/two sessions; available select India cities
Laser (diode 810 nm, 1064 nm) — subdermal eccrine ablation; axillary
janaushadhi_medicines: Botulinum toxin A 100 IU vial (specialist use)
ayurvedic_medicines: TOPICAL

Alum powder (Phitkari / aluminium potassium sulphate) — traditional Indian antiperspirant; applied to damp axilla/feet; aluminium salts reduce eccrine secretion; same mechanism as commercial aluminium chloride antiperspirants; widely available India; natural, economical; effective for mild-moderate axillary hyperhidrosis
Sandalwood powder (Chandan) — astringent; cooling; reduces sweat and odour; traditional Indian dusting powder for heat and sweat; chandan-based underarm powder
Neem powder — antibacterial; reduces osmidrosis (sweat odour); reduces bacterial decomposition of sweat
Rose water spray — cooling; astringent; temporary reduction of palmar and axillary sweat; traditional Indian cooling remedy
Multani Mitti (Fuller's earth) paste — absorbent; draws sweat; cooling; face and scalp pack for craniofacial hyperhidrosis
SYSTEMIC
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — adaptogen; reduces autonomic stress response; reduces emotional-trigger sweating; 300–600 mg extract BD
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — anxiolytic; reduces anxiety-driven hyperhidrosis; GABA modulation
Shatavari — cooling; reduces Pitta excess; summer hyperhidrosis; women's hormonal sweating (perimenopausal)
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors TROPICAL CLIMATE India's hot humid climate means primary hyperhidrosis is exacerbated physiologically; patients with primary hyperhidrosis in India may sweat proportionally more than equivalent patients in cooler climates; clothing culture (synthetics, saris — less ventilating) compounds axillary hyperhidrosis SOCIAL AND PROFESSIONAL IMPACT IN INDIA Palmar hyperhidrosis in software engineers (keyboard/mouse slipping), teachers/lecturers, surgeons, musi
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations ALWAYS EXCLUDE SECONDARY HYPERHIDROSIS BEFORE TREATING AS PRIMARY — ESPECIALLY TB IN INDIA: Generalised night sweats in an Indian patient must be investigated as TB until proven otherwise. India bears 25% of the world's annual TB burden — 2.5 million new cases per year. TB c

diseases.name: Perioral Dermatitis
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS
POD diagnosed clinically by: perioral/periocular/perinasal papulopustular eruption; characteristic lip-sparing clear zone; history of topical steroid use; absence of comedones
DERMOSCOPY
Erythema, fine scales, superficial papules; no comedones; distinguishes from acne; hair follicle involvement pattern
SKIN BIOPSY (rarely needed)
Perifollicular lymphocytic and histiocytic infiltrate; spongiosis; granulomatous variant: sarcoidal or tuberculoid granulomas (without caseation); PAS-negative (no fungal hyphae)
KOH PREPARATION
Rule out tinea faciei (dermatophyte facial infection) as mimic
PATCH TEST
If contact allergen-driven POD suspected (toothpaste, cosmetic ingredient); fragrance mix, balsam of Peru, nickel — common facial contact allergens
generic_medicines: ZERO THERAPY — THE FIRST STEP

ZERO THERAPY: Stop ALL topical steroids, ALL heavy moisturisers, occlusive make-up, fluoride toothpaste on perioral area; stop any OTC fairness creams; this alone causes 30–40% clearance but with initial rebound worsening — patients must be explicitly warned
TOPICAL TREATMENT
Metronidazole 0.75–1% gel/cream — first-line topical; BD for 6–8 weeks; anti-inflammatory, anti-microbial; effective perioral dermatitis standard treatment
Azelaic acid 15–20% gel — anti-inflammatory; anti-microbial; good tolerability; safe alternative to metronidazole

Pimecrolimus 1% cream — calcineurin inhibitor; useful during topical steroid withdrawal phase; anti-inflammatory without steroid; prevents rebound; apply BD during the first 4–6 weeks of steroid cessation
Tacrolimus 0.1% ointment — similar calcineurin inhibitor; alternative to pimecrolimus
Clindamycin 1% solution/gel — topical antibiotic; second-line if metronidazole insufficient; always combine with BPO-free regimen
Ivermectin 1% cream — anti-Demodex; useful if Demodex-associated POD or concurrent rosacea features
SYSTEMIC FOR MODERATE-SEVERE
Doxycycline 50–100 mg OD for 6–8 weeks — anti-inflammatory dose; very effective for moderate-severe POD; India standard of care for persistent POD
Azithromycin 500 mg 3 days/week for 4–6 weeks — alternative to doxycycline; good India data
Tetracycline 250–500 mg OD–BD — alternative; older generation tetracycline; effective
MAINTAIN
After clearance, continue Metronidazole or Azelaic acid 3x/week for 3 months as maintenance
ayurvedic_medicines: TOPICAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY

Aloe vera gel — cooling; anti-inflammatory; reduces perioral erythema and burning sensation; apply BD to perioral area; well tolerated; gentle on perioral sensitive skin during steroid withdrawal period
Multani Mitti (Fuller's earth) face pack — absorbs excess sebum; anti-inflammatory; gentle drying of micropustules; apply as thin paste to perioral area; rinse after 10–15 minutes
Neem (Azadirachta indica) — anti-bacterial; anti-inflammatory; diluted neem water rinse on perioral skin; reduces bacterial component of POD
Chandan (Sandalwood) + Rose water paste — cooling; anti-inflammatory; reduces Pitta-driven erythema and burning sensation
SYSTEMIC PITTA-REDUCING
Haridra (Turmeric/Curcumin) — anti-inflammatory; reduces Pitta; 500 mg BD curcumin; reduces systemic inflammatory drivers
Triphala — systemic cleanser; Pitta balancing; gut-skin axis improvement
Manjistha — blood purifier; reduces facial inflammation
LIFESTYLE
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors TOPICAL STEROID MISUSE — PRIMARY INDIA CAUSE POD is epidemic in India because of the near-universal misuse of facial topical corticosteroids in OTC products, prescribed for non-dermatological purposes by GPs, and self-prescribed. Clobetasol propionate (Class I steroid — most potent) is the most common causative agent in Indian POD because it is present in fairness cream formulations and Quadriderm-type combination creams widely available OTC. Any woman
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations STOP ALL TOPICAL STEROIDS — THE REBOUND IS TEMPORARY BUT THE RECOVERY IS PERMANENT: The most critical management step for steroid-induced perioral dermatitis is complete and permanent cessation of all facial topical corticosteroids — no exceptions, no tapering slowly with th

diseases.name: Striae Distensae
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS
Striae diagnosed clinically and require no specific investigation in typical presentations; history confirms trigger (pregnancy, growth spurt, steroid use, weight change)
WOOD'S LAMP
Striae albae (white) enhanced under Wood's lamp — delineates extent on light skin
DERMOSCOPY
Striae rubrae: linear vascular structures, erythema; striae albae: white linear areas, loss of normal skin markings, collagen fibres visible
SKIN BIOPSY (rarely needed)
Thinned epidermis; flattened rete ridges; collagen fibres horizontally oriented, parallel; decreased elastin fibres; confirm Cushing's if suspected
CUSHING'S SCREENING (if wide violaceous horizontal abdominal striae)
24-hour urinary free cortisol
generic_medicines: STRIAE RUBRAE — MOST RESPONSIVE PHASE
