Tretinoin 0.025–0.05–0.1% cream — topical retinoid; stimulates collagen synthesis; best evidence for striae rubrae; apply at night; apply only on affected striae (photosensitising); avoid in pregnancy/breastfeeding
Glycolic acid 10–20% lotion/cream — alpha-hydroxy acid; exfoliates; stimulates new collagen; safe in pregnancy when used topically; combine with Tretinoin alternating (not together — irritation)
Hyaluronic acid + Centella asiatica combination — stimulates collagen; reduces striae width; some RCT evidence in striae gravidarum prevention
STRIAE ALBAE — PROCEDURAL TREATMENTS MOST EFFECTIVE

Fractional CO2 laser — ablative fractional photothermolysis; gold standard for established striae albae; stimulates collagen remodelling; each session 30–50% improvement; 3–5 sessions monthly required; safe on Indian skin with Fitzpatrick settings
Fractional RF microneedling (Morpheus8, Vivace) — radiofrequency + mechanical micro-injury; excellent for striae albae; stimulates collagen and elastin; darker skin-safe
Platelet-Rich Plasma (PRP) injection into striae — growth factors stimulate collagen synthesis; India widely available; 3–6 monthly sessions
Non-ablative fractional laser (1550 nm Fraxel, 1540 nm) — stimulates dermal collagen; lighter treatment; fewer sessions for mild striae
Microneedling (dermaroller / Dermapen) — multiple collagen induction sessions; affordable in India; 4–6 monthly sessions
PREVENTION (During pregnancy — striae gravidarum)

Moisturiser massage daily — daily abdominal massage with any emollient appears to reduce striae gravidarum incidence slightly; Trofolastin cream (Centella asiatica + hydrolyzed elastin) has RCT evidence for prevention
Adequate hydration + Vitamin E + C supplementation orally
Silicone oil application — experimental; emerging evidence
janaushadhi_medicines: Vitamin E oil / Vitamin E 400 IU capsule
ayurvedic_medicines: TOPICAL AND MASSAGE OILS
Coconut oil (Nariyal tail) — traditional Indian pregnancy massage oil; moisturises; prevents dehydration-related skin fragility; massage reduces skin tension; widely used India; safe in pregnancy
Almond oil (Badam tail) — rich in Vitamin E; emollient; anti-oxidant; traditional striae prevention during pregnancy; gentle massage increases local blood flow
Sesame oil (Til tail) — traditional Abhyanga base oil; Vitamin E and antioxidants; Ayurvedic recommendation for skin flexibility maintenance
Kumkumadi Tailam — saffron-based oil; contains multiple anti-inflammatory and collagen-supporting botanicals; post-delivery striae rubrae; nighttime application
Castor oil — ricinoleic acid; anti-inflammatory; penetrating; promotes collagen turnover; traditionally used for body skin regeneration
CENTELLA ASIATICA — STRONGEST EVIDENCE

Gotu Kola (Manduka-parni / Brahmi / Centella asiatica) — asiaticoside and madecassoside; specifically documented to stimulate Type I and III collagen synthesis in human fibroblasts; published RCTs for striae gravidarum prevention; oral Centella 60–120 mg BD or as topical cream; 'Trofolastin' cream contains Centella asiatica + hydrolysed elastin — this is an Ayurvedic plant used in conventional dermatology trials
Amla (Emblica officinalis) oral — Vitamin C; cofactor for prolyl hydroxylase in collagen synthesis; essential for collagen integrity; 500 mg–1 g/day
Haridra (Turmeric) oral — anti-inflammatory; reduces striae-associated skin inflammation; Curcumin 500 mg BD
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors MULTIPLE PREGNANCIES Indian social and cultural factors (including joint family pressure, limited family planning uptake in rural areas) mean multiple pregnancies are common; each pregnancy adds new striae gravidarum; progressive abdominal striae accumulate TOPICAL STEROID STRIAE Long-term application of potent topical steroids to body skin (groin, trunk, thighs) — from misuse of Betnovate, Quadriderm, or OTC fairness creams on body — causes steroid-in
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations STRIAE TREATMENT IS MOST EFFECTIVE DURING THE RUBRAE (RED) PHASE — ACT EARLY: Striae rubrae (red/pink/purple) are actively forming lesions with ongoing vascular changes and relatively preserved tissue architecture — they are significantly more responsive to all treatments (t

diseases.name: Erectile Dysfunction
tests: CLINICAL HISTORY AND IIEF-5 QUESTIONNAIRE
IIEF-5 (International Index of Erectile Function — 5 item) — validated; 5 questions; scores 5–25; primary diagnostic tool; distinguishes severity; also documents treatment response
NOCTURNAL PENILE TUMESCENCE (NPT) TEST

Rigiscan device — home-based; measures frequency, duration, rigidity of overnight penile tumescence; PRESERVED NPT = psychogenic ED; ABSENT NPT = organic ED; confirms diagnosis direction before investgation
BLOOD TESTS
Fasting plasma glucose OR HbA1c — diabetes
Fasting lipid profile (TC, LDL, HDL, TG) — dyslipidaemia
Serum testosterone (Total testosterone, early morning fasting) — hypogonadism; confirm LOW <300 ng/dL
SHBG + Albumin (calculate Free testosterone if total borderline)
FSH, LH, Prolactin — if low testosterone found; pituitary axis assessment
generic_medicines: PDE5 INHIBITORS — CORNERSTONE TREATMENT
Sildenafil (Viagra) 25–50–100 mg — on-demand oral; taken 45–60 min before; food (fatty meal) delays absorption; avoid with nitrates; first-line; 6–8 hours duration

Tadalafil 10–20 mg on-demand — 2.5–5 mg daily (continuously) — longer duration (36 hours on-demand); daily low dose maintains constant penile tissue oxygenation and allows spontaneous intercourse without timing; PREFERRED for diabetic ED and post-prostatectomy ED due to 24-hour cavernosal oxygenation benefit
Vardenafil 10–20 mg — meal-independent; on-demand
Avanafil 100–200 mg — fastest onset (15 min); newest; less interaction with CYP3A4
CONTRAINDICATION: ALL PDE5 inhibitors + nitrates (GTN, isosorbide) = ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATION → fatal hypotension; also caution with alpha-blockers (doxazosin) — take 4-hour gap minimum
HORMONAL TREATMENT

Testosterone replacement (if hypogonadism confirmed): Testosterone undecanoate injection 1000 mg/12 weeks; testosterone gel 25–50 mg/day; improves libido + ED in hypogonadal men; must screen prostate and haematocrit before starting
Cabergoline — if hyperprolactinaemia confirmed cause
PSYCHOSEXUAL THERAPY
Masters and Johnson sensate focus; CBT; couples therapy — for psychogenic ED; often combined with PDE5 inhibitor to break performance anxiety cycle
VACUUM ERECTION DEVICE (VED)
Mechanical; no drug; useful when PDE5 contraindicated; good for elderly, post-prostatectomy men; creates passive erection via negative pressure
SECOND/THIRD LINE
Intracavernosal Alprostadil (PGE1 injection) — 2nd line; self-injection; very effective any cause; 80–90% response
ayurvedic_medicines: VAJIKARANA RASAYANA

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — adaptogen; reduces cortisol; increases testosterone in stressed men; improves sexual performance in clinical trials (IIEF score improvement in RCT); doses 300–600 mg root extract BD; reduces anxiety component of psychogenic ED; widely used and validated

Shilajit (Asphaltum bituminous) — fulvic acid mineral complex; increases testosterone (RCT data); improves mitochondrial energy metabolism; traditional vajikarana; 250–500 mg BD; evidence for testosterone elevation and sperm quality in infertile men
Safed Musli (Chlorophytum borivilianum) — root extract; traditionally used Vajikarana; adaptogen; some animal and human studies for testicular testosterone production and sexual performance

Gokshura (Tribulus terrestris) — increases LH-like activity; testosterone elevation in some but not all studies; marketed as testosterone booster; evidence weaker than Ashwagandha/Shilajit; traditionally used
Kapikacchu (Mucuna pruriens) — L-DOPA precursor → dopaminergic enhancement → libido and psychogenic ED; also testosterone elevation; has RCT evidence in infertile men; dopamine pathway modulation
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Vajikarana Ghrita — classical Ayurvedic compound; milk + ghee base with vajikarana herbs; traditionally prescribed for sexual debility
Ashwagandha Lehyam — Ashwagandha-based confection; compliance-friendly
Chyawanprash — antioxidant rasayana; improves vitality; Amla-based Vitamin C; overall rejuvenation
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors DIABETES EPIDEMIC India has 77+ million diabetics (2nd highest globally); diabetic ED occurs in 35–90% of male diabetics; multifactorial: (1) autonomic neuropathy → impaired NO release; (2) endothelial dysfunction → vascular; (3) low testosterone — obesity-related; (4) psychological burden of chronic illness; ED is often first presenting complaint that leads to diabetes diagnosis in Indian men TOBACCO USE India: 28% of adults use tobacco (smoking + smo
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations ERECTILE DYSFUNCTION IS A CARDIOVASCULAR WARNING SIGNAL — MUST NOT BE TREATED IN ISOLATION: ED in men under 60 is a cardiovascular sentinel event. The pathophysiology is shared: endothelial dysfunction and atherosclerosis affect small-diameter penile arteries (1–2 mm) earlie

diseases.name: Premature Ejaculation
tests: STOPWATCH IELT MEASUREMENT

Patient and partner measure actual time from penetration to ejaculation with stopwatch; 3–5 representative encounters; mean IELT <1 min = lifelong PE; <3 min = acquired PE; this is the gold standard diagnostic measurement
PREMATURE EJACULATION DIAGNOSTIC TOOL (PEDT)
5-item validated questionnaire; scores 0–20; ≥11 = PE; 9–10 = probable PE; standard diagnostic and outcome tool
Thyroid stimulating hormone — to exclude hyperthyroidism as acquired PE cause (thyroid hormones reduce ejaculatory latency threshold)
PROSTATE ASSESSMENT
Prostate-Specific Antigen (PSA); Urine microscopy/culture; Expressed Prostatic Secretion (EPS); Transrectal Ultrasound (TRUS) — if prostatitis suspected as cause of acquired PE
TESTOSTERONE
Serum testosterone — low testosterone associated with lower ejaculatory control; check if libido also reduced
NEUROLOGICAL ASSESSMENT
generic_medicines: ON-DEMAND ORAL

Dapoxetine 30 mg (up-titrate to 60 mg if needed) — short-acting SSRI; taken 1–3 hours before intercourse; only licensed on-demand pharmacological treatment for PE; increases ejaculatory latency 2–4x over baseline; does not require daily dosing; safe for intermittent use; SE: nausea, dizziness, headache — mostly mild; caution with syncope history; contraindication with MAOIs and thioridazine
DAILY SSRI (CONTINUOUS)

Paroxetine 10–40 mg OD — most potent SSRI for PE; highest ejaculatory delay among SSRIs; daily use; takes 2 weeks to reach effect; SE: sexual side effects (ED, anorgasmia), weight gain, discontinuation syndrome if stopped abruptly
Sertraline 25–200 mg OD — effective; better tolerability than paroxetine; daily
Fluoxetine 20–40 mg OD — PE treatment; longer half-life; good option
Citalopram 20–40 mg OD — alternative
TOPICAL ANAESTHETICS

Lidocaine 2.5% + Prilocaine 2.5% (EMLA cream) — apply 20 min before sex; remove before penetration; reduces glans sensitivity; caution — don't leave on too long (risk of over-numbing; loss of erection)
Lidocaine 4% spray (Fortacin spray) — EU-licensed; ON-demand; 3 metered puffs to glans; very effective; minimal systemic absorption
Topical Benzocaine wipes/gel — available OTC in India; similar mechanism
BEHAVIOURAL TECHNIQUES
Stop-Start technique (Semans) — partner stimulates to near-ejaculation, stops stimulation, resumes after 30–45 seconds; 3 cycles per session; builds ejaculatory control gradually
Squeeze technique (Masters & Johnson) — squeeze the glans corona at near-ejaculation point; breaks ejaculatory reflex; partner or self applied
Pelvic floor exercises — Kegel exercises for men; bulbocavernosus and ischiocavernosus muscle strengthening; recent RCT: 12 weeks pelvic floor exercises → 82% PE patients gained normal control
janaushadhi_medicines: Tramadol 50 mg tablet (as last resort — analgesic with 5-HT3 effect)
ayurvedic_medicines: NERVINE TONICS — REDUCING HYPERSENSITIVITY AND ANXIETY

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — adaptogen; reduces sympathetic nervous system hyperactivation that drives PE; reduces cortisol and anxiety; clinical trial data shows improvement in sex latency and satisfaction in men with PE alongside anxiety; 300–600 mg BD
Kapikacchu (Mucuna pruriens) — L-DOPA precursor; dopaminergic modulation; helps psychogenic PE; also serotonergic indirect effects; increases ejaculatory threshold; 500 mg BD
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — anxiolytic; GABAergic modulation; reduces performance anxiety; nervous system calming; reduces fear of failure that perpetuates PE
Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus) — nervine rejuvenator; reproductive tonic; reduces sexual debility; traditionally used for all male sexual disorders

Jatiphal (Nutmeg / Myristica fragrans) — traditional Ayurvedic-Unani remedy for premature ejaculation; myristic acid content; CNS sedative at therapeutic doses; reduces ejaculatory reflex hypersensitivity; traditional formulation: small amounts in warm milk before intercourse; not for chronic daily use (hepatotoxic in excess)
TOPICAL TRADITIONAL REMEDIES

Clove oil (diluted) — traditional Indian topical for PE; eugenol = mild topical local anaesthetic; apply to glans (diluted in carrier oil) before intercourse — mechanism closely mirrors Lidocaine/Benzocaine topicals; evidence for eugenol as topical anaesthetic is established in dentistry
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
Kokilaksha (Hygrophila spinosa) with Vajikarana — traditional PE formula
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors HIGH CONSULTATION DELAY Indian men with PE often delay consulting doctors for years due to cultural shame; self-treat with Ayurvedic preparations, local remedies, alcohol (reduces sensitivity), multiple masturbations before sex (refractory period strategy), or topical numbing agents; many come with acquired secondary relationship dysfunction by the time they consult MISINFORMATION Widespread misinformation in India: PE attributed to 'semen weakness', '
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations PREMATURE EJACULATION IS THE MOST COMMON MALE SEXUAL DYSFUNCTION AND IS HIGHLY TREATABLE — BUT INDIA'S CONSULTATION RATES REMAIN LOW: PE affects an estimated 20–30% of sexually active men globally, making it the single most prevalent male sexual complaint — more common than

diseases.name: Delayed Ejaculation
tests: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT

Detailed sexual history: onset (lifelong/acquired), situational pattern, masturbation vs intercourse pattern, relationship context, psychological factors; drug history (SSRIs, antipsychotics, alpha-blockers, opioids); surgical history (pelvic, retroperitoneal)
POST-ORGASM URINE MICROSCOPY (RETROGRADE EJACULATION)

Collect urine immediately after attempted ejaculation or orgasm; centrifuge; sperm in urine post-orgasm = retrograde ejaculation (absence of antegrade ejaculate; sperm in bladder); distinguishes retrograde from true anejaculation or DE
SEMEN ANALYSIS
Confirm absent or markedly reduced ejaculate volume; azoospermia vs sperm present
HORMONAL PANEL
Serum testosterone (low T from HPG suppression — opioids, hyperprolactinaemia)
Prolactin — haloperidol/antipsychotic-induced hyperprolactinaemia
FSH, LH — gonadotrophin axis
generic_medicines: ADDRESS UNDERLYING CAUSE FIRST

Review and reduce SSRI dose — if SSRI-induced DE; switch from Paroxetine to lower-risk SSRI (Escitalopram) or change to Mirtazapine/Bupropion; if psychiatric indication requires persistent SSRI, add Sildenafil or Bupropion augmentation

Review alpha-blocker necessity — Tamsulosin-induced retrograde ejaculation often resolves on stopping; switch to Silodosin (more selective; higher retrograde ejaculation rate but some men prefer) or Alfuzosin/Doxazosin (less retrograde ejaculation)
Antipsychotic review — switch to Quetiapine or Aripiprazole (lower prolactin elevation; less sexual dysfunction) from Haloperidol/Risperidone
PHARMACOLOGICAL THERAPY
Bupropion 150–300 mg SR — dopaminergic/noradrenergic; PRO-ejaculatory; used for SSRI-induced sexual dysfunction augmentation; improves DE and all SSRI sexual side effects including anorgasmia

Cyproheptadine 4–8 mg — serotonin antagonist (5-HT2); taken before intercourse; specifically reverses SSRI-induced ejaculatory delay; antihistamine with serotonin antagonist property; can be taken on-demand 30–60 min before sex
Cabergoline 0.25–0.5 mg twice weekly — dopamine agonist; pro-ejaculatory; reduces prolactin; useful for hyperprolactinaemia-induced DE or antipsychotic-induced DE
Testosterone replacement — if confirmed hypogonadism contributing
Amantadine 100 mg OD — dopaminergic; augments SSRI-treated DE
Oxytocin — intranasal; experimental; facilitates orgasmic reflex; not standard therapy
VIBRATORY STIMULATION

Penile vibratory stimulation — medical-grade vibrators (Ferticare, SatoVibe) at shaft/glans; particularly for neurogenic DE (spinal injury, MS, post-surgical); first-line for neurogenic anejaculation in spinal cord injury for fertility
Electroejaculation — rectal probe electrostimulation; for complete anejaculation (SCI patients); sperm harvested for IVF/ICSI; available at andrological fertility centres India
janaushadhi_medicines: Mirtazapine 15 mg / 30 mg tablet (SSRI alternative with neutral sexual profile)
Escitalopram 5 mg / 10 mg tablet (lower ejaculatory side effect than paroxetine)
ayurvedic_medicines: VAJIKARANA — EJACULATORY STIMULATION

Kapikacchu (Mucuna pruriens) — L-DOPA rich; dopaminergic activation; directly relevant to DE as dopamine facilitates ejaculatory reflex; dopamine D2 receptor stimulation lowers ejaculatory threshold; 500 mg BD; has RCT data for improving male sexual function
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — reduces cortisol; improves testosterone; reduces performance anxiety that inhibits ejaculation; for psychogenic DE component; 300–600 mg BD
Gokshura (Tribulus terrestris) — LH-stimulating; testosterone support; reduces DE in hypogonadal context; 500 mg BD
Shilajit — testosterone elevation; mitochondrial energy; traditional androgenic rasayana; fulvic acid minerals; reduces sexual debility including DE
Shatavari — nervine rejuvenator; adaptogen; genitourinary tonic
PSYCHOGENIC DE — MIND-BODY
Brahmi — anxiolytic; helps psychogenic DE through performance anxiety reduction; GABA-A modulation
Jyotishmati (Celastrus paniculata) — cognitive enhancer; nervine; reduces mental blockade inhibiting ejaculatory reflex
WARMING SPICES (INCREASING VAJANA STRENGTH)
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors SSRI-INDUCED DE — INDIA CONTEXT Paroxetine is widely prescribed in India for social anxiety disorder and OCD — both highly prevalent in India; paroxetine has the highest ejaculatory delay among commonly used SSRIs (used therapeutically for PE but causes problematic DE at full doses); non-disclosure of sexual side effects by patients due to cultural shame leads to treatment non-adherence (silent discontinuation of antidepressants); psychiatrists and GPs
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations DELAYED EJACULATION IS MOST COMMONLY DRUG-INDUCED IN CLINICAL PRACTICE — ALWAYS TAKE A FULL MEDICATION HISTORY: In clinical practice, the single most common cause of acquired DE is iatrogenic — from SSRIs (paroxetine, sertraline, fluoxetine) prescribed for depression, anxiet

diseases.name: Male Hypogonadism
tests: TESTOSTERONE MEASUREMENT

Total testosterone — early morning (7–11am) fasting sample (diurnal variation: testosterone is highest in morning); two separate readings required for diagnosis; LOW = <300 ng/dL (10.4 nmol/L) by endocrine society; many labs use lower cut-off; important: Fitzpatrick population norms may differ

Free testosterone — calculated from total T + SHBG + albumin (Vermeulen formula); important when SHBG altered (obesity → low SHBG → normal total T but adequate free T; liver cirrhosis → high SHBG → low free T despite borderline total T)
GONADOTROPHIN PANEL
FSH, LH — distinguish primary (HIGH FSH/LH) from secondary (LOW/NORMAL FSH/LH)
PROLACTIN — elevated in pituitary adenoma/antipsychotic use; suppresses LH
OESTRADIOL — elevated oestradiol in obesity / gynaecomastia evaluation
ADDITIONAL HORMONES
TSH — hypothyroidism mimics hypogonadism symptoms
SHBG — sex hormone-binding globulin; altered by obesity (reduces), liver disease, ageing
generic_medicines: TESTOSTERONE REPLACEMENT THERAPY
Testosterone undecanoate injectable 1000 mg IM (Nebido) — 12-weekly injection after loading dose; most convenient; standard of care for long-term male hypogonadism TRT in India
Testosterone enanthate 250 mg IM injection fortnightly — widely available India; effective; more frequent injection than Nebido

Testosterone gel 1% (50 mg/day sachet) — daily application to shoulders/upper arms/abdomen (not genitals); physiological delivery; no injection discomfort; risk: transfer to female partner/children — wash hands, keep clothing on; adjustable dose
Testosterone undecanoate 40 mg capsule (oral, Andriol) — poor bioavailability; least used; option for mild supplementation
CONTRAINDICATIONS TO TRT
Active prostate cancer; breast cancer; haematocrit >54%; untreated severe BPH with significant LUTS; desire for fertility in near future (TRT will suppress spermatogenesis)
FERTILITY PRESERVATION

Clomiphene citrate 25–50 mg EOD (anti-oestrogen; increases GnRH pulsatility → LH/FSH → endogenous testosterone + spermatogenesis) — used in secondary hypogonadism wanting fertility; does NOT suppress spermatogenesis unlike TRT

hCG (human Chorionic Gonadotrophin) injection — acts as LH; maintains intra-testicular testosterone + spermatogenesis; used when TRT causes azoospermia; or first-line secondary hypogonadism with infertility
FSH injections (Recombinant FSH — Puregon, Gonal-F) — alongside hCG for complete spermatogenesis induction in secondary hypogonadism
MONITORING TRT
After 3 months: testosterone (trough), haematocrit, PSA; then 6–12 monthly; aim testosterone 400–700 ng/dL (mid-normal range); haematocrit <54% threshold — if exceeded, venesect or reduce dose
janaushadhi_medicines: Letrozole 2.5 mg tablet (aromatase inhibitor — obese hypogonadism)
ayurvedic_medicines: TESTOSTERONE-SUPPORTING HERBS

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — gold-standard adaptogen with published RCT data for testosterone elevation; increases testosterone 15–17% in stressed men vs placebo; reduces cortisol (cortisol competitively inhibits testosterone synthesis); 300–600 mg root extract BD

Shilajit (Asphaltum) — fulvic acid mineral complex; RCT published in Andrologia 2015 shows Shilajit 250 mg BD for 90 days increased total testosterone by 23.5%, FSH by 9.4%, LH by 17.7% in healthy men vs placebo; direct action on Leydig cells and HPG axis stimulation

Gokshura (Tribulus terrestris) — steroidal saponins; LH-like activity; small studies show testosterone elevation; body of evidence weaker than Ashwagandha/Shilajit; widely marketed but nuanced evidence

Kapikacchu (Mucuna pruriens) — L-Dopa precursor; dopamine → inhibits prolactin → removes prolactin-mediated testosterone suppression; published RCT in infertile oligozoospermic men showing testosterone recovery and improved seminal parameters
Safed Musli (Chlorophytum borivilianum) — adaptogen; androgenic; traditional vajikarana; animal studies show testicular testosterone production improvement; human RCT data limited
SPERMATOGENESIS SUPPORT
Mucuna Pruriens (Kapikacchu) — RCT: improved spermatogenesis in men with idiopathic infertility
Ashwagandha — published data: 30 mg root extract daily for 12 weeks improved sperm count 167%, motility 57%, semen volume 53% (Ambiye et al., 2013)
Triphala — antioxidant; reduces ROS damage to spermatozoa
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors MUMPS ORCHITIS Mumps orchitis causes primary testicular failure — bilateral orchitis in post-pubertal males causes permanent azoospermia and low testosterone; MMR vaccination (Measles, Mumps, Rubella) — India's MMR uptake is improving but incomplete, particularly in rural areas; mumps continues to cause orchitis-related hypogonadism and infertility in India; any male presenting with infertility + small firm testes + history of childhood/adolescent paro
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations TESTOSTERONE REPLACEMENT THERAPY SUPPRESSES SPERMATOGENESIS — CRITICAL FOR INDIAN MEN WANTING FERTILITY: Testosterone replacement therapy (TRT), while effectively treating symptoms of androgen deficiency, shuts down the pituitary-testicular axis through negative feedback on

diseases.name: Female Sexual Interest Arousal
tests: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT — DSFS / FSFI QUESTIONNAIRE

Female Sexual Function Index (FSFI) — 19-item validated questionnaire; 6 domains: desire, arousal, lubrication, orgasm, satisfaction, pain; total score <26.55 = Female Sexual Dysfunction; standard research and clinical tool
Derogatis Interview for Sexual Function (DISF) — alternative structured interview
HORMONAL PANEL
Total and free testosterone — low free testosterone → FSIAD in peri/post-menopausal women; < 25 ng/dL total testosterone = low FSIAD territory
SHBG — elevated in OCP users; reduces free testosterone
FSH, LH, Estradiol — menopausal transition assessment
Prolactin — elevated: breastfeeding, pituitary adenoma, antipsychotics → HPG suppression
TSH — hypothyroidism causes reduced libido
Fasting glucose, HbA1c, Vitamin D
generic_medicines: ADDRESS MODIFIABLE CAUSE FIRST

Switch SSRI to Bupropion or Mirtazapine if clinically appropriate — significantly improves FSIAD caused by SSRIs; Addyi (Flibanserin) addresses SSRI-induced sexual dysfunction specifically but not licensed India yet
Reconsider OCP formulation — switch to progestogen-only pill or non-hormonal contraception if OCP-induced FSIAD suspected; testosterone suppression from SHBG may reverse
HORMONAL

Testosterone therapy (off-label) — low-dose transdermal testosterone 150–300 mcg/day; approved in Australia and UK (Intrinsa patch — discontinued; off-label systemic testosterone gel at 1/10th male dose); best evidence for post-menopausal FSIAD; improves desire, arousal, orgasm, sexual wellbeing; monitoring: testosterone level + safety screen (virilisation, lipids, liver); Indian practice: off-label use of male testosterone preparation at low dose

Oestrogen therapy — for GSM (Genitourinary Syndrome of Menopause) vulvovaginal atrophy component; vaginal oestrogen cream (cream or pessary — Premarin, Ovestin) — local; minimal systemic absorption; safe even post-ER+ breast cancer (ASCO updated guidelines 2023); significantly improves lubrication, dyspareunia, FSIAD
CENTRAL ACTING

Flibanserin (Addyi) 100 mg HS — FDA-approved for premenopausal FSIAD in USA; 5-HT1A agonist + 5-HT2A antagonist + D4 antagonist; modulates dopamine/norepinephrine balance in PFC; modest effect (1.5 additional satisfying sexual events/month); not currently licensed in India
Bremelanotide (Vyleesi) — melanocortin receptor agonist; injected subcutaneously before sex; approved USA for premenopausal FSIAD; not India-licensed
PSYCHOSEXUAL THERAPY
CBT-based sexual therapy; sensate focus; desire-building exercises; partner communication; addressing spectatoring; trauma therapy if applicable — FIRST-LINE alongside or before pharmacotherapy
janaushadhi_medicines: Testosterone gel 1% (at low dose off-label for women)
Sildenafil 50 mg (limited female FSIAD evidence; rare use for arousal disorder)
ayurvedic_medicines: FEMALE VAJIKARANA / RASAYANA

Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus) — pre-eminent female reproductive tonic in Ayurveda; phytoestrogens (steroidal saponins); adaptogen; reduces oestrogen deficiency symptoms (vaginal dryness, reduced arousal) in perimenopausal women; stimulates reproductive health; published evidence for menopausal symptom improvement; 500 mg–2 g powder/day or root extract BD

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — female FSIAD RCT (Dongre et al., 2015 BioMedResearch International): 300 mg root extract BD for 8 weeks in 50 women with FSIAD → significant improvement in all FSFI domains (desire, arousal, lubrication, orgasm, satisfaction) vs placebo; p<0.001; directly validated for female FSIAD; reduces cortisol and anxiety (psychogenic inhibitory mechanisms)

Safed Musli (Chlorophytum borivilianum) — female reproductive adaptogen; improves moisture and arousal in ayurvedic practice; traditionally prescribed for female sexual weakness ('kshaya') similar to FSIAD
Lodhra (Symplocos racemosa) — female reproductive tonic; anti-inflammatory; genitourinary
SHATAVARI GHRITA / COMBINATIONS
Shatavari Ghrita — clarified butter with Shatavari; traditional female reproductive preparation; lubricating; oestrogenic; vaginal application possible for dryness
Phalaghrita — classical compound for female reproductive health in Ashtanga Hridaya
Chandrprabha Vati — genitourinary tonic; multiple herbs
TOPICAL CARE
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors SILENCE AND STIGMA Female sexual dysfunction in India is vastly under-reported due to cultural silence around female sexuality; women rarely approach doctors voluntarily with FSIAD; typically presents within broader marital conflict complaints, 'not interested in husband', 'feeling cold'; only recently discussed in Indian medical education; GPs rarely ask about sexual function in female consultations ORAL CONTRACEPTIVE PILL (OCP) — SHBG MECHANISM Ethin
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations FEMALE SEXUAL DYSFUNCTION IN INDIA IS A SILENT EPIDEMIC REQUIRING ACTIVE CLINICAL ENQUIRY: Female Sexual Interest/Arousal Disorder affects an estimated 30–40% of women globally at some point in their lives, with India likely having comparable or higher rates given the additi

diseases.name: Hypoactive Sexual Desire Disord
tests: SEXUAL DESIRE INVENTORY (SDI-2)
Validated 14-item questionnaire assessing dyadic and solitary sexual desire; used for research and monitoring treatment response in HSDD
IIEF-5 (FOR MALES)
Specifically the desire domain (Q7 and Q8 of full IIEF-15); scores 1–5 per question; low scores confirm desire deficiency
HORMONAL PANEL — MALE
Serum testosterone (early morning, two separate readings) — the most important; total T <300 ng/dL = low
Prolactin — hyperprolactinaemia (pituitary adenoma, antipsychotics, dopamine antagonist drugs, hypothyroidism)
FSH, LH — distinguish primary vs secondary hypogonadism
TSH — hypothyroidism
HORMONAL PANEL — FEMALE
generic_medicines: MALE HSDD
Testosterone replacement (if confirmed hypogonadism) — same as male hypogonadism sheet: testosterone gel/injection brings testosterone to mid-normal range → improves libido within 3–6 weeks

Cabergoline 0.25–1 mg twice weekly — for hyperprolactinaemia; dopamine agonist; reduces prolactin → restores LH/FSH → testosterone → libido; highly effective for prolactinoma-associated HSDD; also increases sexual drive directly via D2 receptors in limbic system
Bupropion 150–300 mg OD — dopaminergic/noradrenergic; pro-libido; used for SSRI-induced HSDD augmentation; independent mild aphrodisiac effect through norepinephrine-dopamine reuptake inhibition
Switch from SSRI to Wellbutrin (Bupropion) if SSRI-induced HSDD — treats depression without sexual dysfunction
FEMALE HSDD

Low-dose testosterone — transdermal 150–300 mcg/day (off-label India); best evidence in post-menopausal FSIAD/HSDD; Intrinsa patch (300 mcg/day) has 5 RCTs positive; current EMA/NICE guidelines support off-label use; apply 1/10th male testosterone gel dose
Vaginal oestrogen — if atrophic vaginitis contributing to painful intercourse → desire avoidance; local vaginal oestrogen cream/pessary breaks pain-avoidance cycle
Flibanserin 100 mg HS — FDA-approved for premenopausal female HSDD; central 5-HT1A agonist + 5-HT2A antagonist; modestly effective; not India-licensed
Treat depression — antidepressant selection: switch to Bupropion or Mirtazapine if depression driving HSDD; these are less HSDD-inducing than SSRIs
PSYCHOSEXUAL THERAPY
Couples therapy — relationship quality is most powerful predictor of desire in women; emotional intimacy restoration; communication counselling
Mindfulness-based sex therapy — Lori Brotto RCTs; 4-session mindfulness program; highly effective for HSDD in women; available via online programmes
janaushadhi_medicines: Testosterone gel 1% sachet (low dose for women; full dose for men)
ayurvedic_medicines: VAJIKARANA FOR DESIRE

Ashwagandha — reduces HPA axis cortisol hypersecretion; enhances dopaminergic (desire) vs serotonergic (inhibition) balance; clinical evidence for both male and female sexual desire improvement; 300–600 mg BD
Shatavari — female reproductive tonic; phytoestrogenic; reduces post-menopausal HSDD symptoms; lubricating; 1–2 g powder day or extract 500 mg BD
Kapikacchu (Mucuna pruriens) — L-DOPA → dopamine → directly mediates sexual motivation in mesolimbic system; both male and female desire enhancement; 500 mg BD
Safed Musli — adaptogenic; androgenic support; both sexes; traditional vajikarana
SAFFRON (KESAR, CROCUS SATIVUS)

Saffron 30 mg/day — well-conducted RCTs demonstrating SSRI-related sexual dysfunction reversal including desire improvement; saffron modulates dopamine/serotonin in prefrontal cortex; specific India-relevant as saffron is culturally familiar, widely available, easily supplemented as 'kesar milk' (saffron in warm milk — traditional Indian preparation that coincidentally delivers the 30 mg daily dose); safe; well-tolerated
APHRODISIAC SPICES

Cardamom (Elachi) + Clove (Laung) + Nutmeg (Jaiphal) — traditional Indian Unani aphrodisiacs; essential oils; CNS excitatory; limited formal evidence but traditional use basis; pleasant sensory adjunct to intimacy context
COMPOUND FORMULATIONS
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors CULTURAL SEXUAL DESIRE NORMS India's culture around male sexuality includes an implicit expectation of constant male sexual desire and availability; HSDD in men is profoundly culturally discordant → extreme distress, shame, and delay in consultation; men may instead attribute the problem to 'weakness', 'physical exhaustion', 'heat in the body', or 'masturbation as youth' before accepting a medical cause POST-MENOPAUSAL WOMEN'S SEXUALITY INDIA Indian po
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations PROLACTINOMA IS AN IMPORTANT CORRECTABLE CAUSE OF HSDD IN BOTH SEXES — PROLACTIN SHOULD BE ROUTINELY MEASURED: Hyperprolactinaemia (serum prolactin >25 mcg/L in women; >20 mcg/L in men) causes HSDD in both sexes through GnRH suppression → reduced LH/FSH → testosterone defici

diseases.name: Female Orgasmic Disorder
tests: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT — FSFI ORGASM SUBSCALE
Female Sexual Function Index (FSFI) orgasm domain: 3 questions (Q10, Q11, Q12); score out of 6; <3.6 = orgasmic dysfunction; part of full FSFI assessment
SEXUAL HISTORY

Lifetime orgasmic history: has she ever experienced orgasm by any means?; Current pattern: masturbation vs partner; clitoral vs vaginal; with/without vibrator; medication history (SSRIs, antipsychotics); trauma history sensitively; relationship satisfaction
NEUROLOGICAL

Sensory testing of perineum and clitoris: vibration threshold (biothesiometer); bulbocavernosus reflex; pudendal nerve conduction — if neurogenic FOD suspected (SCI, MS, post-pelvic surgery, diabetes)
HORMONAL
Serum testosterone, SHBG, free testosterone — post-menopausal women with reduced clitoral sensitivity
Oestradiol — post-menopausal atrophy; clitoral changes with oestrogen deficiency
Prolactin — elevated prolactin → desire and arousal deficit contributing to orgasmic difficulty
generic_medicines: DIRECTED MASTURBATION — GOLD STANDARD PSYCHOSEXUAL INTERVENTION

Directed masturbation program (Heiman & LoPiccolo): Step 1 — genital self-examination with mirror; Step 2 — exploration of genital sensations without pressure for orgasm; Step 3 — manual clitoral stimulation with or without personal lubricant; Step 4 — introduce vibrator if insufficient stimulation; Step 5 — orgasm transfer to partner setting; completion rate: 80–90% orgasm in previously anorgasmic women with lifelong FOD; this is the most evidence-based treatment for FOD
VIBRATORS

Clitoral vibrators — provide higher intensity direct clitoral stimulation than manual stimulation alone; 80–95% of anorgasmic women can achieve orgasm with vibrator; normalised tool in sex therapy; available in India online; recommended in evidence-based FOD treatment protocols
PHARMACOLOGICAL
Bupropion 150–300 mg OD — reverses SSRI-induced anorgasmia; pro-orgasmic dopaminergic mechanism; also direct effect on sexual arousal independently of SSRI use
Cyproheptadine 4 mg before intercourse — on-demand serotonin antagonist; reverses SSRI-induced anorgasmia acutely
Switch from Paroxetine (highest anorgasmia) → Escitalopram or Bupropion
Low-dose testosterone (off-label) — for post-menopausal reduced clitoral sensitivity and anorgasmia; increases clitoral sensitivity and orgasm intensity
LOCAL TOPICAL
Alprostadil (PGE1) topical clitoral cream — vasodilator; increases clitoral blood flow and sensitivity; available globally (FEMPROX); limited India availability
Personal lubricant (silicon-based or water-based hyaluronic acid) — reduces friction, increases comfort, enhances sensation; first step for situational FOD with inadequate vaginal lubrication
PELVIC FLOOR STRENGTHENING
Kegel exercises (PFE) — strengthens pubococcygeus and bulbocavernosus muscles; increases orgasmic intensity and frequency in women with weak pelvic floor; 100 contractions/day × 6–8 weeks
janaushadhi_medicines: Testosterone gel 1% (low dose off-label for clitoral sensitivity)
Vaginal oestrogen cream (clitoral sensitivity in post-menopause)
Mirtazapine 15 mg tablet (SSRI alternative; neutral/positive sexual function)
ayurvedic_medicines: AROUSAL-ENHANCING HERBS

Ashwagandha — reduces cortisol; clinical RCT (Dongre 2015): 8-week Ashwagandha in women with FSIAD showed significant improvement in FSFI orgasm domain scores; also reduces spectatoring anxiety through cortisol reduction allowing PFC suppression necessary for orgasm; 300 mg BD
Shatavari — oestrogenic; increases clitoral sensitivity via oestrogen receptor activity; post-menopausal orgasm improvement; lubricating (reduces dyspareunia that inhibits orgasm); 500 mg BD
Kapikacchu (Mucuna pruriens) — dopaminergic via L-DOPA; dopamine disinhibits orgasmic reflex; both desire and orgasm improved
TOPICAL AROUSAL ENHANCEMENT
Clary Sage essential oil (diluted in carrier) — topical application lower abdomen/clitoral area; contains phytoestrogens; vasodilatory; traditionally used as female arousal enhancer; safe externally
Sesame oil (Til tail) — daily vulval massage; traditional Abhyanga for perineal area; increases local blood flow; maintains tissue health
Rose (Gulab) — rose essential oil in carrier oil; traditional erotic arousal enhancer; aromatherapy component to sexual context
RELAXATION AND ANTI-SPECTATORING
Brahmi — anxiolytic; reduces spectatoring (the principal psychological block to orgasm); GABA modulation
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors CULTURAL SILENCE AROUND FEMALE ORGASM Female sexual pleasure — particularly orgasm — is culturally not acknowledged in Indian mainstream culture; marriage guides (Grihastha dharma texts) focus on procreation, not female pleasure; many Indian women have never received information about their own sexual anatomy (clitoris location, function) or about the fact that direct clitoral stimulation is required for orgasm in most women; this fundamental education
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations MOST WOMEN REQUIRE DIRECT CLITORAL STIMULATION FOR ORGASM — THIS IS PHYSIOLOGY, NOT DYSFUNCTION: A landmark global survey (Archives of Sexual Behaviour, 2017, n=52,000) found that only 18% of women reliably achieve orgasm from penetrative intercourse alone; 74% require direc

diseases.name: Vaginismus
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS
Vaginismus is diagnosed clinically by: history of penetration failure + examination findings
ATTEMPTED GYNAECOLOGICAL EXAMINATION

Pelvic exam — involuntary levator ani contraction visible/palpable on attempted speculum or digital insertion; diagnostic; examiner notes involuntary vaginal closure; must be performed sensitively with patient control at all times; a normal exam finding except for the muscle contraction
PAIN MAPPING

Cotton tip mapping of vestibule and vaginal opening: Q-tip test — touch at different clock positions around the vestibule; identifies vestibulodynia if localised tenderness (especially 5-7 o'clock) which may coexist with or cause secondary vaginismus
PELVIC FLOOR PHYSIOTHERAPY ASSESSMENT
Perineometry — measures resting pelvic floor tone and voluntary contraction/relaxation; demonstrates hypertonicity; guides physiotherapy
PSYCHOLOGICAL ASSESSMENT
PTSD Checklist (PCL-5) — sexual trauma screening; PHQ-9 (depression); GAD-7 (anxiety); relationship satisfaction; sexual guilt inventory
generic_medicines: VAGINAL DILATOR THERAPY — CORNERSTONE TREATMENT

Graduated vaginal dilators (plastic or silicone; set of 4–6 progressively sized dilators, smallest = finger-sized); patient-directed insertion practice starting smallest → progressive increase; combined with pelvic floor relaxation exercises; success rate 80–90% with guided programme; available in India online; key: patient is in full control of insertion — not partner or examiner

Step programme: (1) Pelvic floor relaxation exercises (daily Kegel − contract AND RELEASE); (2) Mirror genital self-examination and desensitisation; (3) Insert smallest dilator with personal lubricant in relaxed position; (4) Progress size over weeks-months; (5) Transition to partner digital, then penile
PELVIC FLOOR PHYSIOTHERAPY

Pelvic floor physiotherapist (specialist) — biofeedback-assisted pelvic floor rehabilitation; manual internal myofascial release; trigger point therapy for levator ani hypertonicity; most effective when available; increasingly available in metro India
Pelvic floor biofeedback (electromyography) — real-time visual of pelvic floor muscle tension; helps women learn to relax pelvic floor on demand
BOTULINUM TOXIN INJECTION

Botulinum toxin A (10–100 units) injected into levator ani / pubococcygeus under anaesthesia; relaxes muscle spasm for 3–4 months; allows dilator use; particularly for severe or treatment-resistant vaginismus; performed under local or general anaesthesia; under-utilised in India; available at specialist centres
TOPICAL
Lidocaine 2–4% jelly — topical anaesthetic; applied to introitus before dilator insertion during treatment programme; reduces sensation during initial desensitisation; NOT chronic treatment
Topical oestrogen — if post-menopausal atrophic vaginal changes contributing (secondary vaginismus in older women)
PSYCHOLOGICAL
CBT-based sex therapy — addressing sexual guilt, anticipatory anxiety, fear conditioning; MUST accompany physical dilator therapy; particularly important for culturally-conditioned vaginismus India
EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitisation and Reprocessing) — for trauma-associated vaginismus; recommended when sexual trauma identified
janaushadhi_medicines: Vaginal dilator set (graduated; physiotherapy aid — not medication but standard therapy equipment)
Botulinum toxin A 100 IU vial (specialist procedure)
Diazepam 2 mg vaginal suppository (off-label: pelvic floor muscle relaxant; short-term bridge with dilator therapy)
Vaginal oestrogen cream (for atrophic secondary vaginismus)
ayurvedic_medicines: RELAXATION AND ANTI-ANXIETY

Ashwagandha — reduces cortisol and adrenergic hyperarousal; reduces the anticipatory anxiety that triggers levator ani spasm; 300–600 mg BD; adaptogen; helps normalise HPA axis that drives the pelvic floor guarding reflex
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — anxiolytic; GABAergic; directly reduces neurological anxiety that underlies vaginismus reflex; 300 mg BD
Jatamansi (Nardostachys jatamansi) — Indian valerian; GABA modulation; potent anxiolytic; traditional nervine relaxant; useful for anticipatory anxiety
Valerian root — GABA-A receptor potentiation; mild muscle relaxant; sedative; helps with pelvic floor hypertonicity through GABAergic nervous system calming
TOPICAL RELAXANTS

Sesame oil (Til tail) — warm sesame oil gentle perineal massage; traditional Abhyanga (oil treatment) for perineum; reduces tissue hypertonicity; maintains mucosal hydration; Ayurvedic snehana for perineum
Triphala oil application — combined with warm oil massage; anti-inflammatory; traditional vaginal health
MIND-BODY

Yoga (Supta Baddha Konasana — reclined bound angle; Viparita Karani — legs up wall) — pelvic floor release poses; inner thigh and hip adductor stretching; reduces adductor muscle co-contraction that accompanies vaginismus; indirect pelvic floor relaxation
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors UNCONSUMMATED MARRIAGE — INDIA EPIDEMIC Vaginismus is the most common cause of unconsummated marriage in India. Indian sexual medicine clinicians report unconsummated marriage couples as among the most common referrals — many presenting months to years after marriage. Contributing India-specific factors: (1) No premarital sexual experience → no opportunity to identify vaginismus before marriage; (2) Intense cultural pressure for consummation (family as
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations VAGINISMUS IS UNIFORMLY TREATABLE — COUPLES MUST BE HELPED TO UNDERSTAND THIS IS NOT PERMANENT: Vaginismus — primary, secondary, or situational — is one of the most treatable sexual dysfunctions. Clinical data consistently shows 80–90%+ success rates with graduated dilator p

diseases.name: Dyspareunia
tests: GYNAECOLOGICAL EXAMINATION
Speculum exam: atrophic changes, vaginal pH, discharge, cervical lesion/ectropion, candidiasis
Digital bimanual exam: uterine tenderness, cervical motion tenderness (PID), adnexal tenderness, uterine retroversion/fixed uterus (endometriosis), ovarian mass palpation
VULVAR ASSESSMENT
Q-tip test / vestibulometry — 10-N probe mapping of vestibular/vulvar pain; identifies vulvodynia and vestibulodynia location and severity
Dermoscopy of vulva — lichen sclerosus (white plaques, loss of architecture), lichen planus, contact dermatitis
Skin biopsy — if lichen sclerosus or suspicious vulvar lesion
VAGINAL PH + MICROSCOPY
pH>5 = atrophic or BV; KOH prep (hyphae = candida); wet mount (trichomonas motile, BV clue cells)
STI SCREEN
generic_medicines: TREAT UNDERLYING CAUSE — PRINCIPLE
Dyspareunia is a symptom; treatment targets identified aetiology
ATROPHIC VAGINITIS / GSM

Vaginal oestrogen — Cream (Premarin, Ovestin) 0.5–1 g 2–3x/week; Pessary (Vagifem 10 mcg daily × 2 weeks then twice weekly); Ring (Estring); extremely effective; local action with minimal systemic absorption; safe even recent breast cancer (ASCO 2023); restores epithelial thickness, lubrication, elasticity, pH; first-line post-menopausal superficial dyspareunia
Vaginal hyaluronic acid moisturiser (Replens, Hyalofemme) — non-hormonal; twice weekly; good for women declining oestrogen
Ospemifene 60 mg OD (selective oestrogen receptor modulator — SERM) — oral systemic; treats GSM without vaginal oestrogen; approved US/EU; India availability limited
VESTIBULODYNIA
Lidocaine 5% ointment — topical anaesthetic at vestibule; reduces pain for intercourse; nightly application also desensitises
Gabapentin topical 2–6% cream — neuropathic pain at vestibule
Amitriptyline 10–25 mg OD oral — low-dose TCA; neuropathic component vestibulodynia
Duloxetine 30–60 mg — SNRI; neuropathic pain modulation
Vestibulectomy — surgical removal of painful vestibular tissue; effective for provoked vestibulodynia; last-line surgical option
ENDOMETRIOSIS-ASSOCIATED DEEP DYSPAREUNIA
GnRH agonist (Leuprolide Depot 3.75 mg monthly) — reduces oestrogen → suppresses endometriosis; for medical management alongside surgery
NSAIDs (Naproxen, Ibuprofen) — symptomatic dysmenorrhoea and dyspareunia pain reduction
ayurvedic_medicines: ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AND HEALING (ENDOMETRIOSIS/PID COMPONENT)

Haridra (Curcumin/Turmeric) — anti-inflammatory; COX-2 inhibitor; NF-κB inhibitor; most relevant for endometriosis-associated dyspareunia; multiple RCTs of curcumin in gynaecological inflammation; 500–1000 mg curcumin phytosome BD

Ashoka (Saraca asoca bark) — uterine tonic; anti-inflammatory; specifically indicated in Ayurveda for all menstrual and uterine pain conditions including dysmenorrhoea and dyspareunia; analgesic component

Shatavari — anti-inflammatory genitourinary tonic; oestrogen-like effect on vaginal epithelium (reduces atrophic dryness); particularly useful in GSM-associated dyspareunia and post-partum dyspareunia (breastfeeding-related oestrogen deficiency)
Lodhra (Symplocos racemosa) — uterine and vaginal anti-inflammatory; traditional treatment for pradara (vaginal discharge/inflammation complaints)
TOPICAL

Sesame oil (til tail) perineal massage — traditional Yoni Abhyanga (vaginal oil treatment); warm sesame oil; reduces introital dryness; heals episiotomy scar area; anti-inflammatory; maintains tissue elasticity; promotes epithelial healing
Aloe vera gel — lubricating; cooling; anti-inflammatory; safe topical for introital dyspareunia; readily available India
Triphala decoction wash — gentle astringent; anti-microbial; vaginal wash with diluted Triphala kashaya; reduces infection-component dyspareunia; traditional India practice
PAIN MODULATION
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors ENDOMETRIOSIS DIAGNOSTIC DELAY India endometriosis is diagnosed on average 7–10 years after onset of symptoms — one of the longest diagnostic delays globally. Contributing factors: (1) Menstrual pain (dysmenorrhoea) culturally normalised ('it is normal to have painful periods'); (2) Dyspareunia not disclosed due to shame; (3) Low awareness among GPs and family physicians; (4) Transvaginal ultrasound used primarily to look for fibroids/PCOS — misses per
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations DEEP DYSPAREUNIA IN YOUNG INDIAN WOMEN MUST BE INVESTIGATED FOR ENDOMETRIOSIS — DIAGNOSTIC DELAY IS CAUSING INFERTILITY: Endometriosis affects an estimated 10–15% of reproductive-age women globally (25–30 million women in India). It is the most common cause of deep dyspareun

diseases.name: Vulvodynia
tests: Q-TIP TEST (VESTIBULOMETRY)

Cotton-tipped swab pressed gently at 10–12 equidistant points of the vestibule (clock face): 3, 5, 6, 7, 9 o'clock are most sensitive; patient rates pain 0–10; >3/10 at ≥2 sites = consistent with vulvar vestibulitis; this is the PRIMARY diagnostic test for localised provoked vulvodynia; simple, cheap, available in any gynaecology office
ALGESIOMETRY / VULVALGESIOMETER
Quantitative vulval sensory testing — standardised pressure probes; more precise than Q-tip; research settings
MICROBIOLOGICAL EXCLUSION
Vaginal swab microscopy + culture — MUST exclude candida, BV, trichomonas before vulvodynia diagnosis; pH, KOH, wet mount; culture for non-albicans candida
STI SCREEN
HSV serology / PCR — herpes simplex causing recurrent vulvar pain episodes; HSV-2 particularly
HPV status (colposcopy if lesions) — HPV-related VIN
DERMATOLOGICAL EXAMINATION
generic_medicines: TOPICAL TREATMENTS

Lidocaine 5% ointment — nightly application to vestibule; 6–8 weeks; desensitises nociceptors; reduces provoked pain; commonly used during dilator/physical therapy programme for LPV; also applied before intercourse for symptomatic relief

Topical gabapentin 2–6% compounded cream — GABA-analogue applied directly to vestibule; reduces neurogenic pain at vestibular nociceptors; compounded formulation; superiority to systemic gabapentin for localised disease
Topical corticosteroid (clobetasol 0.05%) — limited role; only if inflammatory component identified on exam; NOT for chronic LPV without inflammation
Topical oestrogen / testosterone — reduced local hormones from OCP-related androgen suppression or menopause; restores vestibular integrity; particularly relevant for OCP-associated vulvodynia onset
ORAL TREATMENTS
Amitriptyline 10–75 mg nocte — TCA; neuropathic pain; starting 10 mg, titrate up; most commonly used systemic agent for vulvodynia; benefit at 4–6 weeks
Gabapentin 300 mg BD→TDS — neuropathic pain modulation; particularly for generalised vulvodynia and central sensitisation cases
Duloxetine 30–60 mg OD — SNRI; ascending pain modulation via noradrenergic pathway; evidence for neuropathic pelvic pain
Venlafaxine 37.5–150 mg — alternative SNRI
PHYSICAL THERAPY
Pelvic floor physiotherapy — biofeedback + manual therapy; addresses pelvic floor hypertonicity co-existing with vulvodynia; reduces secondary vaginismus; essential component of LPV management
INTERVENTIONAL

Vestibulectomy — surgical excision of painful vestibular tissue (posterior vestibulectomy with vaginal advancement); 60–80% success rate for severe treatment-resistant LPV; last line after 6+ months failed conservative
Botulinum toxin injection — injection into pelvic floor musculature / vulvar vestibule; reduces muscle hypertonicity and local nociception
janaushadhi_medicines: Clonazepam 0.5 mg tablet (off-label: vestibular neuropathic pain)
ayurvedic_medicines: ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AND NEUROPATHIC PAIN

Haridra (Curcumin) — TRPV1 modulator; curcumin directly downregulates TRPV1 receptor expression and activity; TRPV1 is the key nociceptor in vulvodynia pathophysiology; anti-inflammatory via COX-2 and NF-κB inhibition; 500–1000 mg curcumin phytosome BD; constitutes one of the most biologically rationale Ayurvedic interventions for vulvodynia given the TRPV1 mechanism
Ashwagandha — reduces central sensitisation and HPA axis dysregulation; useful for generalised vulvodynia with central sensitisation features; adapatogen; also addresses co-morbid depression/anxiety
Brahmi — anti-anxiety; reduces central pain amplification; useful adjunct in vulvodynia with significant psychological distress
TOPICAL SOOTHING
Aloe vera gel (fresh / cold) — cooling; anti-inflammatory; TRPV1 antagonism via aloe compounds; readily available; applied to vulva for symptom relief; safe mucous membrane application
Sesame oil (raw cold-pressed) — anti-inflammatory linoleic and oleic fatty acids; traditional Yoni snehana; gentle vulval massage to maintain tissue health; reduce contact friction
Coconut oil (virgin unrefined) — TRPV1 modulating properties (phenols); anti-inflammatory; natural lubricant; often better tolerated than synthetic lubricants in sensitive vulvodynia patients
Chandan (Sandalwood) paste — cooling; anti-inflammatory; traditionally applied for burning vulval conditions; diluted in carrier oil
DIETARY
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors REPEATED ANTIFUNGAL MISDIAGNOSIS In India, vulvodynia is routinely misdiagnosed as recurrent vaginal candidiasis. Burning vulval symptoms → GP prescribes Fluconazole → temporary symptom reduction (or none) → symptoms return → repeat Fluconazole → this cycle repeats for years. The patient is labelled a 'recurrent candidiasis patient' and treated with prolonged antifungal courses, sometimes including oral itraconazole maintenance, without candida ever be
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations VULVODYNIA IS THE MOST MISDIAGNOSED FEMALE GENITAL PAIN CONDITION IN INDIA — ENDING THE ANTIFUNGAL MISDIAGNOSIS CYCLE: The most common diagnostic error in India for vulvodynia is repeated misdiagnosis as recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis. Signs of this misdiagnosis: (1) Chr

diseases.name: Peyronies Disease
tests: CLINICAL EXAMINATION
Palpation of penile shaft — identify plaque (fibrous nodule, usually dorsal); note size, consistency, calcification
Patient self-photograph or office photo of erect penis — documents degree and direction of curvature; baseline for treatment monitoring; Peyronie's Disease Questionnaire (PDQ) measurement
PENILE DOPPLER ULTRASOUND

High-frequency duplex Doppler of penile vasculature (limp + dynamic with intracavernosal PGE1 injection) — identifies: plaque location + echogenicity + calcification; cavernosal artery peak systolic velocity (PSV); end-diastolic velocity (EDV); resistive index: assesses co-existing vasculogenic ED; essential pre-surgical planning
PEYRONIE'S DISEASE QUESTIONNAIRE (PDQ)
Validated: symptom bother, pain, psychological distress; 15-item; measures impact and severity; Phase assessment (active vs stable)
ERECT PENILE PHOTOGRAPH

Curved angle measurement from photograph; or office gonometer measurement during pharmacological erection (PGE1-induced); curvature >30° = clinically significant; >60–90° = surgical correction often needed
MRI PENIS
generic_medicines: ACUTE PHASE (FIRST 12-18 MONTHS)

Colchicine 0.5 mg BD — anti-fibrotic; reduces TGF-β-mediated collagen synthesis; most widely used oral agent for early Peyronie's; some evidence for pain reduction and plaque stabilisation in acute phase; inexpensive

Pentoxifylline 400 mg TDS — phosphodiesterase inhibitor; anti-fibrotic; anti-inflammatory; reduces TGF-β expression; some evidence for plaque reduction and curvature improvement in early disease; also treats co-existing ED component
Tamoxifen 20 mg BD — anti-oestrogen; inhibits fibroblast activity; older evidence; still used in acute phase; some pain reduction benefit
Vitamin E 400 IU OD — antioxidant; reduces reactive oxygen species in fibrotic tissue; minimal evidence at high quality but commonly used; safe
L-arginine + L-citrulline — vasodilation; reduces hypoxic injury; adjunct
INTRALESIONAL INJECTION (SPECIALIST)

Collagenase clostridium histolyticum (CCH / Xiaflex) — enzymatic degradation of collagen in plaque; ONLY FDA-approved intralesional treatment; 0.58 mg injection into plaque + modelling procedure; 2 injections per cycle × 4 cycles; 17–20 degree curvature improvement; significant improvement in severe curvature; NOT available in India (very expensive; not registered)

Verapamil 10 mg intralesional — calcium channel blocker; reduces fibroblast collagen synthesis; locally injected into plaque; available India; reasonable evidence for pain reduction and partial curvature improvement
Interferon α-2b intralesional — anti-fibrotic cytokine; evidence for pain and curvature improvement
PENILE TRACTION DEVICE

Vacuum erection device + penile traction therapy (PTT) — mechanical elongation of contracted tunica; evidence for 1–5 cm length recovery and partial curvature reduction; used 2–4 hours/day; recommended particularly for penile shortening
SURGICAL — CHRONIC STABLE PHASE
Tunical plication (Nesbit procedure) — shortens longer tunica side to correct curvature; for mild-moderate curvature adequate penile length remaining
Plaque incision/excision + grafting — for severe curvature; longer procedure; risk of ED post-op
janaushadhi_medicines: Verapamil 40 mg tablet (systemic; intralesional formulation needs specialist compounding)
Sildenafil 25–50 mg (for co-existing ED; also reduces fibrosis via PDE5 pathway)
ayurvedic_medicines: ANTI-FIBROTIC

Haridra (Curcumin) — TGF-β inhibitor; anti-fibrotic; reduces collagen crosslinking; highly relevant mechanistic rationale for Peyronie's fibrous plaque (TGF-β1 mediated); 500–1000 mg curcumin phytosome BD

Guggulu (Commiphora mukul) — anti-inflammatory; anti-fibrotic; reduces connective tissue proliferation; anti-lipid; traditional Ayurvedic agent for nodular fibrous conditions; Kanchanara Guggulu specifically indicated for fibrous growths/nodules in Ayurveda; 2 tablets BD
Kanchanara (Bauhinia variegata) — Ayurvedic anti-fibrotic; used for glandular enlargements and fibrous masses (gandamaala); Kanchanara Guggulu = classical formulation for fibrous/glandular conditions
Shilajit — mineral resin; mitochondrial coenzyme Q10 activator; anti-fibrotic in connective tissue; reduces ROS-mediated fibrosis; traditionally indicated for male reproductive system conditions
ERECTILE SUPPORT
Ashwagandha — testosterone support; reduces anxiety from Peyronie's deformity; improves erectile quality
Safed Musli (Chlorophytum borivilianum) — PDE5 modulating properties; erectile support; libido enhancement accompanying Peyronie's psychological inhibition
TOPICAL

Nirgundi (Vitex negundo) oil massage — anti-inflammatory; traditionally used for pain and swelling in musculoskeletal conditions; warm application to penile shaft (topical, external only); reduces acute phase inflammation; caution: gentle application only; no aggressive massage in acute phase
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors COMPLETE NON-DISCLOSURE AND LATE PRESENTATION Peyronie's disease is almost entirely invisible in India's healthcare system — not because it is rare (3–9% prevalence implies millions of Indian men affected) but because no Indian man will voluntarily ask a doctor about the shape of his penis. The social script: a man with Peyronie's will avoid the problem, may reduce or stop sexual activity, and will not seek care until the condition is severe. Wives or
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations PEYRONIE'S DISEASE IS UNDER-DIAGNOSED IN INDIA — ACTIVE INQUIRY IS ESSENTIAL: With a global prevalence of 3–9% of men (studies suggest higher with better methodology), India may have 15–30 million men with Peyronie's disease. Yet it is almost invisible in Indian medical prac

diseases.name: Priapism
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS — IMMEDIATE ASSESSMENT
Clinical history: duration (> 4 hours = emergency treatment regardless); association with sexual stimulation; medications; substance use; sickle cell or haematological history
Penile examination: fully rigid, painful corpora (ischaemic) vs partial rigidity, painless (high-flow non-ischaemic)
CAVERNOUS BLOOD GAS ASPIRATION

Diagnostic and therapeutic aspiration: 21-gauge needle into lateral corpus cavernosum; aspirate 5–10 mL blood; send for: pH (<7.25 = acidosis = ischaemic); PO2 (<30 mmHg = ischaemia); PCO2 (>60 mmHg = ischaemia); appearance: dark blood (ischaemic/venous) vs bright red (well-oxygenated = non-ischaemic/high-flow); this test IS the immediate diagnostic AND therapeutic first step
PENILE COLOUR DUPLEX DOPPLER

If high-flow priapism suspected: Doppler of cavernosal arteries; turbulent or absent cavernosal arterial flow = ischaemic; high-velocity flow with fistula = non-ischaemic; identifies arteriovenous fistula in non-ischaemic for embolisation planning
HAEMATOLOGICAL
Full blood count (FBC) — sickle cell, leukaemia, thalassaemia evaluation
Sickle cell preparation / haemoglobin electrophoresis — if SCD suspected; especially tribal India patient
generic_medicines: ISCHAEMIC PRIAPISM — EMERGENCY TREATMENT

Step 1 — Aspiration: Aspirate 20–50 mL blood from corpus cavernosum via lateral approach until drainage blood becomes bright red; usually reduces erection; repeat aspiration PRN; FIRST AND IMMEDIATE STEP
Step 2 — Intracavernosal sympathomimetic injection

Phenylephrine 100–500 mcg intracavernosal every 3–5 minutes × up to 1 hour — PREFERRED agent; α1-adrenergic agonist; minimal cardiovascular effect; recommended AUA 2021; titrated injection into corpus cavernosum; BP and cardiac monitoring mandatory
Epinephrine (adrenaline) 0.01 mg diluted — if phenylephrine unavailable (India: may not be available as IC formulation); significant cardiac risk
Etilefrine (available in India as Effortil) — α-agonist; used for priapism particularly in SCD-associated stuttering priapism; 50 mg self-injection programme; oral tablet for prophylaxis
Step 3 — If aspiration + sympathomimetic fails → Surgical shunting (T-shaped shunt, Al-Ghorab, or Quackels shunt) — urological surgical procedure; corporal-spongiosal shunt; last resort
NON-ISCHAEMIC PRIAPISM
Selective pudendal artery embolisation — gel foam or coil embolisation of AVF; interventional radiology; elective; NOT emergency
SICKLE CELL ASSOCIATED PRIAPISM
Simultaneous SCD crisis management: IV fluids; analgesia (morphine); oxygen; haematology referral for exchange transfusion if indicated
Long-term prophylaxis: Hydroxyurea 500–1500 mg OD — reduces HbS, reduces SCD crisis frequency, reduces stuttering priapism recurrence
PROPHYLAXIS FOR RECURRENT/STUTTERING PRIAPISM

PDE5 inhibitor (paradoxical maintenance therapy): Low-dose Sildenafil 25 mg OD or Tadalafil 5 mg OD daily — paradoxically prevents recurrent ischaemic priapism via upregulation of PDE5 and increased cGMP in smooth muscle; reducing hypersensitivity to nocturnal erections; counter-intuitive but evidence-based
GnRH agonist (Leuprolide) — reduces testosterone → reduces nocturnal erections → reduces stuttering priapism frequency (for refractory SCD-associated cases)
janaushadhi_medicines: Phenylephrine injection (may need specialist pharmacy or hospital procurement — ICU available)
Etilefrine 25 mg tablet (Effortil brand equivalent; α-agonist for prophylaxis / stuttering priapism)
Hydroxyurea 500 mg capsule (for SCD-associated priapism prophylaxis)
Sildenafil 25 mg tablet (paradoxical prophylactic therapy for stuttering priapism)
Morphine / pethedine injection (inpatient SCD crisis analgesia)
ayurvedic_medicines: SICKLE CELL DISEASE — ANTI-SICKLING HERBS

Haridra (Curcumin) — anti-inflammatory; reduces HbS polymerisation; anti-SCD cell adhesion to endothelium; reduces vascular inflammation in SCD crisis; 500–1000 mg curcumin BD; limited but growing evidence base for SCD management
Nilavembu (Andrographis paniculata) — anti-inflammatory; Tamil Ayurveda tradition for blood disorders; possible anti-sickling properties via haematological anti-inflammatory effect
Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — immuno-modulator; anti-inflammatory; reduces vascular endothelial inflammation in SCD-related vascular events
TONIFYING (POST-PRIAPISM ERECTILE RECOVERY)
Ashwagandha — testosterone support; corpus cavernosum smooth muscle health; reduces post-priapism anxiety and secondary psychogenic ED; 300 mg BD after acute episode resolves
Shilajit — mitochondrial support; penile smooth muscle recovery; anti-fibrotic; of theoretical benefit post-priapism for reducing corporal fibrosis from ischaemic insult
Gokshura (Tribulus terrestris) — LH-stimulating; testosterone support; erectile recovery post-priapism
IMPORTANT NOTE

Ischaemic priapism is an emergency — Ayurvedic medicines play NO ROLE in acute management. In India, the greatest risk is patient delay due to shame and the consequent irreversible ED from prolonged ischaemia. Emergency education: any erection > 4 hours that persists without sexual stimulation and becomes painful = EMERGENCY; must go to hospital IMMEDIATELY. Post-successful treatment: Ayurvedic support for penile smooth muscle recovery (Ashwagandha, Shilajit) is appropriate. SCD-associated stuttering priapism: Curcumin as anti-inflammatory adjunct alongside Hydroxyurea. Sickle cell disease management in tribal India requires community awareness — priapism is often the presenting or recurrent crisis complication that goes untreated.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors SICKLE CELL DISEASE — INDIA CONTEXT Sickle cell disease (SCD) is disproportionately prevalent in India: 300,000+ SCD births/year in India (the world's second largest burden after sub-Saharan Africa); concentrated in tribal populations of Chhattisgarh, Odisha, Jharkhand, MP, Maharashtra (Vidarbha), and Gujarat; Sickle cell crisis → sludging of sickled erythrocytes in cavernous sinusoids → ischaemic priapism; SCD-associated priapism represents the most i
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations PRIAPISM IS A UROLOGICAL EMERGENCY — EVERY INDIAN EMERGENCY PHYSICIAN AND GP MUST KNOW THE 4-HOUR WINDOW: Ischaemic priapism causes irreversible damage through progressive corporal hypoxia and acidosis. The timeline of damage: 4–6 hours: hypoxic stress begins; 6–24 hours: en

diseases.name: Retrograde Ejaculation
tests: POST-ORGASM URINALYSIS — GOLD STANDARD

Patient masturbates to orgasm; provides urine sample IMMEDIATELY after (within 5 minutes); centrifuge urine; microscopy of sediment; presence of sperm (>10–15 sperm per high-power field) + fructose in post-orgasm urine = confirms RE; simple, cheap, decisive test
SEMEN ANALYSIS
Hypospermia (<1.5 mL) or anejaculation; low sperm count proportional to retrograde volume; low total sperm count despite normal testicular function
NEUROLOGICAL ASSESSMENT

Detailed history of surgery, diabetes, spinal cord injury, MS, medications; diabetic autonomic neuropathy testing: heart rate variability, postural BP drop, sweating assessment; EMG lumbar nerve root evaluation if spinal cause suspected
MEDICATION REVIEW
Current alpha-blockers, antihypertensives (guanethidine, methyldopa), antipsychotics (chlorpromazine — α-blockade), TCAs (imipramine — α1 agonist effects; paradoxically used to TREAT RE)
SPINAL MRI
If cord injury, MS, or spinal stenosis causing autonomic dysfunction suspected
generic_medicines: PHARMACOLOGICAL — SYMPATHOMIMETIC AGENTS (RESTORE BLADDER NECK CLOSURE)

Imipramine 25–75 mg OD — TCA; the primary pharmacological treatment for RE; α1-adrenergic agonist + noradrenaline reuptake inhibition at bladder neck; achieves antegrade ejaculation in 30–60% of cases; particularly effective for sympathetically mediated RE (post-TURP less effective); start 25 mg OD; increase to 50–75 mg

Pseudoephedrine 30–60 mg before intercourse — sympathomimetic; α1-agonist; increases bladder neck tone; used particularly for intercourse/conception attempts; available OTC India (decongestant formulations); needs to be taken 30–60 min before sexual activity; India availability: common cold preparations contain it
Ephedrine 25–50 mg — similar to pseudoephedrine; older agent; less available in modern pharmacy
Brompheniramine with phenylpropanolamine — combination sympathomimetic (antihistamine + decongestant); older regimen for RE
SPERM RETRIEVAL FOR FERTILITY

Post-orgasm urine alkalinisation + sperm wash → IUI: patient alkalinises urine 12–24 hours before sperm collection (sodium bicarbonate 5g 4× daily); collects post-orgasm urine in sperm-friendly medium; lab washes sperm; uses for IUI (intrauterine insemination); achieves pregnancy in 20–30% of cycles; most common fertility approach for RE in India
IVF-ICSI with retrograde sperm: higher success rates; sperm from alkalinised post-orgasm urine injected into egg; for couples not achieving IUI success
Testicular sperm extraction (TESE) — if pharmacological + urine retrieval fails; surgical sperm aspiration from testis for ICSI
UNDERLYING CAUSE TREATMENT
Optimise diabetes: HbA1c target < 7%; reduces progressive autonomic neuropathy; Metformin, insulin optimisation
Stop/switch alpha-blocker: if RE from Tamsulosin/Silodosin → switch to Alfuzosin (lower RE rate) or 5-ARI (Finasteride/Dutasteride); RE reverses in majority after stopping alpha-blocker
janaushadhi_medicines: Pseudoephedrine-containing cold tablets (e.g., Actifed equivalent — combination formulations)
Sodium bicarbonate powder (for urine alkalinisation pre-sperm retrieval)
Metformin 500/850/1000 mg tablet (for diabetic RE — glycaemic control)
Alfuzosin 10 mg tablet (switch from silodosin/tamsulosin for drug-induced RE)
Finasteride 5 mg tablet (alternative BPH treatment avoiding RE)
ayurvedic_medicines: AUTONOMIC NERVE TROPHIC SUPPORT

Ashwagandha — adaptogen; reduces cortisol; neuroprotective via BDNF upregulation; protects autonomic nervous system from diabetic neuropathic damage; of most value as preventive in diabetic patients at risk for autonomic neuropathy → RE; 300 mg BD

Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — neurotrophin enhancement; acetylcholinesterase inhibition; supports peripheral nerve health; neuroprotective; useful adjunct for autonomic neuropathy-associated ejaculatory dysfunction

Alpha Lipoic Acid + Benfotiamine (Biochemical Ayurveda interface) — antioxidant approach to diabetic neuropathy; reduces oxidative stress damaging autonomic nerve fibres; ALA has RCT evidence for diabetic neuropathy; though synthetic, clinically relevant for diabetic RE
SEMINAL VESICLE AND VAS TONIC
Kapikacchu (Mucuna pruriens) — L-DOPA precursor; dopaminergic neurotransmission; may support ejaculatory reflex control at spinal cord level; traditional Vajikarana for ejaculatory health; 500 mg BD
Gokshura (Tribulus terrestris) — LH stimulation; testosterone support; male reproductive tract tonic; traditional Vajikarana herb for ejaculatory disorders
Shilajit — mitochondrial support; reproductive system tonic; anti-diabetic and antioxidant; supports reproductive tract health and neuroprotection
IMPORTANT NOTE

RE management in fertility context in India requires a practical pathway: Post-orgasm urinalysis (confirm RE) → Imipramine trial (30–60% success for antegrade conversion) → if failed, urine alkalinisation + sperm wash for IUI → IVF-ICSI if needed. In India, the most common reversible cause is alpha-blocker medication — easily addressed by switching to alfuzosin or finasteride. Diabetic RE is the most common organic cause — glycaemic optimisation is both preventive and partially therapeutic. Ashwagandha and Brahmi support the neuroprotective dimension relevant to diabetic autonomic neuropathy underlying RE.
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors DIABETES-ASSOCIATED RETROGRADE EJACULATION India has 90+ million people with type 2 diabetes — the world's largest diabetic population. Diabetic autonomic neuropathy is the most common cause of RE in India (along with TURP/prostate surgery). The sympathetic neural pathway controlling bladder neck closure (hypogastric nerve, L1–L3) is damaged by diabetic peripheral and autonomic neuropathy. RE from DM typically develops after 5–10 years of diabetes, par
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations RETROGRADE EJACULATION IS A TREATABLE AND OFTEN OVERLOOKED CAUSE OF MALE INFERTILITY IN INDIA: Male factor infertility is responsible for 40–50% of couples' inability to conceive. In India, male infertility workup is frequently incomplete — many men with azoospermia or sever

diseases.name: Genito-Pelvic Pain Penetration
tests: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT OF ALL FOUR DIMENSIONS
Dimension 1: sexual history — penetration history; speculum/tampon difficulty; partner account
Dimension 2: gyn examination — vulvar pain mapping (Q-tip), speculum exam, bimanual; endometriosis signs, atrophy, infection
Dimension 3: FSFI fear domain; Female Sexual Distress Scale (FSDS); Pain Catastrophizing Scale (PCS); IES-R if trauma
Dimension 4: pelvic floor assessment — levator ani palpation; perineometry; biofeedback EMG
VALIDATED TOOLS
Female Sexual Function Index (FSFI) — 6 domains; pain domain (Q17-Q19) specifically scores penetration pain; arousal, lubrication, orgasm domains also affected
Female Sexual Distress Scale (FSDS) — measures associated distress; required for diagnosis (clinically significant distress criterion)
Painful Intercourse Self-Efficacy Scale (PISES) — measures confidence to manage pain; relevant Dimension 3
PHYSICAL TESTS
generic_medicines: TREATMENT IS DIMENSION-SPECIFIC AND MULTIMODAL
Dimension 1 Treatment
Graduated vaginal dilator programme — cornerstone for penetration difficulty; patient-directed; sizes XS → XL progressively; 80–90% success rate with guidance
Partner engagement — inclusion in dilator programme; non-demand touch exercises (sensate focus, Masters and Johnson protocol)
Dimension 2 Treatment — depends on organic cause
Vaginal oestrogen — GSM/atrophic dyspareunia; highly effective
Dienogest 2 mg OD / GnRH agonist — endometriosis-associated deep dyspareunia
Amitriptyline 10–50 mg nocte or Gabapentin 300 mg BD — vestibulodynia neuropathic pain
Lidocaine 5% ointment topical — before dilator use or intercourse
Antibiotics/antifungals — if infection confirmed on culture
Dimension 3 Treatment
Cognitive Behavioural Therapy (CBT) — pain catastrophising, fear conditioning; core psychotherapy for Dimension 3; 8–12 sessions evidence-based
Mindfulness-based CBT — reduces hypervigilance toward genital sensations; interoceptive awareness without threat appraisal
EMDR — for trauma-associated fear/anxiety (Dimension 3 with trauma history)
Dimension 4 Treatment
janaushadhi_medicines: Diazepam 2 mg tablet (short term; pelvic floor relaxation adjunct)
ayurvedic_medicines: ANXIETY AND FEAR DIMENSION (DIMENSION 3)

Ashwagandha — cortisol reduction; anticipatory anxiety reduction; HPA axis normalisation; most evidence-based Ayurvedic anxiolytic; particularly relevant for the fear/anxiety dimension (Dimension 3) of GPPPD; 300–600 mg BD
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — GABAergic; anxiolytic; reduces hypervigilance and catastrophising; 300 mg BD
Sarpagandha (Rauwolfia serpentina) — CNS sedative; reserpine depletes monoamines; traditional anti-anxiety; short-term use; supervise for hypotension
PAIN AND INFLAMMATION DIMENSION (DIMENSION 2)
Haridra (Curcumin) — anti-inflammatory; COX-2 inhibitor; anti-endometriosis; TRPV1 modulation for vestibulodynia component; 1000 mg phytosome BD
Ashoka bark — uterine anti-inflammatory; dysmenorrhoea and dyspareunia in endometriosis context
Shatavari — phytooestrogenic; restores vaginal mucosal health in atrophic dyspareunia; reduces dryness; lubricating properties
PELVIC FLOOR AND TOPICAL

Sesame oil warm perineal massage (Yoni Abhyanga) — relaxes pelvic floor superficially; anti-inflammatory; traditional India practice; reduces tissue hypertonicity; maintains tissue health for Dimension 4 indirect support
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors BIOPSYCHOSOCIAL INDIA PROFILE In India, GPPPD arises from the intersection of: (1) Dimension 1 — No sex education → anatomical ignorance → penetration attempts without knowledge; no pre-marital exploration; first attempt on wedding night under performance pressure → traumatic penetration failure; (2) Dimension 2 — Organic causes: endometriosis (delayed diagnosis 7–10 years), episiotomy scar, atrophic vaginitis (post-menopausal), infections (Candida, ST
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations GPPPD UNIFIES THE BIOPSYCHOSOCIAL MODEL OF SEXUAL PAIN — INDIA'S CLINICIANS NEED TO ADOPT THIS FRAMEWORK: The DSM-5 GPPPD diagnostic framework is important for Indian clinical practice because: (1) It moves away from a single symptom label (vaginismus = muscle problem; dyspa

diseases.name: Post-Orgasmic Illness Syndrome
tests: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS — WALDINGER CRITERIA

Diagnosis is primarily clinical: Waldinger 2011 criteria assessment (see symptom section); symptom diary: patient records every sexual/orgasmic episode and correlates with symptom onset, duration, character; retrospective review of past 'illness episodes' often reveals POIS pattern
INTRADERMAL SKIN TEST WITH AUTOLOGOUS SEMEN

Waldinger 2011: intradermal injection of autologous semen (diluted 1:40,000); positive wheal-and-flare reaction = type I IgE-mediated hypersensitivity to own semen; confirms allergy-mediated POIS; approximately 30-50% of tested POIS patients have positive skin test (type I); others have type II (delayed-type, cell-mediated) or cytokine mechanism — negative skin test does NOT exclude POIS
MAST CELL ACTIVATION MARKERS
Serum tryptase (within 1–4 hours of symptom onset) — mast cell activation marker; elevated in mast cell activation syndrome (MCAS); MCAS may underlie POIS in some patients
24-hour urine prostaglandin D2 metabolite — mast cell mediator
CYTOKINE PANEL
IL-6, TNF-α, IFN-γ during symptomatic episode — elevated in POIS; suggests cytokine storm mechanism; research context
STANDARD EXCLUSIONS
generic_medicines: PHARMACOLOGICAL — LIMITED EVIDENCE (POIS IS ULTRA-RARE; RCT EVIDENCE ABSENT)
ANTIHISTAMINES — MOST USED EMPIRICAL TREATMENT

H1-antihistamines before or at first symptom: Fexofenadine 180 mg; Cetirizine 10 mg; Loratadine 10 mg — reduce allergic/mast cell mediated symptom component; first-line empirical treatment given safety profile; taken 30–60 min before anticipated orgasm for prophylaxis
H2-antihistamine: Ranitidine 150 mg / Famotidine 40 mg — combined H1+H2 antihistamine blockade for stronger mast cell suppression
HYPOSENSITISATION / DESENSITISATION PROTOCOL

Autologous semen desensitisation: intradermal escalating dose injections of own semen over weeks → tolerance development; reported by Waldinger to markedly reduce POIS severity; requires specialist allergist; very rarely performed; limited to specialist centres
NSAIDs
Ibuprofen 400–600 mg / Naproxen 500 mg at onset — reduces prostaglandin-mediated systemic inflammation; particularly for muscle aches, fever, malaise component
BENZODIAZEPINES — SHORT-TERM
Lorazepam 0.5–1 mg or Clonazepam 0.5 mg — reduces post-orgasm anxiety and CNS inflammatory response; helps cognitive symptoms and mood component; short-term use only; evidence from case reports
TESTOSTERONE
Testosterone replacement (if low testosterone confirmed) — POIS in hypogonadal men may improve with testosterone normalisation; case reports
SELECTIVE SEROTONIN REUPTAKE INHIBITORS
SSRI (Paroxetine 20 mg, Citalopram 20 mg) — cytoprotective and anti-inflammatory; anti-serotonin-sensitisation; may reduce post-orgasm immunological response; benefit reported in case series
MAST CELL STABILISER
janaushadhi_medicines: Clonazepam 0.5 mg tablet (short-term; CNS component management)
ayurvedic_medicines: IMMUNE MODULATION AND ANTI-ALLERGIC

Haridra (Curcumin) — mast cell stability; inhibits mast cell degranulation and histamine release; COX-2 inhibitor reducing prostaglandin mediators; NF-κB inhibitor reducing cytokine storm; TNF-α and IL-6 reduction; mechanistically one of the most plausible Ayurvedic agents for POIS given the immune-mediated pathophysiology; 500–1000 mg curcumin phytosome BD; taken prophylactically

Tulsi (Holy Basil / Ocimum sanctum) — adaptogen; anti-allergic; mast cell stabilising; COX-1 and COX-2 inhibition; traditional anti-inflammatory; reduces hypersensitivity reactions; 300–500 mg extract BD

Shirisha (Albizia lebbeck) — classical Ayurvedic anti-allergic herb; mast cell stabilisation; used in allergic conditions; Shirisha arishtam for respiratory allergy; anti-histaminic properties from saponins; appropriate for the allergic/mucosal component of POIS
Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) — immuno-modulator; reduces excessive immune activation; adaptogen; anti-inflammatory; reduces cytokine-mediated sickness behaviour
NEURO-INFLAMMATORY COMPONENT
Brahmi — neuroinflammation reduction; reduces post-orgasmic neurological symptoms (brain fog, cognitive impairment, mood disturbance) via anti-neuroinflammatory mechanisms; BDNF support
Shankhpushpi — anxiolytic; cerebral tonic; reduces post-orgasmic psychological distress; supports cognitive recovery
RASAYANA / VITALITY

Ashwagandha — adaptogen; anti-inflammatory; reduces HPA axis dysregulation; supports recovery from each POIS episode; reduces post-orgasm fatigue and weakness; anti-inflammatory via NF-κB inhibition; perhaps most relevant comprehensive Ayurvedic agent for POIS
diseases.india_specific: India-Specific Complications & Risk Factors COMPLETE CLINICAL INVISIBILITY IN INDIA POIS has not been reported in Indian literature and is not recognised in Indian clinical practice. Indian patients with POIS will typically have encountered: (1) Ayurvedic physicians who attribute their symptoms to 'shukra dhatu depletion' or 'veerya kshaya' (semen depletion theory) — which happens to capture the symptomatic pattern from a different mechanistic lens; (2) GPs who diagnose recurrent upper respirato
diseases.important_notes: Important Considerations POIS IS REAL AND INDIA'S MEDICAL COMMUNITY MUST LEARN TO RECOGNISE IT — BREAKING THE CYCLE OF MISDIAGNOSIS: Post-Orgasmic Illness Syndrome likely affects many more people than the handful of reported cases globally — because it is massively under-reported and under-recognise
